items.explanation: 'Avoir' also has irregular imperative forms from the subjunctive: aie (tu), ayons (nous), ayez (vous). 'Aie patience!' is a common expression meaning 'be patient' (literally 'have patience'). French uses 'avoir' where English uses 'be' in many expressions: avoir faim (be hungry), avoir soif (be thirsty), avoir peur (be afraid). So the imperative of 'avoir' often translates to 'be...!' in English, which can surprise learners.

items.question: _____ -moi la vérité !
items.translation: (Tell me the truth!)
items.explanation: The tu-form imperative of 'dire' is 'dis' — keeping the 's' because 'dire' is not an -ER verb. Object pronouns in affirmative imperatives follow the verb and are connected by a hyphen: 'Dis-moi!' Also note: 'me' becomes 'moi' and 'te' becomes 'toi' in affirmative imperatives (stressed forms). In negative imperatives, they revert: 'Ne me dis pas!' This pronoun placement rule is essential for natural-sounding French commands.

items.question: _____ -vous, le spectacle va commencer !
items.translation: (Sit down, the show is about to start!)
items.explanation: Reflexive verbs in the imperative place the reflexive pronoun after the verb with a hyphen: 'Asseyez-vous!' The pronoun changes to stressed form: te becomes toi for tu-form ('Assieds-toi!'), but nous and vous stay the same. In negative form, the pronoun moves before: 'Ne vous asseyez pas!' 'S'asseoir' has variant forms (asseyez/assoyez), but 'asseyez' is standard. This pattern applies to all reflexive imperatives: 'Lève-toi! Dépêchons-nous!'

items.question: _____ attention en traversant la rue !
items.translation: (Pay attention when crossing the street!)
items.explanation: The tu-form imperative of 'faire' is 'fais' (keeping the 's' — only -ER verbs drop it). 'Faire attention' is a common expression meaning 'to pay attention' or 'to be careful.' Other useful 'faire' imperatives: 'Fais de ton mieux!' (Do your best!), 'Ne fais pas ça!' (Don't do that!), 'Faisons la fête!' (Let's party!). The three forms are: fais (tu), faisons (nous), faites (vous) — 'faites' has a unique spelling.

items.question: Ne _____ pas peur, tout ira bien !
items.translation: (Don't be afraid, everything will be fine!)
items.explanation: Negative imperative of 'avoir' (tu form): 'N'aie pas peur!' Remember 'avoir peur' = to be afraid, so French says 'don't have fear' where English says 'don't be afraid.' The 'n'' before 'aie' shows the elision of 'ne' before a vowel. Negative imperatives with 'avoir': 'N'aie pas honte!' (Don't be ashamed!), 'N'ayez pas de regrets!' (Have no regrets!). The structure remains ne + verb + pas regardless of the verb's irregularity.

items.question: _____ ici tout de suite !
items.translation: (Come here right away!)
items.explanation: The tu-form imperative of 'venir' is 'viens' — again keeping the 's' since it's not an -ER verb. 'Venir' imperatives: viens (tu), venons (nous), venez (vous). 'Tout de suite' means 'right away/immediately.' French imperatives can be softened with 's'il te plaît/s'il vous plaît' or strengthened with adverbs like 'tout de suite' or 'immédiatement.' The tone and context determine whether the imperative sounds like a polite request or a stern command.

items.question: _____ -lui un message pour confirmer.
items.translation: (Send him/her a message to confirm.)
items.explanation: 'Envoyer' is an -ER verb, so the tu imperative drops the 's': 'Envoie!' (not 'Envoies!'). The indirect object pronoun 'lui' follows the verb with a hyphen in affirmative imperatives: 'Envoie-lui!' With two pronouns, the order is: verb + direct object + indirect object: 'Envoie-le-lui!' In negative form, everything reverses: 'Ne lui envoie pas de message!' Pronoun placement in imperatives requires practice but follows consistent rules.

items.question: _____ bien ce soir, les enfants !
items.translation: (Sleep well tonight, children!)
items.explanation: Addressing 'les enfants' (children, plural) requires the vous-form: 'Dormez!' For 'dormir': dors (tu), dormons (nous), dormez (vous). Note 'dormir' loses the 'm' in the singular: je dors, tu dors — and the imperative follows the singular present indicative for tu: 'Dors!' The added address term 'les enfants' is a vocative — it identifies who is being commanded. French commonly adds these: 'Écoutez, classe!' 'Viens, mon chéri!'

items.question: _____ -y tout de suite !
items.translation: (Go there right away!)
items.explanation: Here's a tricky exception: 'aller' normally drops the 's' in tu imperative ('Va!'), but when followed by 'y,' the 's' returns for pronunciation ease: 'Vas-y!' The 's' creates a liaison sound [z] between 'vas' and 'y,' making it phonetically smooth. Similarly: 'Donnes-en!' (Give some!) adds an 's' before 'en.' This euphonic 's' only appears before 'y' and 'en' with -ER verb tu imperatives. 'Vas-y!' is extremely common in spoken French.

items.question: _____ ce que je vous dis !
items.translation: (Do what I tell you!)
items.explanation: The vous-form imperative of 'faire' is 'faites' — note the irregular spelling (not 'faisez'). 'Faire' is one of the most common and versatile French verbs, and its imperative forms (fais, faisons, faites) are used constantly. 'Ce que je vous dis' is a relative clause meaning 'what I tell you.' The combination of imperative + relative clause is very natural in French: 'Prenez ce que vous voulez!' (Take what you want!), 'Choisissez ce qui vous plaît!' (Choose what pleases you!).

description: Practice the imperative mood for giving commands, instructions, and making suggestions in French

name: Verbes Pronominaux (Reflexive Verbs)

items.question: Je _____ à sept heures tous les matins.
items.translation: (I wake up at seven o'clock every morning.)
items.explanation: Reflexive verbs require a reflexive pronoun that matches the subject: je → me, tu → te, il/elle → se, nous → nous, vous → vous, ils/elles → se. 'Se réveiller' (to wake up) becomes 'je me réveille.' The pronoun goes directly before the conjugated verb. Many daily routine verbs are reflexive in French but not in English: se réveiller, se lever, se laver, se brosser, s'habiller. This is a key structural difference between the two languages.

items.question: Elle _____ les dents après chaque repas.
items.translation: (She brushes her teeth after every meal.)
items.explanation: 'Se brosser les dents' (to brush one's teeth) uses 'se' for elle. Notice that French says 'les dents' (the teeth) not 'ses dents' (her teeth) — with reflexive verbs involving body parts, French uses the definite article instead of a possessive adjective. The reflexive pronoun already indicates whose teeth: 'elle se brosse les dents' = she brushes THE teeth (her own, implied by 'se'). This rule applies to all body part actions: 'Je me lave les mains.'

items.question: Nous _____ bien amusés à la fête hier soir.
items.translation: (We had a great time at the party last night.)
items.explanation: All reflexive verbs use 'être' as their auxiliary in compound tenses (passé composé, plus-que-parfait, etc.), never 'avoir.' 'S'amuser' becomes 'nous nous sommes amusés.' The structure is: subject + reflexive pronoun + être + past participle. The past participle agrees with the subject: 'nous...amusés' (masculine plural) or 'nous...amusées' (feminine plural). This être-auxiliary rule for reflexive verbs is absolute — memorize it as a non-negotiable rule.

items.question: Tu _____ trop vite, ralentis un peu !
items.translation: (You're getting dressed too quickly, slow down a bit!)
items.explanation: 'S'habiller' (to get dressed) with tu: 'tu t'habilles.' Notice the elision: 'te' becomes 't'' before the vowel 'h' (which is silent in 'habiller'). French reflexive pronouns elide before vowels and silent 'h': je m'habille, tu t'habilles, il s'habille. This elision is mandatory in writing and speech. The verb 'habiller' alone means 'to dress someone else,' while 's'habiller' means 'to dress oneself' — the reflexive adds the self-directed meaning.

items.question: Ils _____ de bonne heure le dimanche.
items.translation: (They go to bed early on Sundays.)
items.explanation: 'Se coucher' (to go to bed) with ils: 'ils se couchent.' Note 'le dimanche' with the definite article means 'on Sundays' (habitual) — a useful pattern we saw with the imparfait. Common reflexive daily routine verbs form a natural sequence: se réveiller (wake up), se lever (get up), se laver (wash), s'habiller (get dressed), se coucher (go to bed), s'endormir (fall asleep). Learning them as a chain helps retention.

items.question: Comment vous _____ ?
items.translation: (What is your name? — literally: How do you call yourself?)
items.explanation: 'S'appeler' (to be called/named) is one of the first reflexive verbs every French learner encounters. 'Comment vous appelez-vous?' or 'Comment vous vous appelez?' Notice the double 'vous' — one is the subject pronoun, the other is the reflexive pronoun. In formal register, inversion places the subject after the verb: 'appelez-vous.' 'S'appeler' doubles the 'l' before silent endings: je m'appelle, but nous nous appelons. This spelling change affects pronunciation.

items.question: Ne _____ pas, tout va s'arranger.
items.translation: (Don't worry, everything will work out.)
items.explanation: In negative commands (negative imperative), the reflexive pronoun goes BEFORE the verb: 'Ne t'inquiète pas!' This is the opposite of affirmative imperatives where it follows: 'Inquiète-toi!' (worry! — rarely used). The pronoun also reverts to unstressed form before the verb: 'toi' becomes 'te/t''. 'S'inquiéter' (to worry) has an accent change in certain forms: inquiète (with è), but inquiéter (with é). Negative reflexive imperatives are very common in reassuring speech.

items.question: Pierre et Marie _____ rencontrés à l'université.
items.translation: (Pierre and Marie met at university.)
items.explanation: 'Se rencontrer' here is reciprocal — they met each other. Reciprocal reflexives describe actions people do to one another: se parler (talk to each other), se regarder (look at each other), s'aimer (love each other). In passé composé: 'ils se sont rencontrés.' Past participle agrees with the subject (rencontrés, masculine plural). Auxiliary is always 'être.' This reciprocal use adds a rich social dimension to reflexive verbs beyond just daily routines.

items.question: Je dois _____ tôt demain pour mon vol.
items.translation: (I have to get up early tomorrow for my flight.)
items.explanation: After modal verbs (devoir, pouvoir, vouloir) or other conjugated verbs, the reflexive verb stays in the infinitive but the pronoun matches the subject: 'je dois ME lever' (not 'se lever'). The pronoun always agrees with the actual subject performing the action. Compare: 'tu peux te reposer,' 'nous devons nous dépêcher,' 'ils veulent s'amuser.' The conjugated verb carries the tense; the reflexive infinitive carries the reflexive meaning.

items.question: Elle _____ les cheveux chaque matin.
items.translation: (She washes her hair every morning.)
items.explanation: 'Se laver' (to wash oneself) with a body part: 'elle se lave les cheveux.' Again, French uses the definite article 'les' (not 'ses') with body parts when a reflexive verb is present. The reflexive pronoun already clarifies possession. If she washed someone else's hair, it would be 'elle lave les cheveux de quelqu'un' (non-reflexive). This definite article rule with reflexive verbs + body parts is consistent and must be respected in both speech and writing.

items.question: Dépêche-_____, nous allons être en retard !
items.translation: (Hurry up, we're going to be late!)
items.explanation: In affirmative imperatives, the reflexive pronoun follows the verb with a hyphen, and 'te' becomes the stressed form 'toi': 'Dépêche-toi!' For nous and vous, the pronouns stay the same: 'Dépêchons-nous!' 'Dépêchez-vous!' But in negative imperatives, the pronoun returns before the verb in its unstressed form: 'Ne te dépêche pas!' This tu/toi alternation (unstressed/stressed) in reflexive imperatives is a detail that distinguishes proficient speakers.

items.question: Ils _____ beaucoup pendant les vacances.
items.translation: (They rested a lot during the holidays.)
items.explanation: 'Se reposer' in passé composé: 'ils se sont reposés.' Three essential elements: reflexive pronoun (se) + auxiliary être (sont) + past participle agreeing with subject (reposés, masculine plural). If the subject were feminine: 'elles se sont reposées.' The agreement rule for reflexive verbs in compound tenses: the participle agrees with the reflexive pronoun when it's a direct object. In 'se reposer,' 'se' is the direct object, so agreement applies.

items.question: Ma fille _____ dans sa chambre sans faire de bruit.
items.translation: (My daughter locked herself in her room without making a sound.)
items.explanation: 'S'enfermer' (to lock oneself in) in passé composé: 's'est enfermée.' The past participle takes an extra 'e' because 'ma fille' is feminine singular. This feminine agreement is reflected in writing but often not in pronunciation (enfermé and enfermée sound the same). However, with some participles, you CAN hear the difference: 'elle s'est assise' (seated, you hear the final 'z' sound) vs. 'il s'est assis.'

items.question: Nous _____ bien entendus dès le premier jour.
items.translation: (We got along well from the first day.)
items.explanation: 'S'entendre' (to get along) is a reciprocal reflexive verb — the action is mutual between two or more people. In passé composé: 'nous nous sommes bien entendus.' The double 'nous' (subject + reflexive pronoun) followed by 'sommes' creates a distinctive pattern that takes practice. Other common reciprocal verbs: se comprendre (understand each other), se disputer (argue with each other), se téléphoner (call each other).

items.question: Elle ne _____ pas maquillée ce matin.
items.translation: (She didn't put on makeup this morning.)
items.explanation: Negative passé composé with reflexive verbs: 'ne' goes before the reflexive pronoun, 'pas' after the auxiliary: 'Elle ne s'est pas maquillée.' The word order is: subject + ne + reflexive pronoun + être + pas + past participle. 'S'a' does not exist — reflexive verbs ALWAYS use être, never avoir. This is a common error for learners. Even verbs that normally take 'avoir' switch to 'être' when made reflexive: 'elle a lavé' becomes 'elle s'est lavée.'

description: Master reflexive verb conjugation and usage for daily routines, reciprocal actions, and pronominal expressions

name: Articles Partitifs (Partitive Articles)

items.question: Je voudrais _____ pain, s'il vous plaît.
items.translation: (I would like some bread, please.)
items.explanation: Partitive articles (du, de la, de l', des) express an unspecified quantity — 'some' or 'any.' 'Du' is used before masculine singular nouns: 'du pain' (some bread). It contracts from 'de + le.' The partitive is essential in French where English often uses no article at all: 'I want bread' becomes 'Je veux du pain.' French requires you to specify whether you mean ALL the bread (le pain), A bread (un pain), or SOME bread (du pain). This distinction doesn't exist in English.

items.question: Elle boit _____ eau minérale tous les jours.
items.translation: (She drinks mineral water every day.)
items.explanation: Before singular nouns starting with a vowel or silent 'h,' the partitive article is 'de l'' (regardless of gender). 'Eau' is feminine, but instead of 'de la eau' (impossible due to vowel clash), we use 'de l'eau.' Other examples: de l'huile (oil, feminine), de l'argent (money, masculine), de l'air (air, masculine). The elision rule prevents two vowel sounds from colliding, maintaining the fluid sound that characterizes French pronunciation.

items.question: Il y a _____ confiture dans le placard.
items.translation: (There is some jam in the cupboard.)
items.explanation: 'De la' is the partitive article for feminine singular nouns: 'de la confiture' (some jam). Knowing noun genders is crucial for selecting the right partitive: du (masculine), de la (feminine), de l' (before vowels), des (plural). 'Confiture' is feminine. There's no shortcut for gender — you must learn each noun with its article. However, patterns help: words ending in '-ure' are often feminine (la confiture, la nature, la nourriture).

items.question: Nous avons acheté _____ fruits au marché.
items.translation: (We bought some fruit at the market.)
items.explanation: 'Des' is the plural partitive article: 'des fruits' (some fruits). It's used for countable items in an unspecified quantity. 'Des' is also the plural indefinite article, and in practice they function similarly. Compare: 'les fruits' (the fruits — specific ones), 'des fruits' (some fruits — unspecified), 'tous les fruits' (all fruits). In French, bare plural nouns without an article are grammatically incorrect — you always need des, les, or another determiner.

items.question: Je ne bois pas _____ café.
items.translation: (I don't drink coffee.)
items.explanation: In negative sentences, partitive articles (du, de la, de l', des) all become simply 'de' (or 'd'' before a vowel). 'Je bois du café' becomes 'Je ne bois pas DE café.' This is a fundamental rule: negation reduces all partitives to 'de.' Exceptions: with 'être' ('Ce n'est pas du sucre, c'est du sel') and when emphasizing a specific item rather than expressing quantity. This negative transformation is heavily tested and must become automatic.

items.question: Tu veux _____ salade avec ton steak ?
items.translation: (Do you want some salad with your steak?)
items.explanation: 'Salade' is feminine singular, so the partitive is 'de la salade.' At a restaurant, partitive articles are everywhere because you're discussing portions of food, not entire categories. Compare: 'Je voudrais de la salade' (some salad — partitive), 'Je voudrais une salade' (a/one salad — indefinite), 'J'aime la salade' (I like salad in general — definite, expressing preference). Context determines which article conveys your exact meaning.

items.question: Il fait _____ sport le week-end.
items.translation: (He does sport on weekends.)
items.explanation: 'Faire du sport' uses the partitive because you're doing SOME sport, not all sport ever. 'Faire' + partitive article is a productive pattern: faire du vélo (cycling), faire de la natation (swimming), faire du shopping, faire de la musique (making music). The partitive here conveys engagement in an activity rather than a specific countable instance. This 'faire + du/de la/des' pattern is essential for discussing hobbies and activities.

items.question: Elle a _____ courage pour affronter cette situation.
items.translation: (She has courage to face this situation.)
items.explanation: Partitive articles aren't only for food — they work with abstract nouns too. 'Du courage' (some courage), 'de la patience' (some patience), 'de la chance' (some luck). French treats abstract qualities as uncountable substances, just like food: you have SOME of them, not a specific, complete amount. This parallels English 'some' but French requires the article explicitly. 'Elle a du talent, de la détermination et de l'ambition.'

items.question: Il n'y a plus _____ lait dans le frigo.
items.translation: (There is no more milk in the fridge.)
items.explanation: 'Ne...plus' (no more/no longer) is a negative expression, so the partitive changes to 'de': 'plus de lait' (no more milk). This rule applies to ALL negative expressions: ne...pas de, ne...plus de, ne...jamais de, ne...guère de. The partitive literally disappears into simple 'de' under any negation. 'Il n'y a plus de pain, plus de beurre, plus de fromage' — each partitive becomes 'de' after 'plus.'

items.question: J'ai acheté beaucoup _____ légumes.
items.translation: (I bought a lot of vegetables.)
items.explanation: After expressions of quantity (beaucoup de, peu de, assez de, trop de, un kilo de, une bouteille de), use 'de' alone — never du/de la/des. 'Beaucoup de légumes' (lots of vegetables), NOT 'beaucoup des légumes.' Exceptions: 'bien des' (many) and 'la plupart des' (most of) keep 'des.' This quantity rule is distinct from the negation rule but has the same result: simple 'de.' Both rules strip away the partitive article.

items.question: Avez-vous _____ nouvelles de Pierre ?
items.translation: (Do you have any news from Pierre?)
items.explanation: 'Des nouvelles' (some news) uses the plural partitive. In questions, the partitive is maintained — it only changes to 'de' in negative statements. 'Avez-vous des nouvelles?' (Do you have news?) vs. 'Je n'ai pas de nouvelles' (I have no news). The question is neutral — it doesn't presume positive or negative — so the full partitive 'des' is used. The transformation to 'de' only occurs in the response if negative.

items.question: Il faut ajouter _____ sel à cette recette.
items.translation: (You need to add some salt to this recipe.)
items.explanation: Cooking instructions beautifully illustrate partitive usage. 'Du sel' means an unspecified amount of salt — not all the salt in the world, not one specific grain. Recipes in French are full of partitives: 'Ajoutez du beurre, de la crème, des oignons et de l'ail.' Each ingredient gets its appropriate partitive based on gender and number. 'Sel' is masculine singular, so 'du sel.' This practical context makes partitive articles tangible and memorable.

items.question: Nous avons écouté _____ musique toute la soirée.
items.translation: (We listened to music all evening.)
items.explanation: 'Musique' is feminine singular: 'de la musique' (some music). 'Écouter de la musique' is a classic partitive usage — you listened to some music, not a specific identifiable piece. Compare: 'écouter de la musique' (listen to music in general) vs. 'écouter la musique' (listen to THE music — something specific playing). The partitive vs. definite distinction often trips up English speakers because English doesn't mark this difference.

items.question: Je prends _____ thé avec un peu de lait.
items.translation: (I have tea with a little milk.)
items.explanation: 'Du thé' — partitive for a masculine noun meaning 'some tea.' Notice 'un peu de lait' uses 'de' because 'un peu de' is a quantity expression (like beaucoup de, trop de). In this one sentence, you see both rules: 'du thé' (partitive — no quantity specified) and 'un peu de lait' (de — after a quantity expression). Understanding when to use du/de la vs. plain 'de' is one of the most practical grammar skills for everyday French.

items.question: Elle ne mange jamais _____ viande.
items.translation: (She never eats meat.)
items.explanation: 'Ne...jamais' (never) is negative, so the partitive 'de la' reduces to 'de': 'Elle ne mange jamais de viande.' If the sentence were positive: 'Elle mange de la viande.' Every negative expression works the same way: ne...pas de, ne...plus de, ne...jamais de, ne...rien de. This consistent rule means you only need to ask: 'Is the sentence negative?' If yes, use 'de.' This simplicity is elegant, though remembering to apply it requires practice.

description: Learn when to use du, de la, de l', and des to express unspecified quantities in French

name: Adjectifs Possessifs (Possessive Adjectives)

items.question: C'est _____ livre préféré.
items.translation: (It's my favorite book.)
items.explanation: French possessive adjectives agree with the NOUN they modify, not with the possessor. 'Livre' is masculine singular, so we use 'mon' regardless of whether the speaker is male or female. This is a key difference from English, where 'my' doesn't change. The full set for je: mon (masculine singular), ma (feminine singular), mes (plural). 'Mon livre, ma maison, mes amis.' The possessive adjective replaces the article — never say 'le mon livre.'

items.question: Elle adore _____ chien.
items.translation: (She loves her dog.)
items.explanation: 'Son' is the masculine singular possessive for il/elle. 'Chien' is masculine, so 'son chien' — even though the possessor is female ('elle'). This confuses English speakers who expect 'her dog' = 'sa chien.' But in French, the possessive matches the possessed object's gender, not the owner's gender. 'Son' can mean 'his,' 'her,' or 'its' depending on context. Only the noun's gender matters: son père (his/her father), sa mère (his/her mother).

items.question: Nous avons invité tous _____ amis à la fête.
items.translation: (We invited all our friends to the party.)
items.explanation: 'Notre' (singular) and 'nos' (plural) are the possessive adjectives for nous. 'Amis' is plural, so 'nos amis.' Unlike mon/ma/mes, the nous forms don't distinguish masculine from feminine — 'notre' serves both: 'notre maison' (our house, feminine), 'notre jardin' (our garden, masculine). Only the singular/plural distinction exists: notre (singular anything) vs. nos (plural anything). This simplification makes the nous/vous forms easier to learn.

items.question: _____ amie s'appelle Sophie.
items.translation: (My friend (female) is called Sophie.)
items.explanation: Here's a crucial exception: before feminine nouns starting with a vowel or silent 'h,' 'ma' becomes 'mon' for pronunciation ease. 'Amie' is feminine, but we say 'mon amie' (not 'ma amie') to avoid the vowel clash. Similarly: 'mon école' (my school, feminine), 'mon histoire' (my story, feminine). This euphonic rule applies to all feminine singular nouns beginning with a vowel sound. The possessive sounds masculine but the meaning is clearly feminine from context.

items.question: Les enfants jouent avec _____ jouets.
items.translation: (The children are playing with their toys.)
items.explanation: 'Leur' (singular) and 'leurs' (plural) are possessive adjectives for ils/elles (they). Multiple possessors + multiple objects = 'leurs': 'leurs jouets' (their toys). Compare: 'leur maison' (their house — one house, many owners) vs. 'leurs maisons' (their houses — multiple houses, multiple owners). 'Leur/leurs' never takes an apostrophe — don't confuse with 'leur' as an indirect object pronoun, which is invariable.

items.question: Tu as oublié _____ clés sur la table.
items.translation: (You forgot your keys on the table.)
items.explanation: The tu-form possessives are: ton (masculine singular), ta (feminine singular), tes (plural). 'Clés' is feminine plural, so 'tes clés.' Note that 'tes' doesn't distinguish gender in plural — just like 'mes' and 'ses.' This simplification is helpful: in the plural, you only need to know mes/tes/ses/nos/vos/leurs. The gender distinction (mon vs. ma, ton vs. ta, son vs. sa) only matters in the singular.

items.question: Où est _____ voiture, Monsieur ?
items.translation: (Where is your car, Sir?)
items.explanation: 'Votre' (singular) and 'vos' (plural) are the vous-form possessives, used in formal address or when speaking to multiple people. 'Voiture' is feminine singular, but 'votre' doesn't change for gender — just like 'notre.' 'Votre voiture' (your car). The formality level must match: if you address someone as 'vous,' their belongings take 'votre/vos,' never 'ton/ta/tes.' Mixing register ('vous' with 'ton') sounds jarring to French ears.

items.question: Il a perdu _____ portefeuille dans le métro.
items.translation: (He lost his wallet in the metro.)
items.explanation: 'Portefeuille' is masculine singular, so 'son portefeuille.' Even though 'portefeuille' contains 'feuille' (leaf, feminine), the compound word itself is masculine — always check the gender of the actual noun, not its components. The son/sa/ses set covers il, elle, and on (informal 'we'). Context clarifies: 'Il a perdu son portefeuille' (his wallet) vs. 'Elle a perdu son portefeuille' (her wallet). The possessive form is identical.

items.question: Marie et Paul adorent _____ grand-mère.
items.translation: (Marie and Paul love their grandmother.)
items.explanation: Multiple possessors (Marie and Paul = ils) + one possessed item = 'leur': 'leur grand-mère' (their grandmother — one grandmother). This is a common error: students often add an 's' for plural possessors, but 'leur' vs. 'leurs' depends on the number of the POSSESSED noun, not the possessors. One grandmother = leur grand-mère. Multiple grandmothers (if each had a different one) = leurs grand-mères.

items.question: Je vais chez _____ parents ce week-end.
items.translation: (I'm going to my parents' house this weekend.)
items.explanation: 'Parents' is plural, so 'mes parents.' The expression 'chez mes parents' (at/to my parents' house) uses 'chez' + person, a uniquely French preposition meaning 'at the home of.' Note that in French, 'parents' can mean both 'parents' and 'relatives' depending on context. The possessive 'mes' is invariable for gender in the plural — 'mes frères' (brothers), 'mes sœurs' (sisters) both use 'mes.'

items.question: Chaque élève doit ranger _____ affaires.
items.translation: (Each student must put away their things.)
items.explanation: 'Chaque élève' (each student) is singular — even though it refers to all students, the grammar treats it as one at a time. Therefore, the possessive is 'ses' (his/her), not 'leurs' (their). 'Ses affaires' = his/her things. This is a subtle point: French follows grammatical number strictly. Similarly: 'tout le monde a apporté son livre' (everyone brought his/her book — singular despite referring to many people).

items.question: Nous aimons beaucoup _____ nouveau professeur.
items.translation: (We really like our new teacher.)
items.explanation: 'Professeur' is singular, so 'notre professeur' (our teacher). 'Notre' is invariable for gender: 'notre professeur' (masculine), 'notre école' (feminine). The circumflex accent on 'nôtre' only appears in the possessive PRONOUN form ('le nôtre' = ours), never in the adjective. This distinction matters in writing: 'notre maison' (adjective, no circumflex) vs. 'la nôtre' (pronoun, with circumflex).

items.question: Tu peux me prêter _____ stylo ?
items.translation: (Can you lend me your pen?)
items.explanation: 'Stylo' is masculine singular: 'ton stylo.' The informal 'tu' form indicates closeness — you'd say this to a friend, family member, or child. The full paradigm for tu: ton cahier (masculine), ta tablette (feminine), tes lunettes (plural). The question uses 'tu peux' (informal) consistently with 'ton' — register matching is essential. Using 'votre' here would clash with the informal 'tu' and sound unnatural.

items.question: À _____ avis, quel est le meilleur restaurant ?
items.translation: (In your opinion, what is the best restaurant?)
items.explanation: 'À votre avis' (in your opinion) is a formal/polite expression using the vous-form possessive. 'Avis' is masculine singular: 'votre avis.' This set phrase is invaluable for conversation — it politely solicits someone's opinion. Other useful expressions with possessives: 'à mon avis' (in my opinion), 'à notre connaissance' (to our knowledge), 'de votre côté' (on your end). Possessives in fixed expressions function as essential communication tools.

items.question: Les voisins ont vendu _____ maison l'année dernière.
items.translation: (The neighbors sold their house last year.)
items.explanation: 'Les voisins' (plural possessors) + 'maison' (singular possessed) = 'leur maison.' This reinforces the crucial rule: 'leur' (no 's') for a singular owned item, 'leurs' (with 's') for plural owned items. The number of owners is irrelevant to the agreement — only the number of the possessed noun counts. 'Leur maison' (their house), 'leur voiture' (their car), but 'leurs enfants' (their children), 'leurs idées' (their ideas).

description: Practice French possessive adjectives (mon, ma, mes, etc.) and their agreement with nouns

name: Adjectifs Démonstratifs (Demonstrative Adjectives)

items.question: _____ livre est très intéressant.
items.translation: (This book is very interesting.)
items.explanation: French has four demonstrative adjectives: ce (masculine singular before consonant), cet (masculine singular before vowel/silent h), cette (feminine singular), ces (plural). 'Livre' is masculine and starts with a consonant, so 'ce livre.' Unlike English, which distinguishes 'this' and 'that,' French uses the same form for both. To specify proximity, add '-ci' (this/here) or '-là' (that/there) after the noun: 'ce livre-ci' (this book) vs. 'ce livre-là' (that book).

items.question: J'adore _____ chanson !
items.translation: (I love this song!)
items.explanation: 'Chanson' is feminine singular, so 'cette chanson.' 'Cette' is the only feminine singular demonstrative — it doesn't change before vowels (no special form like 'cet'). 'Cette amie' (this friend, feminine), 'cette histoire' (this story). The feminine form always has the double 't' — this helps distinguish it from the masculine 'ce.' In speech, 'cette' [set] sounds distinctly different from 'ce' [sə], making the gender audible.

items.question: _____ hôtel a une vue magnifique sur la mer.
items.translation: (This hotel has a magnificent view of the sea.)
items.explanation: 'Hôtel' is masculine and starts with a silent 'h' (treated like a vowel for liaison purposes). Before masculine nouns beginning with a vowel or silent 'h,' French uses 'cet' instead of 'ce' to avoid an awkward pronunciation. 'Cet hôtel,' 'cet ami,' 'cet arbre,' 'cet enfant.' The 't' provides a smooth liaison. Note: before aspirated 'h' (like 'héros'), use 'ce': 'ce héros.' Distinguishing silent from aspirated 'h' is a dictionary skill.

items.question: _____ enfants sont très sages.
items.translation: (These children are very well-behaved.)
items.explanation: 'Ces' is the only plural demonstrative — it works for both masculine and feminine plural nouns. 'Ces enfants' (these children), 'ces maisons' (these houses), 'ces livres' (these books). The simplification in plural is welcome: no gender distinction needed. 'Ces' always sounds like [se], creating a liaison with following vowels: 'ces‿enfants' [se.zɑ̃.fɑ̃]. French demonstratives are simpler than you might expect — just four forms covering all situations.

items.question: Tu connais _____ femme là-bas ?
items.translation: (Do you know that woman over there?)
items.explanation: 'Femme' is feminine singular: 'cette femme.' The addition of 'là-bas' (over there) clarifies that we mean 'that woman' specifically. While 'cette' can mean either 'this' or 'that,' adding '-là' after the noun resolves any ambiguity: 'cette femme-là' = that woman (there). Similarly, '-ci' specifies 'this one here': 'cette femme-ci' (this woman). In everyday speech, 'cette femme' without suffix usually means 'this woman' or is understood from context.

items.question: _____ exercice est difficile.
items.translation: (This exercise is difficult.)
items.explanation: 'Exercice' is masculine and starts with a vowel, triggering 'cet' instead of 'ce.' The rule is purely phonetic: 'ce exercice' would create an awkward hiatus (two vowel sounds colliding), so French inserts the 't': 'cet exercice' flows smoothly. This is the same euphonic principle behind 'mon amie' (not 'ma amie') and 'bel homme' (not 'beau homme'). French consistently modifies forms to maintain its characteristic flowing pronunciation.

items.question: Regarde _____ jolies fleurs dans le jardin !
items.translation: (Look at these pretty flowers in the garden!)
items.explanation: 'Fleurs' is feminine plural: 'ces fleurs.' When an adjective sits between the demonstrative and the noun, 'ces' still agrees with the noun: 'ces jolies fleurs,' 'ces grands arbres,' 'ces petits enfants.' The adjective doesn't affect the demonstrative's form. Note that 'regarder' in the imperative (tu form) is 'Regarde' — an -ER verb dropping the 's.' The demonstrative + adjective + noun pattern is very common in exclamations.

items.question: Je ne comprends pas _____ mot.
items.translation: (I don't understand this word.)
items.explanation: 'Mot' is masculine singular starting with a consonant: 'ce mot.' Demonstrative adjectives are frequently used in classroom and learning contexts: 'ce mot' (this word), 'cette phrase' (this sentence), 'cet exemple' (this example), 'ces exercices' (these exercises). They're also essential in shopping: 'ce pull' (this sweater), 'cette robe' (this dress), 'ces chaussures' (these shoes). Demonstratives point to specific items in your immediate context.

items.question: _____ après-midi, nous allons au cinéma.
items.translation: (This afternoon, we're going to the cinema.)
items.explanation: 'Après-midi' can be masculine or feminine (both are accepted), but since it starts with a vowel, we use 'cet' regardless. 'Cet après-midi' is a fixed expression meaning 'this afternoon.' Demonstratives with time expressions are extremely common: 'ce matin' (this morning), 'ce soir' (this evening), 'cette nuit' (tonight/last night), 'cette semaine' (this week), 'ce mois-ci' (this month), 'cette année' (this year).

items.question: _____ idée est brillante !
items.translation: (This idea is brilliant!)
items.explanation: 'Idée' is feminine singular: 'cette idée.' Even though 'idée' starts with a vowel, the feminine form 'cette' doesn't change (unlike masculine 'ce' → 'cet'). 'Cette' already ends in a consonant sound [t], so there's no hiatus problem: 'cette idée' flows naturally. This asymmetry between masculine (ce/cet variation) and feminine (cette, always) simplifies feminine demonstratives. You never need to wonder about the form for feminine — it's always 'cette.'

items.question: Qui est _____ homme à côté de Marie ?
items.translation: (Who is that man next to Marie?)
items.explanation: 'Homme' is masculine with a silent 'h': 'cet homme.' The silent 'h' in French words of Latin origin functions as if the word begins with a vowel. Compare: 'cet homme' (silent h, use 'cet') vs. 'ce hamster' (aspirated h, use 'ce'). Common silent-h words: homme, hôtel, hôpital, heure, histoire, habitude. Common aspirated-h words: hamster, haricot, héros, hibou. This distinction must be learned word by word.

items.question: _____ gâteau au chocolat est délicieux !
items.translation: (This chocolate cake is delicious!)
items.explanation: 'Gâteau' is masculine singular starting with a consonant: 'ce gâteau.' In conversation about food, demonstratives are constantly used: 'ce gâteau,' 'cette tarte,' 'ces croissants,' 'cet éclair.' They serve the practical function of pointing to specific items — at a bakery, a market, or a restaurant. The exclamation pattern 'Ce/Cette + noun + est + adjective!' is a natural way to express appreciation in French.

items.question: Qu'est-ce que tu penses de _____ restaurant ?
items.translation: (What do you think of this restaurant?)
items.explanation: 'Restaurant' is masculine starting with a consonant: 'ce restaurant.' Demonstratives after prepositions work normally: 'de ce restaurant,' 'dans cette rue,' 'avec ces amis,' 'pour cet enfant.' The preposition doesn't affect the demonstrative form. 'Qu'est-ce que tu penses de...' (What do you think of...) is an invaluable question structure for asking opinions, and demonstratives naturally accompany it to point to the topic.

items.question: _____ année, nous allons voyager en Italie.
items.translation: (This year, we're going to travel to Italy.)
items.explanation: 'Année' is feminine: 'cette année' (this year). Note the difference between 'an' (masculine: 'cet an') and 'année' (feminine: 'cette année'). Similarly: 'ce jour' vs. 'cette journée,' 'ce matin' vs. 'cette matinée,' 'ce soir' vs. 'cette soirée.' The longer feminine forms (-ée) emphasize duration, while the shorter masculine forms focus on the unit. In practice, both are common in time expressions, but 'cette année' is far more frequent than 'cet an.'

items.question: À qui sont _____ lunettes sur la table ?
items.translation: (Whose are these glasses on the table?)
items.explanation: 'Lunettes' (glasses) is always plural in French (like English 'glasses'): 'ces lunettes.' Other always-plural nouns: les ciseaux (scissors), les vacances (holidays), les gens (people). 'À qui sont...' (whose are...) is the standard way to ask about ownership. 'Ces' before a consonant has no liaison, but before a vowel it creates one: 'ces‿affaires' [se.za.fɛʁ]. The plural demonstrative 'ces' is the simplest form — one for all plurals.

description: Master ce, cet, cette, ces to point out specific people and things in conversation

name: Comparatif (Comparisons)

items.question: Paris est _____ grand que Lyon.
items.translation: (Paris is bigger than Lyon.)
items.explanation: French comparisons use three structures: plus...que (more...than), moins...que (less...than), aussi...que (as...as). 'Plus grand que' = bigger than / more big than. Unlike English, French doesn't have special comparative forms like 'bigger' — it always uses 'plus + adjective + que.' This consistency is actually simpler than English: 'plus intelligent,' 'plus rapide,' 'plus grand' — no need to decide between '-er' and 'more.' The adjective still agrees in gender/number with the subject.

items.question: Ce film est _____ intéressant que le livre.
items.translation: (This film is less interesting than the book.)
items.explanation: 'Moins...que' expresses inferiority: 'moins intéressant que' = less interesting than. This structure is used more frequently in French than 'less...than' in English, where speakers often prefer the opposite comparison ('the book is more interesting'). French speakers comfortably use 'moins' in everyday speech. The structure is parallel to 'plus...que': moins + adjective + que. The adjective still agrees normally: 'Cette ville est moins grande que Paris.'

items.question: Ma sœur est _____ grande que moi.
items.translation: (My sister is as tall as me.)
items.explanation: 'Aussi...que' expresses equality: 'aussi grande que' = as tall as. Note that 'grande' has the feminine ending '-e' because it describes 'ma sœur' (feminine). After 'que' in comparisons, French uses stressed pronouns: moi, toi, lui/elle, nous, vous, eux/elles. 'Que moi' (as me/than me), not 'que je.' In negative sentences, 'aussi' often becomes 'si': 'Elle n'est pas si grande que moi' (She's not as tall as me).

items.question: Le TGV est _____ rapide que le train régional.
items.translation: (The TGV is faster than the regional train.)
items.explanation: 'Plus rapide que' = faster than. The TGV (Train à Grande Vitesse — high-speed train) is a perfect context for comparisons. Note that 'que' can be followed by a noun, pronoun, or clause: 'plus rapide que le bus,' 'plus rapide que toi,' 'plus rapide qu'on ne le pense' (faster than one thinks). Before a vowel, 'que' elides to 'qu''. This comparison structure works with any adjective and is grammatically straightforward.

items.question: Cette solution est _____ que l'autre. (better)
items.translation: (This solution is better than the other one.)
items.explanation: 'Bon' (good) has an irregular comparative: 'meilleur(e)' (better), NOT 'plus bon.' This is one of the few irregulars in French comparisons. 'Meilleur' agrees in gender and number: meilleur (masculine singular), meilleure (feminine singular), meilleurs (masculine plural), meilleures (feminine plural). 'Cette solution est meilleure' — feminine because 'solution' is feminine. Don't confuse with 'mieux' (better — adverb for actions): 'elle chante mieux' but 'sa voix est meilleure.'

items.question: Il travaille _____ que ses collègues.
items.translation: (He works more than his colleagues.)
items.explanation: When comparing VERBS (actions) rather than adjectives, 'plus/moins/autant' follow the verb directly: 'il travaille plus que...' (he works more than...). Note: for verbs, the equality form is 'autant' (not 'aussi'): 'il travaille autant que moi' (he works as much as me). This verb vs. adjective distinction is important: 'aussi' with adjectives, 'autant' with verbs. 'Plus' and 'moins' work with both, making them easier to remember.

items.question: Elle parle français _____ que son frère.
items.translation: (She speaks French better than her brother.)
items.explanation: 'Bien' (well — adverb) has the irregular comparative 'mieux' (better), NOT 'plus bien.' Key distinction: 'meilleur' is an adjective (describes nouns), 'mieux' is an adverb (describes verbs). 'Elle parle mieux' (she speaks better — modifies the verb 'parler'). Compare: 'Sa prononciation est meilleure' (her pronunciation is better — 'meilleure' modifies the noun). This meilleur/mieux pair parallels English better (adjective) vs. better (adverb), but French makes the distinction explicit.

items.question: Il y a _____ de touristes en été qu'en hiver.
items.translation: (There are more tourists in summer than in winter.)
items.explanation: When comparing QUANTITIES of nouns, use 'plus de/moins de/autant de + noun + que': 'plus de touristes en été qu'en hiver.' The 'de' is essential between the comparative word and the noun. Don't confuse: 'plus grand que' (comparative adjective, no 'de') vs. 'plus de travail que' (comparative quantity, 'de' required). This three-way system — adjective, verb, and noun comparisons — each has its own syntax that must be respected.

items.question: Ce restaurant est _____ cher que celui d'en face.
items.translation: (This restaurant is less expensive than the one across the street.)
items.explanation: 'Moins cher que' = less expensive than. 'Cher' agrees with the subject: 'ce restaurant' (masculine singular). If comparing feminine nouns: 'cette robe est moins chère.' Note 'celui d'en face' (the one across) — 'celui' is a demonstrative pronoun replacing a masculine noun. The quatre set: celui (masc. sg.), celle (fem. sg.), ceux (masc. pl.), celles (fem. pl.). Comparisons often use these pronouns to avoid repetition.

items.question: Tu as _____ d'expérience que lui dans ce domaine.
items.translation: (You have as much experience as him in this field.)
items.explanation: 'Autant de + noun + que' expresses equal quantity: 'autant d'expérience que lui.' Remember the critical distinction: 'aussi' for adjectives/adverbs ('aussi grand,' 'aussi vite'), 'autant' for quantities ('autant de travail') and after verbs ('je travaille autant'). 'Autant d'' — the 'de' elides before a vowel. This is one of French's trickiest comparison points for English speakers, since English uses 'as much' for both contexts.

items.question: La situation est _____ grave qu'on ne le pensait.
items.translation: (The situation is more serious than we thought.)
items.explanation: After 'que' in comparisons, formal French often adds the expletive 'ne' — 'qu'on ne le pensait.' This 'ne' is NOT negative; it's a formal stylistic element that doesn't change the meaning. You'll encounter it in written French and formal speech. The basic comparison 'plus grave que' works exactly as expected: 'grave' doesn't change here because 'situation' could take either agreement or remain base form as a predicate adjective after 'être.'

items.question: Il court _____ vite que moi.
items.translation: (He runs as fast as me.)
items.explanation: 'Aussi vite que' — 'vite' is an adverb modifying the verb 'courir,' so we use 'aussi' (not 'autant'). The rule: 'aussi' with adjectives AND adverbs, 'autant' with nouns and after verbs. 'Vite' (fast/quickly) is one of French's invariable adverbs — it never changes form. Other common adverbs in comparisons: aussi souvent (as often), aussi bien (as well), aussi longtemps (as long). Adverbs never agree in gender or number.

items.question: Ce gâteau est _____ bon que celui de ma grand-mère.
items.translation: (This cake is less good than my grandmother's.)
items.explanation: Interestingly, 'moins bon' IS acceptable (unlike 'plus bon,' which must be 'meilleur'). There's no special form for 'less good' — 'moins bon(ne)' works perfectly. Some speakers prefer 'pas aussi bon que' (not as good as) for a softer expression, but 'moins bon' is grammatically correct. The irregular comparatives only exist for superiority (meilleur, mieux, pire, moindre). For inferiority and equality, the regular forms are used with 'bon' and 'bien.'

items.question: La vie à Paris est beaucoup _____ chère qu'en province.
items.translation: (Life in Paris is much more expensive than in the provinces.)
items.explanation: To intensify a comparison, add 'beaucoup' (much), 'bien' (much, formal), 'un peu' (a little), or 'légèrement' (slightly) before 'plus/moins': 'beaucoup plus chère' (much more expensive). These intensifiers add nuance: 'un peu plus grand' (a little bigger), 'bien moins cher' (much less expensive). Never use 'très' with comparatives — 'très plus grand' is incorrect. 'Beaucoup' and 'bien' are the standard comparative intensifiers.

items.question: C'est la _____ chose qui pouvait arriver. (worst)
items.translation: (It's the worst thing that could happen.)
items.explanation: 'Mauvais' (bad) has the irregular comparative/superlative 'pire' (worse/worst). 'La pire chose' = the worst thing. While 'plus mauvais' is sometimes used for physical qualities ('Ce vin est plus mauvais'), 'pire' is preferred for abstract or moral judgments ('la pire erreur,' 'le pire scénario'). With the definite article, 'pire' becomes superlative. French has only a handful of irregular comparatives — bon/meilleur, bien/mieux, mauvais/pire — making the system largely predictable.

description: Form comparisons of superiority, inferiority, and equality using plus, moins, and aussi

name: Superlatif (Superlatives)

items.question: C'est _____ beau monument de Paris.
items.translation: (It's the most beautiful monument in Paris.)
items.explanation: French superlatives use 'le/la/les plus' + adjective: 'le plus beau' (the most beautiful). The article agrees with the noun: 'monument' is masculine singular, so 'le plus.' For 'the least,' use 'le/la/les moins.' Unlike English, which alternates between '-est' and 'most,' French consistently uses 'plus' for all adjectives. After a superlative, 'in' is translated as 'de' (not 'dans'): 'le plus beau DE Paris,' 'la plus grande DU monde.'

items.question: Marie est _____ intelligente de la classe.
items.translation: (Marie is the most intelligent in the class.)
items.explanation: Marie is feminine, so the superlative article is 'la': 'la plus intelligente.' The adjective agrees too: 'intelligente' (feminine). The order depends on whether the adjective normally precedes or follows its noun. Short common adjectives (grand, petit, beau, bon) go before: 'le plus grand homme.' Longer adjectives go after: 'la fille la plus intelligente.' Yes — in the second pattern, the article appears TWICE: 'la fille la plus intelligente.'

items.question: Ce sont _____ bons élèves de l'école.
items.translation: (They are the best students in the school.)
items.explanation: Just as 'bon' has the irregular comparative 'meilleur,' the superlative is 'le/la/les meilleur(e)(s)': 'les meilleurs élèves' (the best students — masculine plural). Never say 'les plus bons.' 'Meilleur' agrees fully: le meilleur (m.sg.), la meilleure (f.sg.), les meilleurs (m.pl.), les meilleures (f.pl.). Since 'bon' is a short adjective, 'meilleur' precedes the noun: 'les meilleurs restaurants,' 'la meilleure solution.'

items.question: C'est le film _____ ennuyeux que j'aie jamais vu.
items.translation: (It's the most boring film I have ever seen.)
items.explanation: When the adjective follows the noun, the superlative article is repeated: 'le film LE PLUS ennuyeux.' The first 'le' is the noun's article, the second is part of the superlative. 'Ennuyeux' stays after the noun (it's a long adjective), so the pattern is: article + noun + article + plus + adjective. Also note: after superlatives, the relative clause often uses the subjunctive: 'que j'aie vu' (subjunctive of avoir), not 'que j'ai vu' (indicative).

items.question: C'est _____ petite ville de la région.
items.translation: (It's the smallest town in the region.)
items.explanation: 'Ville' is feminine: 'la plus petite ville.' Since 'petit' is a short adjective that normally precedes the noun, the superlative goes before it too: 'la plus petite ville' (not 'la ville la plus petite,' though this is also possible for emphasis). The preposition 'de' follows superlatives for 'in/of': 'de la région.' This 'de' may contract: 'du pays' (de + le), 'des environs' (de + les).

items.question: C'est elle qui chante _____ dans le groupe.
items.translation: (She's the one who sings the best in the group.)
items.explanation: For ADVERBS (modifying verbs), the superlative is always 'le mieux/le plus/le moins' — the article is invariable 'le,' regardless of the subject's gender. 'Elle chante le mieux' (she sings THE BEST). 'Le' doesn't agree with 'elle' because it modifies the verb, not the person. Compare: 'la meilleure chanteuse' (adjective, agrees with feminine noun) vs. 'elle chante le mieux' (adverb, invariable 'le'). This distinction is subtle but important.

items.question: Le Mont Blanc est _____ haute montagne d'Europe.
items.translation: (Mont Blanc is the highest mountain in Europe.)
items.explanation: 'Montagne' is feminine: 'la plus haute montagne.' Geography and records are natural contexts for superlatives: 'le plus long fleuve' (the longest river), 'la plus grande ville' (the biggest city), 'le pays le plus peuplé' (the most populated country). Note: 'le Mont Blanc est la plus haute montagne' — the subject is masculine ('le Mont Blanc') but the superlative agrees with the predicate noun ('montagne,' feminine): 'la plus haute.'

items.question: C'est le restaurant _____ cher du quartier.
items.translation: (It's the least expensive restaurant in the neighborhood.)
items.explanation: 'Le moins' creates the superlative of inferiority: 'le moins cher' (the least expensive). 'Restaurant' is masculine: 'le restaurant le moins cher.' Since 'cher' can go either before or after the noun, both positions work. 'Du quartier' = 'de + le quartier' (contraction). The inferiority superlative is more commonly used in French than in English, where speakers might say 'the cheapest' instead of 'the least expensive.'

items.question: C'est la chose _____ importante à retenir.
items.translation: (It's the most important thing to remember.)
items.explanation: 'Chose' is feminine: 'la chose la plus importante.' Since 'important' is a longer adjective, it follows the noun, requiring the article to be repeated. This double-article pattern (la chose LA PLUS importante) is one of the distinctive features of French superlatives. It might feel redundant, but it's grammatically required. The infinitive 'à retenir' (to remember) naturally follows, creating a complete and elegant expression of priority.

items.question: Ce sont _____ vieilles maisons de la ville.
items.translation: (These are the oldest houses in the city.)
items.explanation: Plural superlative: 'les plus vieilles maisons.' 'Maisons' is feminine plural, 'vieilles' agrees, and 'les plus' provides the plural superlative article. 'Vieux/vieille' is a short adjective that precedes the noun, so the superlative also precedes: 'les plus vieilles maisons.' The adjective 'vieux' has special forms: vieux (masc. sg.), vieil (masc. sg. before vowel), vieille (fem. sg.), vieux (masc. pl.), vieilles (fem. pl.).

items.question: La Tour Eiffel est le monument _____ visité de France.
items.translation: (The Eiffel Tower is the most visited monument in France.)
items.explanation: 'Monument' is masculine: 'le monument le plus visité.' Past participles used as adjectives follow the same superlative rules: 'le plus visité,' 'la plus connue,' 'les plus lus' (the most read). 'De France' expresses the scope. Note that 'La Tour Eiffel EST le monument' — feminine subject with masculine predicate. The superlative agrees with 'monument' (the noun it directly modifies), not 'La Tour Eiffel.'

items.question: C'est _____ pire erreur de ma vie.
items.translation: (It's the worst mistake of my life.)
items.explanation: 'Pire' is already the superlative/comparative of 'mauvais,' so you don't add 'plus': 'la pire erreur' (the worst mistake), never 'la plus pire.' 'Erreur' is feminine, so 'la pire.' Similarly, 'meilleur' doesn't need 'plus': 'le meilleur ami' (the best friend), not 'le plus meilleur.' These irregular forms (meilleur, pire, moindre) already contain the superlative meaning. Just add the appropriate definite article for the superlative effect.

items.question: C'est l'homme _____ riche du pays.
items.translation: (He's the richest man in the country.)
items.explanation: 'L'homme le plus riche' — 'riche' follows the noun (it's not a BANGS adjective), so the superlative article appears between noun and adjective. 'Du pays' = 'de + le pays.' In French society, superlatives are used with flair: 'le plus beau jour,' 'la meilleure cuisine,' 'les plus beaux paysages.' They're common in advertising, tourism, and daily enthusiastic conversation. Mastering their syntax lets you express strong opinions naturally.

items.question: Ce problème est _____ grave de tous.
items.translation: (This problem is the least serious of all.)
items.explanation: 'Le moins grave' (the least serious) — inferiority superlative. 'Problème' is masculine: 'le moins.' The expression 'de tous' (of all) is a universal scope marker that works with any superlative: 'le plus grand de tous,' 'la moins chère de toutes.' Note: 'tous' (masculine) vs. 'toutes' (feminine) — agreement depends on the group being referenced. Superlatives with 'de tous/toutes' create emphatic, definitive statements.

items.question: Elle a obtenu _____ note à l'examen. (the best)
items.translation: (She got the best grade on the exam.)
items.explanation: 'Note' is feminine: 'la meilleure note.' This is the feminine superlative of 'bon,' and since 'meilleur' is a short adjective, it precedes the noun: 'la meilleure note.' The full paradigm: le meilleur résultat (masc.sg.), la meilleure performance (fem.sg.), les meilleurs scores (masc.pl.), les meilleures chances (fem.pl.). Remember: 'meilleur(e)' for adjectives (modifying nouns), 'le mieux' for adverbs (modifying verbs). 'La meilleure' here clearly modifies the noun 'note.'

description: Express superlatives (the most, the least, the best) with correct article and adjective agreement

name: Pronoms Relatifs (Relative Pronouns)

items.question: C'est le livre _____ j'ai lu la semaine dernière.
items.translation: (It's the book that I read last week.)
items.explanation: 'Que' is a relative pronoun functioning as the DIRECT OBJECT of the relative clause. In 'j'ai lu le livre,' 'le livre' is the direct object of 'lire.' When it becomes the relative pronoun, it turns into 'que': 'le livre que j'ai lu.' The key test: if the relative clause already has a subject ('j'') and a verb ('ai lu'), the pronoun is the object → use 'que.' Before a vowel, 'que' elides to 'qu'': 'le film qu'elle a vu.' Never omit relative pronouns in French as you can in English.

items.question: La fille _____ parle est ma cousine.
items.translation: (The girl who is speaking is my cousin.)
items.explanation: 'Qui' is the relative pronoun functioning as the SUBJECT of the relative clause. In the relative clause 'qui parle,' 'qui' IS the subject performing the action of speaking. The test: if there's no other subject before the verb in the relative clause → use 'qui.' 'Qui' NEVER elides before a vowel: 'l'homme qui arrive' (not 'qu'arrive'). This qui/que distinction (subject vs. object) is the foundation of French relative pronouns. Master it first.

items.question: C'est la maison _____ j'ai grandi.
items.translation: (It's the house where I grew up.)
items.explanation: 'Où' as a relative pronoun indicates PLACE or TIME (not just place as in English 'where'). 'La maison où j'ai grandi' — 'où' replaces a location complement. For time: 'le jour où nous nous sommes rencontrés' (the day when we met). In English, you use 'where' for places and 'when' for times, but French uses 'où' for both. This dual function makes 'où' more versatile than its English counterpart. It replaces 'dans lequel/laquelle' in most cases.

items.question: Le professeur _____ je t'ai parlé est très compétent.
items.translation: (The teacher I told you about is very competent.)
items.explanation: 'Dont' replaces 'de + noun' in relative clauses. 'Parler de quelqu'un' → 'la personne dont je parle.' The verb 'parler' requires 'de' (parler de), so when the complement becomes relative, it uses 'dont.' Key verbs with 'de': parler de, avoir besoin de, avoir peur de, se souvenir de, s'occuper de. 'Dont' is also used for possession: 'l'homme dont la femme est médecin' (the man whose wife is a doctor) — 'dont' replaces 'de l'homme.'

items.question: Voici le restaurant _____ on mange très bien.
items.translation: (Here's the restaurant where you eat very well.)
items.explanation: 'Où' for a place where an action occurs: 'le restaurant où on mange.' The indefinite 'on' (one/you/we) is the subject of the relative clause. 'Où' replaces a locative prepositional phrase: 'on mange dans ce restaurant' → 'le restaurant où on mange.' Other examples: 'la ville où je suis né' (the city where I was born), 'le pays où il fait toujours beau' (the country where the weather is always nice). 'Où' makes descriptions of places fluid and natural.

items.question: La raison pour _____ je suis venu est importante.
items.translation: (The reason why I came is important.)
items.explanation: After prepositions (pour, avec, dans, sur, etc.), French uses compound relative pronouns: lequel (masc.sg.), laquelle (fem.sg.), lesquels (masc.pl.), lesquelles (fem.pl.). 'Raison' is feminine singular: 'pour laquelle.' These agree with the antecedent noun. With 'à' and 'de,' contractions occur: à + lequel = auquel, de + lequel = duquel. Note: for PEOPLE after prepositions, 'qui' is also acceptable: 'la personne pour qui/pour laquelle je travaille.'

items.question: C'est l'animal _____ je parle dans mon livre.
items.translation: (It's the animal I'm talking about in my book.)
items.explanation: 'Parler de' (to talk about) requires 'de,' so the relative pronoun is 'dont': 'l'animal dont je parle.' The golden rule for 'dont': identify if the verb or expression in the relative clause uses 'de.' If yes → dont. Common triggers: avoir besoin de (need), être content de (happy about), être fier de (proud of), faire partie de (be part of), se servir de (use). 'Dont' elegantly replaces 'de + noun' — far simpler than the English 'about which' or 'of which.'

items.question: Les amis avec _____ je voyage sont amusants.
items.translation: (The friends I travel with are fun.)
items.explanation: After 'avec' (with), use the compound relative pronoun: 'avec lesquels' (with whom, masculine plural). 'Amis' is masculine plural → lesquels. For people, 'avec qui' is also correct and more common in everyday speech: 'les amis avec qui je voyage.' But for things, only lequel/laquelle/etc. works: 'le stylo avec lequel j'écris,' not 'le stylo avec qui.' The lequel family is essential for formal French and for things; 'qui' works for people casually.

items.question: La femme _____ le fils est médecin habite ici.
items.translation: (The woman whose son is a doctor lives here.)
items.explanation: 'Dont' expresses possession (whose): 'la femme dont le fils est médecin' = the woman whose son... This is actually 'de la femme + le fils': 'le fils de la femme est médecin' → 'dont' replaces 'de la femme.' After 'dont,' the word order is ALWAYS: dont + subject + verb — never invert. This is different from English 'whose,' which is followed by the possessed noun directly. 'Dont' is perhaps the trickiest relative pronoun for English speakers to master.

items.question: Le moment _____ il est arrivé, tout a changé.
items.translation: (The moment he arrived, everything changed.)
items.explanation: 'Où' for TIME — not just place. 'Le moment où' (the moment when), 'le jour où' (the day when), 'l'époque où' (the era when), 'l'année où' (the year when). English uses 'when' or 'that' for temporal relatives, but French consistently uses 'où.' This might feel strange since 'où' translates as 'where,' but in relative clauses, it covers both spatial and temporal references. 'À l'instant où' (at the instant when) is another common time expression.

items.question: Tout _____ brille n'est pas or.
items.translation: (All that glitters is not gold.)
items.explanation: 'Ce qui' and 'ce que' are used when there's no specific antecedent noun — they mean 'what' or 'that which.' 'Ce qui' is the subject form: 'ce qui brille' (what glitters — 'ce qui' is doing the glittering). 'Ce que' is the object form: 'ce que je veux' (what I want). The 'ce' is necessary because 'qui/que' alone need an antecedent. This proverb beautifully illustrates the subject function: 'ce qui' replaces the thing that performs the action.

items.question: Je ne comprends pas _____ tu dis.
items.translation: (I don't understand what you're saying.)
items.explanation: 'Ce que' is the object form of 'what': 'ce que tu dis' (what you say). Here, 'tu' is the subject, 'dis' is the verb, and 'ce que' is the direct object. Compare: 'ce qui m'intéresse' (what interests me — subject) vs. 'ce que je veux' (what I want — object). The same qui/que logic applies: if the relative clause already has a subject → ce que; if the pronoun IS the subject → ce qui. 'Ce que' elides before vowels: 'ce qu'il dit.'

items.question: C'est _____ j'ai besoin pour le projet.
items.translation: (It's what I need for the project.)
items.explanation: 'Ce dont' combines 'ce' (what/that which) with 'dont' (replacing 'de'). 'Avoir besoin DE' → 'ce dont j'ai besoin' (what I need — literally 'that of which I have need'). Use 'ce dont' whenever the verb requires 'de' and there's no antecedent noun. Other examples: 'ce dont je me souviens' (what I remember — se souvenir de), 'ce dont elle a peur' (what she's afraid of — avoir peur de), 'ce dont il s'agit' (what it's about — s'agir de).

items.question: Les étudiants _____ réussissent travaillent dur.
items.translation: (Students who succeed work hard.)
items.explanation: 'Qui' as subject of the relative clause: 'les étudiants qui réussissent' — the students ARE doing the succeeding. No other subject exists in the relative clause. This is a general truth without a specific antecedent, making 'qui' particularly broad here. Notice: 'qui' can refer to people or things: 'les livres qui sont sur la table' (the books that are on the table). 'Qui' is never limited to people in French the way 'who' is in English.

items.question: La ville dans _____ nous habitons est très agréable.
items.translation: (The city in which we live is very pleasant.)
items.explanation: 'Dans laquelle' (in which) uses the compound relative after the preposition 'dans.' 'Ville' is feminine singular → laquelle. While 'où' could replace 'dans laquelle' here ('la ville où nous habitons'), using 'dans laquelle' is more formal and explicit. After complex prepositions (à côté de, en face de, grâce à, au-dessus de), compound relatives are required: 'la maison en face de laquelle il y a un parc.' 'Où' only replaces simple locative prepositions.

description: Connect clauses using qui, que, dont, and où to build complex and natural-sounding sentences

name: Pronoms COD (Direct Object Pronouns)

items.question: Tu connais Marie ? — Oui, je _____ connais bien.
items.translation: (Do you know Marie? — Yes, I know her well.)
items.explanation: Direct object pronouns (COD) replace nouns that receive the action directly (no preposition). Marie is feminine singular → 'la.' The full set: me (me), te (you), le (him/it masc.), la (her/it fem.), nous (us), vous (you), les (them). COD pronouns go BEFORE the conjugated verb: 'je LA connais.' Before vowels, le/la elide: 'je l'aime,' 'je l'ai vue.' This placement before the verb is the biggest difference from English, where objects follow the verb.

items.question: Ce film ? Je _____ ai vu la semaine dernière.
items.translation: (This film? I saw it last week.)
items.explanation: Before vowels (including the 'a' of 'ai'), 'le' and 'la' elide to 'l'': 'je l'ai vu.' In passé composé, the COD pronoun goes before the auxiliary 'avoir': 'je L'AI vu.' Crucially, when a COD pronoun precedes 'avoir,' the past participle AGREES with the pronoun: 'Ce film? Je l'ai vu' (masculine, no change). But: 'Cette chanson? Je l'ai entenduE' (feminine → add 'e'). This agreement rule is a hallmark of written French grammar.

items.question: Tu aimes les croissants ? — Oui, je _____ adore !
items.translation: (Do you like croissants? — Yes, I love them!)
items.explanation: 'Les croissants' (plural) → 'les' (them). 'Les' is the plural COD pronoun for both masculine and feminine: 'les livres → je les lis,' 'les fleurs → je les arrose.' Don't confuse 'les' (COD pronoun, before verb) with 'les' (definite article, before noun). Context disambiguates: 'je les mange' (pronoun) vs. 'je mange les gâteaux' (article). In passé composé: 'je les ai achetéS' — participle agrees with plural 'les.'

items.question: Pierre nous _____ demain à la gare.
items.translation: (Pierre will meet us tomorrow at the station.)
items.explanation: 'Nous' as COD goes before the verb: 'Pierre nous retrouvera.' In future tense, the pronoun still precedes the conjugated verb — this rule holds for ALL simple tenses (present, future, imparfait, conditional, etc.). Word order: subject + COD + verb. Never put the pronoun after the verb in declarative sentences (only in affirmative imperatives). 'Il nous a vus' (passé composé — agreement with 'nous' if mixed/male group: 'vus').

items.question: Tu me _____ ce soir ?
items.translation: (Will you call me tonight?)
items.explanation: 'Me' as COD before the verb: 'tu m'appelleras' (elision before vowel). Note: 'appeler' (to call someone) takes a direct object, while 'téléphoner' takes an indirect object (téléphoner À quelqu'un). This distinction matters for pronoun choice: 'Tu m'appelleras' (COD — appeler qqn) vs. 'Tu me téléphoneras' (COI — téléphoner à qqn, but 'me' works for both). Many verbs differ from English in whether their object is direct or indirect.

items.question: Cette robe ? Je ne _____ aime pas du tout.
items.translation: (This dress? I don't like it at all.)
items.explanation: In negation, the COD stays between 'ne' and the verb: 'je ne L' aime pas.' The word order is: subject + ne + COD + verb + pas. Before the vowel of 'aime,' 'la' elides to 'l'.' The negation wraps around the pronoun + verb unit. 'Du tout' (at all) intensifies the negation. When you see negative sentences with COD pronouns, remember the pronoun is sandwiched inside the negation: ne + pronoun + verb + pas/jamais/plus.

items.question: Tes devoirs ? _____ -les tout de suite !
items.translation: (Your homework? Do it right away!)
items.explanation: In AFFIRMATIVE imperatives, the COD pronoun goes AFTER the verb with a hyphen: 'Fais-les!' This is the ONE exception to the 'pronoun before verb' rule. For 'faire' (tu form), the imperative is 'Fais.' Combined: 'Fais-les!' (Do them!). The stressed forms are used for me/te: 'Regarde-moi!' (Look at me!), 'Aide-moi!' (Help me!). But in negative imperatives, normal order resumes: 'Ne les fais pas!' (Don't do them!).

items.question: Je peux _____ aider si tu veux.
items.translation: (I can help you if you want.)
items.explanation: With verb + infinitive constructions, the COD goes before the INFINITIVE (the verb it's the object of): 'je peux T'aider.' 'Te' elides to 't'' before 'aider.' The pronoun attaches to the verb it logically belongs to: you're the object of 'aider,' not of 'pouvoir.' Compare: 'je peux t'aider' (can help you), 'je veux le faire' (want to do it), 'je vais les appeler' (going to call them). The pronoun always precedes its infinitive.

items.question: Ces fleurs sont magnifiques ! Qui _____ a offertes ?
items.translation: (These flowers are magnificent! Who gave them?)
items.explanation: 'Ces fleurs' (feminine plural) → 'les.' Note the past participle agreement: 'offertes' (feminine plural) agrees with 'les' because the COD precedes the auxiliary 'avoir.' This agreement rule is specifically triggered by a PRECEDING direct object with 'avoir': 'Qui les a offertes?' The participle 'offert' gains '-es' for feminine plural. Without the preceding COD, no agreement: 'Il a offert des fleurs' (no preceding COD, no agreement).

items.question: La télé ? Je _____ regarde rarement.
items.translation: (TV? I rarely watch it.)
items.explanation: 'La télé' is feminine singular: 'la.' Before consonants, no elision: 'je la regarde' (not 'l'regarde'). Elision only happens before vowels and silent 'h.' The adverb 'rarement' goes after the verb in simple tenses. For frequency adverbs in French: 'Je la regarde souvent/rarement/toujours/jamais.' The COD pronoun's position isn't affected by adverbs — it stays firmly before the verb. Only negation words (ne...pas) can come between subject and COD.

items.question: Votre proposition ? Nous _____ étudierons attentivement.
items.translation: (Your proposal? We'll study it carefully.)
items.explanation: 'Proposition' is feminine singular → 'la,' which elides to 'l'' before 'étudierons' (starts with vowel). The future tense doesn't change pronoun placement: 'nous l'étudierons.' This pattern holds for all simple tenses. The formal register of this sentence (vous/votre, 'attentivement') shows that COD pronouns function identically across registers. In business French, this construction is very common: 'Je l'examinerai,' 'Nous les contacterons.'

items.question: Tu vois cet oiseau ? — Non, je ne _____ vois pas.
items.translation: (Do you see that bird? — No, I don't see it.)
items.explanation: 'Oiseau' is masculine singular → 'le.' Before a consonant (the 'v' of 'vois'), no elision: 'je ne le vois pas.' In negative responses replacing nouns with pronouns, the full structure is: subject + ne + COD + verb + pas. 'Le' before consonant, 'l'' before vowel — this alternation is purely phonetic. 'Non, je ne le vois pas' is the complete natural negative response to any question about seeing a masculine singular thing.

items.question: Je _____ invite à ma fête d'anniversaire.
items.translation: (I'm inviting you (formal/plural) to my birthday party.)
items.explanation: 'Vous' as COD (formal or plural 'you'): 'je vous invite.' 'Vous' doesn't change form whether it's COD, COI, or subject — it's always 'vous.' This makes it simpler than the tu forms (te/toi). 'Inviter' takes a direct object: inviter quelqu'un (not à quelqu'un). The formality of 'vous invite' is appropriate for written invitations, professional contexts, or addressing multiple people. 'Je t'invite' would be the informal equivalent.

items.question: Les clés ? Je crois que je _____ ai laissées dans la voiture.
items.translation: (The keys? I think I left them in the car.)
items.explanation: 'Les clés' (feminine plural) → 'les.' Past participle agreement: 'laissées' with feminine plural '-ées' because 'les' (COD, feminine plural) precedes 'avoir.' The structure 'je crois que + clause' shows the pronoun sits in the subordinate clause before its own verb: 'je crois que je les ai laissées.' Each clause handles its own pronoun placement independently. This agreement in passé composé with preceding COD is essential for written French correctness.

items.question: Ne _____ dérange pas, il est occupé.
items.translation: (Don't disturb him, he's busy.)
items.explanation: Negative imperative + COD: 'Ne le dérange pas!' In negative imperatives, the pronoun goes BEFORE the verb (standard position), unlike affirmative imperatives where it follows. 'Déranger' takes a direct object: déranger quelqu'un. The 'le' refers to 'him' — masculine singular direct object. Compare: 'Ne le dérange pas!' (negative → pronoun before) vs. 'Dérange-le!' (affirmative → pronoun after). This before/after switch in imperatives requires careful attention.

description: Replace direct objects with le, la, les, me, te, nous, vous and master their placement

name: Pronoms COI (Indirect Object Pronouns)

items.question: Je _____ ai envoyé un message hier.
items.translation: (I sent him/her a message yesterday.)
items.explanation: Indirect object pronouns (COI) replace nouns preceded by 'à': envoyer À quelqu'un → 'lui.' The COI set: me (to me), te (to you), lui (to him/her), nous (to us), vous (to you), leur (to them). Unlike COD (le/la = gendered), 'lui' is gender-neutral — it means 'to him' OR 'to her.' This surprises English speakers who expect different forms. COI goes before the verb, just like COD: 'je lui ai envoyé.' After 'avoir,' no participle agreement with COI.

items.question: Tu _____ as téléphoné ce matin ?
items.translation: (Did you call them this morning?)
items.explanation: 'Leur' is the plural COI pronoun (to them), used because 'téléphoner' takes an indirect object: téléphoner À quelqu'un. Don't confuse 'leur' (COI pronoun, invariable) with 'leur/leurs' (possessive adjective, agrees in number). 'Leur' as pronoun never takes an 's' — ever. 'Je leur ai téléphoné' (I called them). Compare with COD: 'je les ai appelés' (appeler takes direct object). The verb determines whether you need COD or COI.

items.question: Ce livre _____ plaît beaucoup.
items.translation: (This book pleases me a lot. / I really like this book.)
items.explanation: 'Plaire' takes an indirect object: ce livre plaît À moi → 'ce livre me plaît.' In French, 'plaire' works opposite to English 'like': the thing liked is the subject, the person who likes is the indirect object. 'Ce livre me plaît' literally = 'this book pleases to me.' This inverted structure is common in French: manquer (to miss → il me manque = I miss him), sembler (to seem → ça me semble bien). COI 'me' goes before the verb as usual.

items.question: Le professeur _____ a expliqué la leçon.
items.translation: (The teacher explained the lesson to us.)
items.explanation: 'Expliquer' takes both a direct object (la leçon) and an indirect object (à nous → nous): 'Le professeur nous a expliqué la leçon.' With double objects, the COI pronoun (nous) works alongside the full noun COD (la leçon). 'Nous' as COI is the same form as 'nous' as COD and as subject — refreshingly consistent. Placement is always before the verb. No past participle agreement because 'nous' here is COI, not COD.

items.question: Je vais _____ offrir des fleurs pour son anniversaire.
items.translation: (I'm going to give her flowers for her birthday.)
items.explanation: With verb + infinitive, the COI goes before the INFINITIVE it belongs to: 'je vais LUI offrir.' 'Offrir' takes à: offrir quelque chose À quelqu'un. 'Lui' = to her (or to him — context clarifies). The pronoun sits between 'vais' and 'offrir.' This is consistent with COD placement: pronouns always precede the verb they're the object of. In compound constructions: 'je vais lui offrir,' 'je peux lui parler,' 'je veux lui écrire.'

items.question: Il ne _____ dit jamais la vérité.
items.translation: (He never tells me the truth.)
items.explanation: 'Dire' takes à: dire quelque chose À quelqu'un → 'il me dit' (he tells me). In negation: 'il ne ME dit jamais.' The COI is sandwiched in the negation: ne + COI + verb + jamais. 'Me' before consonant, 'm'' before vowel: 'il m'a dit' (he told me). The adverb 'jamais' replaces 'pas' in the negation. Other negative forms work the same: 'il ne me dit plus,' 'il ne me dit rien.' The COI position never changes regardless of negation type.

items.question: _____ -moi ce qui s'est passé !
items.translation: (Tell me what happened!)
items.explanation: In affirmative imperatives, the COI follows the verb with a hyphen: 'Raconte-moi!' Note 'me' becomes the stressed form 'moi' after the verb, and 'te' becomes 'toi.' 'Raconter' is an -ER verb so the tu imperative drops the 's': 'Raconte!' Other examples: 'Donne-moi,' 'Parle-moi,' 'Dis-moi,' 'Téléphone-lui,' 'Écrivez-leur.'
