In negative imperative, revert to unstressed form before verb: 'Ne me raconte pas d'histoires!'

items.question: Elle _____ a demandé de l'aide.
items.translation: (She asked us for help.)
items.explanation: 'Demander' takes à: demander quelque chose À quelqu'un → 'elle nous a demandé.' Remember: no past participle agreement with COI. 'Elle nous a demandé' stays 'demandé' — no extra 'e' or 's.' This is because agreement only occurs with PRECEDING DIRECT objects with 'avoir.' COI never triggers agreement. Compare: 'elle nous a vus' (COD nous = agreement: 'vus') vs. 'elle nous a parlé' (COI nous = no agreement: 'parlé').

items.question: Ce cadeau _____ fera très plaisir.
items.translation: (This gift will make him/her very happy.)
items.explanation: 'Faire plaisir' takes à: faire plaisir À quelqu'un → 'lui faire plaisir.' In future: 'ce cadeau lui fera plaisir.' The expression 'faire plaisir à' (to make happy/to please) is extremely common in French. 'Lui' = to him or to her. Context usually makes the gender clear. Other expressions with à that require COI: faire confiance à (trust), faire peur à (frighten), ressembler à (resemble), obéir à (obey), répondre à (answer).

items.question: Je _____ ai prêté ma voiture pour le week-end.
items.translation: (I lent them my car for the weekend.)
items.explanation: 'Prêter' takes à: prêter quelque chose À quelqu'un → 'leur' for plural. 'Je leur ai prêté ma voiture.' 'Leur' (COI) is invariable — no 's' ever. Again, no participle agreement: 'prêté' stays unchanged because 'leur' is COI. The sentence has both COI ('leur') and COD ('ma voiture' — kept as full noun). When both are pronouns, special order applies (covered later), but here only one is pronominalized.

items.question: Tu _____ ressembles beaucoup.
items.translation: (You look a lot like him/her.)
items.explanation: 'Ressembler' takes à: ressembler À quelqu'un → 'tu lui ressembles.' Many verbs that take direct objects in English require 'à' in French: ressembler à (look like), obéir à (obey), plaire à (please), répondre à (answer), téléphoner à (call), survivre à (survive). This means they use COI pronouns (me, te, lui, nous, vous, leur) instead of COD. Always check whether a French verb takes 'à' — it often differs from the English construction.

items.question: Ne _____ parle pas sur ce ton !
items.translation: (Don't speak to me in that tone!)
items.explanation: Negative imperative: 'Ne me parle pas!' — the COI goes before the verb in its unstressed form ('me,' not 'moi'). Compare: 'Parle-moi!' (affirmative → stressed 'moi' after verb) vs. 'Ne me parle pas!' (negative → unstressed 'me' before verb). 'Sur ce ton' (in that tone) is a useful expression. This before/after alternation in imperatives applies equally to COD and COI pronouns, following the same rules.

items.question: Il _____ manque beaucoup depuis son départ.
items.translation: (I miss him/her a lot since his/her departure.)
items.explanation: 'Manquer' in French works inversely to English 'miss': 'il me manque' literally means 'he/she is missing TO ME' = I miss him/her. The person who is missed is the SUBJECT (il/elle), and the person who feels the absence is the INDIRECT OBJECT (me). So 'me' is the COI here. This inverted construction confuses every English speaker: 'tu me manques' = I miss you (literally: you are missing to me). The subject and object are swapped compared to English.

items.question: Nous _____ avons conseillé de voir un médecin.
items.translation: (We advised him/her to see a doctor.)
items.explanation: 'Conseiller' takes à: conseiller À quelqu'un de faire quelque chose → 'nous lui avons conseillé.' The structure 'conseiller à qqn de + infinitif' is a common pattern in French. Similar verbs: demander à qqn de (ask someone to), dire à qqn de (tell someone to), permettre à qqn de (allow someone to), promettre à qqn de (promise someone to). All use COI for the person: 'je lui ai demandé de venir.'

items.question: Cet exercice _____ semble difficile.
items.translation: (This exercise seems difficult to you.)
items.explanation: 'Sembler' takes à: sembler difficile À toi → 'te semble.' 'Cet exercice te semble difficile.' The impersonal/opinion verbs often take COI: 'ça me semble' (it seems to me), 'ça me paraît' (it appears to me), 'ça me convient' (it suits me). 'Te' is the informal COI for 'you.' These constructions elegantly express personal perception: the same exercise might 'sembler facile' to someone else. The COI personalizes the judgment.

description: Use lui, leur, me, te, nous, vous for indirect objects and understand verb constructions requiring them

name: Pronom Y (The Pronoun Y)

items.question: Tu vas à la boulangerie ? — Oui, j'_____ vais maintenant.
items.translation: (Are you going to the bakery? — Yes, I'm going there now.)
items.explanation: 'Y' replaces 'à + place': aller À la boulangerie → j'y vais. It means 'there' when replacing a location introduced by à, en, dans, sur, chez, etc. 'Y' goes before the conjugated verb, just like other pronouns. Before vowels, no elision occurs with 'y' — but 'je' elides: 'j'y vais.' 'Y' is one of French's most useful short pronouns: 'Tu y es?' (Are you there?), 'On y va!' (Let's go!), 'J'y suis!' (I'm there/I get it!).

items.question: Il habite à Paris ? — Oui, il _____ habite depuis cinq ans.
items.translation: (Does he live in Paris? — Yes, he's been living there for five years.)
items.explanation: 'Y' replaces 'à Paris': il habite à Paris → il y habite. With 'depuis' (since/for), French uses the present tense for ongoing actions: 'il y habite depuis cinq ans' (he has been living there for five years). English uses present perfect, but French uses present tense + depuis. 'Y' works with any place introduced by 'à': 'aller au cinéma → y aller,' 'rester à la maison → y rester,' 'travailler à l'hôpital → y travailler.'

items.question: Tu penses à ton examen ? — Oui, j'_____ pense souvent.
items.translation: (Are you thinking about your exam? — Yes, I often think about it.)
items.explanation: 'Y' also replaces 'à + THING' (not person): penser À quelque chose → j'y pense. For abstract complements with 'à,' 'y' is the pronoun. IMPORTANT: for people, use COI pronouns instead: 'je pense à Marie' → 'je pense à elle' (stressed pronoun after 'penser,' which is an exception). But for things: 'penser à l'examen' → 'j'y pense.' Other verbs: s'intéresser à qqch → s'y intéresser, réfléchir à qqch → y réfléchir, participer à qqch → y participer.

items.question: Nous allons au cinéma ce soir. Tu veux _____ venir avec nous ?
items.translation: (We're going to the cinema tonight. Do you want to come?)
items.explanation: With verb + infinitive, 'y' goes before the infinitive: 'tu veux Y venir.' The pronoun attaches to the verb it modifies: you're coming TO a place (venir à un lieu → y venir). Compare: 'je veux y aller,' 'je dois y aller,' 'je peux y rester.' 'Y' never separates from its verb. In passé composé: 'j'y suis allé' — 'y' before the auxiliary 'être.' With 'aller' in present: the common expression 'on y va!' shows 'y' before 'va.'

items.question: N'_____ touche pas, c'est fragile !
items.translation: (Don't touch it, it's fragile!)
items.explanation: 'Toucher à quelque chose' → 'y toucher.' In negative imperative: 'N'y touche pas!' — 'y' before the verb. Note: 'toucher à' (to touch/tamper with) takes 'à,' so we use 'y.' Plain 'toucher' (without 'à') would take a COD: 'Ne LE touche pas!' The preposition determines the pronoun. 'Toucher à' implies meddling or interfering, while 'toucher' is simply physical contact. This nuance changes the pronoun needed.

items.question: Je m'_____ habituerai avec le temps.
items.translation: (I'll get used to it with time.)
items.explanation: 'S'habituer à quelque chose' → 's'y habituer.' 'Y' replaces the 'à + thing' complement. With reflexive verbs: subject + reflexive pronoun + y + verb. 'Je m'y habituerai' (I'll get used to it). The pronoun order matters when multiple pronouns appear: reflexive → y → verb. Other reflexive + y combinations: 's'y intéresser' (to be interested in it), 's'y prendre' (to go about it), 's'y adapter' (to adapt to it).

items.question: La réunion a été utile ? — Oui, j'_____ ai appris beaucoup.
items.translation: (Was the meeting useful? — Yes, I learned a lot there.)
items.explanation: 'Y' replacing 'à la réunion' (there/at the meeting): 'j'y ai appris beaucoup.' In passé composé, 'y' goes before the auxiliary: 'j'y ai appris.' This position — before auxiliary — is consistent with all pronouns in compound tenses. 'Y' in this locative sense simply means 'there,' referring to a place previously mentioned. It's far more common in French than 'there' is in English because French avoids repeating location phrases.

items.question: Allons-_____  ! Le film commence dans dix minutes.
items.translation: (Let's go! The film starts in ten minutes.)
items.explanation: 'Allons-y!' (Let's go!) — in affirmative imperative, 'y' follows the verb with a hyphen. This is one of the most common French expressions. Note: with 'tu' imperative of 'aller,' the 's' returns before 'y' for pronunciation: 'Vas-y!' (Go/Go ahead!). The 'nous' imperative: 'Allons-y!' In negative: 'N'y allons pas!' (Let's not go!). 'Vas-y' is used constantly in conversation to mean 'go ahead,' 'come on,' or 'do it.'

items.question: Elle s'intéresse à la politique ? — Oui, elle s'_____ intéresse beaucoup.
items.translation: (Is she interested in politics? — Yes, she's very interested in it.)
items.explanation: 'S'intéresser à' → 's'y intéresser.' Replacing 'à la politique' (to politics) with 'y.' The reflexive pronoun 's'' and 'y' stack before the verb: 'elle s'y intéresse.' When pronouns accumulate, the order is strict: subject + (ne) + reflexive + y/en + verb + (pas). This order is non-negotiable. 'S'intéresser à' is a perfect example of reflexive verb + à = reflexive pronoun + y.

items.question: Tu crois à la chance ? — Non, je n'_____ crois pas.
items.translation: (Do you believe in luck? — No, I don't believe in it.)
items.explanation: 'Croire à' (to believe in) → 'y croire.' In negation: 'je n'y crois pas.' The 'ne' is placed before 'y' and 'pas' after the verb. 'Croire à' is interesting because 'croire' can take different prepositions with different meanings: 'croire à' (believe in the existence of), 'croire en' (have faith in), 'croire qqn/qqch' (believe someone/something directly). Only 'croire à' uses 'y' — 'croire en' is replaced differently.

items.question: Les clés sont dans le tiroir. — Ah oui, elles _____ sont.
items.translation: (The keys are in the drawer. — Oh yes, they're there.)
items.explanation: 'Y' replaces 'dans le tiroir' (in the drawer). Any locative preposition (dans, sur, sous, chez, en, à) can have its complement replaced by 'y': 'sur la table → elles y sont,' 'chez le docteur → j'y vais,' 'en France → j'y habite.' This broad locative function makes 'y' extremely versatile. In conversation, 'y' avoids tedious repetition of locations: 'Tu es allé au bureau?' — 'Oui, j'y suis allé ce matin.'

items.question: Il _____ a un problème avec cette machine.
items.translation: (There is a problem with this machine.)
items.explanation: 'Il y a' (there is/there are) is French's most essential existence expression, and 'y' is integral to it. Literally: 'it there has.' This expression is invariable — 'il y a' works for singular and plural: 'il y a un chat' (there's a cat), 'il y a des chats' (there are cats). In other tenses: 'il y avait' (there was/were — imparfait), 'il y a eu' (there was — passé composé), 'il y aura' (there will be). 'Il y a' + duration = 'ago': 'il y a deux ans' (two years ago).

items.question: Je n'_____ suis pour rien dans cette affaire.
items.translation: (I have nothing to do with this matter.)
items.explanation: 'Y être pour quelque chose' is an idiomatic expression meaning 'to have something to do with' or 'to be responsible for.' 'Je n'y suis pour rien' = I'm not responsible for any of it. 'Y' here doesn't refer to a specific place — it's part of a fixed expression. French has several idiomatic uses of 'y': 'ça y est!' (that's it/done!), 's'y connaître' (to know about), 's'y prendre' (to go about it). These must be learned as complete expressions.

items.question: Réfléchis-_____ avant de prendre ta décision.
items.translation: (Think about it before making your decision.)
items.explanation: 'Réfléchir à' → 'y réfléchir.' Affirmative imperative: 'Réfléchis-y!' — pronoun follows with hyphen. Note: 'réfléchir' keeps the 's' in tu imperative because it's an -IR verb (not -ER). Other verbs with à in the imperative + y: 'Penses-y!' (Think about it! — note the 's' returns on -ER verb before 'y,' like 'Vas-y'), 'Participez-y!' (Take part in it!). The euphonic 's' on -ER verbs before 'y' prevents the awkward [ə.i] collision.

items.question: On _____ va ! À demain !
items.translation: (Let's go! See you tomorrow!)
items.explanation: 'On y va!' (Let's go! / We're off!) is one of French's most ubiquitous expressions. 'On' (informal 'we') + 'y' (there) + 'va' (go) = literally 'we go there,' but used as a general 'let's go/let's start.' You'll hear it in restaurants (when everyone's ready to order), at parties (when it's time to leave), at work (when starting a task). It's cheerful and decisive. 'À demain!' (Until tomorrow / See you tomorrow) is the perfect casual sign-off.

description: Master the pronoun y replacing locations (à + place) and abstract complements (à + thing)

name: Pronom En (The Pronoun En)

items.question: Tu veux du café ? — Oui, j'_____ veux bien.
items.translation: (Do you want some coffee? — Yes, I'd like some.)
items.explanation: 'En' replaces 'de + noun' — here, 'du café' (partitive = de + le). 'J'en veux' = I want some (of it). 'En' is essential for replacing nouns introduced by partitive articles (du, de la, de l', des). Without 'en,' you'd have to repeat the full noun phrase every time. 'En' goes before the verb like other pronouns. The polite 'j'en veux bien' is softer than 'j'en veux' — 'bien' adds courtesy. This is a daily-use construction in French.

items.question: Combien de frères as-tu ? — J'_____ ai deux.
items.translation: (How many brothers do you have? — I have two.)
items.explanation: When specifying a QUANTITY, 'en' replaces the noun and the number goes at the end: 'J'en ai deux' (I have two of them). You cannot simply say 'J'ai deux' — French requires 'en' to stand for what you have two of. This is mandatory with numbers: 'J'en veux trois,' 'J'en ai mangé six,' 'Il en reste cinq.' With expressions of quantity: 'J'en ai beaucoup,' 'J'en ai peu,' 'J'en ai assez.' The quantity element moves to the end; 'en' replaces the noun.

items.question: Tu as des enfants ? — Oui, j'_____ ai trois.
items.translation: (Do you have children? — Yes, I have three.)
items.explanation: 'Des enfants' → 'en' (replacing des + noun). 'J'en ai trois' — the number 'trois' stays at the end. Note: when the answer is 'oui' without specifying quantity, 'en' still appears: 'Oui, j'en ai.' For negative: 'Non, je n'en ai pas.' With 'un(e)': 'J'en ai un' keeps the article because it functions as a number (one). This is different from 'Je n'en ai pas' where no number is given. The 'en + number' pattern is fundamental for all quantity-related responses.

items.question: Elle parle souvent de son voyage ? — Oui, elle _____ parle tout le temps.
items.translation: (Does she often talk about her trip? — Yes, she talks about it all the time.)
items.explanation: 'En' replaces 'de son voyage' — the complement of 'parler de': 'elle en parle.' For THINGS, 'de + noun' → 'en.' For PEOPLE, it's more complex: 'parler de Marie' → 'parler d'elle' (stressed pronoun after 'de,' not 'en'). This thing vs. person distinction is important: 'J'en parle' (about a thing/topic) vs. 'Je parle d'elle/de lui' (about a person). Some expressions always use 'en' regardless: 's'en aller' (to leave), 'en vouloir à' (to hold a grudge).

items.question: Tu as besoin d'aide ? — Oui, j'_____ ai vraiment besoin.
items.translation: (Do you need help? — Yes, I really need some.)
items.explanation: 'Avoir besoin DE' → 'en avoir besoin.' 'De + aide' is replaced by 'en': 'j'en ai besoin.' Verbs and expressions with 'de' that use 'en': avoir besoin de, avoir envie de, avoir peur de, se souvenir de, profiter de, se servir de, être content de, être sûr de. Whenever you see 'de' in the verb construction and the complement is a thing, 'en' is your pronoun. 'J'en ai besoin' is one of the most practical phrases in French.

items.question: Il revient de la réunion ? — Oui, il _____ revient à l'instant.
items.translation: (Is he coming back from the meeting? — Yes, he's just coming back from it.)
items.explanation: 'En' can replace 'de + place' meaning 'from there': 'revenir de la réunion' → 'en revenir.' This locative use of 'en' mirrors 'y' for 'à/dans/sur + place.' 'Y' = to/at a place, 'en' = from a place. 'J'y vais' (I'm going there) vs. 'j'en reviens' (I'm coming back from there). 'À l'instant' means 'just now' or 'this very moment.' This from-a-place usage of 'en' is less common than its partitive use but equally essential.

items.question: Il y a du lait ? — Non, il n'y _____ a plus.
items.translation: (Is there any milk? — No, there isn't any left.)
items.explanation: In 'il y a du lait,' the 'du lait' (partitive) is replaced by 'en': 'il n'y en a plus.' Notice both 'y' and 'en' in the same sentence — 'il y a' is a fixed expression, and 'en' replaces the partitive complement. The word order is: il + n' + y + en + a + plus. When 'y' and 'en' coexist, 'y' always comes before 'en.' 'Il n'y en a plus' is a very common response meaning 'there's none left.' The pronoun stacking becomes natural with practice.

items.question: Prends-_____ un peu, c'est délicieux !
items.translation: (Take some, it's delicious!)
items.explanation: Affirmative imperative: 'Prends-en!' — 'en' follows the verb with a hyphen. For -ER verbs in tu imperative, the 's' returns before 'en': 'Manges-en!' (Eat some!), 'Donnes-en!' (Give some!). This euphonic 's' prevents a vowel clash. 'Un peu' (a little) specifies the quantity: 'Prends-en un peu.' In negative imperative, 'en' goes back before the verb: 'N'en prends pas!' The affirmative/negative position switch applies to 'en' just like other pronouns.

items.question: Tu es sûr de ta décision ? — Oui, j'_____ suis certain.
items.translation: (Are you sure of your decision? — Yes, I'm certain of it.)
items.explanation: 'Être sûr/certain DE' → 'en être sûr/certain.' 'J'en suis certain' = I'm certain of it. Adjectives that take 'de' use 'en' for their complement: content de → j'en suis content, fier de → j'en suis fier, satisfait de → j'en suis satisfait, conscient de → j'en suis conscient. This pattern extends to many expressions: 'j'en ai assez' (I've had enough of it), 'j'en ai marre' (I'm fed up with it — colloquial).

items.question: Tu veux combien de croissants ? — J'_____ voudrais quatre, s'il vous plaît.
items.translation: (How many croissants would you like? — I'd like four, please.)
items.explanation: The classic bakery order: 'J'en voudrais quatre.' 'En' replaces 'de croissants' and the number follows the verb. Using the conditional 'voudrais' (would like) is the polite form for requests. This en + number construction is essential for shopping: 'J'en prends trois' (I'll take three), 'J'en voudrais un kilo' (I'd like a kilo). The number/quantity always goes at the end, after the verb and any participle: 'J'en ai acheté six.'

items.question: Va-t-_____  ! Tout va bien se passer.
items.translation: (Go on/away! Everything will be fine.)
items.explanation: 'S'en aller' (to go away/leave) is an idiomatic reflexive verb. Imperative: 'Va-t'en!' (Go away! — tu form), 'Allons-nous-en!' (Let's go!), 'Allez-vous-en!' (Go away! — vous form). The 't'' is the reflexive pronoun 'te' elided. 'S'en aller' is one of several fixed expressions with 'en' where the pronoun doesn't have a clear 'de + noun' meaning: 'en vouloir à' (hold a grudge), 'en avoir assez' (have enough), 's'en faire' (to worry).

items.question: Des erreurs, tout le monde _____ fait.
items.translation: (Mistakes, everyone makes them.)
items.explanation: 'Des erreurs' (partitive plural) → 'en.' 'Tout le monde en fait.' This sentence uses topicalization: the noun is placed first for emphasis ('Des erreurs,...'), then 'en' replaces it in the active clause. This is very natural in spoken French. Compare using 'les': 'Les erreurs, tout le monde les fait' (THE mistakes — specific ones). The choice between 'en' (indefinite/partitive) and 'les' (definite) depends on whether you mean 'some mistakes' or 'the mistakes.'

items.question: Elle se souvient de cette histoire ? — Oui, elle s'_____ souvient bien.
items.translation: (Does she remember this story? — Yes, she remembers it well.)
items.explanation: 'Se souvenir DE' → 's'en souvenir.' The reflexive and 'en' stack: 'elle s'en souvient.' The order is always: reflexive pronoun + en + verb. This combination occurs with several reflexive verbs requiring 'de': s'occuper de → s'en occuper, se servir de → s'en servir, se charger de → s'en charger, se passer de → s'en passer. 'Elle s'en souvient bien' means she remembers it well — a complete and natural response.

items.question: Je n'_____ peux plus de ce bruit !
items.translation: (I can't stand this noise anymore!)
items.explanation: 'N'en pouvoir plus' is an idiomatic expression meaning 'can't take it anymore/can't stand it.' 'Je n'en peux plus!' expresses exasperation or exhaustion. The 'en' here is part of a fixed expression — it doesn't clearly replace a 'de + noun.' French has several such idiomatic uses of 'en': 'en avoir assez' (to have had enough), 'en avoir marre' (to be fed up — colloquial), 'en faire voir' (to give someone a hard time). These should be learned as whole units.

items.question: Tu manges de la viande ? — Non, je n'_____ mange jamais.
items.translation: (Do you eat meat? — No, I never eat any.)
items.explanation: 'De la viande' (partitive) → 'en.' In negation: 'je n'en mange jamais.' The negation ne...jamais wraps around the pronoun + verb: ne + en + verb + jamais. When 'en' replaces a partitive in a negative sentence, no additional 'de' is needed — 'en' already contains the partitive meaning. Compare: without pronoun: 'je ne mange jamais DE viande' (partitive → de in negation). With pronoun: 'je n'en mange jamais' — 'en' absorbs the 'de' entirely.

description: Use en to replace de + noun, partitive expressions, and quantities in everyday French

name: Accord du Participe Passé (Past Participle Agreement)

items.question: Elle est _____ à la gare ce matin.
items.translation: (She went to the station this morning.)
items.explanation: With 'être' as auxiliary, the past participle ALWAYS agrees with the SUBJECT in gender and number. 'Elle' is feminine singular → 'allée' (add -e). This is the foundational agreement rule for the 17 'être' verbs (aller, venir, partir, arriver, etc.) plus all reflexive verbs. Masculine singular: allé, feminine singular: allée, masculine plural: allés, feminine plural: allées. This agreement is visible in writing; in speech, the feminine -e is often silent.

items.question: Les filles sont _____ hier soir.
items.translation: (The girls arrived yesterday evening.)
items.explanation: 'Les filles' (feminine plural) + être → participle agrees: 'arrivées' (-é + -es for feminine plural). The agreement adds both '-e' for feminine and '-s' for plural. Arriver is one of the DR MRS VANDERTRAMP verbs that take 'être': Devenir, Revenir, Monter, Retourner, Sortir, Venir, Aller, Naître, Descendre, Entrer, Rentrer, Tomber, Rester, Arriver, Mourir, Partir. All follow this subject-agreement pattern.

items.question: La tarte que j'ai _____ était délicieuse.
items.translation: (The tart that I made was delicious.)
items.explanation: With 'avoir,' the past participle agrees with the DIRECT OBJECT — but ONLY when the direct object PRECEDES the verb. 'La tarte' is feminine singular and it precedes 'ai faite' through the relative pronoun 'que.' So: 'fait' → 'faite' (add -e). Without preceding COD, no agreement: 'J'ai fait une tarte' (COD follows — no agreement). The three triggers for preceding COD: relative pronoun 'que,' direct object pronouns (le/la/les), and interrogative 'quel(le)(s).'

items.question: Ils se sont _____ dans le parc.
items.translation: (They met each other in the park.)
items.explanation: Reflexive verbs use 'être' but agreement depends on the function of the reflexive pronoun. If the reflexive pronoun is a DIRECT object → agree with subject. 'Se rencontrer' — 'se' is the direct object (they met EACH OTHER — 'se' = directObject). 'Ils' masculine plural → 'rencontrés.' Contrast: 'elles se sont téléphoné' — no agreement because 'se' is INdirect (téléphoner À quelqu'un). The direct/indirect function of 'se' determines agreement.

items.question: Les lettres ? Je les ai _____ ce matin.
items.translation: (The letters? I sent them this morning.)
items.explanation: 'Les' (feminine plural COD pronoun, referring to 'les lettres') precedes 'avoir' → the past participle agrees: 'envoyées.' This is the COD pronoun trigger for agreement. 'Les lettres' = feminine plural, so 'envoyé' gains '-ées.' This rule is strict in written French. Steps to determine agreement with 'avoir': 1) Find the COD. 2) Is it BEFORE the verb? 3) If yes, make the participle agree. If no, leave it unchanged. 'Je les ai envoyées' vs. 'J'ai envoyé les lettres.'

items.question: Marie et Sophie sont _____ de vacances.
items.translation: (Marie and Sophie came back from vacation.)
items.explanation: Two feminine subjects (Marie et Sophie) + être → feminine plural agreement: 'revenues.' With 'être,' always look at the subject. When multiple subjects are all feminine → feminine plural (-es). When there's at least one masculine → masculine plural (-s). This 'masculine wins' rule (accord au masculin) is a traditional French grammar convention. 'Revenir' is an être verb (it's in the DR MRS VANDERTRAMP list). The 'revenue' form would be for one female only.

items.question: Elle s'est _____ les mains avant de manger.
items.translation: (She washed her hands before eating.)
items.explanation: Tricky! 'Se laver les mains' — here 'se' is an INDIRECT object (she washed the hands TO HERSELF), and 'les mains' is the DIRECT object. Since the reflexive pronoun is INdirect, NO agreement with the subject: 'elle s'est lavé.' Compare: 'elle s'est lavée' (she washed herself — 'se' IS the direct object, so agreement). The presence of a body part as direct object makes 'se' indirect. This is the most frequently tested advanced agreement rule.

items.question: Les chansons que nous avons _____ étaient magnifiques.
items.translation: (The songs that we heard were magnificent.)
items.explanation: 'Les chansons' (feminine plural) is the direct object, and it precedes the verb through 'que': 'que nous avons entendues.' Agreement with 'avoir' when COD precedes: 'entendu' → 'entendues' (-u + -es for feminine plural). The relative pronoun 'que' represents 'les chansons' and acts as the preceding COD. This is very common in written French — any time you describe a noun with a relative clause using 'que,' check for participle agreement.

items.question: Nous nous sommes _____ à la fête.
items.translation: (We had fun at the party.)
items.explanation: 'S'amuser' is reflexive with 'se' as direct object (amuser soi-même). 'Nous' (masculine plural or mixed group) → 'amusés.' Reflexive verbs that take their reflexive pronoun as COD: s'amuser, se lever, se coucher, se préparer, se rencontrer, se voir. With these, participle agrees with the subject. If 'nous' were all female: 'amusées.' The default masculine plural applies when the group is mixed or the gender is unknown.

items.question: Combien de livres as-tu _____ ?
items.translation: (How many books did you read?)
items.explanation: 'Combien de livres' (masculine plural) is the direct object, and it PRECEDES the verb in this interrogative construction: 'as-tu lus.' The interrogative 'combien de' triggers preceding COD agreement. 'Livres' = masculine plural → 'lu' + '-s' = 'lus.' This is a less commonly known agreement trigger. It also applies with 'quel(le)s': 'Quelles robes as-tu achetées?' Both interrogative and exclamative preceding CODs trigger agreement.

items.question: Pierre et son frère sont _____ en retard.
items.translation: (Pierre and his brother arrived late.)
items.explanation: Two masculine subjects (Pierre et son frère) + être → masculine plural: 'arrivés.' Simple subject-agreement with être. When both subjects are masculine, the masculine plural form is clear. The '-s' marks the plural. Remember: with être verbs, ALWAYS check the subject's gender and number. This rule has NO exceptions (unlike avoir's conditional agreement). All 17 être verbs and all reflexive verbs follow this pattern.

items.question: Elles se sont _____ au téléphone pendant une heure.
items.translation: (They spoke to each other on the phone for an hour.)
items.explanation: No agreement because 'parler' takes an indirect object: parler À quelqu'un. In 'se sont parlé,' 'se' is the INdirect object (they spoke TO each other). When the reflexive pronoun is COI, the participle stays invariable. Other common examples: se téléphoner, s'écrire, se mentir, se sourire, se plaire, se succéder, se ressembler. All have 'à' in their base construction and therefore take no agreement.

items.question: La robe qu'elle a _____ est magnifique.
items.translation: (The dress she chose is magnificent.)
items.explanation: 'La robe' (feminine singular) precedes 'avoir' via 'qu'' (que elided): agreement. 'Choisi' → 'choisie' (+e for feminine singular). This pattern — noun + que + subject + avoir + AGREED participle — is the most common agreement trigger in written French. Train yourself to check: after 'que' + avoir → does the antecedent of 'que' require agreement? 'Le film qu'il a vu' (masc. — no visible change), 'la chanson qu'il a chantée' (fem. — add -e).

items.question: Les enfants se sont _____ les dents.
items.translation: (The children brushed their teeth.)
items.explanation: 'Se brosser les dents' — 'se' is INdirect (brush the teeth TO THEMSELVES), 'les dents' is the direct object. Since 'se' = COI, no agreement: 'brossé' stays invariable. This is identical to 'se laver les mains.' Whenever a reflexive verb has a body part as direct object, the reflexive pronoun is automatically indirect, and no agreement occurs. Key examples: se couper les cheveux, se casser la jambe, se faire mal — all invariable.

items.question: Ma sœur est _____ médecin.
items.translation: (My sister became a doctor.)
items.explanation: 'Devenir' is an être verb. 'Ma sœur' (feminine singular) → 'devenue.' The agreement is straightforward: subject feminine → participle feminine. 'Devenir' follows the standard être agreement pattern. Note that the profession 'médecin' traditionally has no feminine form in French (though 'médecine' is debated). The agreement is on the participle, not the profession. All DR MRS VANDERTRAMP verbs work identically: check subject gender/number, match participle.

description: Master past participle agreement rules with avoir and être in the passé composé

name: Plus-que-parfait (Pluperfect Tense)

items.question: Quand je suis arrivé, le train _____ déjà parti.
items.translation: (When I arrived, the train had already left.)
items.explanation: The plus-que-parfait expresses an action completed BEFORE another past action. It's formed with the IMPARFAIT of the auxiliary (avoir/être) + past participle. 'Partir' takes être → 'était parti.' The action sequence: the train left (earlier past = plus-que-parfait) BEFORE I arrived (later past = passé composé). This tense helps establish chronological order in narratives. Without it, it's impossible to express 'had done' in French.

items.question: Nous _____ fini nos devoirs avant de sortir.
items.translation: (We had finished our homework before going out.)
items.explanation: 'Finir' takes avoir → imparfait of avoir: j'avais, tu avais, il avait, nous avions, vous aviez, ils avaient + participe passé. 'Nous avions fini' = we had finished. The imparfait of 'avoir' provides the 'had' meaning. The plus-que-parfait is used with 'avant de + infinitive' (before doing) to emphasize that one action preceded another. In English, 'had finished before going out' uses the same logic.

items.question: Elle ne savait pas que tu _____ déménagé.
items.translation: (She didn't know that you had moved.)
items.explanation: 'Tu avais déménagé' — the moving happened BEFORE the not-knowing. In reported speech and after verbs like 'savoir,' 'croire,' 'penser' in the past, French uses plus-que-parfait for the 'further back' action. This parallels English 'had + past participle.' The imparfait auxiliary marks the tense: 'avais' for 'tu.' 'Déménager' takes 'avoir': j'avais déménagé, tu avais déménagé, il avait déménagé.

items.question: Les enfants _____ déjà couchés quand nous sommes rentrés.
items.translation: (The children had already gone to bed when we came home.)
items.explanation: Reflexive verb 'se coucher' → être auxiliary → imparfait of être: 'ils s'étaient couchés.' Reflexive verbs always use être, including in plus-que-parfait. The imparfait of être: j'étais, tu étais, il était, nous étions, vous étiez, ils étaient. Combined with reflexive: 'ils s'étaient couchés' (they had gone to bed). Past participle agrees with subject: 'couchés' (masculine plural). The chronological sequence is clear: bedtime before homecoming.

items.question: J'_____ oublié de fermer la porte en partant.
items.translation: (I had forgotten to close the door when leaving.)
items.explanation: 'J'avais oublié' — the forgetting occurred in the past, viewed from another past point. Imparfait of avoir (1st person): 'avais.' Plus-que-parfait with 'oublier de' (to forget to): 'j'avais oublié de fermer.' 'En partant' (upon leaving/when leaving) uses the gérondif — the '-ant' form with 'en.' The plus-que-parfait emphasizes that the oversight happened during the past departure, not at the current moment.

items.question: Si j'_____ su, je ne serais pas venu.
items.translation: (If I had known, I wouldn't have come.)
items.explanation: The plus-que-parfait is used in 'si' (if) clauses for unreal past conditions: 'Si + plus-que-parfait, conditionnel passé.' 'Si j'avais su' (if I had known) → 'je ne serais pas venu' (I wouldn't have come). This is the third conditional pattern: Si + PQP → Conditionnel Passé. Never use the conditional after 'si' in French — always 'si + imparfait/PQP.' This is a fundamental rule with no exceptions.

items.question: Tu m'as dit que tu _____ déjà mangé.
items.translation: (You told me you had already eaten.)
items.explanation: In reported speech (discours indirect), when the reporting verb is past ('tu m'as dit'), the direct speech shifts tenses. Passé composé → Plus-que-parfait: 'J'ai mangé' → 'tu avais mangé.' This concordance of tenses (concordance des temps) is mandatory in formal French. Direct: 'J'ai déjà mangé.' Indirect: 'Il a dit qu'il avait déjà mangé.' The PQP signals one step further back in time from the already-past reporting verb.

items.question: Nous _____ jamais visité ce musée avant hier.
items.translation: (We had never visited this museum before yesterday.)
items.explanation: Negation in plus-que-parfait: 'ne + auxiliary + jamais + participle.' 'Nous n'avions jamais visité.' The word order is the same as passé composé negation but with the imparfait auxiliary. 'Jamais' replaces 'pas' and goes between auxiliary and participle. 'Avant hier' (before yesterday) sets the temporal reference point. The PQP expresses that up until yesterday, the experience had never occurred — a perfect use case for this tense.

items.question: Elles _____ déjà parties quand l'orage a éclaté.
items.translation: (They had already left when the storm broke out.)
items.explanation: 'Partir' uses être → 'elles étaient parties.' The participle agrees with feminine plural subject: 'parties' (-ie + -s). Plus-que-parfait with être: être in imparfait + agreed participle. The two-event structure is classic: they left (earlier = PQP) before the storm broke (later = passé composé). 'Déjà' (already) reinforces the completion. 'Éclater' (to break out/burst) is a vivid verb for storms, wars, scandals.

items.question: Il _____ promis de venir mais il a annulé au dernier moment.
items.translation: (He had promised to come but he cancelled at the last minute.)
items.explanation: 'Il avait promis' — the promise happened BEFORE the cancellation. Two past events: promise (earlier = PQP) → cancellation (later = passé composé). 'Promettre de + infinitif' (to promise to do). 'Au dernier moment' (at the last moment/minute). This contrast between PQP and passé composé creates natural narrative flow. French uses this pairing extensively in storytelling, journalism, and everyday recounting of events.

items.question: Comme il _____ beaucoup plu, les rues étaient inondées.
items.translation: (As it had rained a lot, the streets were flooded.)
items.explanation: 'Il avait plu' — impersonal 'il' + plus-que-parfait of 'pleuvoir.' The rain (PQP = earlier) caused the flooding (imparfait = result/state). 'Comme' (as/since/because) introduces the causal clause. Note the result is in imparfait ('étaient inondées' — describing a state), not passé composé. PQP works beautifully with cause-and-effect in the past: the earlier cause (PQP) produces the observed state (imparfait).

items.question: Je ne savais pas qu'ils _____ mariés.
items.translation: (I didn't know they had gotten married.)
items.explanation: 'Se marier' (reflexive) → être auxiliary → 'ils s'étaient mariés.' In a subordinate clause after a past-tense main verb ('je ne savais pas'), the PQP marks the anterior action. Reflexive PQP: subject + reflexive pronoun + être (imparfait) + participle. 'Mariés' agrees with masculine plural subject. The que/qu' introduces the subordinate clause. This construction is essential for narration and reporting past events.

items.question: On _____ prévu un pique-nique mais il a commencé à pleuvoir.
items.translation: (We had planned a picnic but it started raining.)
items.explanation: 'On' always takes 3rd person singular conjugation: 'on avait prévu.' Despite meaning 'we,' 'on' grammatically behaves as 'il/elle.' Plus-que-parfait: 'avait prévu' (had planned). The disappointed expectation structure is very French: PQP (the plan) + mais + passé composé (the disruption). 'Prévoir' (to plan/foresee) is conjugated like 'voir': prévu, vu, revu. 'Il a commencé à pleuvoir' uses passé composé for the later event.

items.question: _____ -vous déjà essayé ce restaurant avant ?
items.translation: (Had you already tried this restaurant before?)
items.explanation: Question in plus-que-parfait: 'Aviez-vous essayé ?' The inversion question form places the imparfait auxiliary ('aviez') before the subject pronoun ('vous'). 'Déjà' goes between auxiliary and participle. This formal interrogative is common in writing and polite speech. In conversation, the same question would be: 'Vous aviez déjà essayé ce restaurant ?' (rising intonation) or 'Est-ce que vous aviez déjà essayé ?' Less formal but equally valid.

items.question: Après qu'elle _____ terminé son discours, tout le monde a applaudi.
items.translation: (After she had finished her speech, everyone applauded.)
items.explanation: 'Après que' + indicative. While 'avant que' takes subjunctive, 'après que' officially takes the indicative in standard grammar. 'Elle avait terminé' (she had finished) — PQP marks the action completed before the applause. In everyday French, many speakers use subjunctive after 'après que' by analogy with 'avant que,' but prescriptive grammar says indicative. The PQP is perfect here: finishing (earlier) → applauding (later). Note: 'eut terminé' would be passé antérieur (literary).

description: Express actions completed before another past action using the pluperfect tense

name: Futur Antérieur (Future Perfect)

items.question: Quand tu arriveras, j'_____ déjà préparé le dîner.
items.translation: (When you arrive, I will have already prepared dinner.)
items.explanation: The futur antérieur expresses an action that WILL BE completed before another future action. Formation: futur simple of auxiliary (avoir/être) + past participle. 'Avoir' in futur: j'aurai, tu auras, il aura, nous aurons, vous aurez, ils auront. 'J'aurai préparé' = I will have prepared. The 'quand + futur simple' clause sets the future reference point. Important: French uses futur after 'quand' where English uses present ('when you arrive').

items.question: D'ici demain, ils _____ terminé le projet.
items.translation: (By tomorrow, they will have finished the project.)
items.explanation: 'D'ici' (by/within) + time expression triggers futur antérieur for actions completed by that point. 'Ils auront terminé' — they will have finished. 'Auront' is the 3rd person plural of 'avoir' in futur simple. 'D'ici demain' (by tomorrow), 'd'ici la fin du mois' (by the end of the month), 'd'ici 2030' — all naturally pair with futur antérieur. This tense is more common in French than 'will have' is in English.

items.question: Quand vous _____ lu ce livre, dites-moi ce que vous en pensez.
items.translation: (When you have read this book, tell me what you think.)
items.explanation: After 'quand' (when), 'lorsque' (when), 'dès que' (as soon as), 'aussitôt que' (as soon as), French uses futur antérieur where English uses present perfect. 'Quand vous aurez lu' = when you have read/will have read. This is a key grammatical difference: English uses present/present perfect after 'when' for future events, but French uses futur simple or futur antérieur. This rule is non-negotiable in standard French.

items.question: Dès que j'_____ reçu la réponse, je vous appellerai.
items.translation: (As soon as I have received the answer, I'll call you.)
items.explanation: 'Dès que' (as soon as) + futur antérieur: 'dès que j'aurai reçu' → main clause in futur simple: 'je vous appellerai.' The sequence: receive answer (completed future = futur antérieur) → then call (simple future). 'Recevoir' is irregular: reçu. The futur antérieur after 'dès que' is absolutely standard and tested in every French exam. The 'futur after future conjunctions' rule is one of the most distinctive features of French grammar.

items.question: Elle _____ partie avant notre arrivée.
items.translation: (She will have left before our arrival.)
items.explanation: 'Partir' takes être → futur of être: je serai, tu seras, il/elle sera, nous serons, vous serez, ils seront. 'Elle sera partie' = she will have left. Agreement: 'partie' (feminine singular — agrees with 'elle'). The être verbs in futur antérieur: 'il sera venu,' 'elles seront arrivées,' 'nous serons sortis.' All maintain the subject-participle agreement that characterizes être verbs across all compound tenses.

items.question: Il _____ probablement oublié, ce n'est pas grave.
items.translation: (He has probably forgotten, it's not a big deal.)
items.explanation: The futur antérieur can express PROBABILITY or SUPPOSITION about a past event — not a future one! 'Il aura oublié' = he has probably forgotten / he must have forgotten. This is a common and elegant use. Rather than saying 'il a probablement oublié,' French can use the futur antérieur alone to convey the same conjecture. Other examples: 'Elle aura mal compris' (She must have misunderstood), 'Ils se seront perdus' (They probably got lost).

items.question: Lorsqu'il _____ économisé assez d'argent, il achètera une maison.
items.translation: (When he has saved enough money, he'll buy a house.)
items.explanation: 'Lorsque' (when — more formal than 'quand') + futur antérieur: 'lorsqu'il aura économisé.' The same rule applies: future conjunctions (quand, lorsque, dès que, aussitôt que, après que, une fois que) take futur in French. The main clause: 'il achètera' (futur simple). The chronological logic: saving money (completed: futur antérieur) → buying house (next: futur simple). 'Économiser' (to save money) follows regular -er conjugation patterns.

items.question: Une fois que vous _____ compris la règle, ça sera facile.
items.translation: (Once you have understood the rule, it will be easy.)
items.explanation: 'Une fois que' (once) + futur antérieur: 'une fois que vous aurez compris.' This is yet another future conjunction requiring futur in French. 'Comprendre' → 'compris' (irregular past participle). The main clause 'ça sera facile' uses futur simple. 'Une fois que' emphasizes the completeness — 'once' implies the action is fully realized. In everyday French, 'une fois que' is often shortened to 'une fois' in informal speech.

items.question: Nous _____ fini avant minuit, j'en suis sûr.
items.translation: (We will have finished before midnight, I'm sure of it.)
items.explanation: 'Nous aurons fini' — 1st person plural futur antérieur. 'Aurons' is the futur of 'avoir' for 'nous.' The deadline 'avant minuit' (before midnight) pairs naturally with futur antérieur — expressing completion before a future time point. 'J'en suis sûr' (I'm sure of it) — 'en' refers back to the whole proposition. This confident assertion about a future completion is a typical text/conversation use of futur antérieur.

items.question: Quand tu _____ à Paris, appelle-moi.
items.translation: (When you have arrived in Paris, call me.)
items.explanation: 'Arriver' takes être → futur of être (tu seras) + 'arrivé.' The full form: 'quand tu seras arrivé.' After 'quand' for a future event: futur antérieur. The imperative 'appelle-moi' in the main clause gives an instruction for when the future action is complete. With être verbs, the futur antérieur looks like: 'tu seras arrivé/e,' 'il sera venu,' 'elles seront parties.' Agreement rules remain the same as in passé composé.

items.question: Ça _____ été une bonne leçon pour lui.
items.translation: (It will have been a good lesson for him. / That was probably a good lesson for him.)
items.explanation: 'Ça aura été' uses futur antérieur of 'être' (auxiliaire avoir: 'être' uses 'avoir' when it's the main verb itself in compound tenses — 'j'ai été'). Here it conveys probability about a past event: 'that was probably/must have been a good lesson.' This probability use is sophisticated and common in spoken French. 'Aura été' = will have been / must have been. The dual meaning (future completed action or past conjecture) depends on context.

items.question: Aussitôt que la pluie _____ cessé, nous sortirons.
items.translation: (As soon as the rain has stopped, we'll go out.)
items.explanation: 'Aussitôt que' (as soon as) + futur antérieur: 'aussitôt que la pluie aura cessé.' This follows the same pattern as 'dès que,' 'quand,' 'lorsque.' 'Cesser' (to stop/cease) is more formal than 's'arrêter.' Main clause in futur simple: 'nous sortirons.' All these temporal conjunctions marking a future sequence demand futur antérieur for the first-completed action. This is one of French grammar's most systematic and testable rules.

items.question: Elle se _____ habituée à la nouvelle vie d'ici quelques mois.
items.translation: (She will have gotten used to the new life within a few months.)
items.explanation: Reflexive 's'habituer' → être auxiliary → futur of être: 'se sera habituée.' Full: 'elle se sera habituée.' The reflexive pronoun 'se' stays before the auxiliary. Agreement: 'habituée' (feminine singular — agrees with 'elle'). Reflexive futur antérieur: subject + reflexive + être (futur) + participle. 'D'ici quelques mois' (within/in a few months) sets the future completion time. This tense works naturally with 'd'ici' expressions.

items.question: Ils _____ sans doute pris le mauvais chemin.
items.translation: (They must have taken the wrong path.)
items.explanation: Futur antérieur for past hypothesis: 'ils auront pris' = they must have/probably took. 'Sans doute' (probably/no doubt) reinforces the conjectural meaning, but the futur antérieur alone can express probability. 'Prendre le mauvais chemin' (to take the wrong path) is both literal and metaphorical. This conjecture use distinguishes advanced French speakers — instead of 'ils ont probablement pris,' the elegant 'ils auront pris' conveys the same idea more concisely.

items.question: Quand j'_____ tout rangé, je pourrai me reposer.
items.translation: (When I have tidied everything up, I'll be able to rest.)
items.explanation: 'Quand j'aurai tout rangé' = when I will have tidied everything. Futur antérieur after 'quand' for the first-completed future action. 'Ranger' (to tidy/organize) — 'rangé' is the participle. 'Je pourrai' (futur simple of 'pouvoir') in the main clause. The sentence embodies the core futur antérieur logic: complete one future task (quand + FA), then proceed to the next (futur simple). This tense pairing is fundamental in expressing planned sequences.

description: Describe future actions that will be completed before a specific point using the future perfect

name: Conditionnel Passé (Past Conditional)

items.question: Si j'avais su, je _____ venu plus tôt.
items.translation: (If I had known, I would have come earlier.)
items.explanation: The conditionnel passé (past conditional) is formed with the CONDITIONAL of the auxiliary + past participle. 'Venir' takes être → conditionnel of être: serais. 'Je serais venu' = I would have come. This tense appears in the result clause of 3rd conditional sentences: Si + plus-que-parfait, conditionnel passé. It expresses what WOULD HAVE happened if conditions had been different. The structure is parallel to English 'would have + past participle.'

items.question: J'_____ aimé voyager en Australie.
items.translation: (I would have liked to travel to Australia.)
items.explanation: 'Aurais' is the conditionnel of 'avoir' (1st person): j'aurais, tu aurais, il aurait, nous aurions, vous auriez, ils auraient. 'J'aurais aimé' = I would have liked — expressing an unrealized wish. This standalone use (without a 'si' clause) conveys regret or a missed opportunity. 'J'aurais aimé + infinitif' is a polished way to express 'I wish I had...' In conversation, it's more refined than 'je voulais' for expressing past desires.

items.question: Elle _____ dû prendre un parapluie.
items.translation: (She should have taken an umbrella.)
items.explanation: 'Aurait dû' = should have. The conditionnel passé of 'devoir' expresses a past obligation that wasn't fulfilled — 'she should have (but didn't).' 'Devoir' in conditionnel passé = should have / ought to have. Similar construction: 'aurait pu' (could have / might have — pouvoir). 'Elle aurait pu nous aider' (She could have helped us). These modal verbs in conditionnel passé convey hindsight and unfulfilled expectations.

items.question: Sans ton aide, je n'_____ pas réussi.
items.translation: (Without your help, I wouldn't have succeeded.)
items.explanation: 'Sans' (without) + noun can replace a 'si' clause to express a counterfactual: 'sans ton aide' = 'si tu ne m'avais pas aidé.' The conditionnel passé in the main clause: 'je n'aurais pas réussi.' The negation wraps around 'aurais': ne + aurais + pas. Other prepositions creating implicit conditions: 'à ta place' (in your place), 'avec plus de temps' (with more time). These elegant alternatives to 'si' clauses are common in sophisticated French.

items.question: Nous _____ partis plus tôt si nous avions su.
items.translation: (We would have left earlier if we had known.)
items.explanation: 'Partir' takes être → conditionnel of être: serions. 'Nous serions partis' = we would have left. Agreement: 'partis' (masculine plural or mixed group). The 'si' clause is in plus-que-parfait ('si nous avions su'). Note: the 'si' clause can come before or after the main clause — position is flexible. 'Nous serions partis si...' or 'Si nous avions su, nous serions partis.' The meaning is identical; position is stylistic.

items.question: Tu _____ pu m'avertir quand même !
items.translation: (You could have warned me anyway!)
items.explanation: 'Aurais pu' = could have — conditionnel passé of 'pouvoir.' This expresses reproach: 'you could have (but you didn't)!' 'Quand même' (anyway/all the same) adds indignation. 'Pouvoir' in conditionnel passé often conveys: 1) missed possibility: 'on aurait pu se voir' (we could have met), 2) reproach: 'tu aurais pu me dire!' (you could have told me!), 3) polite suggestion: 'vous auriez pu essayer' (you might have tried).

items.question: D'après les médias, il y _____ eu un accident sur l'autoroute.
items.translation: (According to the media, there was reportedly an accident on the highway.)
items.explanation: The conditionnel passé can report UNCONFIRMED past information — the 'journalistic conditional.' 'Il y aurait eu' = there reportedly was / there allegedly was. Journalists use this to maintain distance from unverified claims. 'D'après les médias' (according to the media) is the source attribution. This use is essential in French news: 'Le président aurait décidé...' (The president reportedly decided...). It's equivalent to English 'is said to have' or 'reportedly.'

items.question: À ta place, je me _____ excusé.
items.translation: (In your place, I would have apologized.)
items.explanation: 'À ta place' (in your place/if I were you) creates an implicit condition without 'si.' 'S'excuser' is reflexive → être auxiliary → conditionnel of être: 'je me serais excusé.' Reflexive conditionnel passé: subject + reflexive + être (conditionnel) + participle. Agreement: 'excusé' (masculine singular — agrees with je/speaker). 'À votre place' is the formal equivalent. Both are very common alternatives to explicit si-clauses.

items.question: Il _____ voulu devenir astronaute quand il était petit.
items.translation: (He would have liked to become an astronaut when he was little.)
items.explanation: 'Aurait voulu' = would have liked/wanted — expressing an unrealized childhood dream. The conditionnel passé conveys the gap between desire and reality. 'Devenir' + profession without article: 'devenir astronaute' (not 'un astronaute'). This article omission with professions after 'être' and 'devenir' is a distinctive French rule. The combination of conditionnel passé + 'quand il était petit' evokes nostalgic unrealized aspirations.

items.question: Si elle avait étudié davantage, elle _____ réussi son examen.
items.translation: (If she had studied more, she would have passed her exam.)
items.explanation: The standard 3rd conditional: Si + PQP → Conditionnel Passé. 'Si elle avait étudié' (if she had studied) → 'elle aurait réussi' (she would have passed). 'Davantage' is a formal synonym of 'plus' (more). 'Réussir son examen' = to pass one's exam (not 'passer' which means 'to take/sit' an exam). This false friend is crucial: 'réussir' = pass (succeed), 'passer' = take/sit, 'échouer' or 'rater' = fail.

items.question: J'_____ préféré rester à la maison.
items.translation: (I would have preferred to stay at home.)
items.explanation: 'J'aurais préféré' expresses a past preference that wasn't realized — 'I would have preferred (but I didn't get to).' The infinitive 'rester' follows directly. 'À la maison' = at home. This construction is very common for expressing retrospective preferences or mild regret: 'J'aurais préféré un café' (I would have preferred a coffee), 'On aurait préféré partir en juin' (We would have preferred to leave in June). It's polite and non-confrontational.

items.question: Les enfants _____ voulu aller au zoo.
items.translation: (The children would have liked to go to the zoo.)
items.explanation: 'Ils auraient voulu' — 3rd person plural conditionnel passé: 'auraient.' Conjugation reminder: j'aurais, tu aurais, il aurait, nous aurions, vous auriez, ILS AURAIENT. 'Vouloir' + infinitif: 'auraient voulu aller.' This expresses a wish that wasn't fulfilled — the zoo visit didn't happen. The conditionnel passé is the tense of unfulfilled desires, missed opportunities, and hypothetical past scenarios.

items.question: Vous _____ dû nous prévenir à l'avance.
items.translation: (You should have warned us in advance.)
items.explanation: 'Auriez dû' = you (formal/plural) should have — reproach or missed obligation. 'Prévenir' (to warn/inform) takes a direct object: prévenir quelqu'un. 'À l'avance' (in advance). The conditionnel passé of 'devoir' is the standard way to express 'should have': 'J'aurais dû' (I should have), 'tu aurais dû' (you should have), 'il aurait dû' (he should have). This construction is one of the most practical uses of conditionnel passé in everyday speech.

items.question: On _____ dit qu'il allait neiger, mais il a fait beau.
items.translation: (One would have said it was going to snow, but the weather was nice.)
items.explanation: 'On aurait dit' = one would have said / it looked like. This idiomatic expression describes a past appearance that turned out to be misleading. 'On aurait dit qu'il allait neiger' = it looked like it was going to snow. 'Mais il a fait beau' contradicts the expectation. 'On aurait dit' is extremely common in conversation: 'On aurait dit un fantôme!' (It looked like a ghost!). It's a past version of 'on dirait' (it looks like — présent conditionnel).

items.question: Si nous avions pris le train, nous _____ arrivés à l'heure.
items.translation: (If we had taken the train, we would have arrived on time.)
items.explanation: 'Arriver' takes être → conditionnel of être: 'nous serions arrivés.' Full: 'Si nous avions pris le train, nous serions arrivés à l'heure.' Classic 3rd conditional with être verb — and a real-life scenario (transportation choice → punctuality). Agreement: 'arrivés' (masculine plural or mixed group). 'À l'heure' (on time) is the opposite of 'en retard' (late). The conditional perfectly captures hindsight regret about a past choice.

description: Express regrets, unrealized hypotheses, and past suppositions with the past conditional

name: Emploi du Subjonctif (Subjunctive Usage)

items.question: Il faut que tu _____ tes devoirs avant de sortir.
items.translation: (You must do your homework before going out.)
items.explanation: 'Il faut que' (it is necessary that / must) is the quintessential subjunctive trigger. After 'il faut que,' the subjunctive is ALWAYS required — no exception. 'Faire' in subjunctive: que je fasse, que tu fasses, qu'il fasse, que nous fassions, que vous fassiez, qu'ils fassent. 'Il faut que tu fasses' = you must do. The subjunctive conveys necessity, obligation through an impersonal construction. This is the first subjunctive trigger every learner should master.

items.question: Je veux que vous _____ à l'heure.
items.translation: (I want you to be on time.)
items.explanation: Verbs of WILL/DESIRE trigger subjunctive: vouloir que, souhaiter que, désirer que, exiger que, demander que, ordonner que. 'Être' in subjunctive: que je sois, que tu sois, qu'il soit, que nous soyons, que vous soyez, qu'ils soient. 'Je veux que vous soyez' = I want you to be. Critical: the subjects must be DIFFERENT. If same subject, use infinitive: 'Je veux être à l'heure' (I want to be on time). Two subjects = que + subjunctive.

items.question: Bien que ce soit difficile, elle _____ de travailler dur.
items.translation: (Although it's difficult, she continues to work hard.)
items.explanation: 'Bien que' (although/even though) triggers subjunctive in the subordinate clause: 'bien que ce soit difficile' (although it IS difficult — 'soit' is subjunctive of 'être'). The MAIN clause ('elle continue') uses the indicative — normal mood. The subjunctive is only in the clause after the trigger conjunction. Other concessive conjunctions requiring subjunctive: quoique, malgré que, pour que, afin que, avant que, à moins que, sans que, jusqu'à ce que.

items.question: Je doute qu'il _____ la vérité.
items.translation: (I doubt that he knows the truth.)
items.explanation: Verbs of DOUBT trigger subjunctive: douter que, ne pas être sûr que, ne pas croire que, ne pas penser que. 'Savoir' in subjunctive: que je sache, que tu saches, qu'il sache, que nous sachions, que vous sachiez, qu'ils sachent. Contrast: 'Je pense qu'il sait' (indicative — certainty) vs. 'Je ne pense pas qu'il sache' (subjunctive — doubt). Negating belief/thinking verbs shifts them from certainty to doubt, triggering subjunctive.

items.question: Je suis content que tu _____ là.
items.translation: (I'm glad you're here.)
items.explanation: Expressions of EMOTION trigger subjunctive: être content que, être triste que, être surpris que, être désolé que, être heureux que, avoir peur que, regretter que, craindre que. 'Que tu sois' (that you are — subjunctive). The emotion creates subjectivity — the fact is filtered through a personal feeling, which French grammar encodes with the subjunctive. Compare: 'Je sais que tu es là' (indicative — I know the fact) vs. 'Je suis content que tu sois là' (subjunctive — I feel about it).

items.question: Pour que les enfants _____ bien, il faut de la patience.
items.translation: (In order for children to learn well, patience is needed.)
items.explanation: 'Pour que' (in order that/so that) requires subjunctive. 'Apprendre' in subjunctive: que j'apprenne, que tu apprennes, qu'il apprenne, que nous apprenions, que vous appreniez, qu'ils apprennent. When subjects are different, 'pour que + subjunctive'; when the same, 'pour + infinitive': 'J'étudie pour apprendre' (I study to learn — same subject). Purpose conjunctions with subjunctive: pour que, afin que, de sorte que, de façon que, de manière que.

items.question: Avant que tu ne _____ , j'ai quelque chose à te dire.
items.translation: (Before you leave, I have something to tell you.)
items.explanation: 'Avant que' (before) triggers subjunctive. The 'ne' after 'avant que' is the 'ne explétif' — it's grammatically optional and doesn't make the sentence negative. 'Partir' in subjunctive: que je parte, que tu partes, qu'il parte, que nous partions, que vous partiez, qu'ils partent. Note: 'après que' officially takes indicative (not subjunctive), though many native speakers now use subjunctive after it too. 'Avant que' = always subjunctive.

items.question: Il est possible qu'il _____ en retard.
items.translation: (It's possible that he is/will be late.)
items.explanation: Impersonal expressions of POSSIBILITY or UNCERTAINTY trigger subjunctive: il est possible que, il se peut que, il est peu probable que, il est nécessaire que, il est important que, il est rare que, il vaut mieux que. 'Il est possible qu'il soit' — 'soit' is subjunctive of 'être.' Contrast: 'il est certain qu'il est en retard' (indicative — certainty). The mood shifts with the degree of certainty expressed. Possibility = subjunctive; certainty = indicative.

items.question: Je cherche quelqu'un qui _____ parler chinois.
items.translation: (I'm looking for someone who can speak Chinese.)
items.explanation: Subjunctive in relative clauses expressing something SOUGHT but NOT YET FOUND. 'Je cherche quelqu'un qui puisse...' — the person may not exist. Compare: 'Je connais quelqu'un qui peut parler chinois' (indicative — the person exists). The subjunctive signals uncertainty about existence. After superlatives, after seul/unique/premier/dernier, and after negative antecedents, subjunctive is also used in relative clauses: 'C'est le plus beau film que j'aie vu.'

items.question: À moins qu'il _____ un miracle, nous ne gagnerons pas.
items.translation: (Unless there's a miracle, we won't win.)
items.explanation: 'À moins que' (unless) triggers subjunctive, and typically takes the 'ne explétif': 'à moins qu'il n'y ait.' 'Avoir' in subjunctive: que j'aie, que tu aies, qu'il ait, que nous ayons, que vous ayez, qu'ils aient. 'Il y a' → subjunctive 'il y ait.' The 'ne explétif' after 'à moins que' is very common in formal writing. Other conjunctions with optional ne explétif: avant que, de peur que, de crainte que. It adds elegance without adding negation.

items.question: C'est la plus belle ville que j'_____ jamais visitée.
items.translation: (It's the most beautiful city I've ever visited.)
items.explanation: After SUPERLATIVES (le plus, le moins, le meilleur, le pire), French uses subjunctive in the relative clause. 'La plus belle ville que j'aie visitée' — 'aie' is the subjunctive of 'avoir.' The subjunctive here expresses the speaker's subjective evaluation. Compare: 'le film que j'ai vu' (indicative — neutral fact) vs. 'le meilleur film que j'aie vu' (subjunctive — personal judgment). The participle 'visitée' agrees with 'la ville' (feminine, preceding COD).

items.question: Je ne pense pas qu'elle _____ raison.
items.translation: (I don't think she's right.)
items.explanation: Negative opinion verbs trigger subjunctive: 'ne pas penser que,' 'ne pas croire que,' 'ne pas trouver que.' In the negative, these express doubt rather than certainty. 'Avoir raison' (to be right) → 'qu'elle ait raison.' Contrast: 'Je pense qu'elle a raison' (affirmative = indicative = I believe she's right). The negative form shifts from belief to doubt, and doubt requires subjunctive. This positive/negative mood alternation is a signature feature of French grammar.

items.question: Nous attendrons jusqu'à ce qu'il _____ beau.
items.translation: (We'll wait until the weather is nice.)
items.explanation: 'Jusqu'à ce que' (until) always triggers subjunctive. 'Faire beau' (be nice weather) → 'qu'il fasse beau.' The subjunctive after 'jusqu'à ce que' makes sense: the nice weather hasn't happened yet — it's anticipated, not certain. This is consistent with the subjunctive's core role of marking non-realized or uncertain events. 'Faire' is highly irregular in subjunctive: fasse, fasses, fasse, fassions, fassiez, fassent. Memorize it as a high-frequency verb.

items.question: Quoi que tu _____ , je te soutiendrai.
items.translation: (Whatever you do, I will support you.)
items.explanation: 'Quoi que' (whatever — two words!) triggers subjunctive. Don't confuse with 'quoique' (one word) = although. 'Quoi que tu fasses' = whatever you do. Other indefinite subjunctive expressions: 'qui que tu sois' (whoever you are), 'où que tu ailles' (wherever you go), 'quel que soit' (whatever may be). These expressions of indefiniteness naturally pair with the subjunctive because they refer to unknown or unspecified entities. They are hallmarks of elegant French.

items.question: Il est temps que nous _____ une décision.
items.translation: (It's time for us to make a decision.)
items.explanation: 'Il est temps que' (it's time that) triggers subjunctive. 'Prendre' in subjunctive: que je prenne, que tu prennes, qu'il prenne, que nous prenions, que vous preniez, qu'ils prennent. The nous/vous forms use the imparfait stem: 'prenions.' This is true for many irregular subjunctive verbs — the nous/vous forms resemble the imparfait. 'Il est temps que' implies urgency about something that hasn't happened yet — perfect for the subjunctive mood.

description: Identify subjunctive triggers and practice choosing between indicative and subjunctive moods

name: Voix Passive (Passive Voice)

items.question: La lettre _____ par Marie.
items.translation: (The letter is written by Marie.)
items.explanation: The French passive is built exactly like English: subject + être (to be) + past participle. 'La lettre est écrite par Marie' mirrors 'The letter is written by Marie.' The key difference is that in French, the past participle must AGREE with the subject in gender and number — 'lettre' is feminine singular, so 'écrit' becomes 'écrite.' This agreement rule is the single most important thing to master in French passive constructions and catches many learners off guard.

items.question: Le gâteau _____ par le chef hier.
items.translation: (The cake was made by the chef yesterday.)
items.explanation: When the passive is in the past (passé composé), French uses 'a été' + past participle. Think of it as stacking two layers: 'a été' (has been) + 'fait' (made). The auxiliary 'être' itself gets conjugated in the passé composé with 'avoir' — giving 'a été.' Since 'gâteau' is masculine singular, 'fait' stays unchanged. This double-auxiliary structure (avoir + été + participle) may feel heavy, but it precisely mirrors the English 'was made.'

items.question: Les portes _____ à 8 heures.
items.translation: (The doors will be opened at 8 o'clock.)
items.explanation: For future passive, conjugate 'être' in the future tense: 'seront' (will be) + past participle. 'Portes' is feminine plural, so 'ouvert' must agree → 'ouvertes' (adding -e for feminine, -s for plural). Notice how French handles passive agreement more strictly than English — you always adjust the participle ending. This is a consistent pattern: change the tense of 'être,' keep the participle agreeing with the subject.

items.question: Ce film _____ par des millions de personnes.
items.translation: (This film is watched by millions of people.)
items.explanation: Present passive: 'est' (is) + 'regardé' (watched). 'Film' is masculine singular, so no extra agreement marker on the participle. Notice 'par' introduces the agent (who does the action), similar to English 'by.' In French, you'll use 'par' for most passive agents. Some fixed expressions use 'de' instead (e.g., 'aimé de tous'), but 'par' is the standard. This simple structure — être + participle + par + agent — covers most passive sentences you'll encounter.

items.question: La maison _____ en 1920.
items.translation: (The house was built in 1920.)
items.explanation: Past passive with a feminine subject: 'a été construite.' The participle 'construit' adds -e because 'maison' is feminine. When stating historical facts with a specific date, French typically uses passé composé passive ('a été construite') rather than imparfait. This mirrors English perfectly: 'was built' (completed action). Note that omitting the agent ('par quelqu'un') is very common in passive sentences when the doer is unknown or unimportant.

items.question: Les résultats _____ demain matin.
items.translation: (The results will be announced tomorrow morning.)
items.explanation: Future passive with masculine plural: 'seront annoncés.' The verb 'annoncer' → past participle 'annoncé' + s for plural agreement with 'résultats.' French passive in the future works identically to English: 'will be announced.' The time marker 'demain' (tomorrow) confirms future tense. In everyday French, speakers often avoid passive by using 'on' (one/they): 'On annoncera les résultats demain' — both forms are correct, but passive is more formal.

items.question: Cette chanson _____ dans le monde entier.
items.translation: (This song is known throughout the world.)
items.explanation: Present passive describing a general state: 'est connue.' The verb 'connaître' has an irregular past participle 'connu,' and it gains -e for feminine agreement with 'chanson.' Some passive constructions in French describe ongoing states rather than actions — just like English 'is known.' These stative passives are very common with verbs like 'connaître' (know), 'respecter' (respect), 'aimer' (love). They describe how something IS rather than what happens to it.

items.question: Les livres _____ avant la fin du mois.
items.translation: (The books had been delivered before the end of the month.)
items.explanation: Pluperfect passive: 'avaient été livrés.' This is the most complex passive form — three layers: 'avaient' (had) + 'été' (been) + 'livrés' (delivered). Use this when the passive action happened BEFORE another past event. 'Livres' is masculine plural, so 'livré' gets -s. While this triple-auxiliary feels cumbersome, it perfectly mirrors English 'had been delivered.' Practice building it layer by layer: past participle → add 'été' → conjugate 'avoir' in imparfait.

items.question: La décision _____ par le directeur demain.
items.translation: (The decision will be made by the director tomorrow.)
items.explanation: Future passive with the irregular verb 'prendre' → past participle 'pris.' Since 'décision' is feminine, it becomes 'prise.' The verb 'prendre' (to take) is used idiomatically: 'prendre une décision' = to make a decision. In passive, the decision becomes the subject: 'la décision sera prise.' Note how French uses 'prendre' where English uses 'make' — these verb choice differences between active expressions carry into passive constructions and are worth memorizing.

items.question: Les enfants _____ par leurs grands-parents ce week-end.
items.translation: (The children are being looked after by their grandparents this weekend.)
items.explanation: Present passive: 'sont gardés.' 'Garder' means to look after/watch over. 'Enfants' is masculine plural → 'gardés' with -s. French present passive can express both general truths AND ongoing actions (like English present continuous passive 'are being looked after'). Context and time markers like 'ce week-end' clarify the meaning. The agent 'par leurs grands-parents' follows the same pattern — 'par' + doer. This dual function of present passive is a key difference from English.

items.question: Cette loi _____ en 2005.
items.translation: (This law was adopted in 2005.)
items.explanation: Past passive with feminine subject: 'a été adoptée.' The past participle 'adopté' adds -e for 'loi' (feminine). Note that legal, political, and journalistic French heavily uses passive voice — laws are 'adopted,' decisions are 'taken,' measures are 'announced.' This formal register relies on passive more than conversational French. When you see a specific year, passé composé passive is the standard choice — it marks a completed historical event, just like English 'was adopted.'

items.question: Le match _____ à cause de la pluie.
items.translation: (The match was cancelled because of the rain.)
items.explanation: Past passive: 'a été annulé.' 'Match' is masculine → no extra agreement on 'annulé.' The phrase 'à cause de' (because of) introduces the reason/cause, functioning like 'par' but for non-agent causes. Important distinction: use 'par' for a deliberate agent (a person doing something) and 'à cause de' for reasons/causes (weather, circumstances). This is more nuanced than English, where 'because of' and 'by' serve different roles but French makes the distinction explicitly.

items.question: Toutes les places _____.
items.translation: (All the seats have been reserved.)
items.explanation: Past passive with feminine plural: 'ont été réservées.' 'Places' (seats) is feminine plural → 'réservé' gains -e-s → 'réservées.' The auxiliary 'avoir' is plural too: 'ont' (because the hidden subject of 'être' is compound). When no agent is mentioned, the passive emphasizes the result: ALL seats are now taken. This agentless passive is extremely common in French — signs, announcements, descriptions of states. It focuses on what happened, not who did it.

items.question: Le projet _____ si nous avions eu plus de temps.
items.translation: (The project would have been finished if we had had more time.)
items.explanation: Conditional past passive: 'aurait été terminé' — the most complex passive tense. Four elements: 'aurait' (would have) + 'été' (been) + 'terminé' (finished). This appears in hypothetical/counterfactual sentences with 'si' (if). The 'si' clause uses plus-que-parfait ('si nous avions eu'), and the main clause uses conditionnel passé passive. While this feels very advanced, it follows the same logic: conjugate 'avoir' in conditional + 'été' + agreeing participle. Master this and you can handle any passive form.

items.question: Ces photos _____ par un photographe professionnel.
items.translation: (These photos were taken by a professional photographer.)
items.explanation: Past passive with an irregular participle: 'prendre' → 'pris,' feminine plural → 'prises.' This ticks every box of passive mastery: irregular past participle + full gender/number agreement + agent with 'par.' The verb 'prendre' in passive is especially common with photos (prendre une photo = take a photo). Notice how the entire passive structure 'ont été prises' contains three words before the main participle — each carrying grammatical information. Building passive sentences with irregular verbs is the ultimate test of your French grammar skills.

description: Transform active sentences to passive voice and understand agent expression with par and de

name: Gérondif (Gerund)

items.question: Il écoute de la musique _____ ses devoirs.
items.translation: (He listens to music while doing his homework.)
items.explanation: The French gérondif is formed with 'en' + present participle (-ant form). It expresses simultaneous actions: 'while doing.' To form it: take the 'nous' form of present tense (faisons), drop -ons → fais-, add -ant → faisant, then put 'en' before it → 'en faisant.' Unlike English, French ALWAYS requires 'en' — you cannot just say 'faisant' alone for 'while doing.' The gérondif is one of the most useful constructions for describing two things happening at the same time.

items.question: Elle s'est blessée _____ du ski.
items.translation: (She hurt herself while skiing.)
items.explanation: The gérondif can indicate WHEN or HOW something happened — here, 'while skiing' tells us when she got hurt. The construction 'faire du ski' becomes 'en faisant du ski' in the gérondif. Notice the reflexive 's'est blessée' agrees with 'elle' (feminine). The gérondif always refers to the SAME subject as the main verb — she was skiing AND she hurt herself. This same-subject rule is critical: you cannot use the gérondif if two different people perform the two actions.

items.question: _____ à gauche, vous trouverez la pharmacie.
items.translation: (By turning left, you will find the pharmacy.)
items.explanation: Here the gérondif expresses MEANS or METHOD: 'by turning left.' This is another key function — not just 'while' but 'by doing X, you achieve Y.' The gérondif at the beginning of a sentence often has this instrumental meaning. 'Tourner' follows the regular pattern: nous tournons → tourn- + ant → tournant → en tournant. This 'by doing' usage is extremely practical for giving instructions, directions, or advice. It's more concise than saying 'si vous tournez à gauche.'

items.question: Il a appris le français _____ des films.
items.translation: (He learned French by watching films.)
items.explanation: Another 'by doing' usage: 'en regardant' = by watching. The gérondif here explains the METHOD of learning. 'Regarder' is regular: nous regardons → regard- → regardant → en regardant. This construction is perfect for describing learning strategies, achievements, or how results were obtained. Compare with 'pour regarder' (in order to watch) — that expresses PURPOSE, not method. The gérondif answers HOW, while 'pour + infinitive' answers WHY. This distinction is essential for clear French communication.

items.question: Il est parti _____ la porte.
items.translation: (He left, slamming the door.)
items.explanation: The gérondif can express MANNER — how someone did something. 'En claquant la porte' describes how he left: by slamming the door. This adds vivid detail to the main action. 'Claquer' → nous claquons → claqu- + ant → claquant → en claquant. The simultaneity aspect is important here: the door-slamming and the leaving happen at the same moment. French uses the gérondif for these dramatic, descriptive additions far more naturally than a subordinate clause like 'pendant qu'il claquait la porte.'

items.question: _____ bien, vous réussirez l'examen.
items.translation: (By studying well, you will succeed in the exam.)
