items.question: Elle a _____ compris la leçon.
items.translation: (She understood the lesson well.)
items.explanation: In French, adverbs usually come right after the conjugated verb in compound tenses. So in passé composé, 'bien' sits between 'a' and 'compris' — not at the end of the sentence like in English. This is one of the trickiest habits for English speakers to break. Think of it as the adverb 'hugging' the auxiliary verb. This rule applies to most short, common adverbs like bien, mal, déjà, encore, souvent, and toujours.

items.question: Nous avons _____ mangé.
items.translation: (We already ate.)
items.explanation: The adverb 'déjà' (already) follows the auxiliary 'avons' and precedes the past participle 'mangé' in compound tenses. This placement is consistent across all compound tenses in French. Note the accent grave on the 'a' — 'déjà' not 'déja.' English places 'already' more flexibly (already ate, ate already), but French is strict: auxiliary + adverb + past participle. This pattern becomes second nature with practice.

items.question: Il parle _____ le français.
items.translation: (He speaks French fluently.)
items.explanation: With simple tenses, longer adverbs like 'couramment' (fluently) typically follow the verb directly. Notice French forms adverbs from adjectives: courant → couramment (double 'm' for -amment/-emment patterns). English speakers might want to put the adverb at the end, but in French it sits between the verb and the object. Adverbs ending in -ment are the French equivalent of English -ly adverbs, and their placement mirrors closeness to the verb.

items.question: Tu as _____ fini tes devoirs ?
items.translation: (Have you already finished your homework?)
items.explanation: In questions using compound tenses, the adverb still inserts between the auxiliary and past participle. 'Tu as déjà fini' follows the same auxiliary-adverb-participle order whether it's a statement or question. French word order for adverbs is remarkably consistent compared to English, where 'already' can float around: 'Have you already finished?' vs. 'Have you finished already?' French locks the adverb firmly in one position.

items.question: Marie chante _____.
items.translation: (Marie sings beautifully.)
items.explanation: Longer -ment adverbs derived from adjectives typically come after the verb in simple tenses. 'Magnifiquement' comes from the feminine adjective 'magnifique' + -ment. Unlike shorter adverbs (bien, mal), these longer ones don't squeeze between auxiliary and participle in compound tenses — they go after the participle. The formation rule: feminine adjective + -ment. Since 'magnifique' is already the same in both genders, you just add -ment directly.

items.question: Je ne comprends pas _____.
items.translation: (I don't understand at all.)
items.explanation: The adverbial expression 'du tout' (at all) intensifies a negation and always comes at the end of the sentence, after 'pas.' This is different from most short adverbs that nestle close to the verb. Think of 'du tout' as an intensifier that reinforces the negative — 'pas du tout' is a stronger 'not at all.' In spoken French, you'll hear this constantly: 'pas du tout!' The preposition 'du' (de + le) is fixed in this expression.

items.question: Ils ont _____ voyagé en Asie.
items.translation: (They have often traveled to Asia.)
items.explanation: Frequency adverbs like 'souvent' (often) follow the same compound-tense rule: they insert between auxiliary and past participle. 'Ils ont souvent voyagé' mirrors the pattern of 'ils ont bien compris.' English allows 'They have often traveled' or 'They often traveled' with different placements, but French is consistent. Common frequency adverbs: toujours (always), souvent (often), rarement (rarely), parfois (sometimes). Watch the spelling — no 'e' in souvent.

items.question: Elle court _____.
items.translation: (She runs fast.)
items.explanation: 'Vite' is one of French's essential irregular adverbs — it doesn't end in -ment. While 'rapidement' (rapidly) exists and is grammatically correct, 'vite' is the natural, everyday choice. Unlike 'rapidement' which follows the adjective-to-adverb -ment rule, 'vite' is inherently an adverb and never changes form. It goes directly after the verb in simple tenses. Don't confuse it with 'rapide' (the adjective). In compound tenses: 'Elle a vite couru' — short adverbs go between auxiliary and participle.

items.question: Nous partons _____ à midi.
items.translation: (We are leaving precisely at noon.)
items.explanation: Adverbs of precision like 'précisément' can modify the whole sentence and appear in various positions, but commonly before the time expression they qualify. Formation: précise (feminine adjective) + -ment = précisément. Note the accent on the 'e' — this is crucial for correct spelling. English speakers might place 'precisely' more freely, but in French it often precedes what it modifies. These sentence-level adverbs have more flexibility than verb-modifying adverbs.

items.question: Il a _____ répondu à ma question.
items.translation: (He barely answered my question.)
items.explanation: The adverbial expression 'à peine' (barely/hardly) follows the compound-tense placement rule — it sits between the auxiliary and past participle. Multi-word adverbs like 'à peine' behave just like single-word adverbs in terms of position. This expression conveys a minimal degree — the action was almost not done. Compare with English: 'He barely answered' vs. 'Il a à peine répondu.' French locks it between the two verb parts, while English places it before the main verb.

items.question: _____, il a accepté l'offre.
items.translation: (Finally, he accepted the offer.)
items.explanation: Sentence-initial adverbs like 'Finalement' (finally/eventually) are followed by a comma and set the tone for the entire sentence. These transitional adverbs modify the whole clause, not just the verb. Formation: finale (f. adjective) + -ment. Note: only one 'l' in 'finalement.' English and French align here — both can start sentences with such adverbs. Other examples: Malheureusement (unfortunately), Heureusement (fortunately), Évidemment (obviously). This position gives the adverb emphatic, discourse-level meaning.

items.question: Tu travailles _____.
items.translation: (You work too much.)
items.explanation: 'Trop' (too much) is an adverb of quantity that follows the verb directly in simple tenses. Don't confuse 'trop' with 'très' (very) — 'trop' implies excess while 'très' is just intensification. You can't say 'tu travailles très' alone — 'très' needs an adjective or another adverb. But 'trop' works alone after a verb. In compound tenses: 'Tu as trop travaillé' — between auxiliary and participle. These quantity adverbs are foundational: trop, beaucoup, assez, peu, un peu.

items.question: Elle est _____ gentille.
items.translation: (She is very kind.)
items.explanation: 'Très' (very) modifies adjectives and other adverbs — never verbs alone. 'Elle est très gentille' works perfectly, but 'elle travaille très' is incomplete (you'd need 'elle travaille très bien'). This is a key distinction from 'beaucoup' which modifies verbs: 'elle travaille beaucoup.' English uses 'very' similarly (very kind, but not *very works). Mastering très vs. beaucoup vs. trop prevents a common error English speakers make when translating 'a lot' or 'very.'

items.question: Nous n'avons _____ vu ce film.
items.translation: (We have never seen this movie.)
items.explanation: Negative adverbs like 'jamais' (never) replace 'pas' in the negation structure: ne...jamais. In compound tenses, 'jamais' goes between auxiliary and participle, just like 'pas' would: 'n'avons jamais vu.' You cannot combine 'pas' and 'jamais' — it's either ne...pas or ne...jamais. Other negative adverbs: ne...plus (no longer), ne...rien (nothing), ne...guère (hardly). These all follow the same position pattern, sandwiching between the auxiliary and past participle.

items.question: Il travaille _____ que son frère.
items.translation: (He works more than his brother.)
items.explanation: In comparisons, 'plus...que' (more...than) frames the comparison. When 'plus' modifies a verb, it follows the verb and 'que' introduces the comparison: 'travaille plus que.' Don't confuse this with 'plus de' (more of + noun) or 'ne...plus' (no longer). The word 'plus' has two pronunciations: the 's' is silent in 'ne...plus' (no more) but pronounced in 'plus que' (more than). Context determines meaning — comparison vs. negation. This adverbial comparison structure is essential for expressing degrees.

description: Learn where to position adverbs in relation to verbs in simple and compound tenses

name: Adverbes de Manière

items.question: Il parle _____ en public.
items.translation: (He speaks calmly in public.)
items.explanation: French forms manner adverbs by adding -ment to the feminine adjective: calme (already same form) + -ment = calmement. This mirrors English's adjective + -ly pattern (calm → calmly). The adverb follows the verb directly in simple tenses. Manner adverbs answer 'how?' — comment parle-t-il ? Calmement. Note: some adjectives with identical masculine/feminine forms (like calme, rapide, simple) just add -ment directly. The regular pattern is: masculine → feminine → add -ment.

items.question: Elle chante _____.
items.translation: (She sings softly.)
items.explanation: From doux (m.) → douce (f.) + -ment = doucement. The feminine form 'douce' provides the base. This is the standard -ment adverb formation: always start from the feminine adjective. 'Doucement' means softly, gently, or slowly depending on context. In everyday speech, 'doucement!' alone means 'easy!' or 'take it easy!' Note: no cedilla in doucement — the 'c' before 'e' is naturally soft. French manner adverbs are incredibly productive; almost any quality adjective can become a -ment adverb.

items.question: Ils travaillent _____.
items.translation: (They work seriously.)
items.explanation: From sérieux (m.) → sérieuse (f.) + -ment = sérieusement. Notice the base is always the feminine: sérieuse + ment. The 'euse' ending creates the distinctive 'eusement' adverb ending, common across many French adverbs. Compare: joyeux → joyeuse → joyeusement, heureux → heureuse → heureusement. This -eusement pattern appears in dozens of common adverbs and becomes recognizable quickly. English doesn't have this consistent two-step process.

items.question: Le chat marche _____.
items.translation: (The cat walks silently.)
items.explanation: Following the pattern: silencieux → silencieuse → silencieusement. This is a longer adverb but perfectly regular. Note there's no 't' — unlike English 'silently,' French derives from its own adjective family. French manner adverbs can get quite long (silencieusement = 6 syllables), yet they're standard in both written and spoken French. In compound tenses, such long adverbs typically follow the past participle: 'Le chat a marché silencieusement' rather than inserting between auxiliary and participle.

items.question: Elle a répondu _____.
items.translation: (She answered politely.)
items.explanation: Here's an irregular formation: poli(e) drops the 'e' before adding -ment → poliment (not *poliement). This happens with adjectives ending in a vowel: vrai → vraiment, absolu → absolument, joli → joliment. When the feminine adjective ends in a vowel sound, French avoids the hiatus by dropping the feminine -e. This is an efficiency rule — French avoids consecutive vowel sounds where possible. Compare: douce → doucement (consonant ending, keeps -e-) vs. jolie → joliment (drops -e).

items.question: Il conduit _____.
items.translation: (He drives dangerously.)
items.explanation: Standard -eusement pattern: dangereux → dangereuse → dangereusement. This adverb describes manner of driving — how does he drive? Dangereusement. French traffic vocabulary often uses manner adverbs: prudemment (carefully), lentement (slowly), rapidement (quickly). Each follows its own formation rule. The -eusement ending is rhythmic and recognizable in French speech. Unlike English where 'dangerously' is one simple derivation, French goes through the feminine adjective intermediary step.

items.question: Nous avançons _____ dans le projet.
items.translation: (We are progressing slowly in the project.)
items.explanation: From lent → lente (f.) + -ment = lentement. The insertion of the feminine 'e' is crucial — without it (*lentment), the pronunciation would be impossible. The feminine form provides the necessary vowel before -ment. 'Lentement' is the opposite of 'rapidement' (quickly) or 'vite' (fast). Note that 'vite' is a pure adverb (no adjective form *vit), while 'lentement' is derived from an adjective. This distinction matters: you can say 'un train lent' but not '*un train vite' (you'd say 'rapide').

items.question: Le professeur explique _____.
items.translation: (The teacher explains clearly.)
items.explanation: From clair → claire (f.) + -ment = clairement. A perfectly regular formation. Note however that 'clair' can sometimes be used as an adverb on its own in set expressions: 'parler clair' (to speak plainly), 'voir clair' (to see clearly). These are informal and fixed — you can't freely substitute adjectives for adverbs in French. For formal or standard usage, 'clairement' is always correct. Compare English: 'speak clearly' (adverb) vs. 'speak clear' (informal adjective as adverb).

items.question: Tu dois agir _____.
items.translation: (You must act quickly.)
items.explanation: From rapide (same m./f.) + -ment = rapidement. When the adjective has identical masculine and feminine forms (ending in -e), you simply add -ment directly. No need to change anything. Other examples: simple → simplement, triste → tristement, facile → facilement. This is the easiest -ment formation pattern. Remember: 'vite' and 'rapidement' are near-synonyms, but 'rapidement' is slightly more formal. In everyday speech, French speakers often prefer 'vite' for its brevity.

items.question: Il a _____ terminé son travail.
items.translation: (He easily finished his work.)
items.explanation: From facile (same m./f.) + -ment = facilement. In compound tenses, 'facilement' typically follows the past participle because it's a longer adverb: 'a facilement terminé.' However, both positions are acceptable for medium-length adverbs. This flexibility contrasts with short adverbs (bien, mal, déjà) that must go between auxiliary and participle. The choice often depends on emphasis — between the verbs for neutrality, after the participle for emphasis on the adverb itself.

items.question: Elle s'habille _____.
items.translation: (She dresses elegantly.)
items.explanation: Special rule: adjectives ending in -ant form adverbs with -amment (not -antement). Élégant → élégamment. This doubles the 'm' and drops the 't.' Similarly: brillant → brillamment, courant → couramment, suffisant → suffisamment. This -amment pattern is one of French's key irregular adverb formations. The pronunciation is distinct — the double 'm' creates the nasal 'am' sound. Don't write *élégantement — that's a common error. Remember: -ant adjectives → -amment adverbs.

items.question: Il a réagi _____.
items.translation: (He reacted violently.)
items.explanation: Adjectives ending in -ent form adverbs with -emment (not -entement). Violent → violemment. Despite looking like it has double 'm,' the pronunciation sounds like 'amment.' Similarly: récent → récemment, patient → patiemment, évident → évidemment. This is the companion rule to -ant → -amment. Together they represent the two main irregular adverb patterns. Crucially, -emment and -amment sound identical — the spelling difference reflects the adjective's ending (-ent vs. -ant).

items.question: Le bébé dort _____.
items.translation: (The baby sleeps peacefully.)
items.explanation: From paisible (same m./f.) + -ment = paisiblement. Adjectives already ending in -e (same masculine/feminine) simply add -ment. This regular pattern covers hundreds of adverbs: agréable → agréablement, terrible → terriblement, horrible → horriblement. The result is often a four-syllable adverb, typical of French. Context note: 'paisiblement' evokes peaceful serenity — 'le bébé dort paisiblement' paints a tranquil image. French manner adverbs often carry richer emotional nuance than their English counterparts.

items.question: Elle a _____ refusé l'invitation.
items.translation: (She firmly refused the invitation.)
items.explanation: From ferme (same m./f.) + -ment = fermement. Another regular -e adjective → -ment adverb. Note that 'ferme' can also function as an adverb in fixed expressions: 'tenir ferme' (to hold firm), 'frapper ferme' (to hit hard). But 'fermement' is the standard adverbial form for general use. This dual usage (adjective-as-adverb vs. proper -ment adverb) exists for several French words, adding nuance and richness to expression.

items.question: Les enfants jouent _____.
items.translation: (The children play joyfully.)
items.explanation: From joyeux → joyeuse (f.) + -ment = joyeusement. The -eusement pattern again: the feminine -euse provides the bridge. Note the root stays 'joy' throughout — joyeux, joyeuse, joyeusement. Don't write *joieusement (keeping the noun 'joie' form). The adjective stem is what matters. This pattern is one of French's most productive: curieux → curieusement, nerveux → nerveusement, furieux → furieusement. Each conveys a vivid manner description, making French prose particularly expressive.

description: Form and use adverbs of manner ending in -ment and their irregular counterparts

name: Adverbes de Fréquence

items.question: Je vais _____ au cinéma.
items.translation: (I always go to the movies.)
items.explanation: 'Toujours' (always) is one of French's most common frequency adverbs. It follows the conjugated verb in simple tenses: 'je vais toujours.' In compound tenses, it inserts between auxiliary and participle: 'je suis toujours allé.' Note 'toujours' has a secondary meaning: 'still' — 'il est toujours là' can mean 'he's still there.' Context determines which meaning applies. The 's' at the end is always silent. This dual meaning (always/still) is a distinctly French feature with no exact English parallel.

items.question: Elle ne mange _____ de viande.
items.translation: (She never eats meat.)
items.explanation: 'Jamais' (never/ever) works within the ne...jamais negation structure. The 'ne' comes before the verb, 'jamais' after: 'ne mange jamais.' In spoken French, the 'ne' often drops: 'elle mange jamais de viande.' Note 'jamais' can also mean 'ever' in questions or hypotheticals without 'ne': 'As-tu jamais visité Paris ?' (Have you ever visited Paris?). The negative 'de' replaces the partitive article after negation — 'de viande' not 'de la viande.' Two grammar rules in one sentence!

items.question: Nous _____ le week-end à la campagne.
items.translation: (We sometimes spend the weekend in the countryside.)
items.explanation: 'Parfois' (sometimes) is a frequency adverb that typically follows the conjugated verb. 'Nous passons parfois' = we sometimes spend. While 'parfois' can occasionally appear at the start of a sentence for emphasis ('Parfois, nous allons...'), its default position is after the verb. Synonyms include 'quelquefois' (sometimes) and 'de temps en temps' (from time to time). French has a rich set of frequency expressions ranging from 'toujours' (100%) down to 'jamais' (0%), with parfois/quelquefois around 30-40%.

items.question: Il va _____ chez le médecin.
items.translation: (He rarely goes to the doctor.)
items.explanation: 'Rarement' (rarely) follows the standard -ment formation: rare + -ment = rarement. As a frequency adverb, it tells us how often — very infrequently. Position follows the standard rule: after the conjugated verb. 'Il va rarement' = he rarely goes. In compound tenses: 'Il est rarement allé.' Note 'chez le médecin' — French uses 'chez' (at the place of) for professional visits, not 'au médecin.' Frequency adverbs combine seamlessly with all tenses and don't change form — they're completely invariable.

items.question: Tu bois _____ du café le matin.
items.translation: (You often drink coffee in the morning.)
items.explanation: 'Souvent' (often) is an irregular adverb — it doesn't follow any -ment pattern because it's not derived from an adjective. It's a standalone adverb that must be memorized. Placed after the conjugated verb: 'tu bois souvent.' Watch the spelling: s-o-u-v-e-n-t (not *souvant or *souvement). In compound tenses: 'Tu as souvent bu du café.' Other irregular frequency adverbs: toujours, jamais, parfois are all standalone forms, not derived from adjectives with -ment.

items.question: Elles font _____ du sport.
items.translation: (They regularly do sports.)
items.explanation: From régulier → régulière (f.) + -ment = régulièrement. The accent changes: -ier becomes -ière in the feminine, then -ièrement. This accent shift (è not é) is crucial for correct pronunciation and spelling. 'Faire du sport' is the standard expression for doing/playing sports in French — not 'jouer du sport.' The partitive 'du' is used because sport is an uncountable activity here. Régulièrement implies a consistent schedule, more structured than 'souvent' (often).

items.question: Il se lève _____ à 6 heures.
items.translation: (He usually gets up at 6 o'clock.)
items.explanation: 'Généralement' (generally/usually) indicates typical frequency. It can appear after the verb or at sentence start for emphasis. Formation: général → générale (f.) + -ment = généralement. One 'l' in -lement, not two. Synonym 'd'habitude' (usually) is equally common and more colloquial. Note the reflexive 'se lève' — getting up is reflexive in French (se lever). Time expressions with 'à' are straightforward: à 6 heures, à midi, à minuit. Généralement is slightly more formal than 'en général.'

items.question: Nous mangeons _____ ensemble le dimanche.
items.translation: (We eat together every Sunday.)
items.explanation: From habituel → habituelle (f.) + -ment = habituellement. Note the double 'l' in the feminine gives 'llement.' This is a precise frequency adverb meaning 'habitually,' implying an established routine. 'Le dimanche' with the definite article means 'on Sundays / every Sunday' — this is a key French pattern. Without 'le,' 'dimanche' means 'this Sunday' specifically. The article transforms a specific day into a habitual pattern. Habituellement + le + day = a perfect routine expression.

items.question: Je vois _____ mes grands-parents.
items.translation: (I see my grandparents from time to time.)
items.explanation: 'De temps en temps' (from time to time) is a multi-word adverbial expression indicating occasional frequency. It's invariable and can sit after the verb or at the beginning/end of a sentence. Don't confuse the prepositions: de...en (not à or au). This expression is slightly more literary than 'parfois' but equally common in conversation. Similar multi-word frequency expressions: 'de temps à autre' (now and then), 'tous les deux jours' (every other day). French loves these descriptive frequency phrases.

items.question: Elle _____ en retard.
items.translation: (She is never late.)
items.explanation: The negation ne...jamais wraps around the conjugated verb: n'est jamais. This pattern replaces ne...pas — you don't combine them (*n'est pas jamais is incorrect). 'Jamais' means 'never' within negation, 'ever' without. The ne goes before the verb, jamais after: subject + ne + verb + jamais. In spoken French, 'ne' often drops but is always expected in writing. 'En retard' (late) is a fixed prepositional phrase — memorize it as a unit. Time-related expressions use 'en': en avance (early), en retard (late).

items.question: Tu fais _____ la cuisine ?
items.translation: (Do you cook every day?)
items.explanation: 'Tous les jours' (every day) uses the masculine plural 'tous' because 'jours' is masculine. Don't write 'tout les jours' — before a plural noun with an article, 'tout' must agree: tous (m.pl.), toutes (f.pl.). Similarly: toutes les semaines (every week, because 'semaine' is feminine), tous les mois (every month). The pattern 'tous/toutes les + time noun' is incredibly productive for expressing regular frequency. The final 's' in 'tous' is pronounced here because it precedes 'les.'

items.question: Il téléphone _____ à sa mère.
items.translation: (He calls his mother frequently.)
items.explanation: This follows the -ent → -emment rule: fréquent → fréquemment (not *fréquentement). The 't' disappears and the ending becomes -emment. Despite the spelling with double 'm,' it's pronounced like 'amment.' This is the same pattern as: récent → récemment, patient → patiemment, apparent → apparemment. Remembering this irregular formation prevents a very common spelling error. The -emment/-amment patterns are among the most frequently tested points in French grammar exams and certifications.

items.question: Nous n'allons _____ au restaurant.
items.translation: (We hardly ever go to the restaurant.)
items.explanation: 'Presque jamais' (almost never / hardly ever) combines the adverb 'presque' with 'jamais' to indicate very low frequency — just above zero. Position: 'n'allons presque jamais' — both words sit after the conjugated verb together. 'Presque' modifies 'jamais,' softening the absolute 'never' to 'almost never.' This two-adverb combination follows French's general pattern where modifying adverbs precede the adverbs they modify. Other combinations: presque toujours (almost always), presque souvent (somewhat unusual, though).

items.question: Elle fait du yoga _____ par semaine.
items.translation: (She does yoga twice a week.)
items.explanation: 'Nombre + fois' (number + times) is the standard French pattern for expressing numerical frequency: une fois (once), deux fois (twice), trois fois (three times). 'Par semaine' (per week) uses 'par' for rate/frequency — similar to English 'per.' Compare: par jour (per day), par mois (per month), par an (per year). Note: 'fois' (time/instance) vs. 'temps' (time/weather/era) — these are different words. Don't use 'temps' for counting occurrences. 'Fois' is always feminine and invariable in the singular and plural.

items.question: Je lis _____ avant de dormir.
items.translation: (I always read before sleeping.)
items.explanation: 'Toujours' reinforces here a habitual action: reading before bed. 'Avant de + infinitive' means 'before doing' — note the 'de' is required before the infinitive. This structure (avant de + infinitif) is essential French grammar. You can't say *'avant dormir' — the 'de' bridges the preposition and infinitive. Compare: 'après avoir dormi' (after having slept) — 'après' takes a past infinitive. These temporal prepositional constructions are elegant French structures that sound native once mastered.

description: Express how often actions occur using toujours, souvent, parfois, rarement, and jamais

name: Adverbes de Temps

items.question: Je vais au marché _____.
items.translation: (I go to the market today.)
items.explanation: 'Aujourd'hui' (today) is one of French's most essential time adverbs. Its etymology is fascinating: 'au jour d'hui' where 'hui' itself meant 'today' in Old French (from Latin 'hodie'). So it literally means 'on the day of today' — a double reinforcement over centuries. It usually appears at the end of the sentence or at the beginning for emphasis. Unlike most adverbs, time adverbs have flexible positions. 'Aujourd'hui, je vais...' or 'Je vais... aujourd'hui' — both are perfectly natural French.

items.question: Nous sommes allés au parc _____.
items.translation: (We went to the park yesterday.)
items.explanation: 'Hier' (yesterday) is a short, punchy time adverb. It typically appears at the end of a sentence with past tenses: passé composé, imparfait, or plus-que-parfait. Note 'sommes allés' uses être as auxiliary because 'aller' is a movement verb — one of the DR MRS VANDERTRAMP verbs. The past participle 'allés' agrees with the masculine plural subject 'nous.' Time expressions: avant-hier (day before yesterday), hier matin (yesterday morning), hier soir (last evening). French builds compound time expressions logically.

items.question: Elle arrivera _____.
items.translation: (She will arrive tomorrow.)
items.explanation: 'Demain' (tomorrow) naturally pairs with future tenses. 'Arrivera' is the simple future (futur simple) of 'arriver.' Related expressions: après-demain (day after tomorrow), demain matin (tomorrow morning), demain soir (tomorrow evening). French compounds time expressions by simply juxtaposing words: demain + matin = tomorrow morning. No preposition needed between them. Compare this simplicity with English 'the day after tomorrow,' which French handles in just two words: après-demain. Time adverbs often anchor the entire temporal framework of French sentences.

items.question: Il est _____ parti.
items.translation: (He has already left.)
items.explanation: 'Déjà' (already) occupies the position between auxiliary and participle in compound tenses: 'est déjà parti.' This mandatory position distinguishes French from English, where 'already' moves freely: 'has already left' or 'has left already.' The accent grave on 'à' is essential — without it, 'deja' would be misspelled. 'Déjà' can also express surprise in questions: 'Déjà ?' (Already?! So soon?!). In negative sentences, its opposite is 'ne...pas encore' (not yet): 'Il n'est pas encore parti.' These time adverbs anchor event timing precisely.

items.question: Tu viens _____?
items.translation: (Are you coming now?)
items.explanation: 'Maintenant' (now) denotes the present moment. It usually appears at the end of a sentence or clause, though starting a sentence with it adds urgency: 'Maintenant, tu viens !' (Now, you're coming!). The word breaks down etymically: 'main' (hand) + 'tenant' (holding) — literally 'in the moment one is holding.' This time adverb helps distinguish present actions from habitual ones. 'Je mange' could be 'I eat' (habit) or 'I am eating' (now) — adding 'maintenant' clarifies the present-moment meaning.

items.question: Nous partirons _____.
items.translation: (We will leave soon.)
items.explanation: 'Bientôt' (soon) indicates near-future timing. Note the circumflex accent on 'ô' — this distinguishes it from any other spelling. It pairs naturally with future tenses: 'partirons bientôt.' The expression 'à bientôt' (see you soon) is one of the most common French farewells. Related time adverbs on the soon/late spectrum: tôt (early), tard (late), bientôt (soon), tout à l'heure (in a little while / a little while ago — context-dependent!). Bientôt conveys optimistic anticipation and is inherently positive in tone.

items.question: Elle travaille _____ le soir.
items.translation: (She often works in the evening.)
items.explanation: When both a frequency and time adverb appear, the frequency adverb (souvent) follows the verb, while the time expression (le soir) comes at the end or start. 'Elle travaille souvent le soir' — the adverbs stack in a natural order: verb + frequency + time. French generally follows this sequence: manner → frequency → time. Note 'le soir' with the definite article means 'in the evenings' (habitual), while 'ce soir' means 'tonight' (specific). This article-based distinction between habitual and specific time is uniquely French.

items.question: Il m'a appelé _____.
items.translation: (He called me a little while ago.)
items.explanation: 'Tout à l'heure' is a remarkable French expression that means BOTH 'a little while ago' AND 'in a little while' — past or future! Context determines meaning. With a past tense: 'Il m'a appelé tout à l'heure' = he called recently. With future: 'Je le ferai tout à l'heure' = I'll do it shortly. This ambiguity rarely causes confusion because the verb tense clarifies direction. The phrase literally means 'all at the hour' — at this very hour, whether just passed or coming. It's untranslatable into a single English word.

items.question: _____, j'habitais à Paris.
items.translation: (Before/Previously, I lived in Paris.)
items.explanation: 'Avant' (before/previously) as a sentence-initial adverb sets up a temporal contrast with the present. The imparfait 'habitais' indicates ongoing past state — perfect with 'avant.' Don't confuse 'avant' (before in time) with 'devant' (before/in front of in space). Both translate to 'before' in English, but French distinguishes temporal from spatial: avant le dîner (before dinner) vs. devant la maison (in front of the house). When used standalone as here, 'avant' means 'previously/in the past' — a discourse-level time marker.

items.question: Le train est _____ arrivé.
items.translation: (The train has just arrived.)
items.explanation: 'Juste' in this position means 'just/just now,' indicating very recent action. It sits between the auxiliary and participle. However, the more idiomatic way to say 'just happened' is 'venir de + infinitive': 'Le train vient d'arriver.' This near-past construction is uniquely French and has no structural English equivalent. Both 'est juste arrivé' and 'vient d'arriver' are correct, but 'venir de' is more natural in spoken French. The past participle 'arrivé' agrees with the masculine subject 'train'; with être, agreement is required.

items.question: Je lirai ce livre _____.
items.translation: (I will read this book later.)
items.explanation: 'Plus tard' (later) combines 'plus' (more) + 'tard' (late) = more late, i.e., later. Its opposite is 'plus tôt' (earlier). Don't confuse 'plus tard' with 'en retard' (late/running late) — the first is a time adverb about future timing, the second describes being behind schedule. 'Tard' alone means 'late' in absolute terms: 'il est tard' (it's late). The comparative 'plus tard' shifts it to 'at a later point.' French time expressions are logical compounds: the comparative structure plus + time adverb is predictable and productive.

items.question: Il se lève _____ le matin.
items.translation: (He gets up early in the morning.)
items.explanation: 'Tôt' (early) is the temporal opposite of 'tard' (late). Note the circumflex on 'ô' — without it, the word doesn't exist. 'Tôt' describes when something happens (early in the day/process), not speed. Don't use 'vite' (fast/quickly) when you mean 'tôt' (early). Compare: 'Il se lève tôt' (he gets up early) vs. 'Il se lève vite' (he gets up quickly). French reflexive verbs like 'se lever' (to get up) require the reflexive pronoun — the morning routine involves many: se réveiller, se lever, se laver, se brosser, s'habiller.

items.question: Nous n'avons pas _____ fini.
items.translation: (We haven't finished yet.)
items.explanation: 'Ne...pas encore' (not yet) combines standard negation with the time adverb 'encore.' Position: ne + auxiliary + pas encore + participle. 'Encore' here means 'yet' in negative contexts. But 'encore' is versatile: 'encore' alone means 'again' or 'still' — its meaning shifts dramatically based on context. 'Encore un café' (another coffee), 'il dort encore' (he's still sleeping), 'pas encore' (not yet). This single word covers territory that requires three different English words: again, still, yet.

items.question: _____, nous allions à la plage.
items.translation: (In the past, we used to go to the beach.)
items.explanation: 'Autrefois' (in the past / formerly / once upon a time) evokes a distant past and naturally pairs with the imparfait tense, which describes habitual past actions. It's more literary and nostalgic than 'avant' (before), suggesting a bygone era. The imparfait 'allions' beautifully captures this repeated past action — 'we used to go.' Similar literary time adverbs: jadis (in olden times), naguère (not long ago, somewhat formal). French has a rich vocabulary of time adverbs that convey not just when, but the emotional quality of temporal distance.

items.question: Le magasin ouvre _____ à 9 heures.
items.translation: (The store opens precisely at 9 o'clock.)
items.explanation: 'Pile' (on the dot / exactly) is a colloquial but widely used time adverb. '9 heures pile' = 9 o'clock sharp. It's informal, punchy, and very common in spoken French. The formal equivalent would be 'précisément' or 'exactement.' 'Pile' literally means a battery or a stack — the 'exactly' meaning likely comes from the coin-flip expression 'pile ou face' (heads or tails), where 'pile' is the flat, definitive side. Used after a time expression, it adds precision and emphasis. You'll hear 'midi pile' (noon sharp) and 'minuit pile' (midnight sharp) constantly.

description: Master temporal adverbs for ordering events: déjà, encore, bientôt, autrefois, and désormais

name: Adverbes de Quantité

items.question: Il y a _____ de monde ici.
items.translation: (There are a lot of people here.)
items.explanation: 'Beaucoup de' (a lot of / many / much) is the standard quantity expression before nouns. Crucially, it takes 'de' — not 'des' or 'de la/du.' This is a fixed rule: quantity adverbs + de + noun (no article). 'Beaucoup de monde' not *'beaucoup du monde.' Other quantity adverbs follow the same pattern: peu de (few), trop de (too much), assez de (enough). 'Beaucoup' alone modifies verbs without 'de': 'je travaille beaucoup.' The de-linking rule is one of French's most important grammar points for expressing quantities naturally.

items.question: Elle mange _____ de fruits.
items.translation: (She eats too many fruits.)
items.explanation: 'Trop de' (too much / too many) indicates excessive quantity. Like 'beaucoup,' it takes 'de' before a noun — never 'des.' 'Trop de fruits' not *'trop des fruits.' 'Trop' alone modifies verbs: 'elle mange trop' (she eats too much). The critical distinction: 'trop' (excess, negative connotation) vs. 'beaucoup' (large amount, neutral) vs. 'très' (very, modifies adjectives/adverbs only). English speakers often confuse these three. You cannot say *'très de fruits' — 'très' never precedes 'de + noun.' This is a fundamental French quantity system difference.

items.question: Nous avons _____ de temps.
items.translation: (We have enough time.)
items.explanation: 'Assez de' (enough) indicates sufficient quantity. 'Assez de temps' = enough time. 'Assez' also modifies adjectives: 'assez grand' (tall enough / fairly tall). This double function makes 'assez' versatile: as quantity (assez de + noun) and as degree (assez + adjective). The pronunciation note: the final 'z' sound appears in liaison before vowels: 'assez intéressant' = /asez ɛ̃teʁesɑ̃/. Without liaison: 'assez grand' = /ase gʁɑ̃/. 'Assez' sits at the midpoint of the quantity spectrum — more than 'un peu' but less than 'beaucoup.'

items.question: Il y a _____ de sucre dans ce gâteau.
items.translation: (There is a little sugar in this cake.)
items.explanation: 'Un peu de' (a little / some) indicates a small positive quantity. Critical distinction: 'un peu de' (a little, positive — there IS some) vs. 'peu de' (few/little, negative — there's barely any). 'Un peu de sucre' = there's some sugar (positive). 'Peu de sucre' = there's hardly any sugar (negative). This seemingly small difference changes the entire meaning. English conflates both into 'little' — 'a little sugar' (positive) vs. 'little sugar' (negative). French makes this distinction explicit through the article 'un,' making communication more precise.

items.question: _____ d'étudiants ont réussi l'examen.
items.translation: (Few students passed the exam.)
items.explanation: 'Peu de' (few / little) expresses small quantity with a NEGATIVE implication — almost none. Compare with 'un peu de' (a little, positive — some exists). 'Peu d'étudiants' means disappointingly few students. This is a nuanced quantity adverb that English speakers must master. Note: before a vowel, 'de' becomes 'd'' — 'peu d'étudiants.' The elision rule applies after all quantity adverbs. 'Peu' alone modifies verbs too: 'il mange peu' (he eats little). On the quantity scale: rien < peu < un peu < assez < beaucoup < trop.

items.question: J'ai _____ de travail cette semaine.
items.translation: (I have so much work this week.)
items.explanation: 'Tellement de' (so much / so many) expresses emphatic large quantity. It's more expressive than 'beaucoup' — it conveys emotion (overwhelm, amazement). 'Tellement de travail !' carries exasperation that 'beaucoup de travail' doesn't. 'Tellement' also modifies adjectives and verbs: 'C'est tellement bon !' (It's so good!), 'Il travaille tellement !' (He works so much!). The English equivalent shifts between 'so much,' 'so many,' and 'so' depending on context. French uses 'tellement' across all these situations, making it a powerful emotional intensifier.

items.question: Combien _____ langues parles-tu ?
items.translation: (How many languages do you speak?)
items.explanation: 'Combien de' (how many / how much) is the interrogative quantity expression. Like all quantity adverbs, it takes 'de' — never 'des' or articles. 'Combien de langues' not *'combien des langues.' This rule is absolute: quantity word + de (bare, no article) + noun. 'Combien' also works alone with verbs: 'Combien coûte-t-il ?' (How much does it cost?). In informal speech, 'combien' can end the sentence: 'Tu en veux combien ?' (How many do you want?). The de-rule connects combien to the world of beaucoup, trop, assez, and peu — all take bare 'de.'

items.question: Elle a _____ de patience.
items.translation: (She has more patience.)
items.explanation: 'Plus de' (more) follows the quantity-adverb + de pattern. 'Plus de patience' = more patience. Don't add an article: *'plus de la patience' is wrong. For comparisons, you add 'que': 'plus de patience que toi' (more patience than you). 'Plus' has a fascinating pronunciation rule in French: the 's' is silent in negation 'ne...plus' (no more: /ply/) but pronounced in comparison 'plus que' (more than: /plys/). This phonetic clue actually helps listeners distinguish meaning. The quantity system — moins de (less), autant de (as much), plus de (more) — forms the comparison framework.

items.question: Il y a _____ de places disponibles.
items.translation: (There are fewer seats available.)
items.explanation: 'Moins de' (fewer / less) is the opposite of 'plus de.' 'Moins de places' = fewer seats. Same pattern: quantity adverb + de + noun (no article). For comparisons: 'moins de places que hier' (fewer seats than yesterday). 'Moins' always ends in silent 's' — don't drop it. French doesn't distinguish between 'fewer' (countable) and 'less' (uncountable) like prescriptive English does — 'moins de' covers both: 'moins d'eau' (less water), 'moins de gens' (fewer people). This simplification makes 'moins' easier to use than its English counterparts.

items.question: Nous avons _____ de fromage que vous.
items.translation: (We have as much cheese as you.)
items.explanation: 'Autant de...que' (as much/many...as) expresses equal quantity. 'Autant de fromage que vous' = as much cheese as you. Critical distinction: 'autant' for quantities (autant de + noun) vs. 'aussi' for qualities (aussi + adjective). 'Il est aussi grand' (he's as tall) but 'il a autant d'argent' (he has as much money). English uses 'as much/many...as' for both. This autant/aussi split is a frequent error point for English speakers. 'Tant de' is a literary synonym of 'autant de' that you'll see in literature: 'tant de beauté' (so much beauty).

items.question: Ajoutez _____ de sel.
items.translation: (Add a pinch of salt.)
items.explanation: 'Une pincée de' (a pinch of) is a precise quantity noun acting adverbially. French uses many such expressions for specific amounts: une poignée de (a handful of), une goutte de (a drop of), un morceau de (a piece of), une tranche de (a slice of). These all follow the same structure: quantity expression + de + noun. They're essential for cooking vocabulary and daily life. Unlike vague adverbs (beaucoup, peu), these give vivid, physical imagery. Note: 'pincée' comes from 'pincer' (to pinch) — what you can grab between two fingers.

items.question: Elle a _____ de problèmes en ce moment.
items.translation: (She has plenty of problems right now.)
items.explanation: 'Plein de' (plenty of / full of / lots of) is informal but very common in spoken French. It's technically an adjective used adverbially — 'plein' stays invariable in this construction. Don't agree it: 'plein de problèmes' even for a feminine subject. More formal alternatives: beaucoup de, énormément de. 'Plein de' carries a casual, conversational tone that 'beaucoup de' lacks. In formal writing, prefer 'beaucoup de' or 'de nombreux.' But in everyday speech, 'j'ai plein de choses à faire' (I have tons of things to do) is perfectly natural and ubiquitous.

items.question: Il y a _____ de neige en hiver.
items.translation: (There is enough snow in winter.)
items.explanation: 'Suffisamment de' (sufficiently / enough of) is the formal equivalent of 'assez de.' Formation follows the -amment rule: suffisant → suffisamment (not *suffisantement). It always takes 'de' before nouns. 'Suffisamment' sounds more elegant and formal than 'assez,' making it preferable in written French and formal speech. Compare: 'assez de neige' (casual enough) vs. 'suffisamment de neige' (formally enough). Both are correct, but register differs. The double 'm' in -amment comes from the -ant adjective ending — this rule is consistent across all -ant adjectives becoming adverbs.

items.question: Nous avons _____ de raisons de célébrer.
items.translation: (We have many reasons to celebrate.)
items.explanation: 'Bien des' (many / quite a few) is a somewhat formal quantity expression. Unlike other quantity adverbs, 'bien' takes 'des' (not bare 'de'): 'bien des raisons' not 'bien de raisons.' This is an exception to the standard quantity + de rule and must be memorized. 'Bien des' has a slightly emphatic, literary quality. It's not interchangeable with 'beaucoup de' in tone — 'bien des raisons' sounds more thoughtful, reflective. Other exceptions to the bare-de rule: la plupart des (most of the) also takes 'des.' These exceptions are few but important.

items.question: J'ai _____ de livres à lire.
items.translation: (I have an enormous amount of books to read.)
items.explanation: 'Énormément de' (an enormous amount of / tremendously many) is a strong quantity adverb. Formation: énorme + -ment = énormément. Note the accent on the first 'e' — énormément, not *enormement. It follows the standard quantity + de pattern: 'énormément de livres' not *'énormément des livres.' On the intensity scale, it sits above 'beaucoup' and near 'tellement' but with a more factual than emotional tone. Enormément emphasizes sheer volume, while tellement emphasizes the speaker's reaction to the volume. Both are powerful quantity expressions for advanced French use.

description: Express quantities and degrees with beaucoup, peu, trop, assez, très, and their constructions

title: Prepositions of Place

items.question: Je suis _____ Paris.
items.translation: (I am in Paris.)
items.explanation: French uses 'à' before cities and towns — never 'en' or 'dans.' 'Je suis à Paris' means 'I am in Paris.' This is a fundamental rule: à + city (à Lyon, à Marseille, à Tokyo). For countries, the rules change based on gender: 'en France' (feminine), 'au Japon' (masculine). But for cities, 'à' is virtually universal. English speakers often try 'dans Paris,' which sounds wrong to native ears.

items.question: Elle habite _____ France.
items.translation: (She lives in France.)
items.explanation: 'En' is used before feminine countries and those starting with a vowel. France is feminine (la France), so 'en France.' This contrasts with masculine countries that use 'au' (au Canada, au Brésil) and plural countries using 'aux' (aux États-Unis). The gender of the country determines the preposition — a concept absent in English. Most European countries are feminine, which helps learners predict the correct preposition.

items.question: Le livre est _____ la table.
items.translation: (The book is on the table.)
items.explanation: 'Sur' means 'on/on top of' and indicates physical surface contact. 'Sur la table' parallels English 'on the table' directly. French prepositions of place are quite literal: sur (on), sous (under), dans (in/inside), devant (in front of), derrière (behind). Unlike English, which uses 'at' ambiguously, French is precise about spatial relationships. 'Sur' can also be figurative: 'sur Internet' (on the Internet), 'sur la photo' (in the photo).

items.question: Le chat est _____ le lit.
items.translation: (The cat is under the bed.)
items.explanation: 'Sous' means 'under/beneath.' 'Sous le lit' is a direct equivalent of 'under the bed.' This preposition is straightforward and rarely causes confusion. Note the pronunciation: 'sous' rhymes with English 'sue.' It also appears in common expressions: 'sous la pluie' (in the rain — note French says 'under' the rain, not 'in'), 'sous-marin' (submarine, literally 'under-sea'). French spatial prepositions are precise and consistent.

items.question: Il y a un jardin _____ la maison.
items.translation: (There is a garden behind the house.)
items.explanation: 'Derrière' means 'behind/in back of.' French has dedicated prepositions for each spatial position, unlike English which sometimes reuses words ambiguously. 'Derrière la maison' is clear and precise. Notice that simple position prepositions (devant, derrière, entre) take a direct noun, while compound ones (à côté de, en face de, au-dessus de) require 'de' before the noun. This distinction matters for correct French expression.

items.question: La pharmacie est _____ le supermarché.
items.translation: (The pharmacy is next to the supermarket.)
items.explanation: 'À côté de' (next to/beside) is a compound preposition requiring 'de.' When followed by 'le,' contraction produces 'du': à côté du supermarché. With 'la,' no contraction: à côté de la banque. With 'les': à côté des magasins. These contractions (de+le=du, de+les=des) are mandatory in French and apply to all compound prepositions ending in 'de.' Mastering this contraction pattern unlocks dozens of location expressions.

items.question: La banque est _____ la poste.
items.translation: (The bank is across from the post office.)
items.explanation: 'En face de' means 'across from/facing/opposite.' Like other compound prepositions, it requires 'de' before the noun. 'En face de la poste' — no contraction needed since 'la' doesn't contract with 'de.' Compare: 'en face du cinéma' (du = de + le). English uses 'across from' or 'opposite,' but French's 'en face de' literally means 'facing.' This expression is essential for giving directions in French.

items.question: Les clés sont _____ mon sac.
items.translation: (The keys are inside my bag.)
items.explanation: 'Dans' indicates being inside a container or enclosed space. 'Dans mon sac' (in my bag), 'dans la voiture' (in the car), 'dans la maison' (in/inside the house). While English uses 'in' broadly, French distinguishes 'dans' (physically inside) from 'en' (more abstract/figurative) and 'à' (at/to locations). 'Dans' implies containment — the keys are physically enclosed within the bag. This three-way distinction between dans/en/à is uniquely French.

items.question: Elle est assise _____ Pierre et Marie.
items.translation: (She is sitting between Pierre and Marie.)
items.explanation: 'Entre' means 'between' and is used with two specific items or people. 'Entre Pierre et Marie' (between Pierre and Marie). Don't confuse it with 'parmi' (among), which is used for groups of three or more: 'parmi les étudiants' (among the students). This entre/parmi distinction mirrors English between/among. 'Entre' also appears in useful expressions: 'entre nous' (between us), 'entre-temps' (meanwhile). The two prepositions are never interchangeable.

items.question: Il habite _____ du parc.
items.translation: (He lives near the park.)
items.explanation: 'Près de' means 'near/close to.' Here 'près du parc' uses the contraction du (de + le). Its opposite is 'loin de' (far from). Both are compound prepositions requiring 'de.' 'Il habite près de la gare' (near the station), 'loin de l'aéroport' (far from the airport). These are essential for describing locations and distances. Note: 'proche de' is a synonym but it's an adjective, not a preposition: 'La maison est proche du parc.'

items.question: Le tableau est _____ le mur.
items.translation: (The painting is on/against the wall.)
items.explanation: 'Contre' means 'against' and implies leaning or resting vertically. While 'sur le mur' could work for something flat on a surface, 'contre le mur' suggests something leaning against or hanging on the wall vertically. French makes this distinction that English doesn't: 'contre' for vertical contact, 'sur' for horizontal surface contact. Other uses: 's'appuyer contre' (to lean against), 'lutter contre' (to fight against). The preposition adds precision.

items.question: Le restaurant est _____ de la gare.
items.translation: (The restaurant is far from the station.)
items.explanation: 'Loin de' (far from) is the opposite of 'près de' (near). 'Loin de la gare' — no contraction needed because 'la' doesn't contract with 'de.' Compare 'loin du cinéma' (de + le = du). This symmetrical pair (près de/loin de) is fundamental for describing distances. 'Loin' can also function as an adverb: 'C'est loin' (It's far). In expressions: 'de loin' (by far), 'au loin' (in the distance). French distance vocabulary is built around these two pivotal prepositions.

items.question: Nous allons _____ Japon cet été.
items.translation: (We are going to Japan this summer.)
items.explanation: 'Au' is used before masculine singular countries. Le Japon → au Japon (au = à + le). This contrasts with 'en' for feminine countries (en France, en Italie) and 'aux' for plural countries (aux Pays-Bas, aux États-Unis). The rule: check the country's gender, then apply à+article (masculine) or en (feminine/vowel). Most countries ending in -e are feminine (la Chine, la Russie), except le Mexique, le Cambodge. This gender-based system has no English equivalent.

items.question: Il y a un pont _____ la rivière.
items.translation: (There is a bridge over the river.)
items.explanation: 'Au-dessus de' means 'above/over' without direct contact. A bridge spans above the river. Compare: 'sur' implies contact (on the surface), while 'au-dessus de' implies hovering above without touching. Its opposite is 'en dessous de' (below/underneath). These compound prepositions add precision: 'L'avion vole au-dessus de la ville' (the plane flies over the city). The hyphenated structure (au-dessus, en dessous) is characteristic of French spatial vocabulary.

items.question: La boulangerie est _____ de chez moi.
items.translation: (The bakery is close to my home.)
items.explanation: 'Tout près de' intensifies 'près de' — meaning 'very close to/right near.' 'Tout' acts as an intensifier with prepositions: 'tout près' (very near), 'tout droit' (straight ahead), 'tout au bout' (at the very end). Note 'chez moi' — French uses 'chez + person/pronoun' for someone's home, a concept absent in English. 'Chez moi' (at my place), 'chez Pierre' (at Pierre's place). This chez + person construction is one of French's most elegant and practical expressions.

name: Prépositions de Lieu (Prepositions of Place)
description: Master spatial prepositions for expressing location: à, en, dans, sur, sous, devant, derrière

title: Prepositions of Time

items.question: J'étudie le français _____ trois ans.
items.translation: (I have been studying French for three years.)
items.explanation: 'Depuis' expresses duration continuing into the present — 'since/for (ongoing).' 'Depuis trois ans' means you started three years ago AND still continue. English uses present perfect progressive ('have been studying'), but French uses present tense + depuis. This is a major trap: 'J'étudie depuis...' not *'J'ai étudié depuis.' The present tense with 'depuis' is one of French's most important time constructions, signaling unbroken continuity from past to now.

items.question: J'ai travaillé _____ deux heures.
items.translation: (I worked for two hours.)
items.explanation: 'Pendant' means 'during/for' with completed actions. 'Pendant deux heures' (for two hours) describes a finished period. Key distinction: 'depuis' = ongoing ('I've been...'), 'pendant' = completed ('I did... for X time'). 'J'ai travaillé pendant deux heures' — the work is done. Compare 'Je travaille depuis deux heures' — still working. This depuis/pendant distinction has no direct English equivalent since English uses 'for' for both.

items.question: Il est parti _____ deux jours.
items.translation: (He left two days ago.)
items.explanation: 'Il y a' before a time expression means 'ago.' 'Il y a deux jours' = 'two days ago.' This uses the passé composé because the action is completed and pinpointed in the past. Don't confuse with 'il y a' meaning 'there is/are' — context determines meaning. Structure: il y a + duration = ago. 'Il y a une semaine' (a week ago), 'il y a longtemps' (a long time ago). This expression is extremely common in everyday French conversation.

items.question: Le train part _____ dix minutes.
items.translation: (The train leaves in ten minutes.)
items.explanation: 'Dans' before a time expression means 'in (future).' 'Dans dix minutes' = 'in ten minutes from now.' It always points forward in time. Don't confuse with 'en' which means 'in (duration to complete)': 'en dix minutes' = 'it takes ten minutes.' Compare: 'Le train part dans 10 minutes' (departs in 10 min) vs. 'Le trajet dure en 10 minutes' is wrong — use 'Le trajet prend 10 minutes.' The dans/en distinction is critical for expressing future time correctly.

items.question: Elle a fini son travail _____ une heure.
items.translation: (She finished her work in one hour.)
items.explanation: 'En' + duration means 'in (the time it takes to do something).' 'En une heure' = 'in/within one hour.' This expresses how long an action took to complete. Compare the three: 'dans une heure' (one hour from now — future point), 'en une heure' (took one hour — completion speed), 'pendant une heure' (for one hour — duration). Each preposition gives time a completely different meaning. 'En' emphasizes efficiency and speed of completion.

items.question: Je suis à Paris _____ lundi.
items.translation: (I have been in Paris since Monday.)
items.explanation: 'Depuis' + specific point in time means 'since.' 'Depuis lundi' = 'since Monday' (and still in Paris). With duration: 'depuis trois jours' (for three days, ongoing). With a specific moment: 'depuis 2020' (since 2020), 'depuis hier' (since yesterday). Remember: present tense + depuis in French, not passé composé. 'Je suis à Paris depuis lundi' — the stay continues. This depuis + present tense pattern is tested repeatedly in French exams.

items.question: _____ le dîner, nous avons regardé un film.
items.translation: (After dinner, we watched a movie.)
items.explanation: 'Après' means 'after' and indicates sequence. 'Après le dîner' = 'after dinner.' Note: 'après' + noun is simple, but 'après' + verb requires the infinitive past: 'après avoir mangé' (after having eaten), 'après être parti' (after having left). This construction uses the auxiliary + past participle infinitive form. Meanwhile, 'avant de' + infinitive works for 'before': 'avant de manger' (before eating). The après/avant pair is fundamental for narrating sequences.

items.question: Il faut finir _____ vendredi.
items.translation: (We must finish before Friday.)
items.explanation: 'Avant' means 'before' and sets a deadline. 'Avant vendredi' = 'before Friday.' When followed by a verb, it requires 'de' + infinitive: 'avant de partir' (before leaving). Compare with 'après' which takes a past infinitive. 'Avant' can also be an adverb: 'la nuit d'avant' (the night before). In expressions: 'avant tout' (above all/first of all), 'en avant' (forward). The avant/après pair structures temporal relationships throughout French discourse.

items.question: Nous partons _____ vacances pour deux semaines.
items.translation: (We are leaving on vacation for two weeks.)
items.explanation: 'En vacances' is a fixed expression — 'on vacation.' The preposition 'en' here doesn't follow standard rules but is idiomatic. Meanwhile, 'pour deux semaines' uses 'pour' to express intended/planned duration — something you plan to do for a set time. Distinction: 'pendant' = actual past duration, 'pour' = planned/intended duration. 'Je pars pour deux semaines' (I'm leaving for two weeks — that's the plan). This pour/pendant nuance is subtle but important.

items.question: Le magasin est ouvert _____ 9h _____ 18h.
items.translation: (The store is open from 9am to 6pm.)
items.explanation: 'De... à...' expresses a range — 'from... to...' This works for time, numbers, and distances: 'de 9h à 18h' (from 9 to 6), 'de Paris à Lyon' (from Paris to Lyon), 'de 1 à 10' (from 1 to 10). For emphasis or formality, 'depuis... jusqu'à...' can replace it. Note the structure is clean: de + start point, à + end point. No articles needed with hours. This pattern structures schedules, ranges, and spans throughout French.

items.question: Il lit le journal _____ le petit déjeuner.
items.translation: (He reads the newspaper during breakfast.)
items.explanation: 'Pendant' before an event means 'during.' 'Pendant le petit déjeuner' = 'during breakfast.' While 'pendant' often translates as 'for' with durations, it means 'during' with events and activities. 'Durant' is a formal synonym. Key usage: 'pendant la réunion' (during the meeting), 'pendant les vacances' (during vacation), 'pendant la nuit' (during the night). English speakers must remember that 'during' and 'for' are both 'pendant' in many contexts.

items.question: _____ quelle heure commence le cours ?
items.translation: (At what time does the class start?)
items.explanation: 'À' is used before specific clock times. 'À quelle heure' = 'at what time.' Responses follow: 'à 8 heures' (at 8 o'clock), 'à midi' (at noon), 'à minuit' (at midnight). This differs from English's flexible 'at/on/in' system. French time prepositions are strict: 'à' for clock time, 'en' for months/years/seasons (except 'au printemps'), 'le' for days of the week. Mastering these fixed combinations eliminates hesitation when expressing time.

items.question: Mon anniversaire est _____ mars.
items.translation: (My birthday is in March.)
items.explanation: 'En' is used before months and most seasons. 'En mars' (in March), 'en été' (in summer), 'en hiver' (in winter), 'en automne' (in autumn). The exception is spring: 'au printemps' (not *en printemps). Years also take 'en': 'en 2025.' This 'en' for temporal contexts differs from 'en' for feminine countries or 'en' meaning 'in (completion time).' Context determines meaning. The month/season rule with 'en' is consistent and reliable for learners.

items.question: J'ai un rendez-vous _____ lundi.
items.translation: (I have an appointment on Monday.)
items.explanation: French uses NO preposition before days of the week for specific dates — just the day alone! 'J'ai un rendez-vous lundi' = 'I have an appointment on Monday.' No 'on' needed. However, 'le lundi' (with article) means 'on Mondays' (habitual): 'Le lundi, je fais du sport' (On Mondays, I exercise). This bare-day vs. le+day distinction is crucial: no article = specific date, article = recurring habit. English always uses 'on,' making this zero-preposition rule feel strange.

items.question: Elle est arrivée _____ à l'examen.
items.translation: (She arrived late to the exam.)
items.explanation: 'En retard' means 'late (behind schedule)' while 'tard' means 'late (at a late hour).' 'En retard à l'examen' = late for the exam. Compare: 'Je me couche tard' (I go to bed late — late hour). The pair 'en retard' (behind schedule) / 'en avance' (early/ahead of schedule) / 'à l'heure' (on time) forms a complete set for describing punctuality. These fixed expressions with 'en' are extremely common in daily French and essential for talking about schedules.

name: Prépositions de Temps (Prepositions of Time)
description: Express time relationships using pendant, depuis, pour, en, dans, and il y a

title: Prepositions: Avec, Pour, Sans

items.question: Je vais au cinéma _____ mes amis.
items.translation: (I am going to the cinema with my friends.)
items.explanation: 'Avec' means 'with' and indicates accompaniment. 'Avec mes amis' = 'with my friends.' Unlike English, 'avec' is never contracted or shortened. It works with people (avec Marie), objects (avec un crayon), and abstract concepts (avec plaisir — with pleasure). In questions: 'Avec qui ?' (With whom?). Note that 'avec' doesn't change form — no gender or number agreement needed, making it one of French's most straightforward prepositions.

items.question: Ce cadeau est _____ toi.
items.translation: (This gift is for you.)
items.explanation: 'Pour' means 'for' indicating purpose or recipient. 'Pour toi' = 'for you.' After stressed pronouns: pour moi, pour toi, pour lui/elle, pour nous, pour vous, pour eux/elles. 'Pour' also expresses purpose: 'pour apprendre' (in order to learn), duration of intention: 'pour deux jours' (for two days, planned). Don't confuse 'pour' (beneficiary/purpose) with 'pendant' (actual duration) or 'depuis' (ongoing duration). Each preposition of time has its own specific meaning.

items.question: Il est parti _____ dire au revoir.
items.translation: (He left without saying goodbye.)
items.explanation: 'Sans' means 'without' and directly mirrors English usage. 'Sans dire' = 'without saying.' When followed by a verb, 'sans' takes the infinitive directly — no 'de' needed: 'sans parler' (without speaking), 'sans manger' (without eating). This contrasts with 'avant de' (before), which requires 'de.' 'Sans' + noun drops the article in many cases: 'sans sucre' (without sugar), 'sans problème' (no problem). This article-dropping after 'sans' is a distinctive French pattern.

items.question: Elle travaille _____ une entreprise internationale.
items.translation: (She works for an international company.)
items.explanation: 'Pour' here means 'for' in the sense of employment/service. 'Travailler pour' (to work for someone/a company). However, 'travailler dans' (to work in a field): 'travailler dans la finance.' And 'travailler chez' (to work at a named company): 'travailler chez Google.' These three constructions coexist: pour (employer relationship), dans (sector), chez (named organization). The choice depends on what aspect of work you're describing — a nuance English's single 'for' doesn't capture.

items.question: Un café _____ lait, s'il vous plaît.
items.translation: (A coffee with milk, please.)
items.explanation: 'Au lait' (with milk) uses 'au' (à + le) rather than 'avec' in food/drink contexts. This is idiomatic: 'café au lait,' 'chocolat au lait,' 'tarte aux pommes' (apple pie). French uses à/au/aux for ingredients: 'glace à la vanille' (vanilla ice cream), 'soupe aux légumes' (vegetable soup). With 'avec,' it would mean the milk is served separately alongside. 'Au' implies the milk is mixed in — an integral part of the drink. This à-for-ingredients pattern is essential for ordering food.

items.question: Il a réussi _____ l'aide de ses parents.
items.translation: (He succeeded with the help of his parents.)
items.explanation: 'Avec l'aide de' means 'with the help of.' Here 'avec' introduces the means or instrument of success. Related: 'grâce à' (thanks to — positive), 'à cause de' (because of — negative). All three express causality differently: 'avec' is neutral (with), 'grâce à' is positive (thanks to), 'à cause de' is negative (because of/due to). 'Il a réussi grâce à ses parents' would emphasize gratitude. Choosing the right preposition reveals your attitude toward the cause.

items.question: C'est facile _____ comprendre.
items.translation: (It is easy to understand.)
items.explanation: 'Facile à' + infinitive is a fixed construction: 'facile à comprendre' (easy to understand), 'difficile à faire' (hard to do), 'agréable à lire' (pleasant to read). These adjectives of evaluation take 'à' before infinitives. Contrast with 'il est facile de comprendre' — when starting with impersonal 'il est,' use 'de.' This à/de alternation depends on sentence structure, not meaning. 'C'est facile à faire' but 'Il est facile de le faire.' This nuance is tested frequently.

items.question: Elle est partie _____ parapluie.
items.translation: (She left without an umbrella.)
items.explanation: 'Sans' often drops the article before the noun. 'Sans parapluie' (without an umbrella) — no article needed. Compare English: 'without an umbrella' requires 'an.' French: 'sans argent' (without money), 'sans travail' (without work), 'sans raison' (without reason). However, when the noun is specific or modified, the article appears: 'sans le parapluie que tu m'as donné' (without the umbrella you gave me). The default is article-free after 'sans' — a clean, efficient French pattern.

items.question: _____ qui est ce message ?
items.translation: (For whom / Who is this message for?)
items.explanation: 'Pour qui' means 'for whom.' French question words combine naturally with prepositions at the start: 'Pour qui ?' (For whom?), 'Avec qui ?' (With whom?), 'De qui ?' (About whom?), 'À qui ?' (To whom?). Unlike informal English which strands prepositions at the end ('Who is this for?'), French always places the preposition before 'qui.' This front-loading of prepositions in questions is standard at all register levels in French.

items.question: Je suis _____ cette idée.
items.translation: (I am for/in favor of this idea.)
items.explanation: 'Pour' expressing opinion means 'in favor of/for.' Its opposite is 'contre' (against). 'Je suis pour cette idée' vs. 'Je suis contre cette idée.' In debates: 'Êtes-vous pour ou contre ?' (Are you for or against?). This pour/contre pair is essential for expressing opinions. Note that 'pour' here doesn't mean 'for (purpose)' or 'for (duration)' — context determines which meaning applies. French prepositions are highly polysemous, meaning they carry multiple meanings.

items.question: Merci _____ votre aide.
items.translation: (Thank you for your help.)
items.explanation: 'Merci pour' and 'merci de' both mean 'thank you for,' but usage differs. 'Merci pour' + noun (tangible things): 'Merci pour le cadeau' (thanks for the gift). 'Merci de' + infinitive: 'Merci de m'avoir aidé' (thanks for helping me). Some speakers use 'merci de' + noun formally. The safest rule: 'pour' with nouns, 'de' with verbs. Both are correct and widely used. This pour/de alternation with 'merci' reflects broader French patterns of preposition selection after specific verbs.

items.question: Il a coupé le pain _____ un couteau.
items.translation: (He cut the bread with a knife.)
items.explanation: 'Avec' introduces the instrument or tool used. 'Avec un couteau' = 'with a knife.' 'Écrire avec un stylo' (write with a pen), 'ouvrir avec une clé' (open with a key). Note that in some literary or fixed expressions, 'de' replaces 'avec' for instruments: 'frapper du poing' (strike with one's fist). But in everyday speech, 'avec' is standard for physical tools and instruments. It's the most natural and universally understood way to express means in French.

items.question: Le café est fermé _____ le lundi.
items.translation: (The café is closed on Mondays.)
items.explanation: French uses the definite article 'le' alone (no preposition) for habitual days. 'Le lundi' = 'on Mondays/every Monday.' 'Le café est fermé le lundi' — closed every Monday. This contrasts with a single occurrence: 'Le café est fermé lundi' (closed this Monday — no article). The article signals repetition; its absence signals a specific instance. English uses 'on Mondays' vs. 'on Monday,' but French uses le+day vs. bare day. No preposition is needed in either case.

items.question: Nous sommes prêts _____ partir.
items.translation: (We are ready to leave.)
items.explanation: 'Prêt à' + infinitive is a fixed construction meaning 'ready to.' 'Prêt à partir' (ready to leave), 'prêt à manger' (ready to eat). Many adjectives have fixed prepositions: 'content de' (happy to/about), 'capable de' (capable of), 'utile à' (useful for). These adjective + preposition combinations must be memorized — there's no reliable rule predicting à vs. de. Creating flashcards for the most common ones accelerates mastery of this challenging area of French grammar.

items.question: On ne peut pas vivre _____ eau.
items.translation: (One cannot live without water.)
items.explanation: 'Sans' in generalizations or universal statements drops the article entirely. 'Sans eau' (without water) — no article. 'Vivre sans amour' (live without love), 'sans hésitation' (without hesitation), 'sans doute' (without doubt/probably). This zero-article pattern after 'sans' creates concise, punchy expressions common in both spoken and written French. 'Sans doute' is particularly frequent and is a false friend — it means 'probably' rather than 'without doubt/certainly.'

name: Prépositions Avec et Pour (With & For)
description: Use avec, pour, sans, contre, and entre for accompaniment, purpose, and relationships

title: Prepositions of Movement

items.question: Je vais _____ l'école.
items.translation: (I am going to school.)
items.explanation: 'À' indicates direction/destination — 'to.' 'Aller à l'école' (go to school), 'aller à la gare' (go to the station). Before feminine 'l'école,' 'à' remains unchanged because of the vowel elision. Before 'le': à + le = au (aller au cinéma). Before 'les': à + les = aux (aller aux toilettes). This contraction system is mandatory. English simply says 'to the' everywhere, but French requires you to check the noun's gender and number before building the preposition-article combination.

items.question: Elle rentre _____ maison.
items.translation: (She is going home.)
items.explanation: 'Rentrer à la maison' means 'to go back home.' 'Maison' is feminine (la maison), so 'à la' — no contraction. Compare: 'aller au bureau' (masc.), 'aller à l'hôpital' (vowel). Note that 'chez moi/toi/lui' is an alternative for 'to/at one's home': 'Je rentre chez moi' (I'm going home). Both 'à la maison' and 'chez moi' are correct, but 'chez' is more personal while 'à la maison' is more general. French offers this elegant choice English lacks.

items.question: Le chat a sauté _____ la fenêtre.
items.translation: (The cat jumped through the window.)
items.explanation: 'Par' indicates passage through an opening — 'through/via/by way of.' 'Par la fenêtre' = 'through the window.' 'Entrer par la porte' (enter through the door), 'passer par Lyon' (go through/via Lyon). 'Par' also appears in passive constructions: 'fait par Marie' (done by Marie) and expressions: 'par exemple' (for example), 'par hasard' (by chance), 'par cœur' (by heart). This versatile preposition is one of French's most multi-functional words.

items.question: Nous marchons _____ la plage.
items.translation: (We are walking toward the beach.)
items.explanation: 'Vers' means 'toward/in the direction of' without implying arrival. 'Vers la plage' = walking in the beach's direction, not necessarily reaching it. Compare: 'à la plage' (to the beach — implies arrival). 'Vers' also means 'around' with time: 'vers 8 heures' (around 8 o'clock). This double meaning (direction + approximation) is elegant. 'Vers' suggests movement without commitment to completion, making it perfect for indicating general direction or approximate time.

items.question: Il court _____ le parc.
items.translation: (He runs through the park.)
items.explanation: 'À travers' means 'through/across' an open space. 'À travers le parc' = through the park (crossing it). Compare 'par' which implies through an opening (par la porte), while 'à travers' implies crossing an expanse. Other movement prepositions: 'autour de' (around), 'le long de' (along), 'au-delà de' (beyond). French offers precise spatial movement vocabulary. 'À travers' can also be figurative: 'à travers les siècles' (through the centuries), adding poetic depth.

items.question: Le serpent passe _____ les rochers.
items.translation: (The snake passes between the rocks.)
items.explanation: 'Entre' with movement means 'between' two things — passing through a gap. 'Entre les rochers' = between/through the rocks (a narrow passage). Remember: 'entre' for two items, 'parmi' for a group. With movement: 'se faufiler entre les voitures' (weave between cars), 'passer entre les gouttes' (slip between the raindrops — an idiom meaning to dodge trouble). This entre/parmi distinction applies equally to static position and dynamic movement.

items.question: L'avion vole _____ l'océan.
items.translation: (The plane flies over the ocean.)
items.explanation: 'Au-dessus de' means 'over/above' — spanning across from above. 'Au-dessus de l'océan' = over/above the ocean. This compound preposition implies elevation without contact. For movement over a barrier at ground level, use 'par-dessus': 'sauter par-dessus le mur' (jump over the wall). The distinction: 'au-dessus de' = hovering above, 'par-dessus' = arcing over a barrier. French precision in spatial prepositions exceeds English, giving speakers more exact tools.

items.question: Les enfants courent _____ l'arbre.
items.translation: (The children run around the tree.)
items.explanation: 'Autour de' means 'around' — circular movement or position. 'Autour de l'arbre' = around the tree. This compound preposition requires 'de' before the noun (with standard contractions: autour du parc, autour des maisons). It works for both physical movement ('courir autour de') and abstract concepts: 'autour de cette question' (around this issue). The adverb form 'autour' can stand alone: 'tout autour' (all around). This versatile expression handles circular spatial relationships.

items.question: Nous marchons _____ la rivière.
items.translation: (We walk along the river.)
items.explanation: 'Le long de' means 'along/alongside.' 'Le long de la rivière' = along the river. This compound preposition describes parallel movement or position beside a linear feature. Compare: 'le long du chemin' (along the path), 'le long des murs' (along the walls). It evokes an image of following something's length. Similar expressions: 'tout au long de' (throughout — temporal: 'tout au long de l'année' = throughout the year). French compound prepositions create vivid spatial imagery.

items.question: Elle sort _____ la salle.
items.translation: (She goes out of the room.)
items.explanation: 'Sortir de' means 'to go out of/leave.' 'De' indicates origin or source of movement — moving away from. 'Sortir de la salle' (leave the room), 'venir de Paris' (come from Paris), 'tomber de la table' (fall off the table). This directional 'de' (from/out of) contrasts with 'à' (to/toward). The de/à pair forms French's fundamental movement framework: 'aller à' (go to) vs. 'venir de' (come from). Understanding this axis is key to expressing all French movement.

items.question: Monte _____ l'escalier.
items.translation: (Go up the stairs.)
items.explanation: 'Monter par l'escalier' uses 'par' (by way of/via) — the stairs being the route. However, 'monter l'escalier' without a preposition is also common (direct object). And 'monter dans l'avion' (board the plane — enter the interior). French verbs of movement change meaning with different prepositions: 'monter par' (via), 'monter dans' (enter), 'monter sur' (climb onto). This preposition-dependent meaning shift is a hallmark of French movement verbs and requires careful attention.

items.question: Le bateau navigue _____ la côte.
