items.translation: (The boat navigates toward the coast.)
items.explanation: 'Vers la côte' indicates directional movement — heading toward the coast. 'Vers' doesn't promise arrival, just direction. In maritime context: 'naviguer vers' (sail toward), 'se diriger vers' (head toward). Compare: 'aller à la côte' would imply reaching the destination. 'Vers' maintains ambiguity about completion — the boat is heading that way. This subtlety is useful in narratives where the journey matters more than the arrival.

items.question: La balle est passée _____ le mur.
items.translation: (The ball went over the wall.)
items.explanation: 'Par-dessus' means 'over (arcing above and past).' 'Par-dessus le mur' = over the wall (the ball cleared it). Crucial distinction: 'au-dessus de' = hovering above (no crossing), 'par-dessus' = going over and past (trajectory). Similarly: 'par-dessous' (going under and past). 'Sauter par-dessus la clôture' (jump over the fence). 'Par-dessus' implies a complete arc from one side to the other, while 'au-dessus de' is static or sustained elevation. This precision is characteristically French.

items.question: Ils sont entrés _____ le bâtiment.
items.translation: (They entered the building.)
items.explanation: 'Entrer dans' (to enter/go into) uses 'dans' to indicate movement into an enclosed space. 'Dans le bâtiment' = into the building. This is one of the most common movement + preposition combinations. Note: 'entrer dans' (go in), 'sortir de' (go out) form a natural pair. Be careful: 'entrer en' exists but only in fixed expressions like 'entrer en guerre' (enter into war), 'entrer en vigueur' (come into effect). For physical spaces, 'dans' is always correct.

items.question: Le train passe _____ un tunnel.
items.translation: (The train passes through a tunnel.)
items.explanation: 'À travers un tunnel' emphasizes crossing the full length of the tunnel — in one side and out the other. 'Par un tunnel' would also work, emphasizing the tunnel as a route. The nuance: 'à travers' highlights the crossing experience, 'par' highlights the route choice. Both are correct here, but 'à travers' is more vivid and descriptive. French offers multiple prepositions for 'through,' each with slightly different emphasis, giving speakers fine-grained control over expressing movement.

name: Prépositions de Mouvement (Prepositions of Movement)
description: Express direction and movement using à, en, au, aux, vers, and country-specific rules

title: Compound Prepositions

items.question: Le supermarché est _____ la banque.
items.translation: (The supermarket is next to the bank.)
items.explanation: 'À côté de' (next to/beside) is a three-word compound preposition. With contraction: 'à côté du cinéma' (de + le → du), 'à côté des magasins' (de + les → des). Without contraction: 'à côté de la banque' (de + la stays). These contractions follow standard French rules and apply to ALL compound prepositions ending in 'de.' Mastering contractions with compound prepositions is essential — they appear constantly in directions, descriptions, and everyday location talk.

items.question: L'hôtel est _____ la gare.
items.translation: (The hotel is opposite/across from the station.)
items.explanation: 'En face de' means 'opposite/facing/across from.' 'En face de la gare' = across from the station. This preposition implies direct visual alignment — you can see one from the other. Compare: 'à côté de' (next to — side by side), 'en face de' (facing — across from). In directions: 'L'arrêt de bus est en face de la pharmacie.' Both are essential for giving clear French directions and represent spatial relationships English expresses with different words.

items.question: Il y a une fontaine _____ la place.
items.translation: (There is a fountain in the middle of the square.)
items.explanation: 'Au milieu de' means 'in the middle of.' 'Au milieu de la place' = in the center of the square. This prepositional phrase is formed with 'au' (à + le) + noun + 'de.' Other similar compound prepositions: 'au bout de' (at the end of), 'au fond de' (at the back/bottom of), 'au bord de' (at the edge of). These 'au + noun + de' constructions form a productive pattern in French, creating precise spatial descriptions absent from simpler preposition systems.

items.question: La pharmacie se trouve _____ la rue.
items.translation: (The pharmacy is at the end of the street.)
items.explanation: 'Au bout de' means 'at the end of/at the tip of.' 'Au bout de la rue' = at the end of the street. This differs from 'à la fin de' which is temporal (at the end of a period). 'Au bout' implies a physical terminus: 'au bout du couloir' (at the end of the corridor), 'au bout du monde' (at the end of the world — an expression meaning very far away). Spatial 'au bout de' vs. temporal 'à la fin de' is a distinction French makes clearly.

items.question: Les toilettes sont _____ le couloir.
items.translation: (The restrooms are at the back of the corridor.)
items.explanation: 'Au fond de' means 'at the back of/at the bottom of/deep inside.' 'Au fond du couloir' = at the far end (inside). Subtle difference: 'au bout' = at the terminus/tip (viewed from outside), 'au fond' = at the deepest point (viewed from inside). 'Au fond de la mer' (at the bottom of the sea), 'au fond du jardin' (at the back of the garden). This 'depth' preposition adds spatial nuance that enriches descriptions beyond simple 'at the end.'

items.question: Sa maison est _____ la mer.
items.translation: (His house is near the sea.)
items.explanation: 'Au bord de' means 'at the edge of/on the shore of/by.' 'Au bord de la mer' = by the sea/at the seaside. This implies being right at the boundary or edge. 'Au bord de la route' (by the roadside), 'au bord du lac' (by the lake), 'au bord des larmes' (on the verge of tears — figurative). This elegant preposition works for physical edges and figurative boundaries alike, making it versatile for both descriptive writing and everyday conversation.

items.question: Le magasin est _____ la rue.
items.translation: (The store is on the corner of the street.)
items.explanation: 'Au coin de' means 'on the corner of/at the corner of.' 'Au coin de la rue' = on the street corner. This is essential for giving directions: 'Tournez au coin' (turn at the corner). The word 'coin' also means 'corner' in general: 'le coin de la table' (the corner of the table), 'un petit coin tranquille' (a quiet little spot). 'Au coin du feu' is a lovely expression meaning 'by the fireside.' French compound prepositions often carry both practical and poetic weight.

items.question: Il y a un balcon _____ l'immeuble.
items.translation: (There is a balcony at the top of the building.)
items.explanation: 'En haut de' means 'at the top of' — the vertical counterpart to 'au fond de.' 'En haut de l'immeuble' = at the top of the building. Its opposite is 'en bas de' (at the bottom of). Note the structure uses 'en' rather than 'au': 'en haut' (up/upstairs), 'en bas' (down/downstairs). These can be used alone as adverbs: 'Monte en haut' (Go upstairs), 'Descends en bas' (Go downstairs). The en haut/en bas pair covers vertical spatial orientation completely.

items.question: _____ ses efforts, il n'a pas réussi.
items.translation: (Despite his efforts, he didn't succeed.)
items.explanation: 'Malgré' means 'despite/in spite of.' Unlike most compound prepositions, 'malgré' is a single word and takes no 'de' — just 'malgré + noun.' 'Malgré ses efforts' (despite his efforts), 'malgré la pluie' (despite the rain), 'malgré tout' (despite everything). It expresses concession — an unexpected outcome given circumstances. Its formal equivalent 'en dépit de' is used in writing. 'Malgré' is one of French's most useful connectors for expressing that something happened contrary to expectations.

items.question: Elle a réussi _____ sa persévérance.
items.translation: (She succeeded thanks to her perseverance.)
items.explanation: 'Grâce à' means 'thanks to' — always positive causation. 'Grâce à sa persévérance' = thanks to her perseverance. Compare: 'à cause de' (because of — negative), 'en raison de' (due to — neutral/formal). 'Grâce à toi' (thanks to you — something good happened), 'à cause de toi' (because of you — something bad happened). This three-way distinction (grâce à/à cause de/en raison de) for causation is uniquely French and reveals the speaker's judgment about whether the cause was positive, negative, or neutral.

items.question: L'accident est arrivé _____ verglas.
items.translation: (The accident happened because of black ice.)
items.explanation: 'À cause de' means 'because of' with negative connotation. 'À cause du verglas' — 'du' is the contraction of de + le (verglas is masculine). This preposition assigns blame or identifies a negative cause. You would never say 'grâce au verglas' unless being ironic, since 'grâce à' is positive. 'À cause de la tempête' (because of the storm), 'à cause du bruit' (because of the noise). The mandatory de-contractions (du/de la/des/de l') apply here as with all compound prepositions.

items.question: La réunion est reportée _____ la grève.
items.translation: (The meeting is postponed due to the strike.)
items.explanation: 'En raison de' means 'due to/owing to' — neutral and formal. 'En raison de la grève' = due to the strike. This is preferred in official contexts: announcements, formal letters, news. It doesn't carry the negative judgment of 'à cause de.' Compare: 'Le vol est annulé en raison du mauvais temps' (formal/neutral) vs. 'à cause du mauvais temps' (blaming the weather). Administrative French heavily favors 'en raison de' for its objective tone. Learning this elevates your French register immediately.

items.question: _____ aller au cinéma, nous sommes restés à la maison.
items.translation: (Instead of going to the cinema, we stayed home.)
items.explanation: 'Au lieu de' means 'instead of.' 'Au lieu d'aller' = instead of going. Before a vowel, 'de' becomes 'd'' (elision). This compound preposition takes a noun or infinitive: 'au lieu du cinéma' (instead of the cinema), 'au lieu de travailler' (instead of working). It expresses substitution — one action replacing another. The expression 'avoir lieu' (to take place) uses 'lieu' differently: 'La réunion a lieu à 15h.' 'Au lieu de' is essential for expressing alternatives and counter-choices.

items.question: Nous avons marché _____ trois heures.
items.translation: (We walked for about three hours.)
items.explanation: 'Pendant environ' combines the time preposition 'pendant' (for/during) with the approximation adverb 'environ' (about/approximately). 'Pendant environ trois heures' = for approximately three hours. Other approximation tools: 'à peu près' (more or less), 'autour de' (around — also used figuratively for approximate numbers: 'autour de 50 personnes'). French has rich vocabulary for hedging precision, useful when exact figures aren't known. 'Environ' is the most standard approximation word.

items.question: _____ moi, ce projet est excellent.
items.translation: (In my opinion, this project is excellent.)
items.explanation: 'Selon' means 'according to/in the opinion of.' 'Selon moi' = in my opinion. Alternatives: 'd'après moi' (in my view — slightly less formal), 'à mon avis' (in my opinion — most common in conversation), 'pour moi' (for me — casual). All express personal perspective. 'Selon' is also used for sources: 'selon le journal' (according to the newspaper), 'selon les experts' (according to experts). Having multiple ways to attribute opinions makes French argumentation nuanced and precise.

name: Prépositions Composées (Compound Prepositions)
description: Master complex prepositions like à côté de, en face de, au-dessus de, and grâce à

title: Coordinating Conjunctions

items.question: Je veux un café _____ un croissant.
items.translation: (I want a coffee and a croissant.)
items.explanation: 'Et' means 'and' — the most fundamental French conjunction. Like English 'and,' it joins words, phrases, and clauses. 'Café et croissant' — clean and simple. French coordinating conjunctions are memorized with the mnemonic 'Mais Où Est Donc Ornicar?' (mais, ou, et, donc, or, ni, car). Unlike English, French never uses a comma before 'et' in a list (no Oxford comma). 'Et' is also never contracted or modified — it stays 'et' in all contexts, making it wonderfully predictable.

items.question: Tu veux du thé _____ du café ?
items.translation: (Do you want tea or coffee?)
items.explanation: 'Ou' means 'or' — presenting alternatives. 'Thé ou café' = tea or coffee. Critical distinction: 'ou' (or — no accent) vs. 'où' (where — with accent on ù). They sound identical but function completely differently. 'Ou' offers choice: 'Fromage ou dessert ?' In exclusive contexts: 'C'est lui ou moi' (It's him or me). 'Ou bien' adds emphasis: 'Tu viens ou bien tu restes ?' (Are you coming or are you staying?). This ou/où homophone pair is a classic French learning challenge.

items.question: J'aime le chocolat, _____ je ne peux pas en manger.
items.translation: (I like chocolate, but I can't eat any.)
items.explanation: 'Mais' means 'but' — introducing contrast or opposition. 'J'aime le chocolat, mais...' sets up contradiction. 'Mais' is always preceded by a comma in writing. It can start a sentence for emphasis: 'Mais c'est impossible !' (But that's impossible!). The expression 'non mais !' expresses indignation. 'Mais' is one of the seven coordinating conjunctions (mais, ou, et, donc, or, ni, car) and is the primary French tool for expressing contrast alongside 'cependant' and 'pourtant.'

items.question: Il pleut, _____ je prends mon parapluie.
items.translation: (It's raining, so/therefore I'm taking my umbrella.)
items.explanation: 'Donc' means 'so/therefore' — expressing consequence or conclusion. 'Il pleut, donc je prends mon parapluie' — cause leads to effect. 'Donc' is extremely common in spoken French, often used as a filler: 'Donc, qu'est-ce qu'on fait ?' (So, what do we do?). In formal logic: 'Je pense, donc je suis' (I think, therefore I am — Descartes). 'Donc' can appear mid-sentence or at the start, making it versatile for connecting ideas in both conversation and writing.

items.question: Il ne boit _____ café _____ thé.
items.translation: (He drinks neither coffee nor tea.)
items.explanation: 'Ni... ni...' means 'neither... nor...' — correlative negation. 'Il ne boit ni café ni thé' — notice the 'ne' before the verb and NO articles after 'ni.' This is crucial: 'ni café ni thé' (not *'ni du café ni du thé'). The articles disappear. In longer lists: 'ni café, ni thé, ni jus.' 'Ni' can start a sentence: 'Ni Pierre ni Marie ne sont venus' (Neither Pierre nor Marie came). The verb after double-subject 'ni...ni' takes plural. This construction is elegant and precise.

items.question: Je reste à la maison, _____ je suis malade.
items.translation: (I'm staying home, for/because I am sick.)
items.explanation: 'Car' means 'for/because' — a formal conjunction introducing a reason. 'Car je suis malade' explains the cause. 'Car' is more literary than 'parce que' (because) — they mean the same thing but differ in register. 'Car' always connects two independent clauses and is preceded by a comma. You cannot start a sentence with 'car' (unlike 'parce que'). In writing: 'Il a échoué, car il n'avait pas étudié.' Using 'car' elevates your French to a more polished, written register.

items.question: _____, il a changé d'avis.
items.translation: (However/Now, he changed his mind.)
items.explanation: 'Or' is a formal conjunction meaning 'however/now/but' — introducing a new, often contrasting element in reasoning. 'Or, il a changé d'avis' signals a turn in the argument. This is the least known of the seven coordinating conjunctions but appears frequently in formal writing and argumentation. 'Or' always starts a new clause or sentence. Don't confuse conjunction 'or' with English 'or' — they're false friends. French 'or' ~ 'now/however,' English 'or' ~ French 'ou.' This subtle word elevates academic French immediately.

items.question: Il fait froid _____ il pleut.
items.translation: (It's cold and it's raining.)
items.explanation: 'Et' connecting two clauses of equal weight — simple coordination. 'Il fait froid et il pleut' — both facts presented equally. When three or more elements are listed, 'et' appears only before the last: 'froid, venteux et pluvieux.' Unlike English, no comma before 'et' in standard French lists. For emphasis or dramatic effect, 'et' can appear before each element: 'et froid, et venteux, et pluvieux' — this adds rhetorical weight. Polysyndeton (repeated 'et') is a literary device used effectively in French.

items.question: Dépêche-toi, _____ tu vas être en retard.
items.translation: (Hurry up, or else you'll be late.)
items.explanation: 'Sinon' means 'otherwise/or else' — introducing a negative consequence. 'Dépêche-toi, sinon tu vas être en retard.' While not one of the seven traditional coordinating conjunctions, 'sinon' is essential for expressing warnings and conditions. 'Sinon' = 'si + non' (if not). Compare: 'ou' presents neutral alternatives, 'sinon' presents undesirable alternatives as warnings. 'Travaille bien, sinon tu échoueras' (Work hard, otherwise you'll fail). This word adds urgency that plain 'ou' cannot convey.

items.question: Il est intelligent _____ paresseux.
items.translation: (He is intelligent but lazy.)
items.explanation: 'Mais' between adjectives creates a contrast. 'Intelligent mais paresseux' — acknowledging a quality while introducing a flaw. This structure is common in character descriptions. Note that 'mais' can connect words (adj + mais + adj), phrases, or full clauses. Stronger contrast words exist: 'cependant' (however), 'pourtant' (yet), 'néanmoins' (nevertheless). But 'mais' is the everyday go-to for any opposition. Its simplicity and versatility make it the most frequently used contrasting connector in spoken French.

items.question: Il n'est _____ riche _____ célèbre.
items.translation: (He is neither rich nor famous.)
items.explanation: 'Ni... ni...' with adjectives works the same way as with nouns — double negation. 'Il n'est ni riche ni célèbre.' The 'ne' before the verb is required in writing (often dropped in casual speech). With adjectives, there's no article issue. You can chain more: 'ni riche, ni célèbre, ni intelligent.' In formal style: 'Il n'est ni l'un ni l'autre' (He is neither one nor the other). This construction replaces awkward 'pas... et pas...' patterns — it's more elegant and natural in French.

items.question: Tu es fatigué ? _____ va te coucher.
items.translation: (You're tired? Then go to bed.)
items.explanation: 'Alors' means 'then/so' — drawing a practical consequence. 'Alors va te coucher' = then go to bed. While 'donc' is more formal/logical ('therefore'), 'alors' is conversational and practical ('then/so'). 'Alors' is one of the most common French words in everyday speech. Other uses: 'alors que' (while/whereas — subordinating), 'ça alors !' (well, well! — exclamation), 'et alors ?' (so what?). The word shifts meaning by context: consequence, surprise, simultaneous contrast.

items.question: J'ai cherché partout, _____ je ne l'ai pas trouvé.
items.translation: (I searched everywhere, yet I didn't find it.)
items.explanation: 'Pourtant' means 'yet/however/nevertheless' — expressing unexpected contrast. 'J'ai cherché partout, pourtant je ne l'ai pas trouvé' — the outcome contradicts the effort. 'Pourtant' is stronger than 'mais' and appears in mid-sentence or at the start: 'Pourtant, il avait promis.' Synonyms by register: 'cependant' (formal), 'toutefois' (very formal), 'néanmoins' (literary). In everyday speech, 'pourtant' strikes the right balance — it's emphatic without being overly formal. It signals surprise at an unexpected result.

items.question: Je ne mange pas de viande, _____ je mange du poisson.
items.translation: (I don't eat meat; however, I eat fish.)
items.explanation: 'En revanche' means 'on the other hand/in contrast' — introducing a compensating fact. It's positive contrast: 'I don't do X, but I do Y instead.' Compare: 'par contre' (informal equivalent — some purists reject it, but it's widely used), 'en revanche' (formally correct). The distinction: 'mais' is general contrast, 'en revanche' specifically compensates — one negative balanced by a positive. This nuanced connector elevates discourse and shows sophisticated command of French argumentation vocabulary.

items.question: L'hôtel est cher, _____ il est très bien situé.
items.translation: (The hotel is expensive; however, it is very well located.)
items.explanation: 'Cependant' means 'however/nevertheless' — formal concession. 'L'hôtel est cher, cependant il est bien situé' acknowledges a drawback then introduces a redeeming quality. The hierarchy: 'mais' (everyday), 'pourtant' (emphatic), 'cependant' (formal), 'néanmoins' (literary), 'toutefois' (academic). All mean approximately 'however/but,' and having several at your disposal allows register-appropriate expression. Using 'cependant' in writing immediately signals a competent French user comfortable with formal discourse.

name: Conjonctions de Coordination (Coordinating Conjunctions)
description: Connect independent clauses using mais, ou, et, donc, or, ni, car (MAIS OÙ EST DONC ORNICAR)

title: Subordinating Conjunctions

items.question: Je reste à la maison _____ il pleut.
items.translation: (I'm staying home because it's raining.)
items.explanation: 'Parce que' means 'because' and introduces a direct cause. Before vowels: 'parce qu'il' (elision). 'Parce que' answers 'pourquoi ?' (why?). Compare: 'car' (similar but coordinating, more formal, never starts a sentence), 'puisque' (since — acknowledged cause), 'comme' (as/since — beginning of sentence). 'Parce que' is the default 'because' in French — use it freely in both speech and writing. Its variants add nuance: 'puisque tu le sais...' (since you already know...).

items.question: _____ tu le sais, pourquoi tu demandes ?
items.translation: (Since you know it, why are you asking?)
items.explanation: 'Puisque' means 'since/given that' — the cause is already known or obvious to both speakers. 'Puisque tu le sais' implies 'we both know this fact.' Key difference: 'parce que' introduces new information about cause, 'puisque' references shared knowledge. 'Puisque c'est comme ça, je m'en vais' (Since that's how it is, I'm leaving) — the situation is already established. 'Puisque' often carries slight impatience or emphasis — 'I'm stating what's obvious.' This subtlety has no English equivalent.

items.question: Je lis _____ j'attends le bus.
items.translation: (I read while I wait for the bus.)
items.explanation: 'Pendant que' means 'while/during the time that' — two simultaneous actions. 'Pendant que j'attends' = while I wait. Uses indicative mood (not subjunctive). 'Tandis que' is a synonym that can also mean 'whereas' (contrast): 'Il dort tandis que je travaille' — either simultaneous or contrasting. 'Alors que' can also mean 'while/whereas.' These three overlapping conjunctions (pendant que, tandis que, alors que) let French express simultaneity with varying degrees of contrast embedded.

items.question: Ferme la porte _____ tu partes.
items.translation: (Close the door before you leave.)
items.explanation: 'Avant que' means 'before' and REQUIRES the subjunctive. 'Avant que tu partes' (subjunctive of 'partir'). This is mandatory — even in casual speech, the subjunctive follows 'avant que.' Compare: 'après que' technically takes the indicative (though subjunctive is increasingly used in modern French). The avant que/après que asymmetry surprises learners: before = subjunctive, after = indicative (in strict grammar). This subjunctive requirement makes 'avant que' one of the most tested grammar points in French exams.

items.question: _____ il ait de l'argent, il n'est pas heureux.
items.translation: (Although he has money, he is not happy.)
items.explanation: 'Bien que' means 'although/even though' and REQUIRES the subjunctive. 'Bien qu'il ait' — 'ait' is the subjunctive of 'avoir.' This conjunction expresses concession: an unexpected situation despite circumstances. Synonyms: 'quoique' (interchangeable, also + subjunctive), 'même si' (+ indicative — easier to use). Many learners default to 'même si' to avoid the subjunctive, but 'bien que' is essential for formal writing and shows grammatical sophistication. Practice forming subjunctive after 'bien que' until automatic.

items.question: Je t'appelle _____ j'arrive.
items.translation: (I'll call you when I arrive.)
items.explanation: 'Quand' means 'when' as a conjunction. 'Quand j'arrive' — uses present tense for future events, same as English. But in sentences with future meaning in both clauses, French uses future in both: 'Quand tu arriveras, je serai là' (When you arrive, I will be there) — future tense after 'quand' where English uses present. This is a major difference. The quand + future rule catches anglophone learners who translate word-for-word. French says 'when you WILL arrive.'

items.question: Je sortirai _____ il ne pleut pas.
items.translation: (I'll go out if it doesn't rain.)
items.explanation: 'Si' means 'if' and follows strict tense rules. 'Si + present → future main clause': 'Si il ne pleut pas, je sortirai.' Critical: 'si' + 'il' contracts to 's'il' (but 'si elle' does NOT contract). NEVER use future or conditional after 'si' — this is an unbreakable rule. 'Si j'avais...' (imperfect, not conditional). English allows 'if I would...' informally, but French absolutely forbids future/conditional after 'si.' This si-tense restriction is the most tested conditional grammar point.

items.question: Je continuerai _____ tu me le demandes.
items.translation: (I will continue as long as you ask me to.)
items.explanation: 'Tant que' means 'as long as/so long as' — expressing duration dependent on a condition. 'Tant que tu me le demandes' — the action continues while the condition holds. Uses indicative. Compare: 'aussi longtemps que' (as long as — more formal). In negative contexts: 'Je n'irai pas tant que tu n'auras pas fini' (I won't go as long as you haven't finished). 'Tant que' creates a temporal condition — the main action persists while the subordinate condition remains true. Extremely useful for expressing ongoing conditional situations.

items.question: Je te prête mon livre _____ tu me le rendes demain.
items.translation: (I'll lend you my book provided that you return it tomorrow.)
items.explanation: 'À condition que' means 'provided that/on the condition that' and REQUIRES the subjunctive. 'Que tu me le rendes' — subjunctive of 'rendre.' This conjunction sets a strict prerequisite. Alternatives: 'pourvu que' (provided that — also + subjunctive), 'à moins que' (unless — also + subjunctive). All three express conditional requirements with mandatory subjunctive. 'À condition de' + infinitive when the subject is the same: 'à condition de travailler dur' (provided one works hard — same subject).

items.question: Il parle fort _____ tout le monde l'entende.
items.translation: (He speaks loudly so that everyone hears him.)
items.explanation: 'Pour que' means 'so that/in order that' and REQUIRES the subjunctive. 'Pour que tout le monde entende' — subjunctive expressing purpose. When subjects are the same, use 'pour' + infinitive instead: 'Il parle fort pour être entendu' (He speaks loudly to be heard). This pour que/pour alternation based on same/different subjects is critical. Similar: 'afin que' (so that — formal, + subjunctive), 'afin de' (in order to — formal, same subject). Purpose conjunctions consistently demand the subjunctive in French.

items.question: _____ tu viennes ou non, la fête aura lieu.
items.translation: (Whether you come or not, the party will take place.)
items.explanation: 'Que... ou non' means 'whether... or not' and takes the subjunctive. 'Que tu viennes ou non' — the outcome doesn't depend on the condition. This structure shows indifference to the stated possibility. Compare 'si' (if — hypothetical condition) vs. 'que' in this construction (the outcome is certain regardless). 'Que la situation soit bonne ou mauvaise' (whether the situation is good or bad). This que + subjunctive for 'whether' is a more advanced construction but appears regularly in native French speech.

items.question: Il est parti _____ personne ne le voie.
items.translation: (He left without anyone seeing him.)
items.explanation: 'Sans que' means 'without (someone doing something)' and REQUIRES the subjunctive. 'Sans que personne ne le voie' — the subjunctive 'voie' from 'voir.' Note the expletive 'ne' — after 'sans que,' a 'ne' often appears without negating (it's stylistic, especially in formal writing). When subjects match: 'sans' + infinitive instead: 'Il est parti sans dire au revoir' (He left without saying goodbye). The sans que + subjunctive pattern elegantly expresses actions occurring without someone's awareness or participation.

items.question: Il a réussi _____ il était très motivé.
items.translation: (He succeeded because he was very motivated.)
items.explanation: 'Comme' at the beginning of a sentence means 'as/since' — introducing a known cause. 'Comme il était très motivé' = since/as he was very motivated. 'Comme' in this causal sense MUST start the sentence — it cannot appear mid-sentence (use 'parce que' or 'car' instead). 'Comme' also means 'like/as' in comparisons: 'blanc comme neige' (white as snow). And 'how' in exclamations: 'Comme c'est beau !' (How beautiful!). Context determines which meaning — causal, comparative, or exclamatory.

items.question: Je te dirai la vérité _____ tu me promettes de ne rien dire.
items.translation: (I'll tell you the truth provided you promise not to say anything.)
items.explanation: 'Pourvu que' means 'provided that/as long as' and REQUIRES the subjunctive. 'Pourvu que tu me promettes' — subjunctive of 'promettre.' Also used as a wish: 'Pourvu qu'il fasse beau demain !' (Let's hope it's nice tomorrow! / If only the weather is good!). This dual function — condition and wish — makes 'pourvu que' distinctive. In its wish function, it stands alone as a sentence expressing hope for a desired outcome. This versatile subjunctive conjunction is a hallmark of expressive French.

items.question: Il travaille dur _____ il puisse réussir.
items.translation: (He works hard so that he can succeed.)
items.explanation: 'Afin que' means 'so that/in order that' — formal equivalent of 'pour que.' Both require the subjunctive and express purpose. 'Afin qu'il puisse' — subjunctive of 'pouvoir.' The formal register hierarchy: 'pour que' (standard) → 'afin que' (formal). Same-subject alternative: 'afin de' + infinitive. 'Il travaille dur afin de réussir' (same structure as pour/pour que). Purpose conjunctions in French form a clean system: different-subject uses que + subjunctive, same-subject uses de + infinitive.

name: Conjonctions de Subordination (Subordinating Conjunctions)
description: Build complex sentences with que, quand, comme, si, puisque, and bien que

title: Cause & Consequence Links

items.question: Il est fatigué _____ il a trop travaillé.
items.translation: (He is tired because he worked too much.)
items.explanation: 'Parce que' is the default all-purpose 'because.' 'Parce qu'il a trop travaillé' — answers 'pourquoi est-il fatigué ?' From a discourse standpoint, French distinguishes: 'parce que' (factual cause), 'puisque' (assumed/known cause), 'comme' (sentence-initial cause), 'car' (literary connective cause). Each positions the cause differently in the argument. Learners should default to 'parce que' and gradually add the others for stylistic variety. It uses indicative mode and carries no register restrictions.

items.question: Il a trop mangé, _____ il a mal au ventre.
items.translation: (He ate too much, so he has a stomachache.)
items.explanation: 'C'est pourquoi' means 'that is why/therefore' — explicitly stating the consequence. 'C'est pourquoi il a mal au ventre' presents the result as a logical conclusion. Alternatives by register: 'donc' (standard), 'alors' (conversational), 'c'est pourquoi' (explicit/formal), 'par conséquent' (very formal), 'de ce fait' (administrative). Having multiple consequence connectors lets you match the register: use 'donc' in speech, 'par conséquent' in essays, 'c'est pourquoi' for clear explicit reasoning.

items.question: _____ la grève, les trains ne circulent pas.
items.translation: (Because of the strike, the trains are not running.)
items.explanation: 'À cause de' + noun means 'because of' with negative connotation. 'À cause de la grève' = because of the strike (bad consequence). This preposition phrases cause as a complaint or problem. Remember the trio: 'à cause de' (negative), 'grâce à' (positive), 'en raison de' (neutral/formal). You would say 'Grâce à la grève, j'ai pu dormir' if treating the strike as positive for you personally. Choosing the right one reveals your attitude. This three-option system has no English equivalent.

items.question: Il a été puni _____ avoir menti.
items.translation: (He was punished for having lied.)
items.explanation: 'Pour' + infinitive past expresses the cause of a punishment or consequence. 'Pour avoir menti' = for having lied. This structure uses 'pour' + past infinitive (avoir/être + past participle). 'Condamné pour avoir volé' (convicted for stealing), 'récompensé pour avoir sauvé' (rewarded for saving). When the cause is a noun: 'puni pour son mensonge' (punished for his lie). The pour + past infinitive construction elegantly explains why consequences occurred, bridging cause and effect in a compact structure.

items.question: Il travaille beaucoup ; _____, il réussit bien.
items.translation: (He works a lot; consequently, he does well.)
items.explanation: 'Par conséquent' means 'consequently/as a result' — formal consequence connector. Used in writing, reports, and formal speech. It usually follows a semicolon or starts a new sentence. Alternatives in descending formality: 'par conséquent' → 'en conséquence' → 'c'est pourquoi' → 'donc' → 'alors' → 'du coup' (very informal, colloquial). Knowing this register scale lets you calibrate your French precisely. 'Du coup' is the most trendy/casual, while 'par conséquent' is the most scholarly.

items.question: Il est si fatigué _____ il ne peut plus marcher.
items.translation: (He is so tired that he can no longer walk.)
items.explanation: 'Si... que' is a consequence structure meaning 'so... that.' 'Si fatigué qu'il ne peut plus' — the degree triggers a consequence. Similar patterns: 'tellement... que' (so much... that), 'tant... que' (so much... that — with nouns). These three overlap: 'si' + adjective/adverb, 'tellement' + adjective/adverb/verb, 'tant' + noun. 'Il a tellement mangé qu'il a mal' (He ate so much that he hurts). 'Tant de problèmes que...' (So many problems that...). These degree-consequence links are common in French.

items.question: _____ à son courage, les enfants ont été sauvés.
items.translation: (Thanks to his courage, the children were saved.)
items.explanation: 'Grâce à' means 'thanks to' — positive causation. 'Grâce à son courage' — the courage led to a good outcome. Remember: never use 'grâce à' for negative results — that would be ironic or sarcastic. The structure: 'grâce à' + noun/pronoun (grâce à toi, grâce à lui). For negative causes, switch to 'à cause de.' For neutral/official: 'en raison de.' This word choice itself communicates your judgment, making it a subtle but powerful rhetorical tool in French discourse.

items.question: Le voyage a été annulé. _____, nous sommes restés chez nous.
items.translation: (The trip was canceled. Therefore, we stayed home.)
items.explanation: 'De ce fait' means 'as a result/because of this' — formal consequence. Like 'par conséquent' but referenced to a specific prior fact. Used in academic and professional writing. In a meeting: 'Les ventes ont baissé. De ce fait, nous devons réduire les coûts.' This connector explicitly binds a specified cause to its consequence. Compare: 'donc' is general, 'de ce fait' explicitly references 'ce fait' (this fact). This specificity makes argumentation more precise and traceable in formal French contexts.

items.question: Étant donné _____ la situation est grave, nous devons agir.
items.translation: (Given that the situation is serious, we must act.)
items.explanation: 'Étant donné que' means 'given that/considering that' — a formal causal conjunction. 'Étant donné que la situation est grave' — the cause is presented as an established fact. Uses indicative. 'Étant donné' alone works as a preposition: 'Étant donné les circonstances' (given the circumstances — no 'que' needed before nouns). Similar expressions: 'vu que' (seeing that), 'du fait que' (due to the fact that). These formal cause connectors are essential in essays, reports, and argumentative writing.

items.question: Il a tellement de travail _____ il n'a pas le temps de dormir.
items.translation: (He has so much work that he doesn't have time to sleep.)
items.explanation: 'Tellement de... que' means 'so much/many... that.' 'Tellement de travail qu'il...' — the excessive quantity causes a consequence. 'Tellement' is the most versatile intensity word: with adjectives ('tellement fatigué'), verbs ('il court tellement'), and nouns ('tellement de travail'). 'Si' only works with adjectives/adverbs, 'tant' is more literary. 'Tellement' covers all contexts, making it the safest choice for expressing degree-consequence relationships in everyday French.

items.question: Il n'a pas étudié ; _____, il a échoué à l'examen.
items.translation: (He didn't study; as a result, he failed the exam.)
items.explanation: 'Du coup' is an extremely common informal French expression meaning 'so/as a result/because of that.' 'Du coup, il a échoué' — casual consequence. While some prescriptivists disapprove of 'du coup' as overused, it dominates colloquial French. It's the go-to casual consequence marker among younger speakers. Register equivalent: 'du coup' (casual) = 'donc' (standard) = 'par conséquent' (formal). Using 'du coup' in conversation marks you as comfortable with everyday French. Avoid it in formal essays.

items.question: L'autoroute est bloquée ; _____, prenons un autre chemin.
items.translation: (The highway is blocked; therefore, let's take another road.)
items.explanation: 'En conséquence' means 'consequently/as a consequence' — formal register. Nearly interchangeable with 'par conséquent,' but slightly more administrative. Used in official announcements: 'La route est fermée. En conséquence, les bus sont déviés.' (The road is closed. Consequently, buses are diverted.) The consequence connector hierarchy: 'du coup' (very informal) → 'alors/donc' (standard) → 'c'est pourquoi' (explicit) → 'par conséquent/en conséquence' (formal). Knowing this spectrum helps match register to context.

items.question: _____ il fait beau, nous irons à la plage.
items.translation: (As/Since it's nice out, we'll go to the beach.)
items.explanation: 'Comme' at the start of a sentence means 'as/since' — presenting an obvious or established cause. 'Comme il fait beau, nous irons...' — the nice weather is a known fact, not new information. Critical rule: causal 'comme' MUST begin the sentence. You cannot say *'nous irons à la plage comme il fait beau' with causal meaning (that would mean 'as if'). This sentence-initial position requirement distinguishes French causal 'comme' from its other meanings (like, as if, how).

items.question: Il pleuvait trop ; _____, le match a été reporté.
items.translation: (It was raining too much; accordingly, the match was postponed.)
items.explanation: 'De sorte que' means 'so that/with the result that.' With indicative, it expresses actual consequence: 'de sorte que le match a été reporté' (= it was postponed — fact). With subjunctive, it means purpose: 'de sorte qu'il puisse jouer' (= so that he can play — intent). This double function (consequence with indicative vs. purpose with subjunctive) is a sophisticated French grammar point. The mood of the verb after 'de sorte que' completely changes the sentence's meaning.

items.question: Vu _____ tu es malade, reste au lit.
items.translation: (Seeing that you are sick, stay in bed.)
items.explanation: 'Vu que' means 'seeing that/given that' — a semi-formal causal conjunction. 'Vu que tu es malade' = given that you're sick. Uses indicative. 'Vu' alone works as a preposition before nouns: 'vu les circonstances' (given the circumstances). Similar: 'étant donné que,' 'attendu que' (legal French). The 'vu que' construct is versatile — slightly more casual than 'étant donné que' but more precise than 'parce que.' It frames the cause as a visual observation, making the reasoning feel evidence-based and concrete.

name: Liens de Cause et Conséquence (Cause & Consequence)
description: Express cause and consequence using parce que, car, puisque, donc, and c'est pourquoi

title: Contrast & Opposition Links

items.question: Il est riche, _____ il n'est pas heureux.
items.translation: (He is rich, yet he is not happy.)
items.explanation: 'Pourtant' means 'yet/nevertheless/however' — strong unexpected contrast. 'Il est riche, pourtant il n'est pas heureux' — wealth didn't produce the expected happiness. 'Pourtant' is more emphatic than 'mais' and highlights surprise at the contradiction. It can start a sentence: 'Pourtant, j'avais tout prévu' (And yet, I had planned everything). The word carries emotional weight — a sense of bewilderment or frustration at things not matching expectations. It's the go-to connector when outcomes defy logic.

items.question: Ce restaurant est cher ; _____, la cuisine est excellente.
items.translation: (This restaurant is expensive; however, the food is excellent.)
items.explanation: 'En revanche' means 'on the other hand/in contrast' — balanced opposition presenting a compensating positive. 'Cher, en revanche excellent' — one negative balanced by one positive. Compare: 'par contre' (informal equivalent — widely used despite purist objections). 'En revanche' always follows a negative statement with a positive counterpart, or vice versa. It implies compensation rather than pure contrast. This makes it different from 'cependant' (pure obstacle) or 'pourtant' (surprise contrast).

items.question: _____ il soit intelligent, il fait des erreurs stupides.
items.translation: (Although he is intelligent, he makes stupid mistakes.)
items.explanation: 'Bien que' + subjunctive means 'although/even though.' 'Bien qu'il soit intelligent' — subjunctive is mandatory. This conjunction introduces a concessive clause: acknowledging a fact that would predict the opposite outcome. Similar: 'quoique' (identical meaning and grammar), 'même si' + indicative (easier alternative avoiding subjunctive). In essays: 'Bien que cette théorie soit populaire, elle présente des limites.' Using 'bien que' correctly with subjunctive demonstrates grammatical competence and is essential for DELF B2+ writing.

items.question: Marie travaille beaucoup, _____ Pierre est paresseux.
items.translation: (Marie works a lot, whereas Pierre is lazy.)
items.explanation: 'Tandis que' means 'whereas/while' — expressing contrast between two situations. 'Marie travaille, tandis que Pierre est paresseux' — direct comparison. 'Tandis que' has double meaning: simultaneity (while — doing at the same time) and contrast (whereas — opposing two facts). Context determines which. 'Alors que' functions nearly identically. Both use indicative. The contrast meaning is more common in modern French: 'Le Nord est froid, tandis que le Sud est chaud.' This conjunction is essential for comparative essays.

items.question: Il a promis de venir, _____ il n'est pas là.
items.translation: (He promised to come, yet he's not here.)
items.explanation: 'Or' as a narrative conjunction means 'now/but/however' — introducing a complicating fact or twist. 'Or, il n'est pas là' signals: 'but here's the thing...' This is one of French's seven coordinating conjunctions (mais, ou, et, donc, or, ni, car) and is the most formal. It's common in logical reasoning and storytelling: 'Il devait arriver à 8h. Or, il n'est pas venu.' 'Or' always starts a new clause or sentence. Don't confuse with English 'or' — French 'or' means something completely different.

items.question: Je comprends son problème ; _____, je ne peux pas l'aider.
items.translation: (I understand his problem; nevertheless, I cannot help him.)
items.explanation: 'Néanmoins' means 'nevertheless/nonetheless' — literary/formal concession. 'Néanmoins, je ne peux pas l'aider' — despite understanding, help is impossible. This is more formal than 'pourtant' and less common in everyday speech. The formality hierarchy: 'mais' (basic) → 'pourtant' (emphatic everyday) → 'cependant' (formal) → 'néanmoins/toutefois' (literary/academic). Using 'néanmoins' in a French essay or professional email instantly elevates the register. It signals deliberate, sophisticated argumentation.

items.question: Tu aimes le sport ; _____, tu ne fais jamais d'exercice.
items.translation: (You like sports; yet, you never exercise.)
items.explanation: 'Cependant' means 'however/nevertheless' — formal opposition. 'Cependant, tu ne fais jamais d'exercice' — the behavior contradicts the claimed preference. 'Cependant' sits at the center of the formality scale — appropriate for both careful speech and writing. It's more neutral than 'pourtant' (which carries surprise) and less literary than 'néanmoins.' In argumentative writing: 'Cette approche est efficace. Cependant, elle présente des risques.' 'Cependant' is the workhorse concession connector for essays and reports.

items.question: _____ ses erreurs passées, on lui a donné une nouvelle chance.
items.translation: (In spite of his past mistakes, he was given a new chance.)
items.explanation: 'En dépit de' means 'in spite of/despite' — formal equivalent of 'malgré.' 'En dépit de ses erreurs' = despite his mistakes. Both take nouns directly (no subjunctive needed). 'En dépit de' is more literary; 'malgré' is all-purpose. For clauses (with a verb), you need 'bien que' + subjunctive or 'même si' + indicative. Preposition vs. conjunction: 'malgré la pluie' (noun — preposition), 'bien qu'il pleuve' (clause — conjunction). Understanding this preposition/conjunction distinction helps structure concessive expressions correctly.

items.question: Paul est grand _____ son frère est petit.
items.translation: (Paul is tall whereas his brother is short.)
items.explanation: 'Alors que' means 'whereas/while' — contrasting two facts. 'Paul est grand, alors que son frère est petit' — direct opposition. Like 'tandis que,' it has double meaning: contrast (whereas) and simultaneity (while). 'Alors que je dormais, il est parti' (while I was sleeping — simultaneous). 'Il est optimiste, alors qu'elle est pessimiste' (whereas — contrast). Both meanings use indicative. 'Alors que' is slightly more common in speech than 'tandis que.' Together they form French's primary contrast conjunction pair.

items.question: _____ si le test est difficile, je vais essayer.
items.translation: (Even if the test is difficult, I will try.)
items.explanation: 'Même si' means 'even if' — concession with hypothesis. 'Même si le test est difficile' uses indicative, unlike 'bien que' which requires subjunctive. This is why many learners prefer 'même si' — it's simpler grammatically. However, 'bien que' carries more formal weight. 'Même si' works for real conditions: 'Même si j'ai peur, je le ferai' (Even if I'm scared — I am scared). For hypothetical: 'Même si j'avais tort, j'essaierais' (Even if I were wrong). 'Même si' covers all concessive ground without subjunctive stress.

items.question: Son idée est intéressante ; _____, elle semble irréalisable.
items.translation: (His idea is interesting; however, it seems unfeasible.)
items.explanation: 'Toutefois' means 'however/nevertheless' — the most formal concession connector. 'Toutefois, elle semble irréalisable' — a measured, academic objection. 'Toutefois' is preferred in legal documents, scientific papers, and high-register journalism. It signals that the writer acknowledges a positive or fact before introducing a qualification. Within the formality scale: 'mais' (casual) → 'pourtant' (everyday) → 'cependant' (formal) → 'toutefois/néanmoins' (very formal/literary). Knowing this hierarchy lets you match tone precisely.

items.question: Le projet est ambitieux. _____, il est tout à fait réalisable.
items.translation: (The project is ambitious. Nevertheless, it is entirely feasible.)
items.explanation: 'Néanmoins' here introduces a rebuttal to an implied concern — ambition doesn't mean infeasibility. The word literally comes from 'nul en moins' (none the less), paralleling English 'nevertheless.' In argumentation, 'néanmoins' acknowledges a potential objection then rebuts it — a powerful rhetorical move. Example pattern: '[Acknowledge problem]. Néanmoins, [rebut problem].' This structure is gold for essays: 'Le coût est élevé. Néanmoins, les bénéfices justifient l'investissement.' Master this and your French argumentation improves dramatically.

items.question: Elle est timide ; _____, elle parle très bien en public.
items.translation: (She is shy; surprisingly, she speaks very well in public.)
items.explanation: 'Pourtant' here highlights the surprise — shyness should prevent good public speaking, yet it doesn't. This emotional undertone differentiates 'pourtant' from neutral connectors like 'cependant.' 'Pourtant' implies: 'this is unexpected given what we know.' In stories: 'Il avait tout pour réussir. Pourtant, il a échoué.' (He had everything going for him. Yet, he failed.) The emotional coloring makes 'pourtant' perfect for narrative twists, personal reflections, and any situation where expectations are defied.

items.question: Le temps est mauvais, _____ nous sortons quand même.
items.translation: (The weather is bad, but we're going out anyway.)
items.explanation: 'Mais' combined with 'quand même' (anyway/still) creates strong opposition. 'Mais nous sortons quand même' — we go out despite the bad weather. 'Quand même' reinforces defiance or persistence: 'Je suis fatigué mais je continue quand même' (I'm tired but I keep going anyway). 'Quand même' can also express surprise: 'C'est quand même bizarre !' (That's pretty strange!). The mais + quand même combination is extremely common in spoken French, expressing a determined 'despite everything' attitude.

items.question: Au lieu _____ regarder la télé, lis un livre.
items.translation: (Instead of watching TV, read a book.)
items.explanation: 'Au lieu de' means 'instead of' — substituting one action for another. 'Au lieu de regarder' — 'de' + infinitive when giving an alternative. This compound preposition contrasts what someone does/should do against what they actually do. 'Au lieu de travailler, il joue.' (Instead of working, he plays.) For nouns: 'au lieu du bus, prends le métro' (instead of the bus, take the metro). 'Au lieu de' structures choices and suggestions, making it invaluable for giving advice, making comparisons, and expressing preferences in French.

name: Liens d'Opposition (Opposition & Contrast)
description: Express contrast and opposition with mais, cependant, pourtant, en revanche, and malgré

title: Addition & Elaboration Links

items.question: Il parle français, et _____ il parle espagnol.
items.translation: (He speaks French, and also he speaks Spanish.)
items.explanation: 'En plus' means 'in addition/moreover/also.' 'Et en plus, il parle espagnol' — adding extra information. In colloquial French, 'en plus' is extremely common: 'En plus, c'est gratuit !' (Plus, it's free!). It can appear at the start, middle, or end of a sentence. More formal equivalents: 'de plus' (moreover), 'en outre' (furthermore), 'par ailleurs' (furthermore — different topic). The register hierarchy: 'en plus' (casual) → 'de plus' (standard) → 'en outre' (formal) → 'qui plus est' (literary).

items.question: Ce restaurant est bon ; _____, les prix sont raisonnables.
items.translation: (This restaurant is good; furthermore, the prices are reasonable.)
items.explanation: 'De plus' means 'moreover/furthermore' — adding a reinforcing argument. 'De plus, les prix sont raisonnables' adds a second positive point. This is the standard formal addition connector, appropriate in both writing and careful speech. It always introduces information that supports the previous statement: good restaurant AND reasonable prices. Compare: 'en revanche' introduces contrasting info, 'de plus' introduces supporting info. Building arguments with 'de plus' creates a cumulative positive or negative case.

items.question: L'hôtel est confortable. _____, il offre un petit déjeuner gratuit.
items.translation: (The hotel is comfortable. In addition, it offers free breakfast.)
items.explanation: 'En outre' means 'in addition/furthermore' — formal. 'En outre, il offre un petit déjeuner gratuit' — a bonus feature reinforcing the hotel's appeal. 'En outre' is literary and used primarily in writing. It carries more weight than 'de plus' because it's rarer and therefore more emphatic. In business reports: 'Les ventes ont augmenté. En outre, la satisfaction client s'est améliorée.' 'En outre' signals that you're building a comprehensive case by layering supporting evidence.

items.question: Non seulement il parle français, _____ il parle aussi chinois.
items.translation: (Not only does he speak French, but he also speaks Chinese.)
items.explanation: 'Non seulement... mais aussi/mais encore' means 'not only... but also.' This correlative structure emphasizes both elements: 'Non seulement il parle français, mais il parle aussi chinois.' The structure can be intensified: 'non seulement... mais encore' (not only... but furthermore), 'non seulement... mais en plus' (casual intensification). In academic writing: 'Non seulement cette théorie explique X, mais elle prédit aussi Y.' This construction is powerful for building cumulative arguments that exceed expectations.

items.question: Le film est ennuyeux ; _____, il est trop long.
items.translation: (The movie is boring; what's more, it's too long.)
items.explanation: 'Qui plus est' means 'what is more/moreover' — literary addition. 'Qui plus est, il est trop long' — piling on criticism. This expression is the most elevated form of addition connectors and appears in sophisticated writing and formal speeches. It literally means 'who more is' (which moreover is). Usage signals high register competence. The addition connector scale by formality: 'en plus' (casual) → 'aussi/de plus' (standard) → 'en outre/par ailleurs' (formal) → 'qui plus est/de surcroît' (literary).

items.question: _____, je voudrais ajouter une remarque.
items.translation: (Furthermore/Also, I would like to add a comment.)
items.explanation: 'Par ailleurs' means 'furthermore/in addition/on another note' — introducing a new but related point. 'Par ailleurs, je voudrais ajouter...' — shifting to an additional topic. Key nuance: 'de plus' adds more of the same argument, 'par ailleurs' introduces a different aspect of the same broader topic. In meetings: 'Par ailleurs, nous devons discuter du budget' (Moreover, we need to discuss the budget — new subtopic). This connector helps structure multi-point discussions and written arguments naturally.

items.question: Il est gentil, _____ très patient.
items.translation: (He is kind, and moreover very patient.)
items.explanation: 'Et même' means 'and even/and moreover' — escalating addition. 'Gentil, et même très patient' — patience exceeds the expected kindness. 'Même' adds emphasis and escalation: 'Il est intelligent, et même brillant' (intelligent, and even brilliant). Used alone, 'même' means 'even': 'Même moi, je le sais' (Even I know it). 'Même si' (even if), 'quand même' (anyway), 'tout de même' (all the same). The word 'même' is one of French's most versatile modifiers, appearing in countless useful expressions.

items.question: J'ai visité Paris, Lyon, _____ Marseille.
items.translation: (I visited Paris, Lyon, as well as Marseille.)
items.explanation: 'Ainsi que' means 'as well as/along with' — elegant addition in lists. 'Paris, Lyon, ainsi que Marseille' — the final item gets special emphasis through this connector instead of simple 'et.' 'Ainsi que' is more formal than 'et' and suggests a carefully curated list. In formal writing: 'Les étudiants, ainsi que les professeurs, sont invités.' Note: with 'ainsi que,' verb agreement follows the first subject: 'Le directeur, ainsi que son adjoint, est présent' (not *sont). This agreement rule is frequently tested.

items.question: L'exercice physique est bon pour la santé. _____, il améliore l'humeur.
items.translation: (Physical exercise is good for health. Additionally, it improves mood.)
items.explanation: 'D'autre part' means 'on the other hand/additionally' — introducing a second point. Often paired with 'd'une part' (on one hand): 'D'une part, c'est bon pour la santé; d'autre part, ça améliore l'humeur.' This paired structure ('on one hand... on the other hand...') is excellent for organizing binary arguments. Unlike English's 'on the other hand' (which often implies contrast), French 'd'autre part' can be purely additive — listing supporting points. Use it to structure balanced, well-organized French arguments.

items.question: Le projet coûte cher. _____, il prend beaucoup de temps.
items.translation: (The project is expensive. Moreover, it takes a lot of time.)
items.explanation: 'De surcroît' means 'moreover/in addition/on top of that' — very formal literary addition. 'De surcroît, il prend beaucoup de temps' piles on problems. 'Surcroît' literally means 'addition/surplus.' This connector is reserved for elevated writing — academic papers, editorials, legal documents. In everyday speech, you'd use 'en plus' or 'de plus.' The progression of negative accumulation: 'C'est cher. De surcroît, c'est lent. Qui plus est, c'est inefficace.' Each connector adds weight to the growing criticism.

items.question: Elle est belle, _____ intelligente.
items.translation: (She is beautiful, and besides intelligent.)
items.explanation: 'D'ailleurs' means 'besides/moreover/incidentally' — casually introducing additional or tangential information. 'Et d'ailleurs intelligente' adds an extra quality almost as an afterthought. 'D'ailleurs' is common in conversation: 'D'ailleurs, j'ai oublié de te dire...' (By the way, I forgot to tell you...). It can introduce genuinely new information or pretend something is casual when it's actually important. This versatile connector adds naturalness to French speech by allowing smooth topic additions and digressions.

items.question: Le musée est gratuit. _____, il est ouvert tous les jours.
items.translation: (The museum is free. Moreover, it is open every day.)
items.explanation: 'De plus' here reinforces the first positive fact with another. Free AND open daily — cumulative advantages. This is classic argumentative structure: point 1 + 'de plus' + point 2 = stronger case. In essays, you might chain: 'Premièrement... De plus... En outre... Enfin...' (Firstly... Moreover... Furthermore... Finally...) — this four-step structure builds a comprehensive argument. Each connector adds one more supporting element, creating a persuasive cumulative effect characteristic of well-structured French writing.

items.question: Il est doué en musique ; _____, il excelle en peinture.
items.translation: (He is talented in music; in addition, he excels in painting.)
items.explanation: 'En outre' here links two complementary talents — both support the picture of a multi-talented person. In academic contexts, 'en outre' introduces evidence that strengthens a thesis: 'Cette molécule est non-toxique. En outre, elle est biodégradable.' (This molecule is non-toxic. Furthermore, it is biodegradable.) Each additional point reinforces the central argument. 'En outre' signals that the writer has more supporting evidence — a reassuring signal to readers that the argument is comprehensive and well-researched.

items.question: _____, c'est un bon investissement.
items.translation: (In the end / All in all, it's a good investment.)
items.explanation: 'En somme' means 'in short/all in all/to sum up' — wrapping up multiple points into a conclusion. 'En somme, c'est un bon investissement' — summarizing after listing positives and negatives. Similar: 'en résumé' (in summary), 'bref' (in short — casual), 'en définitive' (ultimately), 'tout compte fait' (all things considered). These summary connectors signal you're concluding. 'Bref' is the most conversational: 'Bref, c'était nul' (In short, it was terrible). Choose by register and context.

items.question: Le spectacle était magnifique ; _____, les acteurs étaient excellents.
items.translation: (The show was magnificent; in particular, the actors were excellent.)
items.explanation: 'Notamment' means 'in particular/notably/especially' — narrowing from general to specific. 'Spectacle magnifique, notamment les acteurs' — specifying what made it great. 'Notamment' introduces examples or highlights: 'Plusieurs pays, notamment la France et l'Allemagne...' (Several countries, particularly France and Germany...). Similar: 'en particulier' (in particular), 'surtout' (especially — casual). 'Notamment' is the standard choice for academic and professional contexts when illustrating a general claim with specific examples.

name: Liens d'Addition (Addition & Emphasis)
description: Add information using de plus, en outre, par ailleurs, d'ailleurs, and non seulement...mais aussi

title: Noun Gender Rules

items.question: _____ soleil brille.
items.translation: (The sun is shining.)
items.explanation: 'Le soleil' is masculine — 'le' is the masculine singular definite article. French assigns gender to every noun, which determines articles (le/la), adjective endings, and pronoun forms. There's no logical reason why 'soleil' (sun) is masculine and 'lune' (moon) is feminine — it's arbitrary. However, patterns exist: nouns ending in -age, -ment, -isme are usually masculine. Learning the article WITH the noun (le soleil, not just soleil) is the single best strategy for mastering French gender.

items.question: _____ lune est belle ce soir.
items.translation: (The moon is beautiful tonight.)
items.explanation: 'La lune' is feminine — 'la' is the feminine singular definite article. Most nouns ending in -e are feminine (la table, la porte, la lune), BUT there are many exceptions (le livre, le monde, le musée). The -e ending is only a rough guide. Reliable feminine endings include: -tion (la nation), -sion (la décision), -ité (la liberté), -ure (la nature), -ence/-ance (la différence). These suffixes predict gender with 90%+ accuracy, making them invaluable shortcuts for learners building French vocabulary.

items.question: _____ arbre est très grand.
items.translation: (The tree is very tall.)
items.explanation: 'L'arbre' uses the elided article 'l'' because 'arbre' starts with a vowel. When 'le' or 'la' precedes a vowel or silent 'h,' they become 'l'' — this is mandatory elision. You cannot say *'le arbre' or *'la eau.' 'L'eau' (f), 'l'homme' (m), 'l'école' (f). This means you can't tell gender from 'l'' alone — you must learn whether 'arbre' is masculine (it is) through the indefinite article: 'un arbre.' Elision makes gender invisible, so learners must memorize gender separately.

items.question: _____ maison a un grand jardin.
items.translation: (The house has a big garden.)
items.explanation: 'La maison' is feminine. Nouns ending in -aison are always feminine: raison, saison, maison, liaison, combinaison. This -aison pattern is 100% reliable — no exceptions exist. Similarly reliable: -tion (action, nation), -sion (décision, télévision), -ié (amitié, moitié). Memorizing these suffix patterns dramatically reduces gender guessing. Instead of memorizing individual genders for thousands of nouns, learners can apply ~15 suffix rules covering 60-70% of common French nouns accurately.

items.question: J'ai acheté _____ nouveau livre.
items.translation: (I bought a new book.)
items.explanation: 'Un livre' — masculine indefinite article. 'Livre' (book) is masculine despite ending in -e. Nouns ending in -vre, -tre, -cle, -ble are often masculine: libre, théâtre, article, problème. Meanwhile, 'une livre' (a pound) is feminine — same spelling, different meaning and gender! These rare homonyms with different genders include: le tour (tour/turn) vs. la tour (tower), le poste (position) vs. la poste (post office). Context and article together disambiguate such pairs.

items.question: _____ liberté est un droit fondamental.
items.translation: (Freedom is a fundamental right.)
items.explanation: 'La liberté' is feminine. All nouns ending in -té are feminine: liberté, égalité, fraternité, beauté, vérité, qualité, société, université. This is one of French's most reliable gender rules with virtually no exceptions. The French national motto itself — 'Liberté, Égalité, Fraternité' — is entirely feminine. Learning this one suffix rule instantly gives you the gender of hundreds of abstract nouns. These -té words often correspond to English -ty words: liberty, equality, quality, society.

items.question: _____ problème est difficile.
items.translation: (The problem is difficult.)
items.explanation: 'Le problème' is masculine — one of the most common exceptions to the '-e = feminine' guideline. Other masculine nouns ending in -ème: thème, système, poème. Also masculine despite -e: le musée, le lycée, le trophée (nouns ending in -ée are usually feminine: la journée, la année, BUT -ée words from Greek are masculine). The word 'problème' is one of the top gender errors English speakers make. Memorizing these common exceptions prevents embarrassing mistakes in both speaking and writing.

items.question: _____ voiture est garée dehors.
items.translation: (The car is parked outside.)
items.explanation: 'La voiture' is feminine. Nouns ending in -ure are feminine: voiture, nature, culture, aventure, structure, peinture, nourriture. This -ure pattern is highly reliable. French gender patterns based on endings work as probabilistic rules — not absolute, but right most of the time. Combining several rules: -ure (fem), -ment (masc), -tion (fem), -age (masc), -té (fem) — these five suffixes alone cover hundreds of common nouns. Learning them as a set is one of the most efficient gender-learning strategies available.

items.question: _____ gouvernement a pris une décision.
items.translation: (The government made a decision.)
items.explanation: 'Le gouvernement' is masculine. Nouns ending in -ment are masculine: gouvernement, moment, mouvement, développement, changement, fonctionnement. This is another highly reliable pattern — virtually all -ment nouns are masculine. The suffix -ment creates nouns from verbs (changer → changement) similarly to English -ment (govern → government). Pairing this with the feminine -tion (décider → décision), you can see how French verb-to-noun transformations predictably assign gender through their suffixes.

items.question: _____ paysage est magnifique.
items.translation: (The landscape is magnificent.)
items.explanation: 'Le paysage' is masculine. Nouns ending in -age are almost always masculine: paysage, voyage, fromage, garage, message, passage, village. The only common exception is 'la plage' (beach) and 'une image' (image). The -age suffix often creates nouns from verbs: voyager → voyage, maquiller → maquillage. This -age = masculine rule works in 95%+ of cases. Combined with -ment (masculine) and -tion (feminine), three suffix rules cover an enormous proportion of French abstract and derived nouns.

items.question: Il a montré beaucoup de _____.
items.translation: (He showed a lot of courage.)
items.explanation: 'Le courage' is masculine — another -age noun following the masculine pattern. 'Beaucoup de' + noun drops the article ('beaucoup de courage,' not *'beaucoup du courage'). After quantity expressions (beaucoup de, peu de, trop de, assez de), 'de' replaces any article. This de-without-article rule applies to all quantity adverbs. Meanwhile, 'courageux' (masc. adj.) and 'courageuse' (fem. adj.) are the adjective forms. French cleanly separates nouns (courage) from adjectives (courageux) — a distinction that helps students master both word classes.

items.question: _____ nationalité de Pierre est française.
items.translation: (Pierre's nationality is French.)
items.explanation: 'La nationalité' is feminine — the -té ending guarantees feminine gender. Notice: 'française' (with lowercase and -e ending) is the feminine adjective agreeing with 'nationalité.' In French, nationalities as adjectives are lowercase: 'il est français,' 'elle est française.' As nouns referring to people, they're capitalized: 'un Français,' 'une Française.' This capitalization distinction (lowercase adjective vs. uppercase noun) doesn't exist in English and is a key French writing convention that signals grammatical awareness.

items.question: _____ tourisme est important pour l'économie.
items.translation: (Tourism is important for the economy.)
items.explanation: 'Le tourisme' is masculine. Nouns ending in -isme are always masculine: tourisme, capitalisme, romantisme, organisme, mécanisme. This -isme = masculine rule has zero exceptions. English cognates ending in -ism map directly: tourism, capitalism, organism. Pairing -isme (masculine) with -iste (either gender for people): 'le tourisme' but 'un/une touriste.' The -iste suffix for people takes the gender of the person: 'un pianiste' (male pianist), 'une pianiste' (female pianist).

items.question: _____ science progresse chaque jour.
items.translation: (Science progresses every day.)
items.explanation: 'La science' is feminine. Nouns ending in -ence/-ance are feminine: science, patience, différence, importance, élégance, distance. This pattern is very reliable. English cognates ending in -ence/-ance follow the same pattern: patience, difference, importance. French learners can leverage English-French cognates: if the English word ends in -ence/-ance, the French equivalent is almost certainly feminine. This cross-linguistic transfer strategy accelerates gender acquisition for English-speaking French learners significantly.

items.question: J'ai besoin d'_____ information.
items.translation: (I need a piece of information.)
items.explanation: 'Une information' is feminine — nouns ending in -tion are feminine: information, nation, éducation, situation, communication, révolution. The -tion rule is one of French's most reliable (99%+ accuracy) and covers thousands of words. English speakers benefit enormously since English -tion words map directly to French -tion words. However, 'information' in French is countable ('une information' = a piece of news/information), while English treats it as uncountable. This countability difference is a subtle trap separate from gender.

name: Règles du Genre (Gender Rules)
description: Learn patterns for predicting French noun gender based on endings, categories, and exceptions

title: Plural Formation

items.question: Les _____ sont dans le jardin.
items.translation: (The cats are in the garden.)
items.explanation: Regular French plurals add -s: chat → chats. This silent -s parallels English but with a key difference — in French, the -s is never pronounced! 'Chat' and 'chats' sound identical. Plurality is signaled by the article and context, not pronunciation: 'le chat' (one cat) vs. 'les chats' (cats). This silent plural -s means French speakers rely heavily on articles (le/les, un/des) to distinguish singular from plural. Listening for articles is therefore critical for French listening comprehension — they carry the number information.

items.question: J'ai acheté deux _____ au marché.
items.translation: (I bought two cakes at the market.)
items.explanation: Nouns ending in -eau form plurals with -eaux: gâteau → gâteaux, chapeau → chapeaux, bateau → bateaux, oiseau → oiseaux. No exceptions! This -eau → -eaux pattern is 100% reliable. Similarly, -au → -aux: noyau → noyaux, tuyau → tuyaux. These plurals look dramatic but sound the same as the singular — 'gâteau' and 'gâteaux' are pronounced identically. Again, the article signals plurality: 'le gâteau' vs. 'les gâteaux.' This spelling change without pronunciation change is characteristically French.

items.question: Les _____ sont beaux en automne.
items.translation: (The trees are beautiful in autumn.)
items.explanation: Regular plural: arbre → arbres. Most French nouns simply add -s for the plural. This covers the vast majority of French nouns. The -s is silent: 'arbre' and 'arbres' sound identical. Note the agreement: 'les arbres sont beaux' — the adjective 'beau' becomes 'beaux' (masculine plural) to agree. In French, articles, adjectives, and sometimes past participles must all agree in number with the noun. This cascading agreement system means one plural noun triggers multiple changes across the sentence.

items.question: Il y a trois _____ dans ma famille.
items.translation: (There are three boys/sons in my family.)
items.explanation: Regular plural: garçon → garçons. The cedilla (ç) remains in the plural — it's part of the base word's spelling, not affected by pluralization. The cedilla under 'c' before a/o/u makes it sound like 's' instead of 'k': 'garçon' = [garsɔ̃]. Without it, 'garcon' would be pronounced [garkɔ̃]. Other words with cedilla: leçon → leçons, français → no change (already -s). French spelling marks like cedilla, accents, and circumflex carry pronunciation information and must be maintained in all forms.

items.question: Les _____ sont rouges.
items.translation: (The noses are red.)
items.explanation: Nouns ending in -z, -s, or -x don't change in the plural: nez → nez, bras → bras, voix → voix, souris → souris, fois → fois. These are invariable — the plural looks identical to the singular. Once again, the article distinguishes: 'le nez' (singular) vs. 'les nez' (plural). This -s/-x/-z invariability rule is easy to remember: since these endings already contain a letter that could be a plural marker, nothing more is added. It simplifies spelling for these specific word groups.

items.question: J'ai lu ces _____ pendant les vacances.
items.translation: (I read these newspapers during the vacation.)
items.explanation: Nouns ending in -al form plurals with -aux: journal → journaux, animal → animaux, cheval → chevaux, hôpital → hôpitaux. This -al → -aux change is one of French's most important irregular plural patterns. Exceptions: bal → bals, festival → festivals, carnaval → carnavals (loanwords and newer words tend to keep -als). The dominant pattern is -aux, and all common everyday nouns follow it. 'Les journaux' demonstrates how articles tell you the number since 'journal' and 'journaux' differ only in the final vowel sound.

items.question: Les _____ jouent dans le parc.
items.translation: (The children are playing in the park.)
items.explanation: Regular plural: enfant → enfants. While 'enfant' is simple, note that it can be masculine or feminine: 'un enfant' (a boy child), 'une enfant' (a girl child). The plural 'les enfants' covers both genders. When a group contains mixed genders, French traditionally uses the masculine plural. This 'masculine as default' rule is a feature of French grammar that has generated contemporary debate. Regardless of this discussion, 'les enfants' remains the standard plural for all groups of children.

items.question: Il porte des _____ noirs.
items.translation: (He wears black shoes.)
items.explanation: Regular plural: chaussure → chaussures. Note 'des chaussures noirs' would be wrong — 'chaussures' is feminine, so the adjective must be 'noires' (feminine plural). The full correct sentence: 'des chaussures noires.' This cascading agreement illustrates why knowing noun gender matters for more than just articles — it affects every adjective attached to the noun. Even color adjectives must agree: bleus/bleues, blancs/blanches, rouges (same for both — already ends in -e).

items.question: Mes _____ sont sur la table.
items.translation: (My eyes are on the table [looking at something on the table].)
items.explanation: 'Œil → yeux' is one of French's most irregular plurals — the word completely changes form. Other highly irregular plurals: 'monsieur → messieurs,' 'madame → mesdames,' 'mademoiselle → mesdemoiselles.' These must simply be memorized. 'Yeux' is used constantly: 'les yeux bleus' (blue eyes), 'fermer les yeux' (close one's eyes). The irregularity of 'yeux' comes from Latin evolution (oculus → oil → œil, oculi → olz → yeux). Such historical irregularities persist in modern French's most common vocabulary.

items.question: Les _____ de cette région sont magnifiques.
items.translation: (The castles of this region are magnificent.)
items.explanation: 'Château → châteaux' follows the reliable -eau → -eaux pattern. 'Les châteaux de la Loire' is a classic French cultural reference — the Loire Valley castles. Note the circumflex (â) is preserved in the plural. This accent indicates a historical 's' that was lost: Old French 'chasteau' → 'château.' The 'x' plural ending in -eaux words also has historical origins — it was originally an abbreviation for '-us' in medieval manuscripts. These etymological traces make French spelling a window into language history.

items.question: Il y a des _____ dans le ciel.
items.translation: (There are clouds in the sky.)
items.explanation: Regular plural: nuage → nuages. 'Nuage' ends in -age (masculine) and follows standard pluralization. Notice 'des nuages' — the plural indefinite article 'des' replaces both 'un/une' in plural contexts. After negation: 'Il n'y a pas de nuages' — 'des' becomes 'de/d'' after negation. This des → de change in negative sentences is a fundamental French grammar rule. 'J'ai des amis' → 'Je n'ai pas d'amis.' The article reduction signals negation alongside 'ne...pas.'

items.question: J'ai mangé des _____ au dessert.
items.translation: (I ate fruits for dessert.)
items.explanation: Regular plural: fruit → fruits. Note the English cognate 'fruit' can be singular or plural (uncountable), but French 'fruit' is fully countable: 'un fruit' (one fruit), 'des fruits' (several fruits). This false friend trap catches English speakers who might say *'J'ai mangé du fruit' (which means 'I ate some fruit' as a substance, not individual pieces). For specific fruits: 'des pommes' (apples), 'des oranges' (oranges). French countability rules differ from English in numerous cases.

items.question: Les _____ de cette histoire sont intéressants.
items.translation: (The details of this story are interesting.)
items.explanation: Regular plural: détail → détails. Note that 'détails' is masculine plural, so the adjective is 'intéressants' (masculine plural). Some -ail words have irregular plurals: travail → travaux, vitrail → vitraux, corail → coraux. But 'détail → détails' is regular. The irregular -ail → -aux group is small: travail, vitrail, corail, émail, bail, soupirail. Most -ail nouns form regular -ails plurals: détail, portail, éventail. Distinguishing regular from irregular -ail plurals requires memorization of the short irregular list.

items.question: Tous les _____ travaillent dans ce bureau.
items.translation: (All the men work in this office.)
items.explanation: Regular plural: homme → hommes. Note that 'homme' starts with a silent 'h' (h muet), so elision and liaison apply: 'l'homme' not *'le homme,' 'les hommes' with liaison [lezɔm].
