French has two types of 'h': silent h (h muet — elision/liaison occur: l'homme, les hommes) and aspirated h (h aspiré — no elision/liaison: le héros, les héros without liaison). This h muet/aspiré distinction is marked in dictionaries because it affects pronunciation but has no visual difference in the word itself.

items.question: J'adore les _____ de mon quartier.
items.translation: (I love the shops in my neighborhood.)
items.explanation: Regular plural: magasin → magasins. Watch the spelling: 'magasin' (store/shop) — not 'magazine' which is 'un magazine' (also masculine, from the same root). French shops have specific names: 'boulangerie' (bakery), 'boucherie' (butcher), 'pharmacie' (pharmacy), 'librairie' (bookshop — false friend! Not 'library' which is 'bibliothèque'). The 'librairie/library' false friend is one of the most frequently cited across all French learning materials. Understanding these naming conventions helps navigate French daily life.

name: Formation du Pluriel (Plural Formation)
description: Master regular and irregular plural patterns including -aux, -eux, and compound noun plurals

title: Compound Nouns

items.question: J'ai perdu mon _____.
items.translation: (I lost my wallet.)
items.explanation: 'Porte-monnaie' (wallet, literally 'carry-money') is an invariable compound noun — it doesn't change in plural: 'un porte-monnaie,' 'des porte-monnaie.' The verb component ('porte' from 'porter') never takes -s. This verb + noun structure is common: porte-clés (keychain), porte-parole (spokesperson), porte-avions (aircraft carrier). The general rule: in verb + noun compounds, the verb is always invariable. The noun component may or may not take -s depending on logic (porte-monnaie: always carrying money, so no plural needed).

items.question: L'arc-en-ciel est apparu après la pluie. Les _____ sont rares ici.
items.translation: (Rainbows are rare here.)
items.explanation: 'Arc-en-ciel' (rainbow, literally 'arc-in-sky') pluralizes only the noun component: arcs-en-ciel. The preposition 'en' and its complement 'ciel' stay singular. Rule: in noun + preposition + noun compounds, only the first noun takes the plural mark. 'Pomme de terre → pommes de terre' (potatoes — only 'pomme' gets -s). 'Chef-d'œuvre → chefs-d'œuvre' (masterpieces). This pattern makes linguistic sense: there are multiple arcs, but still one sky; multiple potatoes, but same earth.

items.question: Les _____ sont délicieuses avec du beurre.
items.translation: (Potatoes are delicious with butter.)
items.explanation: 'Pomme de terre' (potato, literally 'apple of earth') follows the noun + de + noun pattern: only the first noun pluralizes. 'Des pommes de terre' — 'pommes' gets -s, 'terre' stays singular. This compound is one of the most common in French daily life. Similarly: 'salle de bains' (bathroom — 'salle' can be plural, 'bains' is conventionally plural), 'chemin de fer' (railway — only 'chemin' pluralizes). The preposition 'de' blocks pluralization of the second noun in most compounds.

items.question: Ma _____ m'a offert un cadeau.
items.translation: (My mother-in-law gave me a gift.)
items.explanation: 'Belle-mère' (mother-in-law/stepmother) pluralizes both elements: 'belles-mères.' In adjective + noun compounds, both parts take the plural mark because both are nouns/adjectives and both logically refer to multiple entities. Similarly: 'beau-père → beaux-pères' (fathers-in-law), 'belle-sœur → belles-sœurs' (sisters-in-law), 'grand-mère → grands-mères' (grandmothers — note: traditionally 'grand-mères' without -s on 'grand' for feminine, but modern spelling accepts 'grands-mères'). Family compound nouns follow regular adjective+noun rules.

items.question: Fermez les _____.
items.translation: (Close the shutters.)
items.explanation: 'Volet' (shutter) forms a regular plural: volets. While not a compound noun itself, it contrasts with compound alternatives. French has many everyday compound nouns: 'abat-jour' (lampshade — invariable), 'cache-nez' (scarf — invariable), 'casse-croûte' (snack — invariable). Most verb + noun compounds are invariable because the verb component can't logically take a plural mark. Understanding this logic (verbs don't pluralize, nouns do) helps predict compound plurals even for new vocabulary encountered in context.

items.question: Les _____ sont les meilleurs amis de l'homme.
items.translation: (Dogs are man's best friends.)
items.explanation: 'Chien → chiens' is a regular plural, but this sentence illustrates a French cultural saying: 'Le chien est le meilleur ami de l'homme' (The dog is man's best friend). In French, generic statements use the definite article: 'Les chiens sont...' (Dogs are... — speaking generally). English uses zero article for generalizations, but French always uses 'le/la/les.' This generic article rule is fundamental: 'J'aime les chats' (I like cats in general), not *'J'aime chats.' The definite article serves double duty — specific AND generic.

items.question: Cette autoroute a beaucoup de _____.
items.translation: (This highway has a lot of roundabouts.)
items.explanation: 'Rond-point' (roundabout, literally 'round-point') pluralizes both components: 'ronds-points.' In adjective + noun compounds, both words take the plural mark: 'rond' → 'ronds,' 'point' → 'points.' Similar compounds: 'coffre-fort → coffres-forts' (safes/strongboxes), 'plate-forme → plates-formes' (platforms). The rule: when both elements are nouns or adjectives (not verbs or prepositions), both pluralize. This makes linguistic sense — there are multiple round shapes at multiple points. France is famous for its many 'ronds-points.'

items.question: Il a plusieurs _____ dans son salon.
items.translation: (He has several floor lamps in his living room.)
items.explanation: 'Lampadaire' (floor lamp/street lamp) is a standard single-word noun that pluralizes regularly: lampadaires. Not all French nouns describing objects are compounds — many are simple words derived from Latin or Greek roots. 'Lampadaire' comes from Greek 'lampas' (torch). French has both compound constructions (porte-monnaie, arc-en-ciel) and single derived words (lampadaire, bibliothèque). Knowing which form a word takes requires vocabulary knowledge, but the pluralization rules follow standard patterns for each type.

items.question: Les _____ sont fermés le dimanche.
items.translation: (The supermarkets are closed on Sundays.)
items.explanation: 'Supermarché' (supermarket) is written as one word, not hyphenated. It pluralizes regularly: supermarchés. French has prefix+noun words that look like compounds but are single words: supermarché, hypermarché, autoroute, aéroport. These are not true compounds and follow standard plural rules. True compounds with hyphens (porte-monnaie, arc-en-ciel) have special rules. The distinction: hyphenated = compound plural rules, single word = standard -s plural. This means knowing whether a word is hyphenated or not affects its pluralization.

items.question: Ma sœur a trois _____.
items.translation: (My sister has three children.)
items.explanation: 'Petit-enfant' (grandchild, literally 'small-child') pluralizes both parts: 'petits-enfants.' This adjective+noun compound follows the standard rule — both elements take the plural mark. The 'petit' here isn't about size but about generation: 'petit-fils' (grandson), 'petite-fille' (granddaughter), 'arrière-petit-fils' (great-grandson). French kinship compound terms are systematic: petit = one generation removed, arrière = two generations. These family vocabulary compounds are essential for describing family relationships.

items.question: Les _____ sont souvent bouchés le matin.
items.translation: (The highways are often jammed in the morning.)
items.explanation: 'Autoroute' (highway/motorway) is written as one word (no hyphen) and pluralizes regularly: autoroutes. It's feminine: 'l'autoroute est bouchée' (the highway is jammed — feminine agreement). Despite starting with 'auto-' (from automobile), it's a fused word. Compare: 'automobile' → 'automobiles.' French road vocabulary: autoroute (highway), route (road), rue (street), boulevard (boulevard), avenue (avenue), impasse (dead end), chemin (path). Each word has specific urban planning connotations.

items.question: Elle adore les _____.
items.translation: (She loves sunflowers.)
items.explanation: 'Tournesol' (sunflower, literally 'turn-sun') is now written as one word in modern French, though historically hyphenated. Regular plural: tournesols. Many former compounds have fused into single words over time: 'portefeuille' (wallet, formerly porte-feuille), 'passeport' (passport, formerly passe-port). The 2016 French spelling reform simplified many compound rules, fusing more words and regularizing plurals. However, both old and new spellings remain accepted, creating some variation in current usage.

items.question: Les _____ ont résolu le problème.
items.translation: (The spokespeople resolved the problem.)
items.explanation: 'Porte-parole' (spokesperson, literally 'carry-word') is invariable — it doesn't change in the plural: 'un porte-parole,' 'des porte-parole.' Both components remain unchanged. The verb 'porte' never pluralizes (verbs don't take -s). The noun 'parole' stays singular because the concept is 'carrying the word' — one message regardless of how many speakers. This invariable compound illustrates how meaning determines form in French: logical analysis of the compound's internal semantics guides its plural behavior.

items.question: J'ai vu plusieurs _____ rouges ce matin.
items.translation: (I saw several ladybugs this morning.)
items.explanation: 'Coccinelle' (ladybug) is a regular feminine noun: une coccinelle → des coccinelles. Despite being a charming word, it's not a compound — it comes from Latin 'coccineus' (scarlet). French nature vocabulary mixes compounds and simple words: 'arc-en-ciel' (rainbow — compound), 'coccinelle' (ladybug — simple), 'tournesol' (sunflower — fused compound), 'papillon' (butterfly — simple). Understanding whether a word is compound or simple determines which plural rules apply, making this distinction practically important.

items.question: Il faut acheter des _____ pour la soirée.
items.translation: (We need to buy corkscrews for the party.)
items.explanation: 'Tire-bouchon' (corkscrew, literally 'pull-cork') pluralizes only the noun: 'tire-bouchons.' The verb 'tire' (pulls) stays invariable. The noun 'bouchon' (cork) takes the plural mark because multiple corkscrews have multiple corks to pull. This follows the verb+noun compound pattern: 'ouvre-boîte → ouvre-boîtes' (can openers), 'essuie-glace → essuie-glaces' (windshield wipers). The logic: the verb describes the action, the noun describes the object — only objects can be multiple. This active/item logic governs all verb+noun compound plurals.

name: Noms Composés (Compound Nouns)
description: Form and pluralize compound nouns (porte-monnaie, arc-en-ciel) with correct hyphenation rules

title: Collective & Abstract Nouns

items.question: La _____ est en retard.
items.translation: (The crowd is late.)
items.explanation: 'La foule' (the crowd) is a feminine collective noun treated as SINGULAR in French. 'La foule est en retard' — singular verb. English sometimes allows plural verbs with collectives ('the crowd are'), but French strictly uses singular: 'la foule marche' (the crowd walks), never *'la foule marchent.' Other collective nouns: 'le groupe' (the group), 'l'équipe' (the team), 'la famille' (the family), 'le public' (the audience). All take singular verbs in French regardless of how many individuals comprise the group.

items.question: _____ gens sont sympathiques.
items.translation: (These people are nice.)
items.explanation: 'Les gens' (people) is an unusual noun — always plural (no singular form) and requires interesting agreement. Adjectives BEFORE 'gens' are feminine: 'les bonnes gens' (good people), but adjectives AFTER are masculine: 'les gens heureux' (happy people). 'Ces gens sont sympathiques' — 'sympathiques' (after) uses masculine plural. This before/after gender flip is unique to 'gens' and is one of French's strangest grammatical features. In practice, most speakers simply use masculine agreement and avoid pre-positioning adjectives.

items.question: Le _____ de musiciens joue ce soir.
items.translation: (The group of musicians is playing tonight.)
items.explanation: 'Le groupe' (the group) is masculine and singular. 'Le groupe joue' — singular verb, even though the group contains multiple musicians. French collective noun + de + plural noun always takes a singular verb when the collective is the grammatical subject: 'Un groupe de touristes visite le musée' (not *visitent). However, when emphasis is on the members: 'Un grand nombre de personnes ont répondu' — here 'nombre' is quantifying, so plural verb is possible. This distinction between collective subject and quantifier is subtle but important.

items.question: La _____ est la vertu des forts.
items.translation: (Patience is the virtue of the strong.)
items.explanation: 'La patience' is an abstract feminine noun. In French, abstract concepts use the definite article: 'La patience est une vertu' (Patience is a virtue). English uses no article for abstract generalizations, but French ALWAYS uses le/la/les: 'L'amour est beau' (Love is beautiful), 'La liberté est précieuse' (Freedom is precious). This mandatory article for abstract nouns is a fundamental structural difference. French treats even intangible concepts as definite entities, giving them articles as if they were concrete, identifiable things.

items.question: _____ est un sentiment puissant.
items.translation: (Love is a powerful feeling.)
items.explanation: 'L'amour' uses elision (l' before vowel). 'Amour' is masculine singular but has a quirk: in literary French, it becomes feminine in the plural — 'les amours éternelles.' This masculine-singular-to-feminine-plural shift also applies to 'délice' (delight) and 'orgue' (organ — the instrument). These three words are the only ones in French that change gender between singular and plural. In modern everyday French, 'amour' stays masculine throughout: 'les amours passés.' The gender shift is now literary/poetic.

items.question: Il a fait preuve de beaucoup de _____.
items.translation: (He showed a lot of courage.)
items.explanation: 'Le courage' is masculine and uncountable in French — you use 'du courage' (some courage), 'beaucoup de courage' (a lot of courage). After 'beaucoup de,' no article appears (see partitive rules). Abstract nouns can be either countable or uncountable: 'un sentiment' (a feeling — countable), 'du courage' (courage — uncountable). This mirrors English sometimes, but French can make some English uncountable nouns countable: 'une information' (a piece of information), 'un conseil' (a piece of advice). These differences require careful attention.

items.question: _____ majorité des étudiants a réussi.
items.translation: (The majority of students passed.)
items.explanation: 'La majorité' is feminine singular. 'La majorité des étudiants a réussi' — the verb agrees with 'majorité' (singular), not 'étudiants' (plural). Similar: 'la plupart' takes plural: 'la plupart des gens pensent...' (this is the exception — 'la plupart' always triggers plural verbs). 'La majorité/la moitié/un tiers' take singular verbs when you emphasize the fraction, but plural when you emphasize the group. This context-dependent agreement reflects whether French speakers conceptualize the group as a unit or as individuals.

items.question: La _____ de la Tour Eiffel est impressionnante.
items.translation: (The height of the Eiffel Tower is impressive.)
items.explanation: 'La hauteur' (height) is a feminine abstract noun formed from the adjective 'haut' (tall/high). French creates abstract nouns from adjectives systematically: haut → hauteur, long → longueur, large → largeur, grand → grandeur, profond → profondeur. These -eur derivatives are all feminine. Similarly: blanc → blancheur (whiteness), doux → douceur (sweetness/softness). This adjective → -eur noun transform is productive and reliable, creating an elegant system for expressing abstract qualities of physical properties.

items.question: Le _____ de médecins recommande ce traitement.
items.translation: (The group/body of doctors recommends this treatment.)
items.explanation: 'Le corps' (body/corps) has multiple meanings: physical body, corps/body of professionals, body of text. 'Le corps médical' (the medical profession collectively). Note: 'corps' ends in -s so it doesn't change in plural: 'un corps, des corps.' This invariability (ending already in -s) is shared by: 'bras' (arm), 'mois' (month), 'poids' (weight), 'temps' (time/weather). These words that don't visibly change between singular and plural rely entirely on articles and context for number information.

items.question: L'_____ entre les deux pays s'améliore.
items.translation: (The friendship between the two countries is improving.)
items.explanation: 'L'amitié' (friendship) is feminine — -ié ending signals feminine. Abstract relationship nouns: 'l'amitié' (friendship), 'l'inimitié' (enmity), 'la rivalité' (rivalry), 'la solidarité' (solidarity). These concepts take definite articles in French when discussed generally: 'L'amitié est précieuse' (Friendship is precious). Note: 'amitié' is singular; for specific friendships you might say 'les amitiés' (friendships), but the concept itself is uncountable in the abstract. French handles abstract vs. specific instances through article choice and context.

items.question: La plupart des _____ sont en vacances.
items.translation: (Most of the employees are on vacation.)
items.explanation: 'La plupart de' (most of/the majority of) is unique: it ALWAYS takes a plural verb, regardless of its own singular form. 'La plupart des employés sont en vacances' — plural 'sont,' not *'est.' This is the main exception to the collective-noun-takes-singular-verb rule. 'La plupart du temps' (most of the time) also triggers plural: 'La plupart des gens pensent...' ALL other fraction/proportion words (la majorité, la moitié, un tiers) allow singular. 'La plupart' is the lone holdout demanding plural verbs.

items.question: Le _____ des habitants augmente.
items.translation: (The number of inhabitants is increasing.)
items.explanation: 'Le nombre' (the number/quantity) is masculine. Crucial distinction: 'le nombre de' (the number/amount of — singular verb) vs. 'un certain nombre de' / 'bon nombre de' (a certain number of / many — plural verb possible). 'Le nombre des habitants augmente' — singular because 'nombre' is the subject. 'Un nombre d'habrants' can take plural verb emphasizing the many individuals. Also: 'nombre' (quantity) vs. 'numéro' (assigned number: phone number, room number) vs. 'chiffre' (digit/figure). These three are NOT interchangeable in French.

items.question: La _____ de vivre en France est élevée.
items.translation: (The quality of life in France is high.)
items.explanation: 'La qualité' (quality) is feminine (-té ending = feminine). 'Qualité de vie' (quality of life) is a fixed expression using 'de' without article. Abstract compound expressions with 'de' often drop the article: 'mode de vie' (way of life), 'niveau de vie' (standard of living), 'espérance de vie' (life expectancy). These set phrases function as fixed units. French creates elegant compound concepts through noun + de + noun patterns, producing concise expressions for complex abstract ideas that English often handles with multiple words.

items.question: Tout le _____ est invité à la fête.
items.translation: (Everyone is invited to the party.)
items.explanation: 'Tout le monde' (everyone/everybody) is a fixed expression treated as masculine singular: 'Tout le monde est invité' (everyone is invited — singular verb). Never *'Tout le monde sont invités.' Despite referring to multiple people, the grammatical subject is 'le monde' (the world) — singular. This parallels English 'everyone is...' (not *'everyone are...'). 'Tout le monde' is one of the most frequently used expressions in French daily conversation. It elegantly uses 'the whole world' to mean 'everybody,' a lovely French metonymy.

items.question: L'_____ de ce pays est en crise.
items.translation: (The economy of this country is in crisis.)
items.explanation: 'L'économie' (economy) is feminine — -ie ending signals feminine. Related words: 'économique' (economic — adjective), 'économiser' (to save money — verb), 'économe' (thrifty — adjective). French word families share roots but have distinct suffixes for different parts of speech: -ie (noun), -ique (adjective), -iser (verb), -e (person/adjective). Recognizing these suffix patterns helps learners decode new vocabulary: seeing '-ique' signals an adjective, '-iser' a verb, '-ie/-tion' a noun. This systematic derivation is a powerful reading comprehension strategy.

name: Noms Collectifs et Abstraits (Collective & Abstract Nouns)
description: Use collective and abstract nouns with correct articles, agreement, and verb conjugation

title: Abstract & Derived Nouns

items.question: La _____ de cette ville est remarquable.
items.translation: (The beauty of this city is remarkable.)
items.explanation: 'La beauté' (beauty) is an abstract noun derived from the adjective 'beau/belle' (beautiful). The -té suffix creates feminine abstract nouns from adjectives: bon → bonté (goodness), libre → liberté (freedom), vrai → vérité (truth), faux → fausseté (falsehood). This derivation pattern is productive: knowing the adjective lets you predict the abstract noun form. English mirrors this with -ty (beauty, liberty) and -ness (goodness, truthfulness). French -té nouns are always feminine — no exceptions — making gender prediction effortless.

items.question: Le _____ de cet événement reste inconnu.
items.translation: (The reason for this event remains unknown.)
items.explanation: 'Le motif' (reason/motive/pattern) is masculine. French has subtly different words for 'reason': 'raison' (general reason), 'motif' (specific motive/justification), 'cause' (cause). In legal contexts: 'sans motif apparent' (without apparent motive). In art: 'un motif décoratif' (a decorative pattern). This polysemy (multiple meanings) is common in French abstract vocabulary. 'Motif' can mean reason, motive, or pattern depending on context — a single word covering a semantic range that English distributes across several words.

items.question: Son _____ pour les langues est impressionnant.
items.translation: (His talent for languages is impressive.)
items.explanation: 'Le talent' (talent) is masculine. French-English cognates sharing the same meaning and similar spelling are numerous in abstract vocabulary: talent, courage, patience, silence, importance, intelligence. These shared words (originating from Latin or French influence on English) make abstract French vocabulary accessible to English speakers. However, watch for false cognates: 'le talent' matches, but 'le coin' (corner, not coin), 'la librairie' (bookshop, not library). Learning to distinguish true from false cognates is a key intermediate skill.

items.question: La _____ de la situation exige de l'action.
items.translation: (The gravity/seriousness of the situation demands action.)
items.explanation: 'La gravité' (gravity/seriousness) is feminine (-té ending). It has two meanings: physical gravity (la gravité terrestre) and seriousness (la gravité de la situation). Both derive from Latin 'gravitas.' Related words: 'grave' (serious — adjective), 'gravement' (seriously — adverb), 'aggraver' (to worsen — verb). This word family demonstrates French's systematic derivation: adjective 'grave,' abstract noun 'gravité,' adverb 'gravement,' verb 'aggraver.' Each form serves a specific grammatical function while sharing the semantic core.

items.question: Le _____ entre les deux versions est minimal.
items.translation: (The difference between the two versions is minimal.)
items.explanation: 'L'écart' (gap/difference/deviation) is masculine. This word captures a quantitative or spatial difference: 'l'écart de prix' (price gap), 'l'écart de température' (temperature difference), 'à l'écart' (apart/aside). Compare: 'la différence' (general difference), 'la distinction' (subtle distinction), 'l'écart' (measurable gap). French has nuanced vocabulary for types of differences, allowing precise expression. Each word emphasizes a different aspect: 'différence' is general, 'écart' is measurable, 'distinction' is intellectual.

items.question: La _____ de ce problème est difficile.
items.translation: (The resolution of this problem is difficult.)
items.explanation: 'La résolution' (resolution/solving) is feminine (-tion ending = always feminine). Derived from the verb 'résoudre' (to solve/resolve). French verb → noun derivations follow patterns: résoudre → résolution, décider → décision, construire → construction, détruire → destruction. The -tion suffix creates feminine action nouns from verbs — similar to English where 'resolve' → 'resolution.' These parallels between English and French -tion words provide learners with an enormous ready-made vocabulary of recognizable abstract nouns.

items.question: Le _____ est un facteur essentiel du succès.
items.translation: (Perseverance is an essential factor of success.)
items.explanation: 'L'effort' (effort) is masculine. This is a direct cognate with English, sharing Latin roots. In French expressions: 'faire un effort' (to make an effort), 'sans effort' (effortlessly), 'effort physique' (physical effort). Note: 'effort' is countable in French: 'des efforts' (efforts). Abstract nouns' countability varies between French and English — 'un effort' is natural in both languages, but 'une information' (a piece of information) is countable in French whereas English treats 'information' as uncountable. Each case must be learned individually.

items.question: La _____ de cette découverte est immense.
items.translation: (The importance of this discovery is immense.)
items.explanation: 'L'importance' (importance) is feminine (-ance ending = feminine). Word family: 'important' (adjective), 'importance' (noun), 'importer' (verb — to matter). 'C'est important' (It's important), 'Quelle est l'importance de...?' (What is the importance of...?), 'Peu importe' (It doesn't matter). The -ance/-ence suffix system creates abstract nouns from adjectives: important → importance, patient → patience, intelligent → intelligence. English follows the same pattern, making these words naturally accessible to English-speaking learners.

items.question: Le _____ de cet artiste est unique.
items.translation: (The style of this artist is unique.)
items.explanation: 'Le style' (style) is masculine — another -e ending masculine exception (le style, le domaine, le vocabulaire, le silence). In French art vocabulary: 'le style baroque,' 'le style impressionniste,' 'un style de vie' (a lifestyle). 'Style' is a perfect cognate between English and French, both from Latin 'stilus' (writing instrument, then manner of writing). French art and culture vocabulary is rich with such cognates: 'l'art,' 'la musique,' 'le théâtre,' 'la sculpture' — all easily recognizable to English speakers.

items.question: Son _____ pour la justice est admirable.
items.translation: (His commitment to justice is admirable.)
items.explanation: 'L'engagement' (commitment/engagement) is masculine (-ment ending = masculine). From 'engager' (to commit/engage). French -ment nouns from verbs: changer → changement, développer → développement, commencer → commencement. This pattern is systematic and productive. The noun captures the process or result of the verb's action. English shares this pattern for some words (engagement, development) but not all (changement → 'change,' not *'changement'). Recognizing French -ment nouns helps decode unfamiliar vocabulary by connecting it to known verbs.

items.question: La _____ de lire développe l'imagination.
items.translation: (The habit of reading develops imagination.)
items.explanation: 'L'habitude' (habit/custom) is feminine (-ude ending = feminine: attitude, certitude, solitude, multitude). Word family: 'habitude' (noun), 's'habituer à' (to get used to — reflexive verb), 'habituel' (usual — adjective), 'habituellement' (usually — adverb). French constructs complete word families around core roots, each with a predictable suffix indicating its part of speech. 'Avoir l'habitude de' (to be used to / to have the habit of) is an essential French expression used daily in conversation.

items.question: Le _____ entre théorie et pratique est important.
items.translation: (The link between theory and practice is important.)
items.explanation: 'Le lien' (link/connection/bond) is masculine. French has several words for 'connection': 'lien' (bond/link), 'liaison' (connection/link — also a romantic relationship), 'relation' (relationship), 'rapport' (relationship/report/ratio). Each has nuances: 'lien' is general, 'liaison' implies active connection, 'relation' emphasizes the dynamic between parties, 'rapport' is more formal and analytical. This semantic richness lets French speakers precisely describe different types of connections — a specificity English handles with modifiers rather than distinct words.

items.question: L'_____ sociale est un objectif important.
items.translation: (Social equality is an important objective.)
items.explanation: 'L'égalité' (equality) is feminine (-té ending). Part of France's national motto: 'Liberté, Égalité, Fraternité.' Word family: 'égal' (equal — adjective), 'également' (equally/also — adverb), 'égalité' (equality — noun), 'égaliser' (to equalize — verb). 'Ça m'est égal' is a very common expression meaning 'I don't mind / It doesn't matter to me.' French political vocabulary draws heavily from these Enlightenment-era abstract nouns, making them culturally as well as linguistically essential for understanding French discourse.

items.question: Le _____ de cette entreprise est ambitieux.
items.translation: (The project of this company is ambitious.)
items.explanation: 'Le projet' (project/plan) is masculine. This cognate is a classic example of a word that looks the same but has subtle usage differences: French 'projet' can mean both 'project' and 'plan/intention.' 'Projet de loi' (bill/proposed law), 'projet de vie' (life plan). The verb 'projeter' means 'to project' or 'to plan.' 'Se projeter dans l'avenir' (to envision oneself in the future). The word carries a forward-looking energy — it's about planning and intention. French business and political vocabulary uses 'projet' extensively.

items.question: La _____ est la base de toute relation.
items.translation: (Trust is the basis of every relationship.)
items.explanation: 'La confiance' (trust/confidence) is feminine (-ance ending). CAREFUL: 'confiance' (trust) ≠ English 'confidence' (self-assurance). The French for English 'confidence' is 'l'assurance' or 'la confiance en soi.' This is a classic false friend. 'Faire confiance à quelqu'un' (to trust someone), 'avoir confiance en' (to have confidence in), 'la confiance en soi' (self-confidence). Meanwhile, 'une confidence' in French means 'a secret/confession shared privately.' These overlapping but shifted meanings require careful attention.

name: Vocabulaire Abstrait (Abstract Vocabulary)
description: Build advanced vocabulary for expressing abstract concepts, emotions, and philosophical ideas

name: Pronoms Toniques (Stressed Pronouns)

items.question: C'est _____ qui ai gagné.
items.translation: (It's me who won.)
items.explanation: After 'c'est,' you must use the stressed pronoun 'moi' instead of the subject pronoun 'je.' Stressed pronouns (moi, toi, lui, elle, nous, vous, eux, elles) are used for emphasis, after prepositions, and in compound subjects. This is a fundamental difference from English where 'I' can serve in many positions.

items.question: Je pense à _____.
items.translation: (I'm thinking about you.)
items.explanation: After prepositions like 'à,' 'de,' 'pour,' 'avec,' you must use stressed pronouns. 'Toi' is the stressed form of 'tu/te.' You cannot say *'Je pense à tu' — prepositions always require the stressed (disjunctive) form of the pronoun in French.

items.question: Qui veut du gâteau ? _____!
items.translation: (Who wants cake? Me!)
items.explanation: When a pronoun stands alone (without a verb), the stressed form is required. You cannot say *'Je!' by itself. Stressed pronouns function independently: 'Moi!' (Me!), 'Toi!' (You!), 'Lui!' (Him!). They carry enough weight to stand as complete answers or exclamations.

items.question: Mon frère et _____ allons au cinéma.
items.translation: (My brother and I are going to the cinema.)
items.explanation: In compound subjects (two or more people), French uses stressed pronouns: 'Mon frère et moi' (My brother and I). The verb then conjugates for 'nous' because 'moi' + another person = 'nous.' This differs from English where 'I' is used in compound subjects.

items.question: Elle est plus grande que _____.
items.translation: (She is taller than me.)
items.explanation: After 'que' in comparisons, French requires stressed pronouns. 'Plus grande que moi' (taller than me). While English informally uses 'me' and formally uses 'I,' French consistently uses the stressed pronoun form after comparison words like 'que.'

items.question: _____, je préfère le chocolat.
items.translation: (As for me, I prefer chocolate.)
items.explanation: When you want to emphasize or contrast the subject, place the stressed pronoun at the beginning followed by a comma, then restate with the subject pronoun: 'Moi, je préfère...' This emphatic construction is very common in spoken French to highlight personal preference or contrast with others.

items.question: C'est _____ qui a raison.
items.translation: (It's him who is right.)
items.explanation: After 'c'est,' use stressed pronouns for emphasis: 'C'est lui' (It's him), 'C'est elle' (It's her). The structure 'C'est + stressed pronoun + qui + verb' is a powerful emphatic construction in French, used to single someone out or correct a misunderstanding.

items.question: Ce cadeau est pour _____.
items.translation: (This gift is for her.)
items.explanation: After the preposition 'pour,' use stressed pronouns: 'pour elle' (for her), 'pour moi' (for me), 'pour eux' (for them). Note that 'lui' is only the stressed pronoun for 'he/him' — for 'she/her,' the stressed form is 'elle,' which is identical to the subject pronoun.

items.question: Eux, ils ne comprennent pas. Et _____, vous comprenez ?
items.translation: (They don't understand. And you, do you understand?)
items.explanation: The stressed pronoun for formal/plural 'you' is 'vous' — identical to the subject pronoun form. In emphatic constructions contrasting different people, stressed pronouns create clear distinctions: 'Eux... et vous...' Here 'vous' is used both as stressed emphasis and subject.

items.question: Je suis arrivé avant _____.
items.translation: (I arrived before them.)
items.explanation: After prepositions of time and place like 'avant' (before), 'après' (after), 'devant' (in front of), use stressed pronouns. 'Eux' is the masculine plural stressed pronoun. For feminine plural, use 'elles.' These forms are essential for natural-sounding prepositional phrases.

items.question: Ni toi ni _____ ne pouvons décider.
items.translation: (Neither you nor I can decide.)
items.explanation: In constructions with 'ni...ni...' (neither...nor...), stressed pronouns are used. 'Ni toi ni moi' follows the same compound subject rule — the verb conjugates to match 'nous' since toi + moi = nous. Stressed pronouns always appear after conjunctions and in coordinated structures.

items.question: Il a fait ce travail _____.
items.translation: (He did this work himself.)
items.explanation: Stressed pronouns combine with '-même' to create emphatic reflexive forms: moi-même (myself), toi-même (yourself), lui-même (himself), elle-même (herself), nous-mêmes, vous-mêmes, eux-mêmes, elles-mêmes. These emphasize that someone did something personally, without help.

items.question: On doit penser à _____ aussi.
items.translation: (One must think of oneself too.)
items.explanation: 'Soi' is the special stressed pronoun used with indefinite subjects like 'on,' 'chacun,' 'tout le monde,' or 'quelqu'un.' While 'lui/elle' refer to specific people, 'soi' refers to an unspecified person. 'Penser à soi' (to think of oneself) uses this indefinite stressed form.

items.question: Selon _____, le film était excellent.
items.translation: (According to her, the film was excellent.)
items.explanation: After the preposition 'selon' (according to), use stressed pronouns: 'selon moi,' 'selon toi,' 'selon lui,' 'selon elle.' This is one of many fixed prepositional expressions where stressed pronouns are obligatory. Others include 'chez moi,' 'grâce à toi,' and 'd'après lui.'

items.question: _____ aussi, tu veux venir ?
items.translation: (You too, do you want to come?)
items.explanation: When a pronoun appears with 'aussi' (also/too) or 'non plus' (neither), the stressed form is required: 'Moi aussi' (me too), 'Toi aussi' (you too), 'Lui non plus' (him neither). These are among the most frequently used stressed pronoun constructions in everyday French conversation.

description: Use moi, toi, lui, elle, nous, vous, eux, elles for emphasis, after prepositions, and in comparisons

name: Doubles Pronoms (Double Object Pronouns)

items.question: Je _____ le donne.
items.translation: (I give it to you.)
items.explanation: When two object pronouns appear together, there's a strict order. With 'me/te/se/nous/vous' + 'le/la/les,' the indirect pronoun comes first: 'Je te le donne' (I give it to you). The order is: me/te/se/nous/vous before le/la/les before lui/leur before y before en.

items.question: Il _____ la montre.
items.translation: (He shows it to us.)
items.explanation: When combining indirect object pronouns (nous) with direct object pronouns (la), 'nous' comes first: 'Il nous la montre.' The rule is that first/second person pronouns (me, te, nous, vous) always precede third-person direct objects (le, la, les) in French double pronoun constructions.

items.question: Elle le _____ explique.
items.translation: (She explains it to them.)
items.explanation: When combining 'le/la/les' with 'lui/leur,' the direct object pronoun comes first: 'Elle le leur explique.' This reverses the rule for first/second person — with third-person indirect objects (lui/leur), the order is le/la/les BEFORE lui/leur. This distinction is crucial for correct double pronoun placement.

items.question: Tu _____ en donnes ?
items.translation: (Are you giving me some?)
items.explanation: 'Me' becomes 'm'' before 'en' (which starts with a vowel): 'Tu m'en donnes.' When combining pronouns with 'en,' the indirect pronoun always comes first. Note the elision: me→m', te→t', se→s' before 'en.' This is mandatory in both written and spoken French.

items.question: Je vous _____ envoie demain.
items.translation: (I'll send them to you tomorrow.)
items.explanation: With 'vous' + 'les,' the indirect pronoun 'vous' comes first: 'Je vous les envoie.' Remember the double pronoun order rule: me/te/se/nous/vous always precede le/la/les. This order is consistent and never changes, regardless of the verb or tense used.

items.question: Ne me _____ dis pas !
items.translation: (Don't tell it to me!)
items.explanation: In negative commands, double pronouns stay before the verb, maintaining the standard order: 'Ne me le dis pas.' The 'ne' comes first, then the pronouns in their normal order, then the verb, then 'pas.' This is different from affirmative commands where pronouns follow the verb.

items.question: Donne-_____-moi !
items.translation: (Give it to me!)
items.explanation: In affirmative commands, pronouns follow the verb and are connected by hyphens. The order changes: direct object comes first, then indirect: 'Donne-le-moi!' Also note that 'me' becomes 'moi' and 'te' becomes 'toi' in affirmative commands. This is the opposite of regular sentence order.

items.question: Il y _____ a beaucoup.
items.translation: (There are a lot of them.)
items.explanation: 'Y' and 'en' can appear together in the fixed expression 'il y en a' (there is/are some of it/them). 'Y' always comes before 'en' — never the reverse. This construction is extremely common: 'Il y en a trois' (There are three of them), 'Il n'y en a plus' (There aren't any more).

items.question: Je _____ y emmène.
items.translation: (I'm taking you there.)
items.explanation: 'Te' becomes 't'' before 'y' (which starts with a vowel): 'Je t'y emmène.' The pronoun order places personal pronouns before 'y': me/te/se/nous/vous + y. This elision is mandatory in writing. In spoken French, these double pronoun constructions are very common with 'y' meaning 'there.'

items.question: Elle ne _____ les a pas rendus.
items.translation: (She didn't return them to us.)
items.explanation: In compound tenses with negation, double pronouns go between 'ne' and the auxiliary: 'Elle ne nous les a pas rendus.' The past participle 'rendus' agrees with 'les' (masculine plural direct object preceding the verb). Both pronoun placement and participle agreement rules apply simultaneously.

items.question: Je vais te _____ présenter.
items.translation: (I'm going to introduce her to you.)
items.explanation: With infinitive constructions, double pronouns go before the infinitive, not before the conjugated verb: 'Je vais te la présenter.' The pronouns always attach to the verb they logically depend on. Since 'présenter' takes both objects, the pronouns precede it, maintaining the standard order.

items.question: Il le lui _____ depuis longtemps.
items.translation: (He has been saying it to her for a long time.)
items.explanation: With 'le/la/les' + 'lui/leur,' the direct object comes first: 'Il le lui dit.' This order (direct before indirect) only applies when BOTH pronouns are third person. When one is first/second person (me, te, nous, vous), that one comes first regardless of direct/indirect function.

items.question: Montrez-_____-leur !
items.translation: (Show it to them!)
items.explanation: In affirmative commands with two third-person pronouns, direct comes before indirect: 'Montrez-le-leur!' All pronouns follow the verb with hyphens. Note: 'leur' never takes an 's' as a pronoun — 'leurs' only exists as a possessive adjective. This hyphenated chain can feel unusual but is standard French.

items.question: Ne le _____ dites pas.
items.translation: (Don't tell it to him/her.)
items.explanation: In negative statements, the order 'le/la/les + lui/leur' is maintained between 'ne' and the verb: 'Ne le lui dites pas.' The negation wraps around the entire pronoun-verb unit: ne + pronouns + verb + pas. Mastering this word order is essential for fluent negative constructions with multiple objects.

items.question: Elle s'_____ souvient.
items.translation: (She remembers it.)
items.explanation: 'Se souvenir de' (to remember) uses 'en' to replace the thing remembered because of the preposition 'de': 'Elle s'en souvient.' The reflexive 'se' comes first, then 'en.' When a reflexive verb is followed by 'en' or 'y,' the reflexive pronoun always precedes them. This is a very common pattern with pronominal verbs.

description: Master the order and placement of two object pronouns before a single verb

name: Pronoms Indéfinis (Indefinite Pronouns)

items.question: _____ est venu à la fête.
items.translation: (Everyone came to the party.)
items.explanation: 'Tout le monde' (everyone/everybody) is always singular in French — it takes singular verb conjugation: 'Tout le monde est venu,' NOT *'sont venus.' This is a common mistake for English speakers who associate 'everyone' with plural meaning. Think of it as 'all the world' — a singular collective concept.

items.question: _____ a téléphoné pour toi.
items.translation: (Someone called for you.)
items.explanation: 'Quelqu'un' (someone/somebody) is invariable and always singular. It refers to an unspecified person. Note the apostrophe in the middle — it's a contraction of 'quelque un.' For 'some people' (plural), use 'quelques-uns' (masculine) or 'quelques-unes' (feminine) instead.

items.question: _____ ne peut résoudre ce problème.
items.translation: (Nobody can solve this problem.)
items.explanation: 'Personne' as an indefinite pronoun means 'nobody/no one' and requires 'ne' before the verb: 'Personne ne peut...' Note that 'personne' without negation means 'person' (une personne = a person). As a negative pronoun, it's always singular and can also come after the verb: 'Je ne vois personne' (I see nobody).

items.question: Il n'y a _____ dans le réfrigérateur.
items.translation: (There is nothing in the fridge.)
items.explanation: 'Rien' means 'nothing' and pairs with 'ne': 'Il n'y a rien.' In compound tenses, 'rien' goes between the auxiliary and past participle: 'Je n'ai rien mangé' (I ate nothing). This differs from 'personne,' which goes after the past participle: 'Je n'ai vu personne.' The placement rule is essential.

items.question: _____ a ses propres problèmes.
items.translation: (Each person has their own problems.)
items.explanation: 'Chacun' (each one/everyone) is a pronoun and stands alone — don't confuse it with 'chaque' (each), which is an adjective needing a noun: 'chaque personne.' 'Chacun' is always singular: 'Chacun a...' The feminine form is 'chacune.' It emphasizes individuality within a group.

items.question: J'ai lu _____ les livres.
items.translation: (I read all the books.)
items.explanation: 'Tous' (all, masculine plural) is used before 'les' + plural noun: 'tous les livres.' Note: when 'tous' is a pronoun standing alone, the final 's' is pronounced: 'Ils sont tous [toos] venus.' But as an adjective before 'les,' the 's' is silent: 'tous [too] les jours.' This pronunciation rule is crucial.

items.question: _____ dit qu'il va pleuvoir.
items.translation: (They say it will rain. / It is said that it will rain.)
items.explanation: 'On' is a versatile indefinite pronoun meaning 'one/people/they/we' in general statements. 'On dit' (they say/it is said) is very common. Despite its broad meaning, 'on' always takes third-person singular conjugation. In informal French, 'on' frequently replaces 'nous': 'On y va!' (Let's go!) instead of 'Nous y allons.'

items.question: Les uns parlaient, les _____ écoutaient.
items.translation: (Some were speaking, others were listening.)
items.explanation: 'Les uns...les autres' (some...others) is a reciprocal or contrastive pronoun pair used to distinguish two groups. It can also express reciprocity: 'Ils s'aident les uns les autres' (They help each other). The feminine forms are 'les unes...les autres.' This elegant construction has no direct equivalent in English.

items.question: _____ de mes amis habitent à Paris.
items.translation: (Some of my friends live in Paris.)
items.explanation: 'Certains' (some/certain ones) is a plural indefinite pronoun. As a pronoun, it stands alone or with 'de': 'Certains de mes amis.' Don't confuse with the adjective 'certain' which precedes a noun: 'un certain nombre.' The feminine plural form is 'certaines.' It implies a definite but unspecified subset.

items.question: _____ sont arrivés en retard.
items.translation: (Several arrived late.)
items.explanation: 'Plusieurs' (several) is an invariable pronoun — it has no gender variation and is always plural. Unlike 'quelques' (which is an adjective needing a noun: 'quelques personnes'), 'plusieurs' can stand as a pronoun: 'Plusieurs sont venus.' It indicates more than two but not a large number.

items.question: _____ n'est parfait.
items.translation: (No one is perfect.)
items.explanation: 'Nul' (no one) is a formal/literary synonym for 'personne.' Like 'personne,' it requires 'ne' and takes singular verbs: 'Nul ne sait' (No one knows). It appears mainly in proverbs, legal texts, and formal writing. 'Nul n'est censé ignorer la loi' (No one is supposed to be ignorant of the law) is a famous legal maxim.

items.question: Je n'en veux _____.
items.translation: (I don't want any.)
items.explanation: 'Aucun' (none/not one) is used with 'ne' and emphasizes zero quantity more strongly than 'pas de': 'Je n'en veux aucun.' Unlike 'rien' (nothing) and 'personne' (nobody), 'aucun' refers to quantity and agrees in gender: 'aucune idée' (no idea). The plural 'aucuns/aucunes' exists but is extremely rare.

items.question: Il y a _____ chose que je veux te dire.
items.translation: (There is something I want to tell you.)
items.explanation: 'Quelque chose' (something) is a fixed expression treated as a masculine indefinite pronoun. Adjectives modifying it require 'de': 'quelque chose de beau' (something beautiful), 'quelque chose d'intéressant' (something interesting). Never say *'quelque chose belle' — always use 'de' + masculine adjective.

items.question: Tu veux boire _____ ?
items.translation: (Do you want to drink something?)
items.explanation: 'Quelque chose' functions as a single unit meaning 'something' or 'anything' in questions and affirmative sentences. In questions, it translates as 'anything': 'Tu veux quelque chose ?' (Do you want anything?). For 'nothing,' switch to 'rien': 'Je ne veux rien boire.' Note the contrasting pair: quelque chose ↔ rien.

items.question: N'importe _____ peut le faire.
items.translation: (Anyone can do it.)
items.explanation: 'N'importe qui' (anyone/anybody) expresses indifference about which person. This 'n'importe' construction is versatile: 'n'importe quoi' (anything), 'n'importe où' (anywhere), 'n'importe quand' (anytime), 'n'importe comment' (anyhow). Each combines 'n'importe' with a question word to create powerful indefinite expressions.

description: Use quelqu'un, personne, quelque chose, rien, chacun, and tout for vague or general reference

name: Pronoms Possessifs (Possessive Pronouns)

items.question: C'est ton livre ? Oui, c'est _____.
items.translation: (Is it your book? Yes, it's mine.)
items.explanation: Possessive pronouns replace 'possessive adjective + noun.' 'Le mien' replaces 'mon livre.' They have article + possessive form: le mien, la mienne, les miens, les miennes (mine). Unlike possessive adjectives (mon, ma, mes), possessive pronouns stand alone and must agree with the noun they replace, not the owner.

items.question: J'ai ma voiture. Où est _____ ?
items.translation: (I have my car. Where is yours?)
items.explanation: 'La tienne' replaces 'ta voiture' (your car). Since 'voiture' is feminine singular, the pronoun must be feminine: 'la tienne.' The full paradigm for 'yours' is: le tien (m.sg.), la tienne (f.sg.), les tiens (m.pl.), les tiennes (f.pl.). Agreement follows the possessed object, not the possessor.

items.question: Nos enfants jouent avec _____.
items.translation: (Our children play with theirs.)
items.explanation: 'Les leurs' replaces 'leurs enfants' (their children). After prepositions, the full possessive pronoun (with article) is used: 'avec les leurs.' The article contracts normally: 'à + les leurs' = 'aux leurs,' 'de + les leurs' = 'des leurs.' This contraction follows standard article rules.

items.question: Ma maison est grande. _____ est petite.
items.translation: (My house is big. His/Hers is small.)
items.explanation: 'La sienne' can mean 'his' or 'hers' — French possessive pronouns don't distinguish the owner's gender, only the gender of the possessed noun. 'La sienne' (his/her, f.sg.) replaces 'sa maison.' Context determines whether it means 'his' or 'hers.' This is a key difference from English.

items.question: Vos résultats sont meilleurs que _____.
items.translation: (Your results are better than ours.)
items.explanation: 'Les nôtres' replaces 'nos résultats.' Note the circumflex accent: 'nôtre/nôtres' (pronoun) vs. 'notre' (adjective). This accent changes pronunciation too — 'nôtre' has a longer, more closed 'o' sound. The same applies to 'vôtre/vôtres' vs. 'votre.' The accent is a reliable visual cue.

items.question: Ce n'est pas mon problème, c'est _____.
items.translation: (It's not my problem, it's yours.)
items.explanation: 'Le vôtre' replaces 'votre problème' (your problem). With 'c'est,' possessive pronouns follow naturally: 'C'est le mien/le tien/le sien/le nôtre/le vôtre/le leur.' This construction is very common for establishing ownership or responsibility in conversation: 'C'est le vôtre, pas le mien.'

items.question: Il a pris ses affaires et _____.
items.translation: (He took his things and mine.)
items.explanation: 'Les miennes' replaces 'mes affaires' (my things). Since 'affaires' is feminine plural, the pronoun must be 'les miennes' (not 'les miens'). In coordinated structures, possessive pronouns let you avoid repetition: 'ses affaires et les miennes' instead of 'ses affaires et mes affaires.'

items.question: Ton idée est intéressante mais je préfère _____.
items.translation: (Your idea is interesting but I prefer his/hers.)
items.explanation: After verbs like 'préférer,' possessive pronouns function as regular direct objects: 'je préfère la sienne.' The article is an integral part of the pronoun — never drop it. You cannot say *'je préfère sienne.' The article also contracts with 'à' and 'de': 'Je pense au sien' (I'm thinking about his/hers).

items.question: Chacun _____ tour.
items.translation: (Each one's turn. / Everyone takes a turn.)
items.explanation: While 'à son tour' uses a possessive adjective (not pronoun), the expression 'chacun le sien' (to each his own) uses the possessive pronoun: 'À chacun le sien.' This common French saying illustrates how possessive pronouns convey philosophical ideas concisely. The possessive pronoun carries strong ownership emphasis.

items.question: J'ai oublié mon parapluie. Tu peux me prêter _____ ?
items.translation: (I forgot my umbrella. Can you lend me yours?)
items.explanation: 'Le tien' replaces 'ton parapluie' — masculine singular because 'parapluie' is masculine. In requests and practical situations, possessive pronouns are essential: 'Prête-moi le tien' is much more natural than repeating the noun. This is one of the most common everyday uses of possessive pronouns in French.

items.question: Ses parents habitent à Lyon. Et _____ ?
items.translation: (Her parents live in Lyon. And yours?)
items.explanation: 'Les tiens' replaces 'tes parents' — masculine plural because 'parents' is masculine. In follow-up questions, possessive pronouns create natural, concise exchanges: 'Et les tiens ?' (And yours?). This elliptical style (omitting the verb) is very common in spoken French and relies on possessive pronouns.

items.question: Cette opinion n'est pas la mienne, c'est _____.
items.translation: (This opinion is not mine, it's his/hers.)
items.explanation: Contrasting possession uses opposing possessive pronouns: 'pas la mienne...la sienne.' This structure is elegant and common in debates: 'Ce n'est pas ma faute, c'est la sienne' (It's not my fault, it's his/hers). The parallel structure naturally highlights the contrast between possessors.

items.question: Ils font les _____ sans se plaindre.
items.translation: (They do their share without complaining.)
items.explanation: In the expression 'faire les leurs/les siens' (to do one's part/share), the possessive pronoun is used idiomatically. French has several fixed expressions with possessive pronouns: 'y mettre du sien' (to do one's part), 'les siens' (one's family/loved ones), 'à la vôtre!' (cheers!/to yours!).

items.question: Ce sac est à qui ? C'est _____ de Marie.
items.translation: (Whose bag is this? It's Marie's.)
items.explanation: To say 'Marie's' (possessive with a name), French uses demonstrative pronouns + 'de': 'celui de Marie' (the one belonging to Marie). You cannot use possessive pronouns with proper names (*'le sien de Marie'). This 'celui/celle de + name' construction is the standard way to express named possession.

items.question: À la _____ !
items.translation: (Cheers! / To yours!)
items.explanation: 'À la vôtre!' is a traditional French toast meaning 'To yours!' (implying 'to your health'). It uses the possessive pronoun without the article 'la' being part of the pronoun — here 'à la' is the preposition phrase. Similarly, 'À la tienne!' is the informal version. These toasting expressions are deeply embedded in French social culture.

description: Replace possessive adjective + noun with le mien, la tienne, les siens, and other possessive pronouns

name: Pronoms Interrogatifs (Interrogative Pronouns)

items.question: _____ faites-vous ce soir ?
items.translation: (What are you doing tonight?)
items.explanation: 'Que' (what) is used at the beginning of questions with inversion: 'Que faites-vous ?' It's the standard interrogative pronoun for things/actions in formal French. In informal speech, 'Qu'est-ce que' replaces it: 'Qu'est-ce que vous faites ?' Both mean the same thing but differ in register.

items.question: À _____ pensez-vous ?
items.translation: (What are you thinking about?)
items.explanation: After prepositions (à, de, avec, pour, sur), use 'quoi' instead of 'que': 'À quoi pensez-vous ?' You cannot say *'À que pensez-vous ?' The rule is simple: 'que' begins questions, 'quoi' follows prepositions. 'De quoi parlez-vous ?' (What are you talking about?), 'Avec quoi mangez-vous ?' (What do you eat with?).

items.question: _____ a dit cela ?
items.translation: (Who said that?)
items.explanation: 'Qui' (who) asks about people and can be subject or object. As subject: 'Qui a dit cela ?' — no inversion needed because 'qui' IS the subject. As object with inversion: 'Qui avez-vous vu ?' (Whom did you see?). 'Qui' never changes form regardless of gender, number, or function — it's always 'qui' for people.

items.question: _____ robe préférez-vous ?
items.translation: (Which dress do you prefer?)
items.explanation: 'Quel/quelle/quels/quelles' (which/what) is an interrogative adjective that agrees with the noun it modifies. 'Robe' is feminine singular, so 'quelle' is required. The four forms are: quel (m.sg.), quelle (f.sg.), quels (m.pl.), quelles (f.pl.). Unlike 'qui' and 'que,' 'quel' always accompanies a noun.

items.question: _____ est votre nom ?
items.translation: (What is your name?)
items.explanation: 'Quel' before 'être' asks for identification or definition: 'Quel est votre nom ?' This use of 'quel' + 'être' is extremely common: 'Quelle est votre adresse ?' (What is your address?), 'Quels sont vos projets ?' (What are your plans?). Here 'quel' functions as a predicate adjective agreeing with the subject.

items.question: _____ de ces deux films avez-vous vu ?
items.translation: (Which of these two films did you see?)
items.explanation: 'Lequel' (which one) is an interrogative pronoun used when choosing from a specific set. It replaces 'quel + noun' when the noun is understood. It has four forms: lequel (m.sg.), laquelle (f.sg.), lesquels (m.pl.), lesquelles (f.pl.). It contracts with 'à' and 'de': auquel, duquel, auxquels, desquels.

items.question: À _____ de ces restaurants allons-nous ?
items.translation: (To which of these restaurants are we going?)
items.explanation: 'Lequel' contracts with 'à': auquel (m.sg.), à laquelle (f.sg.), auxquels (m.pl.), auxquelles (f.pl.). 'À laquelle' does NOT contract — only the masculine forms do. 'Auquel de ces restaurants...' is asking someone to choose a specific option from a known set, making it more precise than 'à quel restaurant.'

items.question: Qu'est-ce _____ se passe ?
items.translation: (What is happening?)
items.explanation: 'Qu'est-ce qui' asks about THINGS as SUBJECTS: 'Qu'est-ce qui se passe ?' (What's happening?). The pattern is: 'Qu'est-ce qui + verb' (thing as subject) vs. 'Qu'est-ce que + subject + verb' (thing as object). The 'qui' indicates subject function, 'que' indicates object function. This distinction is fundamental to French question formation.

items.question: Qui est-ce _____ vous avez rencontré ?
items.translation: (Whom did you meet?)
items.explanation: 'Qui est-ce que' asks about PEOPLE as OBJECTS: 'Qui est-ce que vous avez rencontré ?' vs. 'Qui est-ce qui + verb' (people as subject). The system is logical: first word (qui/que) indicates person/thing, last word (qui/que) indicates subject/object. 'Qui est-ce que' = person + object.

items.question: _____ est-ce que tu veux manger ?
items.translation: (What do you want to eat?)
items.explanation: 'Qu'est-ce que' (what, as object) is perhaps the most common question structure in informal French. 'Que' becomes 'qu'' before 'est-ce': 'Qu'est-ce que tu veux ?' This long form avoids inversion and sounds natural in conversation. It's equivalent to the shorter formal form 'Que veux-tu ?' but far more commonly used in daily speech.

items.question: De _____ as-tu besoin ?
items.translation: (What do you need?)
items.explanation: After 'de,' use 'quoi' for things: 'De quoi as-tu besoin ?' (What do you need? — literally 'Of what do you have need?'). The verb 'avoir besoin de' (to need) requires the preposition 'de,' which then requires 'quoi' instead of 'que.' Many French verbs have fixed prepositions that determine which interrogative form to use.

items.question: Pour _____ travaillez-vous ?
items.translation: (Who do you work for?)
items.explanation: After prepositions referring to people, use 'qui': 'Pour qui travaillez-vous ?' (Who do you work for?), 'Avec qui sortez-vous ?' (Who are you going out with?), 'Chez qui habitez-vous ?' (Whose place do you live at?). Remember: 'qui' = people, 'quoi' = things after prepositions. This rule is consistent and reliable.

items.question: Ces deux robes sont belles. _____ préfères-tu ?
items.translation: (These two dresses are beautiful. Which one do you prefer?)
items.explanation: 'Laquelle' (which one, f.sg.) replaces 'quelle robe.' Since we're choosing between specific dresses (feminine), 'laquelle' is correct. Using 'lequel/laquelle' instead of 'quel/quelle' signals you're asking someone to choose from an already-mentioned set, making the question more specific and contextually precise.

items.question: _____ heure est-il ?
items.translation: (What time is it?)
items.explanation: 'Quelle heure est-il ?' is a fixed expression for asking the time. 'Heure' is feminine, requiring 'quelle.' Other common 'quel' expressions: 'Quel temps fait-il ?' (What's the weather?), 'Quel âge avez-vous ?' (How old are you?), 'Quelle chance !' (What luck!). In exclamations, 'quel' expresses surprise or admiration.

items.question: Je ne sais pas _____ choisir.
items.translation: (I don't know which one to choose.)
items.explanation: 'Lequel' can also function in indirect questions (embedded questions): 'Je ne sais pas lequel choisir.' Unlike direct questions, indirect questions use normal word order after the interrogative word. This embedded use of 'lequel' is common with verbs like 'savoir,' 'demander,' 'se demander,' and 'dire.'

description: Master qui, que, quoi, lequel and their forms for asking precise questions in all registers

name: Passé Simple

items.question: Il _____ la porte et entra dans la pièce.
items.translation: (He opened the door and entered the room.)
items.explanation: The passé simple of 'ouvrir' (to open) is 'ouvrit' for the third-person singular. This literary past tense is used almost exclusively in formal writing, novels, and historical texts. Unlike the passé composé used in speech, the passé simple conveys a completed action in a narrative context. Regular -ir verbs form it with -is, -is, -it, -îmes, -îtes, -irent.

items.question: Les soldats _____ toute la nuit.
items.translation: (The soldiers marched all night.)
items.explanation: The passé simple of 'marcher' (to march/walk) for third-person plural is 'marchèrent.' Regular -er verbs follow the pattern: -ai, -as, -a, -âmes, -âtes, -èrent. This tense appears in literary prose to narrate successive events in the past. In everyday conversation, French speakers always use the passé composé instead.

items.question: Elle _____ un cri de surprise.
items.translation: (She let out a cry of surprise.)
items.explanation: 'Poussa' is the passé simple of 'pousser' (to push/let out). For -er verbs, third-person singular ends in -a: je poussai, tu poussas, il/elle poussa. This tense is the backbone of French literary narration, used to move the story forward with completed, sequential actions. It often contrasts with the imparfait for background descriptions.

items.question: Nous _____ la vérité bien des années plus tard.
items.translation: (We learned the truth many years later.)
items.explanation: The passé simple of 'apprendre' (to learn) follows the irregular pattern of 'prendre': je pris, tu pris, il prit, nous prîmes, vous prîtes, ils prirent. For 'apprendre': j'appris, nous apprîmes. The circumflex accent on î in the first-person plural is a characteristic feature of the passé simple that helps distinguish it from other tenses.

items.question: Le roi _____ ses conseillers.
items.translation: (The king summoned his advisors.)
items.explanation: 'Convoqua' is the passé simple of 'convoquer' (to summon/convene). As a regular -er verb, it follows the standard pattern. The passé simple is essential for reading classic French literature — from Victor Hugo to Albert Camus. While you rarely need to produce it in speech, recognizing it is crucial for understanding written French at an advanced level.

items.question: Ils _____ dans la forêt pendant trois jours.
items.translation: (They lived in the forest for three days.)
items.explanation: The passé simple of 'vivre' (to live) is highly irregular: je vécus, tu vécus, il vécut, nous vécûmes, vous vécûtes, ils vécurent. Many irregular verbs in the passé simple use -us endings. This tense emphasizes the bounded nature of the action — they lived there for exactly three days, a completed timeframe.

items.question: Marie _____ de joie en apprenant la nouvelle.
items.translation: (Marie cried with joy upon hearing the news.)
items.explanation: 'Pleura' is the passé simple of 'pleurer' (to cry), a regular -er verb. In literary French, the passé simple often appears with participle phrases like 'en apprenant' (upon learning). The combination creates elegant prose that you'll encounter throughout French literature. Notice how the passé simple punctuates a specific moment in the narrative.

items.question: Le professeur _____ un long discours.
items.translation: (The professor gave a long speech.)
items.explanation: The passé simple of 'faire' (to do/make) is one of the most common irregular forms: je fis, tu fis, il fit, nous fîmes, vous fîtes, ils firent. 'Faire' is among the handful of verbs whose passé simple you should memorize, as they appear constantly in French texts. The short, one-syllable 'fit' creates a punchy, decisive narrative effect.

items.question: Soudain, la lumière _____.
items.translation: (Suddenly, the light went out.)
items.explanation: The passé simple of 's'éteindre' (to go out/extinguish) is 's'éteignit.' Verbs ending in -indre form their passé simple with -ignis: j'éteignis, il éteignit. The adverb 'soudain' (suddenly) naturally pairs with the passé simple, which captures sudden, punctual events. This combination is a hallmark of French narrative style.

items.question: Les enfants _____ dans le jardin.
items.translation: (The children ran in the garden.)
items.explanation: The passé simple of 'courir' (to run) uses the -us stem: je courus, tu courus, il courut, nous courûmes, vous courûtes, ils coururent. Note the double 'r' that's characteristic of 'courir' in most tenses. The passé simple here indicates a bounded, completed action rather than an ongoing activity (which would use the imparfait: 'couraient').

items.question: Elle _____ la lettre sans dire un mot.
items.translation: (She read the letter without saying a word.)
items.explanation: The passé simple of 'lire' (to read) is: je lus, tu lus, il/elle lut, nous lûmes, vous lûtes, ils lurent. Like 'vivre' and 'courir,' it uses the -us pattern. 'Lut' is extremely common in novels — narrating a character reading something is a frequent literary device. The circumflex on 'lûmes' marks the first and second person plural.

items.question: Le navire _____ à l'aube.
items.translation: (The ship departed at dawn.)
items.explanation: The passé simple of 'partir' (to leave/depart) follows the -ir verb pattern: je partis, tu partis, il partit. In historical and maritime narratives, you'll frequently encounter the passé simple to describe decisive moments like departures, arrivals, and battles. The combination with 'à l'aube' (at dawn) anchors the action at a specific point in time.

items.question: On _____ un bruit étrange venant de la cave.
items.translation: (A strange noise was heard coming from the cellar.)
items.explanation: The passé simple of 'entendre' (to hear) follows the -re verb pattern: j'entendis, tu entendis, il entendit. The pronoun 'on' is commonly used in literary French as a narrative device, creating a sense of generality — 'one heard' rather than specifying who. This construction with 'on' + passé simple is a classic feature of French mystery and suspense writing.

items.question: Le poète _____ ses plus beaux vers cette nuit-là.
items.translation: (The poet wrote his finest verses that night.)
items.explanation: The passé simple of 'écrire' (to write) is: j'écrivis, tu écrivis, il écrivit, nous écrivîmes, vous écrivîtes, ils écrivirent. Like many -ire verbs, it uses the -ivis pattern. In literary contexts, the passé simple elevates the register and gives weight to the action. 'Cette nuit-là' (that night) functions as a temporal anchor typical of narrative prose.

items.question: Les voyageurs _____ enfin à destination.
items.translation: (The travelers finally arrived at their destination.)
items.explanation: 'Arrivèrent' is the passé simple of 'arriver' (to arrive), third-person plural. As a regular -er verb, it takes -èrent in the plural. The adverb 'enfin' (finally) emphasizes the completion of the journey, pairing naturally with the passé simple's perfective aspect. This tense signals that the action is over and done — the story can now move forward to what happens next.

description: Recognize and understand the literary simple past tense used in formal writing and literature

name: Passé Antérieur

items.question: Quand il _____ son repas, il sortit.
items.translation: (When he had finished his meal, he went out.)
items.explanation: The passé antérieur ('eut terminé') is formed with the passé simple of the auxiliary (avoir → eut) + past participle. It expresses an action completed immediately before another past action in the passé simple. It's the literary equivalent of the plus-que-parfait but used exclusively in formal written French with time conjunctions like 'quand,' 'dès que,' 'après que,' and 'lorsque.'

items.question: Dès qu'elle _____, tout le monde applaudit.
items.translation: (As soon as she had finished singing, everyone applauded.)
items.explanation: The passé antérieur with 'dès que' (as soon as) indicates immediate anteriority: the singing ended right before the applause began. The construction is: dès que + passé antérieur, main clause in passé simple. This is one of the most common contexts for the passé antérieur in literature. The auxiliary 'eut' (passé simple of 'avoir') signals the literary register.

items.question: Lorsqu'ils _____ la montagne, ils s'arrêtèrent pour contempler le paysage.
items.translation: (When they had climbed the mountain, they stopped to contemplate the landscape.)
items.explanation: 'Eurent gravi' uses 'eurent' (passé simple of 'avoir,' third-person plural) + 'gravi' (past participle of 'gravir'). The passé antérieur works with 'lorsque' (when) to sequence events precisely: first the climbing was completed, then the stopping occurred. This tense creates a tight chronological link between two past events in literary narrative.

items.question: Après qu'il _____ la porte, le silence revint.
items.translation: (After he had closed the door, silence returned.)
items.explanation: 'Après que' (after) triggers the passé antérieur in literary French to show that one completed action preceded another. Note that 'après que' takes the indicative (not subjunctive), unlike 'avant que.' This is a common error even among native French speakers. The passé antérieur here ('eut fermé') precisely sequences: door closing → silence returning.

items.question: Aussitôt qu'elle _____ dans la salle, elle remarqua le changement.
items.translation: (As soon as she had entered the room, she noticed the change.)
items.explanation: 'Entrer' uses être as its auxiliary, so the passé antérieur is 'fut entrée' (passé simple of être + past participle with feminine agreement). Verbs of motion (entrer, sortir, arriver, partir, etc.) always take être. 'Aussitôt que' (as soon as) is another conjunction that pairs with the passé antérieur to express immediate anteriority.

items.question: Quand nous _____ le travail, nous pûmes enfin nous reposer.
items.translation: (When we had completed the work, we could finally rest.)
items.explanation: The first-person plural passé antérieur uses 'eûmes' (passé simple of 'avoir,' nous form) + past participle. The circumflex accent on 'eûmes' is a characteristic marker of the passé simple/passé antérieur. The main clause uses 'pûmes' (passé simple of 'pouvoir'). Both the subordinate and main clauses maintain the literary register consistently.

items.question: Une fois qu'il _____ sa décision, rien ne put le faire changer d'avis.
items.translation: (Once he had made his decision, nothing could make him change his mind.)
items.explanation: 'Prendre une décision' (to make a decision) uses 'eut pris' in the passé antérieur. 'Une fois que' (once) is another temporal conjunction that triggers this tense in literary writing. The idiomatic expression 'changer d'avis' (to change one's mind) is very common in French. Together with the passé antérieur, it creates an emphatic sense of finality.

items.question: Sitôt qu'ils _____ les premiers coups de feu, les habitants se réfugièrent dans les caves.
items.translation: (As soon as they had heard the first gunshots, the inhabitants took shelter in the cellars.)
items.explanation: 'Sitôt que' (as soon as) is a more formal variant of 'dès que' and naturally pairs with the passé antérieur. 'Eurent entendu' (had heard) shows the hearing was immediately followed by taking shelter. This construction is typical of historical and war narratives in French literature. The passé antérieur creates urgency by tightly linking cause and effect.

items.question: Quand elle _____ au sommet, elle fut émerveillée par la vue.
items.translation: (When she had arrived at the summit, she was amazed by the view.)
items.explanation: 'Arriver' takes être as its auxiliary, so the passé antérieur is 'fut arrivée' (with feminine agreement on the past participle). The agreement rule applies just as it does with the passé composé and plus-que-parfait: verbs conjugated with être agree in gender and number with the subject. This is a consistent rule across all compound tenses in French.

items.question: Dès qu'il _____ la vérité, il décida de tout avouer.
items.translation: (As soon as he had discovered the truth, he decided to confess everything.)
items.explanation: 'Découvrir' has the irregular past participle 'découvert.' The passé antérieur 'eut découvert' maintains the literary register established by 'décida' (passé simple) in the main clause. When reading French novels, you'll notice that the passé antérieur appears almost exclusively in subordinate clauses introduced by temporal conjunctions, never in main clauses.

items.question: Lorsqu'elle _____ de la voiture, il se mit à pleuvoir.
items.translation: (When she had gotten out of the car, it started to rain.)
items.explanation: 'Descendre' (to get out/descend) takes être as its auxiliary when used intransitively (without a direct object). The passé antérieur 'fut descendue' includes feminine agreement. 'Se mettre à' (to start/begin) is a key idiomatic expression followed by an infinitive. The sequence shows the temporal relationship: she got out first, then the rain began.

items.question: Après qu'ils _____ toutes les provisions, ils repartirent.
items.translation: (After they had gathered all the supplies, they set off again.)
items.explanation: 'Rassembler' (to gather/assemble) forms a regular passé antérieur with 'eurent' + 'rassemblé.' The prefix 're-' in 'repartirent' (set off again) indicates repetition — they departed once more. The passé antérieur in the subordinate clause and passé simple in the main clause create the standard literary past-tense narrative structure in French.

items.question: À peine _____-il _____ que le téléphone sonna.
items.translation: (He had barely sat down when the telephone rang.)
items.explanation: 'À peine' (barely/scarcely) + passé antérieur + inversion + 'que' is an elegant literary construction meaning 'no sooner had...than.' 'S'asseoir' (to sit down) uses être, giving 'fut assis.' This inverted structure is quintessentially literary French and appears frequently in novels. It emphasizes how little time elapsed between the two actions.

items.question: Quand le soleil _____, les oiseaux commencèrent à chanter.
items.translation: (When the sun had risen, the birds began to sing.)
items.explanation: 'Se lever' (to rise) is a reflexive verb that takes être. Its passé antérieur is 'se fut levé': reflexive pronoun + passé simple of être + past participle. With reflexive verbs, the past participle generally agrees with the subject when the reflexive pronoun is a direct object. This dawn scene is a classic literary setting where the passé antérieur sequences natural events.

items.question: Dès que nous _____ de l'avion, nous sentîmes la chaleur tropicale.
items.translation: (As soon as we had gotten off the plane, we felt the tropical heat.)
items.explanation: 'Descendre' takes être when intransitive, giving 'fûmes descendus' (first-person plural, with masculine plural agreement). The circumflex on 'fûmes' marks the passé simple of être (nous fûmes). 'Sentîmes' (passé simple of 'sentir') maintains the literary register. This tense pairing — passé antérieur + passé simple — is the gold standard for sequencing events in formal French narrative.

description: Identify the past anterior tense in literary texts, used with temporal conjunctions

name: Subjonctif Imparfait

items.question: Il fallait qu'il _____ avant la nuit.
items.translation: (It was necessary that he leave before nightfall.)
items.explanation: The subjonctif imparfait of 'partir' is formed from the passé simple stem (parti-) + endings: -sse, -sses, -^t, -ssions, -ssiez, -ssent. For il/elle: 'partît' (with circumflex). This tense is used in literary French when the main verb is in a past tense (here 'fallait'). In modern French, the present subjunctive ('parte') replaces it in speech, but it remains essential for reading literature.

items.question: Je souhaitais qu'elle _____ heureuse.
items.translation: (I wished that she were happy.)
items.explanation: The subjonctif imparfait of 'être' is: fusse, fusses, fût, fussions, fussiez, fussent. 'Fût' (with circumflex on û) is the third-person singular. When the main verb is in the imparfait ('souhaitais'), literary French requires the subjonctif imparfait in the subordinate clause. This is called 'la concordance des temps' (sequence of tenses), a rule strictly observed in formal writing.

items.question: Bien qu'il _____ fatigué, il continua de travailler.
items.translation: (Although he was tired, he continued working.)
