items.explanation: 'Bien que' (although) always requires the subjunctive. In literary French with a main verb in a past tense ('continua,' passé simple), the subjonctif imparfait is expected. 'Fût' elegantly conveys the concessive meaning. In everyday French, you'd simply use 'soit' regardless of the main clause tense. The subjonctif imparfait adds gravitas and formality to the prose.

items.question: Le directeur exigea que tous les employés _____ à l'heure.
items.translation: (The director demanded that all employees arrive on time.)
items.explanation: The subjonctif imparfait of 'arriver' for the third-person plural is 'arrivassent.' Regular -er verbs take -asse, -asses, -ât, -assions, -assiez, -assent. After 'exigea' (passé simple of 'exiger'), literary concordance requires the subjonctif imparfait. While this form sounds archaic to modern ears, recognizing it is essential for understanding classical French texts.

items.question: Il était peu probable qu'il _____ le faire seul.
items.translation: (It was unlikely that he could do it alone.)
items.explanation: The subjonctif imparfait of 'pouvoir' is: pusse, pusses, pût, pussions, pussiez, pussent. The circumflex on 'pût' distinguishes it from 'put' (passé simple). This is one of the most commonly encountered subjonctif imparfait forms because 'pouvoir' appears so frequently after expressions of doubt, possibility, and necessity. The distinction between 'pût' and 'put' is crucial in literary French.

items.question: Je craignais qu'il ne _____ trop tard.
items.translation: (I feared he might come too late.)
items.explanation: The subjonctif imparfait of 'venir' is: vinsse, vinsses, vînt, vinssions, vinssiez, vinssent. Note the 'ne' explétif after 'craindre que' — this 'ne' doesn't negate but is a formal literary convention after verbs of fearing. 'Vînt' with its circumflex accent is a hallmark of elevated French style. The combination of 'ne' explétif + subjonctif imparfait marks the highest register.

items.question: On aurait voulu qu'il _____ la vérité.
items.translation: (One would have wanted him to know the truth.)
items.explanation: The subjonctif imparfait of 'savoir' is: susse, susses, sût, sussions, sussiez, sussent. 'Sût' (with circumflex) is used here because the main clause has a past conditional ('aurait voulu'). This form of 'savoir' in the subjunctive is relatively common in literary French, especially after verbs expressing desire, will, or preference in a past context.

items.question: Avant qu'il _____ répondre, elle avait déjà quitté la pièce.
items.translation: (Before he could respond, she had already left the room.)
items.explanation: 'Avant que' (before) requires the subjunctive, and with a past-tense context (plus-que-parfait 'avait quitté'), literary French calls for 'pût.' The circumflex distinguishes this subjunctive form from 'put' (passé simple indicative of 'pouvoir'). This distinction — pût (subjunctive) vs. put (indicative) — is one of the few cases where the circumflex carries grammatical significance.

items.question: Elle cherchait quelqu'un qui _____ l'aider.
items.translation: (She was looking for someone who could help her.)
items.explanation: After an indefinite antecedent ('quelqu'un qui') with a search/desire verb in the past ('cherchait'), the subjonctif imparfait is required in literary French. The subjunctive signals that the person sought may not exist or hasn't been found yet. In spoken French, 'puisse' would replace 'pût,' but in writing, the concordance des temps governs the choice.

items.question: Le juge ordonna qu'on _____ le prisonnier.
items.translation: (The judge ordered that the prisoner be released.)
items.explanation: The subjonctif imparfait of 'libérer' is regular: libérasse, libérasses, libérât, libérassions, libérassiez, libérassent. After 'ordonna que' (ordered that), the subjunctive is mandatory. The circumflex on 'libérât' (third-person singular) is the hallmark of the subjonctif imparfait for -er verbs. This legal/judicial context is one where the subjonctif imparfait still occasionally appears in contemporary formal writing.

items.question: Quoiqu'il _____ la réponse, il préféra se taire.
items.translation: (Although he knew the answer, he preferred to remain silent.)
items.explanation: 'Quoique' (although/even though) is another conjunction that always takes the subjunctive. With 'préféra' (passé simple) in the main clause, literary concordance requires 'sût.' The verb 'se taire' (to remain silent) is an important reflexive verb — very different from 'taire' (to silence someone). The subjonctif imparfait after concessive conjunctions is a strong marker of literary style.

items.question: Il ne semblait pas que cette solution _____ acceptable.
items.translation: (It did not seem that this solution was acceptable.)
items.explanation: 'Il ne semble pas que' (it doesn't seem that) triggers the subjunctive because it expresses doubt. With the main verb in the imparfait ('semblait'), literary French uses the subjonctif imparfait 'fût.' This is one of the most elegant and recognizable uses of the subjonctif imparfait — judging whether something meets a standard or expectation. In modern speech, 'soit' would be used.

items.question: Pour qu'il _____ comprendre, il fallait tout lui expliquer.
items.translation: (For him to understand, everything had to be explained to him.)
items.explanation: 'Pour que' (in order that/so that) always requires the subjunctive in French. With 'fallait' (imparfait) in the main clause, the subjonctif imparfait 'pût' maintains concordance. 'Tout lui expliquer' (to explain everything to him) shows the indirect object pronoun 'lui' placed before the infinitive. This is standard word order with infinitive constructions in French.

items.question: Sans qu'il le _____, une révolution se préparait.
items.translation: (Without his knowing it, a revolution was being prepared.)
items.explanation: 'Sans que' (without) requires the subjunctive and often takes the 'ne' explétif in highly formal registers (though it's optional here). 'Sût' (subjonctif imparfait of 'savoir') maintains concordance with the imparfait 'se préparait.' The reflexive passive 'se préparait' (was being prepared) is a common alternative to the passive voice in French, especially when the agent is unspecified.

items.question: Il était essentiel que chacun _____ son rôle.
items.translation: (It was essential that everyone know their role.)
items.explanation: The subjonctif imparfait of 'connaître' is: connusse, connusses, connût, connussions, connussiez, connussent. After 'il était essentiel que' (it was essential that), the subjunctive is required. The subjonctif imparfait 'connût' mirrors the past tense of the main clause. 'Chacun' (each one/everyone) takes a singular verb, which is a common grammar point for learners to internalize.

description: Recognize the imperfect subjunctive in literary French and understand its modern equivalents

name: Subjonctif Plus-que-parfait

items.question: Je doutais qu'il _____ la vérité.
items.translation: (I doubted that he had told the truth.)
items.explanation: The subjonctif plus-que-parfait is formed with the subjonctif imparfait of the auxiliary (avoir → eusse/eût) + past participle. 'Eût dit' expresses a completed action in the past that is doubted. This is the most formal compound subjunctive tense in French, used exclusively in literary writing. It's the past counterpart of the subjonctif passé, just as the plus-que-parfait indicative is to the passé composé.

items.question: Bien qu'elle _____ tout préparé, le dîner fut un désastre.
items.translation: (Although she had prepared everything, the dinner was a disaster.)
items.explanation: The subjonctif plus-que-parfait 'eût préparé' is split across the blank and the following word. After 'bien que' with a past-tense main clause ('fut'), literary concordance requires this tense. The contrast between careful preparation and disastrous results creates irony — a frequent literary device. In modern spoken French, 'ait préparé' (subjonctif passé) replaces this form entirely.

items.question: Il ne croyait pas que nous _____ réussi sans aide.
items.translation: (He did not believe that we had succeeded without help.)
items.explanation: The subjonctif plus-que-parfait for 'nous' uses 'eussions' (subjonctif imparfait of 'avoir,' first-person plural) + past participle 'réussi.' After 'ne croyait pas que' (did not believe that), the subjunctive expresses doubt about a past event. The form 'eussions' is distinctly literary — you'll encounter it in authors like Proust, Flaubert, and other great stylists of French prose.

items.question: Avant qu'ils _____ terminé, l'orage éclata.
items.translation: (Before they had finished, the storm broke out.)
items.explanation: 'Eussent terminé' is the third-person plural subjonctif plus-que-parfait. 'Avant que' (before) always takes the subjunctive, and with the passé simple 'éclata' in the main clause, literary concordance demands this tense. 'Éclater' (to burst/break out) is commonly used for storms, wars, and crises. The subjonctif plus-que-parfait here indicates incompletion — they hadn't finished when the storm hit.

items.question: Quoiqu'il _____ longtemps vécu à Paris, il ne connaissait pas ce quartier.
items.translation: (Although he had lived in Paris for a long time, he didn't know this neighborhood.)
items.explanation: 'Quoique' + subjonctif plus-que-parfait ('eût vécu') expresses a concessive past fact in literary register. The adverb 'longtemps' (for a long time) is placed between the auxiliary and the past participle, which is standard French word order for compound tenses. 'Quartier' (neighborhood/district) is a key vocabulary word for describing Paris, which is traditionally divided into 20 arrondissements.

items.question: Je ne pensais pas qu'elle _____ pu faire cela.
items.translation: (I didn't think she could have done that.)
items.explanation: 'Eût pu' is the subjonctif plus-que-parfait of 'pouvoir': avoir (subjunctive imperfect) + pu (past participle). After 'ne pensais pas que' in a past context, this tense expresses doubt about a past possibility. The subjonctif plus-que-parfait can also function as a literary conditional past ('Si j'eusse su' = 'Si j'avais su'), a usage seen in the finest French prose but considered archaic today.

items.question: Sans qu'on _____ remarqué sa présence, il s'éclipsa.
items.translation: (Without anyone having noticed his presence, he slipped away.)
items.explanation: 'Sans que' (without) requires the subjunctive. 'Eût remarqué' (subjonctif plus-que-parfait) indicates that the noticing didn't happen before the slipping away. 'S'éclipser' (to slip away/steal away) is an elegant reflexive verb implying a discreet departure. The combination of 'sans que' + subjonctif plus-que-parfait creates a sophisticated literary expression of simultaneous negation and anteriority.

items.question: Il semblait impossible qu'il _____ accompli un tel exploit.
items.translation: (It seemed impossible that he had accomplished such a feat.)
items.explanation: After 'il semblait impossible que' (it seemed impossible that), the subjunctive is mandatory. The subjonctif plus-que-parfait 'eût accompli' indicates a completed past action whose reality is questioned. 'Un exploit' (a feat/achievement) is a strong noun suggesting something remarkable. This structure — evaluation adjective + subjunctive — is fundamental to expressing judgments about past events in literary French.

items.question: Pour peu qu'il _____ hésité, c'en était fait de lui.
items.translation: (Had he hesitated even slightly, it would have been over for him.)
items.explanation: 'Pour peu que' (if only slightly / had...even) is a sophisticated conjunction requiring the subjunctive. Here, the subjonctif plus-que-parfait expresses an unrealized condition in the past — he didn't hesitate, so he survived. 'C'en était fait de lui' is a fixed expression meaning 'it would have been over for him.' This shows how the subjonctif plus-que-parfait can substitute for the conditional past in literary French.

items.question: Nul ne croyait que la guerre _____ fini si vite.
items.translation: (No one believed the war had ended so quickly.)
items.explanation: 'Nul' (no one) is a more literary equivalent of 'personne.' After 'ne croyait que' in the past, the subjonctif plus-que-parfait expresses doubt about a completed event. The adverb 'si vite' (so quickly) modifies the past participle 'fini.' In formal academic and journalistic French, this construction occasionally still appears, though the subjonctif passé ('ait fini') is far more common today.

items.question: Je regrettais qu'il ne _____ pas venu à la fête.
items.translation: (I regretted that he had not come to the party.)
items.explanation: 'Venir' takes être as its auxiliary, so the subjonctif plus-que-parfait is 'fût venu' (subjonctif imparfait of être + past participle with agreement). After 'regrettais que' (regretted that), the subjunctive expresses emotion about a past event. The negation 'ne...pas' wraps around the auxiliary: 'ne fût pas venu.' This structure is a hallmark of polished literary French.

items.question: Bien que le médecin _____ arrivé à temps, il ne put rien faire.
items.translation: (Although the doctor had arrived in time, he could do nothing.)
items.explanation: With 'arriver' (être auxiliary), the subjonctif plus-que-parfait is 'fût arrivé' (no feminine agreement since 'le médecin' is masculine). 'Bien que' + this tense emphasizes the ironic contrast: despite arriving on time, the doctor was helpless. 'Ne put rien faire' (passé simple of 'pouvoir' + nothing) reinforces the literary register throughout.

items.question: Quelque effort qu'il _____ fait, le résultat restait le même.
items.translation: (Whatever effort he had made, the result remained the same.)
items.explanation: 'Quelque...que' (whatever/however) is a concessive construction requiring the subjunctive. 'Eût fait' (subjonctif plus-que-parfait of 'faire') indicates repeated past efforts that all failed. This structure appears frequently in philosophical and moral writing, expressing the futility of certain actions despite earnest attempts. It's one of literary French's most powerful rhetorical devices.

items.question: Il était inconcevable qu'elle _____ partie sans prévenir personne.
items.translation: (It was inconceivable that she had left without telling anyone.)
items.explanation: 'Partir' takes être, giving 'fût partie' (with feminine agreement). After 'il était inconcevable que' (it was inconceivable that), the subjunctive expresses disbelief about a past action. 'Sans prévenir personne' (without telling anyone) uses the infinitive after 'sans' — a cleaner construction than 'sans que + subjunctive' when the subjects are the same.

items.question: A moins qu'on ne lui _____ menti, il savait tout.
items.translation: (Unless he had been lied to, he knew everything.)
items.explanation: 'À moins que' (unless) requires the subjunctive and takes the 'ne' explétif in formal French. 'Eût menti' is the subjonctif plus-que-parfait of 'mentir.' This construction sets up a condition about the past — if no one lied, then he knew everything. 'À moins que' + subjonctif plus-que-parfait is among the most sophisticated grammatical structures in written French, appearing in legal, academic, and literary texts.

description: Identify the pluperfect subjunctive in formal texts for unrealized past conditions

name: Futur dans le Passé

items.question: Il a dit qu'il _____ plus tard.
items.translation: (He said he would come later.)
items.explanation: The 'futur dans le passé' (future in the past) uses the conditional present form to express what was future at a past moment. When the main verb is in the past ('a dit'), the future tense in direct speech ('Je viendrai') becomes conditional in indirect speech ('il viendrait'). This is the same tense-shifting rule as English 'will come' → 'would come.' It's essential for reported speech in French.

items.question: Elle pensait qu'il _____ beau le lendemain.
items.translation: (She thought it would be nice weather the next day.)
items.explanation: 'Faire beau' (to be nice weather) shifts from 'il fera beau' (future) to 'il ferait beau' (conditional/futur dans le passé) after a past-tense main verb ('pensait'). The time marker also shifts: 'demain' (tomorrow) becomes 'le lendemain' (the next day). These paired shifts — tense + time expressions — are crucial for mastering French indirect speech and narration at an advanced level.

items.question: Nous savions qu'ils _____ en retard.
items.translation: (We knew they would be late.)
items.explanation: 'Être en retard' (to be late) uses 'seraient' as the futur dans le passé of 'être.' After 'savions' (imparfait of 'savoir'), the future is transposed to the conditional. The distinction is temporal: 'ils seront en retard' was the original thought (future), but narrated from a past perspective, it becomes 'seraient.' This rule applies consistently whether the main verb is passé composé, imparfait, or passé simple.

items.question: Je lui ai promis que je _____ à l'heure.
items.translation: (I promised him that I would be on time.)
items.explanation: After 'j'ai promis que' (I promised that), the future 'je serai' becomes 'je serais' (conditional/futur dans le passé). The only visible difference between 'serai' (future) and 'serais' (conditional) is the final -s, but the pronunciation differs: /sə.ʁe/ vs. /sə.ʁɛ/. Context determines which tense is meant. This minimal pair is important for both writing accuracy and listening comprehension.

items.question: On m'a averti que le train _____ du retard.
items.translation: (I was warned that the train would be delayed.)
items.explanation: 'Avoir du retard' (to be delayed/late) uses 'aurait' as the futur dans le passé. 'On m'a averti' (I was warned / they warned me) uses 'on' as an impersonal pronoun, common in French for passive-like constructions. The whole sentence demonstrates how future information conveyed in the past gets transposed: 'le train aura du retard' → 'le train aurait du retard.'

items.question: Marie m'a dit qu'elle _____ demain.
items.translation: (Marie told me she would call tomorrow.)
items.explanation: After 'Marie m'a dit que' (Marie told me that), direct speech 'Je téléphonerai demain' becomes 'elle téléphonerait demain.' Note that in informal French, 'demain' sometimes isn't shifted to 'le lendemain' if the context is clear — especially when the call is still in the future relative to the speaker. This flexibility distinguishes spoken French from the strict concordance rules of formal writing.

items.question: Ils ont annoncé qu'ils _____ le projet la semaine suivante.
items.translation: (They announced they would finish the project the following week.)
items.explanation: 'Terminer' (to finish) becomes 'termineraient' in the futur dans le passé (third-person plural conditional). The time expression 'la semaine prochaine' shifts to 'la semaine suivante' (the following week) in indirect speech. This systematic shift of temporal markers is a defining feature of French discourse: demain → le lendemain, hier → la veille, prochaine → suivante, dernière → précédente.

items.question: Tu m'avais dit que tu _____ me chercher.
items.translation: (You had told me you would come pick me up.)
items.explanation: After 'tu m'avais dit que' (plus-que-parfait), 'je viendrai' becomes 'tu viendrais.' 'Venir chercher' (to come pick up/fetch) is a very common two-verb construction in everyday French. The futur dans le passé here conveys a promise that was made but implies it may not have been kept — the plus-que-parfait main verb suggests looking back at an earlier point in the past.

items.question: Le médecin a expliqué que les résultats _____ dans une semaine.
items.translation: (The doctor explained that the results would arrive in a week.)
items.explanation: After 'a expliqué que' (explained that), future 'arriveront' becomes conditional 'arriveraient.' This medical context shows how the futur dans le passé appears in everyday professional situations, not just literature. 'Les résultats' (the results) is a common plural noun in medical, academic, and business French. The construction is identical to the English 'would arrive.'

items.question: Elle était sûre qu'il _____ l'examen.
items.translation: (She was sure he would pass the exam.)
items.explanation: 'Réussir' (to pass/succeed) becomes 'réussirait' after the past-tense 'était sûre que.' Note that 'réussir un examen' means to pass an exam, while 'passer un examen' means to take/sit an exam — a common source of confusion for English speakers. The futur dans le passé expresses her confidence about his future success, viewed from a past perspective.

items.question: On nous a informés que le vol _____ à 14 heures.
items.translation: (We were informed that the flight would depart at 2 PM.)
items.explanation: 'On nous a informés que' (we were informed that) uses the past participle 'informés' with masculine plural agreement (since 'nous' represents the speakers). After this past-tense main clause, 'le vol partira' shifts to 'le vol partirait.' Travel and transportation are common contexts where the futur dans le passé appears — announcing schedule changes, delays, and plans previously communicated.

items.question: Je croyais que tu _____ la réponse.
items.translation: (I thought you would know the answer.)
items.explanation: 'Savoir' (to know) uses the irregular conditional stem 'saur-' (same as future: 'saurai'), giving 'saurais' for the futur dans le passé. After 'je croyais que' (I believed that), this expresses a past expectation about the future. Several verbs have irregular conditional/future stems: savoir → saur-, avoir → aur-, être → ser-, faire → fer-, pouvoir → pourr-, vouloir → voudr-.

items.question: Le professeur a prévenu que l'examen _____ difficile.
items.translation: (The teacher warned that the exam would be difficult.)
items.explanation: After 'a prévenu que' (warned that), 'l'examen sera difficile' shifts to 'l'examen serait difficile.' 'Prévenir' (to warn) is an important verb that's conjugated like 'venir' (je préviens, j'ai prévenu). The futur dans le passé here conveys information that was communicated as a warning — the difficulty was anticipated at the time of speaking but lay in the future relative to that past moment.

items.question: Ma mère m'a assuré qu'elle _____ un gâteau pour mon anniversaire.
items.translation: (My mother assured me she would make a cake for my birthday.)
items.explanation: 'Faire un gâteau' (to make a cake) uses 'ferait' (conditional of 'faire,' irregular stem 'fer-'). After 'ma mère m'a assuré que' (my mother assured me that), direct speech 'Je ferai' becomes 'elle ferait.' 'Assurer' (to assure) is followed by 'que' + indicative (including conditional), unlike verbs of doubt which take the subjunctive. This distinction is key for correct mood selection in French.

items.question: Ils espéraient qu'il _____ les aider.
items.translation: (They hoped he would be able to help them.)
items.explanation: 'Pouvoir' uses the irregular conditional stem 'pourr-' (with double r), giving 'pourrait.' After 'espéraient que' (hoped that), the future 'il pourra' becomes 'il pourrait.' Note that 'espérer' (to hope) takes the indicative, not the subjunctive — this is an exception that surprises many learners. The futur dans le passé expresses a hoped-for possibility that existed in the future of a past moment.

description: Express future actions from a past perspective using the conditional as future-in-the-past

name: Expressions avec Avoir
description: Master idiomatic expressions built around the verb avoir

items.question: J'_____ faim après le cours de sport.
items.translation: (I am hungry after the sports class.)
items.explanation: In French, physical sensations use 'avoir' (to have) rather than 'être' (to be). Where English says 'I am hungry,' French says 'J'ai faim' (literally: I have hunger). This is one of the most fundamental differences between French and English expression patterns. Other common avoir expressions for physical states include 'avoir soif' (to be thirsty), 'avoir chaud' (to be hot), and 'avoir froid' (to be cold). Memorizing these avoir expressions early will help you sound natural in French.

items.question: Elle _____ soif, elle veut de l'eau.
items.translation: (She is thirsty, she wants water.)
items.explanation: Just like 'avoir faim,' the expression for thirst in French is 'avoir soif' — literally 'to have thirst.' So 'She is thirsty' becomes 'Elle a soif.' This pattern extends to many physical and emotional states in French. The verb 'être' (to be) would be incorrect here because French conceptualizes these sensations as things you 'have' rather than things you 'are.' This is a key conceptual shift English speakers must make when learning French.

items.question: Nous _____ besoin d'aide pour ce projet.
items.translation: (We need help for this project.)
items.explanation: 'Avoir besoin de' means 'to need' (literally: to have need of). This is another essential avoir expression. Unlike English where 'need' is a standalone verb, French expresses necessity through the avoir construction: 'J'ai besoin de...' The preposition 'de' is required after 'besoin' to introduce what is needed. This expression works with nouns ('avoir besoin d'aide'), infinitives ('avoir besoin de dormir'), and pronouns ('j'en ai besoin'). It's used constantly in everyday French conversation.

items.question: Tu _____ raison, c'est la bonne réponse.
items.translation: (You are right, that's the correct answer.)
items.explanation: 'Avoir raison' means 'to be right' — literally 'to have rightness.' Its opposite is 'avoir tort' (to be wrong, literally 'to have wrongness'). This is yet another case where French uses 'avoir' where English uses 'to be.' 'Tu as raison' = You are right; 'Il a tort' = He is wrong. These expressions are extremely common in debates, discussions, and everyday exchanges. Note there's no article before 'raison' or 'tort' — they function as fixed expressions.

items.question: Les enfants _____ peur du tonnerre.
items.translation: (The children are afraid of thunder.)
items.explanation: 'Avoir peur de' means 'to be afraid of' (literally: to have fear of). Like other emotional states in French, fear is expressed with avoir: 'J'ai peur' (I'm afraid), 'Tu as peur' (You're afraid). The preposition 'de' introduces the source of fear. Before nouns with articles, 'de' contracts normally: 'peur du tonnerre' (de + le = du). This expression can also be followed by an infinitive: 'J'ai peur de tomber' (I'm afraid of falling). It's essential for expressing emotions in French.

items.question: Mon grand-père _____ quatre-vingts ans.
items.translation: (My grandfather is eighty years old.)
items.explanation: Age in French is expressed with 'avoir' + number + 'ans' (literally: to have X years). 'J'ai vingt ans' = I am twenty years old. This is dramatically different from English, which uses 'to be.' Never say '*Je suis vingt ans' — that's a direct English calque and sounds very wrong to French ears. Note that 'ans' (years) is required; you cannot just say 'J'ai vingt.' This avoir + age construction is universal across all Romance languages, not just French.

items.question: Vous _____ l'air fatigué aujourd'hui.
items.translation: (You look tired today.)
items.explanation: 'Avoir l'air' means 'to look' or 'to seem' (literally: to have the air/appearance of). 'Vous avez l'air fatigué' = You look tired. The adjective after 'l'air' can agree with either the subject or with 'air' (masculine singular), though agreement with the subject is more common in modern French. This expression is very useful for describing how someone appears: 'Elle a l'air contente' (She looks happy), 'Il a l'air malade' (He looks sick). It's more colloquial than 'sembler' or 'paraître.'

items.question: J'_____ envie d'un café.
items.translation: (I feel like having a coffee.)
items.explanation: 'Avoir envie de' expresses desire or craving — 'to feel like' or 'to want.' It's softer than 'vouloir' (to want) and implies more of a passing fancy or craving. 'J'ai envie d'un café' = I feel like a coffee. It can be followed by a noun ('envie d'un gâteau') or an infinitive ('envie de dormir'). Note the contraction: 'envie d'un' (de + un). This expression is extremely frequent in spoken French and is essential for expressing wishes and desires naturally.

items.question: Il _____ honte de son comportement.
items.translation: (He is ashamed of his behavior.)
items.explanation: 'Avoir honte de' means 'to be ashamed of' (literally: to have shame of). Like peur, honte is an emotion expressed with avoir in French. 'J'ai honte' = I'm ashamed; 'Il a honte de son comportement' = He's ashamed of his behavior. The preposition 'de' introduces the cause of shame. This can be followed by nouns or infinitives: 'avoir honte de mentir' (to be ashamed of lying). Related: 'avoir honte pour quelqu'un' means to feel second-hand embarrassment for someone.

items.question: Nous _____ de la chance d'être ici.
items.translation: (We are lucky to be here.)
items.explanation: 'Avoir de la chance' means 'to be lucky' (literally: to have some luck). Note the partitive article 'de la' before 'chance.' 'Nous avons de la chance' = We are lucky. You can also say 'avoir la chance de + infinitive' (to be lucky enough to): 'J'ai la chance de voyager' (I'm lucky enough to travel). The opposite is 'ne pas avoir de chance' or 'avoir de la malchance.' This expression is common in both formal and informal French for acknowledging good fortune.

items.question: Tu _____ du mal à comprendre cette leçon ?
items.translation: (Are you having trouble understanding this lesson?)
items.explanation: 'Avoir du mal à' means 'to have trouble/difficulty doing something.' 'Tu as du mal à comprendre' = You have trouble understanding. Note the partitive article 'du' (de + le) before 'mal' and the preposition 'à' before the infinitive. This is more colloquial than 'avoir de la difficulté à' and very commonly used in everyday speech. The opposite idea can be expressed with 'n'avoir aucun mal à' (to have no trouble with). It's an essential expression for describing struggles.

items.question: Elle _____ l'habitude de se lever tôt.
items.translation: (She is used to getting up early.)
items.explanation: 'Avoir l'habitude de' means 'to be used to' or 'to be in the habit of' (literally: to have the habit of). 'Elle a l'habitude de se lever tôt' = She is used to getting up early. This is different from 'être habitué(e) à,' which emphasizes the result of becoming accustomed. 'Avoir l'habitude' focuses on an established routine. Related expressions: 'prendre l'habitude de' (to get into the habit of), 'perdre l'habitude de' (to get out of the habit of). Very useful for describing daily routines.

items.question: Les étudiants _____ hâte de partir en vacances.
items.translation: (The students can't wait to go on vacation.)
items.explanation: 'Avoir hâte de' means 'to look forward to' or 'can't wait to' (literally: to have haste to). 'Ils ont hâte de partir' = They can't wait to leave. This expression conveys eager anticipation. The 'h' in 'hâte' is aspirated, so there's no liaison: 'ils ont / hâte' (not 'ils ont‿hâte'). This is followed by 'de' + infinitive. It's warmer and more enthusiastic than simply saying 'vouloir' (to want). Used constantly in French to express excitement about upcoming events.

items.question: Vous _____ tort de penser cela.
items.translation: (You are wrong to think that.)
items.explanation: 'Avoir tort de' means 'to be wrong to' (literally: to have wrongness to). 'Vous avez tort de penser cela' = You are wrong to think that. This is the opposite of 'avoir raison de' (to be right to). Both expressions use 'de' + infinitive to introduce the action. These are among the most frequently confused avoir expressions for English speakers because English uses 'to be' for both. Remember: in French, being right or wrong is something you HAVE, not something you ARE.

items.question: Il _____ lieu de s'inquiéter.
items.translation: (There is reason to worry.)
items.explanation: 'Il y a lieu de' is a formal expression meaning 'there is reason to' or 'it is appropriate to.' It uses the impersonal 'il y a' (there is) construction. 'Il y a lieu de s'inquiéter' = There is reason to worry. The negative 'Il n'y a pas lieu de s'inquiéter' means 'There is no reason to worry.' This is a more formal register expression, often found in administrative or legal texts. Related: 'avoir lieu' means 'to take place' — 'La réunion a lieu à 15h' (The meeting takes place at 3 PM).

name: Expressions avec Faire
description: Master idiomatic expressions built around the verb faire

items.question: Il _____ beau aujourd'hui, allons nous promener.
items.translation: (The weather is nice today, let's go for a walk.)
items.explanation: Weather expressions in French use 'faire' (to do/make), not 'être' (to be). 'Il fait beau' = The weather is nice/It's a beautiful day. Other common weather expressions with faire: 'il fait chaud' (it's hot), 'il fait froid' (it's cold), 'il fait mauvais' (the weather is bad), 'il fait du vent' (it's windy), 'il fait du soleil' (it's sunny). The impersonal 'il' here doesn't refer to any specific subject — it functions like English 'it' in 'it's raining.' This is one of the first faire expressions to master.

items.question: Je _____ du sport trois fois par semaine.
items.translation: (I do sports three times a week.)
items.explanation: 'Faire du sport' means 'to do/play sports.' In French, 'faire' is the default verb for most sports and activities, used with the partitive article: 'faire du football' (play soccer), 'faire de la natation' (swim), 'faire du vélo' (ride a bike). The choice between 'faire' and 'jouer' depends on the sport: 'jouer' is used with ball/team sports preceded by 'à' (jouer au tennis), while 'faire' works with all sports using 'de' (faire du tennis also works). 'Faire du sport' is the general term.

items.question: _____ attention en traversant la rue !
items.translation: (Be careful crossing the street!)
items.explanation: 'Faire attention' means 'to be careful' or 'to pay attention' (literally: to make attention). The imperative 'Fais attention !' is one of the most common warnings in French. It can be followed by 'à' + noun ('Fais attention à la marche' = Watch the step) or 'à' + infinitive ('Fais attention à ne pas tomber' = Be careful not to fall). Unlike English which uses 'be' (be careful), French uses 'faire.' This expression is essential for warnings, instructions, and safety-related communication.

items.question: Elle _____ la cuisine tous les soirs.
items.translation: (She cooks every evening.)
items.explanation: 'Faire la cuisine' means 'to cook' or 'to do the cooking.' French frequently uses 'faire + definite article + noun' for household chores: 'faire la vaisselle' (do the dishes), 'faire le ménage' (do the cleaning), 'faire la lessive' (do the laundry), 'faire les courses' (do the shopping/groceries). Note the definite article (la, le, les) is required — these are fixed expressions. While 'cuisiner' (to cook) also exists, 'faire la cuisine' is more colloquial and widely used.

items.question: Ce médicament _____ effet rapidement.
items.translation: (This medication takes effect quickly.)
items.explanation: 'Faire effet' means 'to take effect' or 'to work' (for medicine, actions). 'Ce médicament fait effet rapidement' = This medication works quickly. Note there's no article before 'effet' in this expression. Related expressions: 'faire de l'effet' (to make an impression), 'faire bon effet' (to make a good impression), 'faire mauvais effet' (to make a bad impression). This is a formal-register expression useful in medical, professional, and analytical contexts.

items.question: Nous _____ de notre mieux pour réussir.
items.translation: (We do our best to succeed.)
items.explanation: 'Faire de son mieux' means 'to do one's best.' The possessive changes with the subject: 'je fais de mon mieux,' 'tu fais de ton mieux,' 'il fait de son mieux,' 'nous faisons de notre mieux.' This expression is followed by 'pour + infinitive' to express purpose: 'faire de son mieux pour réussir' (do one's best to succeed). It's a very natural and frequently used expression in both spoken and written French, appropriate for encouraging someone or describing effort.

items.question: Tu _____ partie de l'équipe maintenant.
items.translation: (You are part of the team now.)
items.explanation: 'Faire partie de' means 'to be part of' or 'to belong to' (literally: to make part of). 'Tu fais partie de l'équipe' = You are part of the team. This is essential for expressing membership and belonging: 'faire partie d'un club' (to belong to a club), 'faire partie d'un groupe' (to be part of a group). English speakers often mistakenly use 'être partie de' — this is incorrect. The expression always uses 'faire,' never 'être.' It works with any noun representing a group or whole.

items.question: Cet accident lui _____ très peur.
items.translation: (This accident frightened him/her a lot.)
items.explanation: 'Faire peur à' means 'to frighten/scare' (literally: to make fear to). 'Cet accident lui a fait très peur' = This accident frightened him/her greatly. Note the indirect object pronoun 'lui' (to him/her) and the passé composé 'a fait.' While 'avoir peur' means to BE afraid, 'faire peur' means to CAUSE fear in someone else. The structure is: subject + faire peur + à + person. Other emotional causatives: 'faire plaisir' (to please), 'faire mal' (to hurt), 'faire rire' (to make laugh).

items.question: Ne t'inquiète pas, je _____ comme si de rien n'était.
items.translation: (Don't worry, I'll act as if nothing happened.)
items.explanation: 'Faire comme si' means 'to act as if' or 'to pretend.' 'Je ferai comme si de rien n'était' = I'll act as if nothing happened. This expression is followed by the imperfect tense (matching English 'as if + past'). Other examples: 'Il fait comme s'il ne comprenait pas' (He acts as if he doesn't understand). The set phrase 'comme si de rien n'était' (as if nothing had happened) is extremely common and worth memorizing as a unit. 'Ferai' is the future tense of 'faire.'

items.question: Cette robe vous _____ dix ans de moins.
items.translation: (This dress makes you look ten years younger.)
items.explanation: 'Faire + age expression' is used to describe how old someone looks. 'Vous faites dix ans de moins' = You look ten years younger. Similarly, 'Elle fait jeune pour son âge' (She looks young for her age), 'Il fait vieux' (He looks old). This is distinct from 'avoir X ans' (to be X years old). 'Faire' here relates to appearance, while 'avoir' states actual age. This usage of faire is very idiomatic and common in everyday conversation when commenting on someone's appearance.

items.question: Le professeur _____ l'appel au début du cours.
items.translation: (The teacher takes roll call at the beginning of class.)
items.explanation: 'Faire l'appel' means 'to take roll call/attendance.' French uses 'faire' for many administrative and procedural actions: 'faire un discours' (give a speech), 'faire un exposé' (give a presentation), 'faire une annonce' (make an announcement), 'faire le point' (take stock/summarize). These are fixed collocations — the verb 'faire' pairs with specific nouns to create meanings that can't be predicted from the individual words. Learning these collocations is essential for fluent French.

items.question: Ça ne _____ rien si tu arrives en retard.
items.translation: (It doesn't matter if you arrive late.)
items.explanation: 'Ça ne fait rien' means 'it doesn't matter' or 'never mind.' This is an extremely common conversational expression. 'Ça fait' is also used for elapsed time: 'Ça fait deux heures que j'attends' (I've been waiting for two hours). Other idiomatic uses: 'Ça fait combien ?' (How much is it?), 'Ça fait mal' (That hurts). The impersonal 'ça fait' construction is one of the most versatile in spoken French and appears in countless everyday situations.

items.question: Il _____ semblant de dormir pour éviter la discussion.
items.translation: (He pretends to sleep to avoid the discussion.)
items.explanation: 'Faire semblant de' means 'to pretend to' (literally: to make a show of). 'Il fait semblant de dormir' = He pretends to be sleeping. This is followed by 'de' + infinitive. It's more direct than 'feindre de' (to feign) and much more commonly used in everyday speech. The noun 'semblant' comes from 'sembler' (to seem). This expression is invaluable for describing deceptive behavior: 'Elle fait semblant de ne pas entendre' (She pretends not to hear).

items.question: Nous _____ face à de nombreux défis.
items.translation: (We face many challenges.)
items.explanation: 'Faire face à' means 'to face' or 'to deal with' (literally: to make face to). 'Nous faisons face à de nombreux défis' = We face many challenges. This is a slightly formal expression used for confronting problems, challenges, or difficult situations. Related expressions: 'faire front' (to stand firm), 'faire face à ses responsabilités' (to face one's responsibilities). It's commonly used in news, professional contexts, and formal speech. The preposition 'à' is always required.

items.question: Les enfants _____ la queue devant le cinéma.
items.translation: (The children are standing in line in front of the cinema.)
items.explanation: 'Faire la queue' means 'to stand in line/queue' (literally: to make the tail). This is the standard French expression for lining up, used universally across France. Other daily-life faire expressions: 'faire les courses' (go grocery shopping), 'faire du shopping' (go shopping for pleasure), 'faire une promenade' (take a walk), 'faire un tour' (take a stroll/ride). These are all essential vocabulary for describing everyday activities. Note: 'font' is the third person plural of 'faire.'

name: Expressions avec Être
description: Master idiomatic expressions and constructions with the verb être

items.question: Je _____ en train de lire un roman.
items.translation: (I am reading a novel [right now].)
items.explanation: 'Être en train de' + infinitive expresses an ongoing action — it's the French equivalent of the English present continuous. 'Je suis en train de lire' = I am (in the process of) reading. While French often uses the simple present for ongoing actions, 'être en train de' adds emphasis that the action is happening RIGHT NOW. It's more specific than the simple present: 'Je lis' (I read/I'm reading) vs. 'Je suis en train de lire' (I am reading right now). This construction works with any verb.

items.question: Nous _____ sur le point de partir.
items.translation: (We are about to leave.)
items.explanation: 'Être sur le point de' + infinitive means 'to be about to' — expressing imminent action. 'Nous sommes sur le point de partir' = We are about to leave. This is stronger and more immediate than 'aller + infinitive' (futur proche). Compare: 'Je vais partir' (I'm going to leave — could be soon or later) vs. 'Je suis sur le point de partir' (I'm about to leave — very imminent). This construction is useful in both spoken and written French for conveying urgency or immediacy.

items.question: Elle _____ censée arriver à huit heures.
items.translation: (She is supposed to arrive at eight o'clock.)
items.explanation: 'Être censé(e)' + infinitive means 'to be supposed to.' 'Elle est censée arriver à huit heures' = She is supposed to arrive at eight. Note the agreement: 'censé' for masculine, 'censée' for feminine, 'censés/censées' for plural. This expression implies expectation without certainty. It's more formal than 'devoir' (must/to be supposed to) and carries a nuance of 'reportedly' or 'allegedly.' Common error: confusing with 'sensé' (sensible) — 'censé' (supposed to) has a 'c.'

items.question: Il _____ d'accord avec cette proposition.
items.translation: (He agrees with this proposal.)
items.explanation: 'Être d'accord' means 'to agree' (literally: to be in agreement). 'Il est d'accord avec cette proposition' = He agrees with this proposal. This is followed by 'avec' (with) to specify what one agrees with, or 'pour + infinitive' to agree to do something. Asking for agreement: 'Tu es d'accord ?' (Do you agree?). Expressing strong agreement: 'Je suis tout à fait d'accord' (I completely agree). This is one of the most essential expressions in French conversation and negotiation.

items.question: Nous _____ en retard pour le spectacle.
items.translation: (We are late for the show.)
items.explanation: 'Être en retard' means 'to be late.' Its opposites are 'être en avance' (to be early) and 'être à l'heure' (to be on time). 'Nous sommes en retard pour le spectacle' = We are late for the show. The preposition 'pour' introduces what you're late for. 'De' is used with a time duration: 'être en retard de dix minutes' (to be ten minutes late). Unlike English where 'late' is simply an adjective, French treats it as a prepositional phrase with 'en.' This trio of time expressions is essential for daily life.

items.question: Cette maison _____ à vendre depuis un an.
items.translation: (This house has been for sale for a year.)
items.explanation: 'Être à + infinitive' can express purpose or availability. 'Être à vendre' = to be for sale. 'Être à louer' = to be for rent. 'C'est à voir' = That remains to be seen. 'C'est à faire' = It needs to be done. This construction is concise and elegant. Note how 'depuis un an' (for a year) combines with the present tense in French to express duration — where English uses present perfect ('has been'), French uses present + depuis. This is a fundamental difference between the two languages.

items.question: Tu _____ au courant de la nouvelle ?
items.translation: (Are you aware of the news?)
items.explanation: 'Être au courant de' means 'to be aware of' or 'to know about' (literally: to be in the current of). 'Tu es au courant de la nouvelle ?' = Are you aware of the news? This expression implies being informed or up-to-date. Related: 'mettre au courant' (to inform/update someone), 'se mettre au courant' (to get up to speed), 'tenir au courant' (to keep informed). It's widely used in professional and casual settings. Note: 'courant' here is a noun (the current/flow of information).

items.question: Je _____ en mesure de vous aider.
items.translation: (I am able to help you / I am in a position to help you.)
items.explanation: 'Être en mesure de' means 'to be in a position to' or 'to be able to.' It's more formal than 'pouvoir' (can) and implies capability or readiness. 'Je suis en mesure de vous aider' = I am in a position to help you. The negative form 'ne pas être en mesure de' is commonly used in formal refusals: 'Nous ne sommes pas en mesure de satisfaire votre demande' (We are unable to fulfill your request). This expression is frequent in business correspondence and official communication.

items.question: Les enfants _____ contents de leur cadeau.
items.translation: (The children are happy with their gift.)
items.explanation: 'Être content(e) de' means 'to be happy with/about.' Unlike emotions expressed with 'avoir' (avoir peur, avoir honte), satisfaction and happiness use 'être' in French. 'Les enfants sont contents de leur cadeau' = The children are happy with their gift. The adjective agrees in gender and number: 'contente' (feminine), 'contents' (masculine plural), 'contentes' (feminine plural). Similar expressions: 'être satisfait de' (to be satisfied with), 'être ravi de' (to be delighted with).

items.question: Ce livre _____ destiné aux débutants.
items.translation: (This book is intended for beginners.)
items.explanation: 'Être destiné à' means 'to be intended for' or 'to be destined for.' 'Ce livre est destiné aux débutants' = This book is intended for beginners. Note the contraction 'aux' = à + les. The past participle 'destiné' agrees with the subject: 'Cette formation est destinée aux professionnels.' This expression is common in descriptions, marketing, and formal contexts. Related: 'être prévu pour' (to be planned/designed for), 'être conçu pour' (to be designed for).

items.question: Il _____ question de changer les horaires.
items.translation: (There is talk of changing the schedules.)
items.explanation: 'Il est question de' means 'there is talk/mention of' or 'it's about.' 'Il est question de changer les horaires' = There is talk of changing the schedules. This impersonal construction is formal. In informal speech, 'il s'agit de' (it's about) is more common. Related: 'il n'en est pas question' (that's out of the question) — a strong refusal. 'De quoi est-il question ?' (What is it about?). This expression is essential for formal discussions, meetings, and written communication.

items.question: Nous _____ obligés de reporter la réunion.
items.translation: (We are obliged to postpone the meeting.)
items.explanation: 'Être obligé(e) de' means 'to be obliged to/forced to.' Unlike 'devoir' (must), which is a simple modal verb, 'être obligé de' emphasizes the external constraint or necessity. 'Nous sommes obligés de reporter la réunion' = We are forced to postpone the meeting. Agreement rules apply: 'obligé' (masc. sg.), 'obligée' (fem. sg.), 'obligés' (masc. pl.), 'obligées' (fem. pl.). Similar: 'être contraint de' (to be constrained to), 'être forcé de' (to be forced to) — all more emphatic than 'devoir.'

items.question: Elle _____ en train de se préparer quand je suis arrivé.
items.translation: (She was getting ready when I arrived.)
items.explanation: 'Être en train de' works in all tenses. In the imperfect, 'était en train de' emphasizes an ongoing past action interrupted by another event. 'Elle était en train de se préparer quand je suis arrivé' = She was (in the process of) getting ready when I arrived. The imperfect of 'être en train de' is particularly useful for narration, equivalent to English past continuous. It adds more immediacy than the simple imperfect: 'elle se préparait' vs. 'elle était en train de se préparer.'

items.question: Ce vin _____ à consommer avant 2025.
items.translation: (This wine is to be consumed before 2025.)
items.explanation: 'Être à + infinitive' can express necessity or instruction, meaning 'is to be (done).' 'Ce vin est à consommer avant 2025' = This wine is to be consumed before 2025. This construction is common on labels, instructions, and formal directives: 'C'est à refaire' (It needs to be redone), 'C'est à prendre ou à laisser' (Take it or leave it). It's more concise than 'devoir être + past participle.' This pattern is very productive in French and worth mastering for both reading and writing.

items.question: Je _____ conscient du problème.
items.translation: (I am aware of the problem.)
items.explanation: 'Être conscient(e) de' means 'to be aware of' or 'to be conscious of.' 'Je suis conscient du problème' = I am aware of the problem. Note 'du' = de + le. This is more formal than 'être au courant de' (to be informed about) and implies deeper understanding rather than just knowing. Similar expressions: 'être inconscient de' (to be unaware of), 'prendre conscience de' (to become aware of). The adjective agrees: 'consciente' for feminine. Essential for formal discussions and professional communication.

name: Verbes Pronominaux Idiomatiques
description: Master idiomatic pronominal verbs with unique meanings in French

items.question: Je _____ souviens de mon enfance.
items.translation: (I remember my childhood.)
items.explanation: 'Se souvenir de' means 'to remember' and is exclusively pronominal — it cannot be used without the reflexive pronoun. The conjugation follows the subject: 'je me souviens,' 'tu te souviens,' 'il se souvient,' 'nous nous souvenons.' Compare with 'se rappeler' (also to remember), which takes a direct object without 'de': 'Je me rappelle mon enfance.' This distinction is important: 'se souvenir DE quelque chose' but 'se rappeler quelque chose.' Both are common but 'se souvenir de' is more frequently used.

items.question: Elle _____ moque toujours de son frère.
items.translation: (She always makes fun of her brother.)
items.explanation: 'Se moquer de' means 'to make fun of' or 'to mock.' This verb is essentially pronominal — 'moquer' without 'se' is archaic and no longer used. 'Elle se moque de son frère' = She makes fun of her brother. The pronoun changes with the subject: 'je me moque,' 'tu te moques,' 'nous nous moquons.' It can also mean 'to not care about': 'Je m'en moque' (I don't care about it). The colloquial 'je m'en fiche' or 'je m'en fous' (vulgar) express the same indifference.

items.question: Nous _____ entendons bien avec nos voisins.
items.translation: (We get along well with our neighbors.)
items.explanation: 'S'entendre (bien/mal) avec' means 'to get along (well/poorly) with.' The non-pronominal 'entendre' means 'to hear,' but the pronominal 's'entendre' shifts to 'to get along.' This is a perfect example of how adding 'se' to a French verb can completely change its meaning. 'Nous nous entendons bien' = We get along well. Other common pairs: 'rendre' (to return) → 'se rendre' (to go/surrender), 'mettre' (to put) → 'se mettre à' (to start), 'passer' (to pass) → 'se passer' (to happen).

items.question: Il _____ plaint toujours de la nourriture.
items.translation: (He always complains about the food.)
items.explanation: 'Se plaindre de' means 'to complain about.' 'Il se plaint de la nourriture' = He complains about the food. This is one of the most frequently used pronominal verbs. Without 'se,' 'plaindre' means 'to pity': 'Je le plains' (I pity him). So the pronominal form completely changes the meaning. Past participle: 'plaint' (invariable as it takes an indirect object with 'de'). Conjugation: 'je me plains,' 'tu te plains,' 'il se plaint,' 'nous nous plaignons.' Note the 'gn' pronunciation in plural forms.

items.question: Tu _____ rends compte de la situation ?
items.translation: (Do you realize the situation?)
items.explanation: 'Se rendre compte de' means 'to realize' or 'to become aware of' (literally: to render account to oneself of). 'Tu te rends compte ?' is a very common exclamation meaning 'Can you believe it?' or 'Do you realize?' Without 'se,' 'rendre compte' means 'to report/account for.' This expression is essential in everyday French. In passé composé: 'Je me suis rendu compte que...' (I realized that...). Note: 'rendu' does NOT agree with the subject here — it's invariable because 'compte' is the direct object.

items.question: Ils _____ méfient des inconnus.
items.translation: (They are wary of strangers.)
items.explanation: 'Se méfier de' means 'to be wary/suspicious of' or 'to distrust.' 'Ils se méfient des inconnus' = They are wary of strangers. Note 'des' = de + les. The imperative is common as a warning: 'Méfie-toi !' (Be careful! / Watch out!) or 'Méfiez-vous de lui' (Be wary of him). The verb 'méfier' doesn't exist without 'se' in modern French — it's exclusively pronominal. Related: 'se fier à' (to trust), which is the opposite. Both are essential for expressing trust and caution.

items.question: Elle _____ doute de quelque chose.
items.translation: (She suspects something.)
items.explanation: 'Se douter de' means 'to suspect' or 'to have a feeling about' — quite different from 'douter de' (to doubt). Compare: 'Je doute de sa sincérité' (I doubt his sincerity) vs. 'Je me doute de quelque chose' (I suspect something). Adding 'se' reverses the meaning from uncertainty to suspicion! 'Je m'en doutais !' = I suspected as much! / I knew it! This semantic flip is one of the trickiest aspects of pronominal verbs for English speakers. It's extremely common in conversation.

items.question: Les enfants _____ amusent dans le jardin.
items.translation: (The children are having fun in the garden.)
items.explanation: 'S'amuser' means 'to have fun' or 'to enjoy oneself.' Without 'se,' 'amuser' means 'to amuse/entertain (someone else).' 'Les enfants s'amusent' = The children are having fun (themselves). Note the elision: 'se' becomes 's'' before a vowel. This is a reciprocal pronominal verb — the children are entertaining themselves. In imperative: 'Amuse-toi bien !' (Have fun!). In passé composé with être: 'Ils se sont amusés' (They had fun) — the participle agrees with the subject.

items.question: Nous _____ trouvons à Paris en ce moment.
items.translation: (We are located in Paris right now.)
items.explanation: 'Se trouver' means 'to be located' or 'to find oneself.' While 'trouver' means 'to find (something),' 'se trouver' shifts to location. 'Nous nous trouvons à Paris' = We are (located) in Paris. This is more formal than simply using 'être.' It's commonly used for giving locations: 'L'hôtel se trouve près de la gare' (The hotel is located near the station). It can also mean 'to happen to be': 'Il se trouve que j'étais là' (It happens that I was there). Very useful for descriptions and directions.

items.question: Je _____ sers de ce dictionnaire tous les jours.
items.translation: (I use this dictionary every day.)
items.explanation: 'Se servir de' means 'to use' (literally: to serve oneself with). Without 'se,' 'servir' means 'to serve.' 'Je me sers de ce dictionnaire' = I use this dictionary. This is a natural alternative to 'utiliser.' The preposition 'de' is required. In passé composé: 'Je me suis servi de...' (I used...). Related: 'Ça ne sert à rien' (It's useless/pointless), 'servir à' (to be used for). The meaning shift from 'serve' to 'use' demonstrates how pronominal forms create new meanings.

items.question: Elle _____ trompe souvent de chemin.
items.translation: (She often takes the wrong road.)
items.explanation: 'Se tromper de' means 'to get wrong' or 'to mistake one thing for another.' It's different from 'tromper' (to deceive/cheat). 'Elle se trompe de chemin' = She takes the wrong road. Other common uses: 'se tromper de numéro' (to dial the wrong number), 'se tromper de date' (to get the date wrong), 'se tromper de porte' (to go to the wrong door). Without 'de + noun,' 'se tromper' simply means 'to be mistaken': 'Tu te trompes !' (You're wrong!). This is one of the most used pronominal verbs in daily French.

items.question: Il _____ passe de café depuis un mois.
items.translation: (He has been going without coffee for a month.)
items.explanation: 'Se passer de' means 'to do without' or 'to go without.' Without 'se,' 'passer' means 'to pass' and 'se passer' means 'to happen.' Adding 'de' creates a third meaning: to go without. 'Il se passe de café' = He goes without coffee. 'Je ne peux pas m'en passer' (I can't do without it) is an extremely common expression. This triple meaning (pass / happen / go without) based on pronominality and preposition is a great example of French verb versatility.

items.question: Nous _____ attendons à des difficultés.
items.translation: (We expect difficulties.)
items.explanation: 'S'attendre à' means 'to expect.' Without 'se,' 'attendre' means 'to wait for.' 'Nous nous attendons à des difficultés' = We expect difficulties. The preposition 'à' is required. It can be followed by 'à ce que' + subjunctive: 'Je m'attends à ce qu'il vienne' (I expect him to come). In passé composé: 'Je ne m'y attendais pas' (I didn't expect that — note 'y' replaces 'à cela'). This verb beautifully illustrates how pronominal forms create entirely new meanings from common verbs.

items.question: Tu _____ aperçois de ton erreur maintenant ?
items.translation: (Do you notice your mistake now?)
items.explanation: 'S'apercevoir de' means 'to notice' or 'to realize,' while 'apercevoir' (without se) means 'to catch sight of' or 'to glimpse.' 'Tu t'aperçois de ton erreur' = You notice your mistake. Note the elision: 'te' → 't'' before a vowel. In passé composé: 'Je me suis aperçu que j'avais oublié' (I realized I had forgotten). This verb is similar to 'se rendre compte de' but slightly more literary. The cedilla on 'ç' in 'aperçois' ensures the soft 's' sound before 'o.'

items.question: Les étudiants _____ plaignent du bruit.
items.translation: (The students complain about the noise.)
items.explanation: 'Se plaindre de' means 'to complain about.' 'Les étudiants se plaignent du bruit' = The students complain about the noise. Note 'du' = de + le. This verb is irregular: 'je me plains, tu te plains, il se plaint, nous nous plaignons, vous vous plaignez, ils se plaignent.' In passé composé: 'Ils se sont plaints' (They complained — past participle agrees with subject). Related noun: 'une plainte' (a complaint). In formal contexts, 'porter plainte' means 'to file a complaint.' This is essential for expressing dissatisfaction.

name: Verbes à Prépositions
description: Master French verbs that require specific prepositions different from English

items.question: Elle pense _____ ses vacances.
items.translation: (She is thinking about her vacation.)
items.explanation: 'Penser à' means 'to think about' (focusing thoughts on something). This is different from 'penser de' which means 'to think of/have an opinion about': 'Que penses-tu de ce film ?' (What do you think of this film?). 'Elle pense à ses vacances' = She is thinking about her vacation. This à/de distinction is crucial: 'à' = directing thoughts toward, 'de' = having an opinion about. With pronouns: 'J'y pense' (I'm thinking about it) for things, 'Je pense à elle' (I'm thinking about her) for people.

items.question: Il joue _____ piano depuis cinq ans.
items.translation: (He has been playing piano for five years.)
items.explanation: 'Jouer de' is used for musical instruments (literally: to play of). 'Jouer du piano' (de + le = du), 'jouer de la guitare,' 'jouer de l'harmonica.' For sports and games, French uses 'jouer à': 'jouer au football' (à + le = au), 'jouer aux cartes' (à + les = aux). This à/de split is one of the most tested grammar points in French: 'de' for instruments, 'à' for sports/games. The definite article is always required after the preposition. 'Depuis cinq ans' with present tense = for five years.

items.question: Je m'intéresse _____ l'histoire de France.
items.translation: (I am interested in the history of France.)
items.explanation: 'S'intéresser à' means 'to be interested in.' English uses 'in' but French uses 'à.' 'Je m'intéresse à l'histoire' = I'm interested in history. With pronouns: 'Je m'y intéresse' (I'm interested in it). The non-pronominal form 'intéresser' means 'to interest': 'Ce sujet m'intéresse' (This subject interests me). Common error for English speakers: using 'en' or 'dans' instead of 'à.' This preposition mismatch between English and French is why learning verb + preposition pairs is so critical.

items.question: Nous avons décidé _____ partir demain.
items.translation: (We decided to leave tomorrow.)
items.explanation: 'Décider de' + infinitive means 'to decide to.' Many French verbs require 'de' before an infinitive: 'essayer de' (to try to), 'refuser de' (to refuse to), 'oublier de' (to forget to), 'accepter de' (to agree to), 'proposer de' (to propose to). This is in contrast to verbs that take 'à': 'commencer à' (to begin to), 'apprendre à' (to learn to), 'réussir à' (to succeed in). And others take no preposition: 'vouloir' (to want to), 'pouvoir' (can), 'devoir' (must). Learning which preposition each verb takes is essential.

items.question: Elle ressemble _____ sa mère.
items.translation: (She looks like her mother.)
items.explanation: 'Ressembler à' means 'to look like' or 'to resemble.' English doesn't use a preposition here, but French requires 'à.' 'Elle ressemble à sa mère' = She looks like her mother. With pronouns: 'Elle lui ressemble' (She looks like her — 'lui' replaces 'à sa mère'). For things: 'Ça ressemble à un chat' (That looks like a cat); 'Ça y ressemble' (It looks like it). Note: never use 'comme' with this verb — that's a common anglicism. 'Se ressembler' (reciprocal) means 'to look alike': 'Ils se ressemblent.'

items.question: Tu as réussi _____ finir le projet ?
items.translation: (Did you manage to finish the project?)
items.explanation: 'Réussir à' + infinitive means 'to succeed in' or 'to manage to.' 'Tu as réussi à finir' = You managed to finish. This is one of the most common 'à' + infinitive verbs. Compare with 'réussir' + noun (no preposition needed): 'réussir un examen' (to pass an exam). The à/de distinction for verb + infinitive has no simple rule — it must be memorized verb by verb. Other essential 'à' verbs: 'arriver à' (to manage to), 'hésiter à' (to hesitate to), 'tenir à' (to insist on).

items.question: Il s'est habitué _____ vivre seul.
items.translation: (He got used to living alone.)
items.explanation: 'S'habituer à' means 'to get used to.' 'Il s'est habitué à vivre seul' = He got used to living alone. Related: 'être habitué à' (to be used to — result state), 'avoir l'habitude de' (to be in the habit of — ongoing habit). Note these use different prepositions! The mismatch is a common source of errors. With pronouns: 'Je m'y suis habitué' (I got used to it). In English, 'used to' works differently depending on context, but French has distinct expressions for each nuance.

items.question: Nous tenons _____ vous remercier.
items.translation: (We want to thank you / We insist on thanking you.)
items.explanation: 'Tenir à' + infinitive means 'to insist on' or 'to be keen to.' 'Nous tenons à vous remercier' = We want to thank you (with emphasis on the desire). Without infinitive, 'tenir à' + noun means 'to value' or 'to be attached to': 'Je tiens à ce livre' (I value this book). Compare: 'tenir de' means 'to take after': 'Elle tient de sa mère' (She takes after her mother). Three completely different meanings based on the preposition! This versatility makes 'tenir' one of the richest verbs in French.

items.question: Ils ont profité _____ beau temps pour sortir.
items.translation: (They took advantage of the nice weather to go out.)
items.explanation: 'Profiter de' means 'to take advantage of' or 'to enjoy.' 'Ils ont profité du beau temps' = They took advantage of the nice weather. Note 'du' = de + le. English speakers often wrongly omit the preposition, but 'profiter' always requires 'de.' With pronouns: 'J'en ai profité' (I took advantage of it — 'en' replaces 'de + noun'). You can also add 'pour + infinitive' to express purpose: 'profiter de l'occasion pour...' (to take the opportunity to...). Essential vocabulary for everyday conversation.

items.question: Elle a commencé _____ apprendre le français.
items.translation: (She started learning French.)
items.explanation: 'Commencer à' + infinitive means 'to start to/begin doing.' 'Elle a commencé à apprendre le français' = She started learning French. Note: 'commencer par' + infinitive means 'to begin by doing': 'Commencez par lire le texte' (Start by reading the text). The distinction matters: 'à' introduces what you start doing, 'par' introduces the first step in a sequence. Similarly, 'finir de' = to finish doing, 'finir par' = to end up doing. These preposition pairs (à/par, de/par) are crucial for precision in French.

items.question: Il dépend _____ ses parents financièrement.
items.translation: (He depends on his parents financially.)
items.explanation: 'Dépendre de' means 'to depend on.' Where English uses 'on,' French uses 'de.' 'Il dépend de ses parents' = He depends on his parents. Impersonal: 'Ça dépend' (It depends) — the most casual usage. 'Ça dépend de toi' (It depends on you). Common error: using 'sur' (a direct calque from English 'on'). With pronouns: 'Ça en dépend' (It depends on that). The noun form is 'la dépendance' (dependence) and 'l'indépendance' (independence). This preposition mismatch is a classic English-French trap.

items.question: Tu te souviens _____ notre premier voyage ?
items.translation: (Do you remember our first trip?)
items.explanation: 'Se souvenir de' means 'to remember' and always requires 'de.' 'Tu te souviens de notre premier voyage ?' = Do you remember our first trip? With pronouns: 'Tu t'en souviens ?' (Do you remember it? — 'en' replaces 'de + noun'). For people: 'Je me souviens de lui' (I remember him). Compare with 'se rappeler' which takes a direct object (no 'de'): 'Je me rappelle ce voyage.' Both mean 'to remember' but have different constructions. Getting these prepositions right is a hallmark of advanced French.

items.question: Nous avons besoin _____ temps pour réfléchir.
items.translation: (We need time to think.)
items.explanation: 'Avoir besoin de' means 'to need.' 'Nous avons besoin de temps' = We need time. Note that before an uncountable noun without article, plain 'de' is used. With a specific thing: 'J'ai besoin du livre' (I need the book — de + le = du). With pronouns: 'J'en ai besoin' (I need it/some). With infinitive: 'J'ai besoin de dormir' (I need to sleep). This is one of the most essential 'de' constructions in French, used dozens of times daily. Never omit the 'de' — it's not optional like in English.

items.question: Il a renoncé _____ son projet.
items.translation: (He gave up on his project.)
items.explanation: 'Renoncer à' means 'to give up' or 'to renounce.' 'Il a renoncé à son projet' = He gave up on his project. This verb takes 'à' before nouns and infinitives: 'renoncer à fumer' (to give up smoking). With pronouns: 'Il y a renoncé' (He gave up on it). Similar verbs with 'à': 'résister à' (to resist), 'obéir à' (to obey), 'désobéir à' (to disobey), 'succéder à' (to follow/succeed). These are often called 'indirect transitive verbs' because the object is introduced by a preposition.

items.question: Elle rêve _____ devenir médecin.
items.translation: (She dreams of becoming a doctor.)
items.explanation: 'Rêver de' means 'to dream of/about.' 'Elle rêve de devenir médecin' = She dreams of becoming a doctor. Note: 'rêver à' also exists but is more literary and means 'to daydream about' or 'to muse on.' The standard expression for aspirational dreaming is 'rêver de.' With pronouns: 'J'en rêve' (I dream of it). Past tense: 'J'ai rêvé de toi' (I dreamed about you). The noun is 'un rêve' (a dream). This verb is a good example of how seemingly simple French verbs require careful preposition selection.

name: Registre Soutenu (Formal Register)
description: Master the elevated, formal French used in literature, official documents, and elegant speech

items.question: Nous vous saurions gré de bien vouloir _____ votre réponse.
items.translation: (We would be grateful if you would kindly send your response.)
items.explanation: In formal French, 'transmettre' (to transmit/forward) is preferred over 'envoyer' (to send) when addressing officials or in business correspondence. The expression 'nous vous saurions gré' is an extremely polished way of saying 'we would be grateful.' This conditional form of 'savoir gré' belongs to the highest register of French and appears frequently in formal letters, legal documents, and diplomatic communications.

items.question: Je vous prie d'agréer, Madame, l'expression de mes salutations _____.
items.translation: (Please accept, Madam, the expression of my distinguished regards.)
items.explanation: This is the classic French formal letter closing, equivalent to 'Yours sincerely' but far more elaborate. 'Salutations distinguées' means 'distinguished regards.' French formal correspondence follows strict conventions — the closing must match the opening in formality. Using 'bonnes' would be too casual, while 'gentilles' or 'grandes' would sound bizarre. This formula has survived centuries and remains mandatory in professional French writing.

items.question: Il _____ de noter que cette décision est irrévocable.
items.translation: (It is appropriate to note that this decision is irrevocable.)
items.explanation: 'Il convient de' is a formal equivalent of 'il faut' (it is necessary), but with a nuance of propriety and appropriateness rather than strict obligation. It literally means 'it is fitting/appropriate.' This construction appears extensively in legal texts, academic papers, and official reports. While 'il faut' is perfectly correct, choosing 'il convient de' signals that you are operating at a higher register of language and understand the subtle distinctions French prizes.

items.question: Veuillez trouver _____ les documents demandés.
items.translation: (Please find enclosed the requested documents.)
items.explanation: 'Ci-joint' is the formal French term for 'enclosed' or 'attached,' used exclusively in professional correspondence and official documents. It combines 'ci' (here) with 'joint' (joined/attached). Note: when 'ci-joint' precedes the noun without an article, it remains invariable — 'ci-joint les documents.' When it follows the noun, it agrees: 'les documents ci-joints.' This grammatical subtlety is a hallmark of polished French writing that educated speakers take pride in mastering.

items.question: Nous avons l'honneur de porter à votre _____ que le dossier a été traité.
items.translation: (We have the honour of bringing to your attention that the file has been processed.)
items.explanation: 'Porter à votre connaissance' (to bring to your knowledge) is far more formal than 'informer' or 'dire.' The entire phrase 'nous avons l'honneur de' (we have the honour of) elevates the register to the highest level of official French. This type of formulaic language is essential in administrative French — government notices, legal communications, and diplomatic correspondence. While it may seem overly elaborate to English speakers, it remains standard practice in French institutional writing.

items.question: Suite à notre entretien, je me permets de vous _____ à ce sujet.
items.translation: (Following our meeting, I take the liberty of contacting you on this matter.)
items.explanation: 'Se permettre de' (to take the liberty of) is a key formal register phrase that softens a potentially imposing action. 'Relancer' means to follow up or chase — it's the professional term for contacting someone again about a pending matter. In French business culture, 'relancer quelqu'un' is perfectly acceptable and even expected. The combination of 'je me permets' with 'relancer' creates the ideal balance of assertiveness and politeness that French professional communication demands.

items.question: Auriez-vous l'_____ de me transmettre ce document ?
items.translation: (Would you be so kind as to send me this document?)
items.explanation: 'Auriez-vous l'amabilité de' (would you have the kindness to) is one of the most elegant ways to make a request in formal French. While 'gentillesse' and 'bonté' also mean kindness, 'amabilité' carries a distinctly refined, professional tone. The conditional 'auriez-vous' adds another layer of politeness. This construction is ideal for emails to superiors, formal requests, or any situation where maximum courtesy is required. It transforms a simple request into a graceful social interaction.

items.question: Il est _____ de rappeler les termes du contrat.
items.translation: (It is fitting to recall the terms of the contract.)
items.explanation: 'Opportun' (opportune/fitting/timely) belongs to the formal register and is far more sophisticated than 'bon' or 'bien.' In formal French, choosing precise, elevated vocabulary signals education and professionalism. 'Il est opportun de' suggests that the timing and circumstances make this action particularly appropriate — it carries strategic nuance that simpler alternatives lack. This adjective appears frequently in boardroom discussions, legal arguments, and political discourse where precision of expression matters.

items.question: Nous vous prions de bien vouloir _____ toute erreur éventuelle.
items.translation: (We kindly ask you to excuse any possible error.)
items.explanation: 'Nous vous prions de bien vouloir excuser' is the formal way to apologize for potential errors. The layered politeness — 'nous vous prions' (we pray/ask you), 'de bien vouloir' (to be willing to), 'excuser' (to excuse) — creates maximum deference. While 'pardonner' is also formal, 'excuser' is the standard choice in this fixed expression. 'Éventuelle' means 'possible/potential,' not 'eventual' — a subtle distinction that trips up many English speakers learning French formal register.

items.question: En _____ de quoi, les parties ont signé le présent acte.
items.translation: (In witness whereof, the parties have signed the present deed.)
items.explanation: 'En foi de quoi' is a legal formula meaning 'in witness/faith whereof,' used at the end of contracts and official deeds. 'Foi' means faith — the expression literally means 'in faith of which.' This is pure legal register French that has remained unchanged for centuries. 'Le présent acte' (the present deed) is another formal legal term. Understanding these fixed expressions is crucial for anyone working with French legal documents, notarial acts, or official contracts.

items.question: Il m'_____ de vous informer de ma décision.
items.translation: (It falls upon me to inform you of my decision.)
items.explanation: 'Il m'incombe de' (it falls upon me to/it is incumbent upon me) is highly formal and conveys a sense of duty and responsibility. While 'il m'appartient de' is also formal, 'incombe' carries a weightier sense of obligation. This verb comes from Latin 'incumbere' and is used almost exclusively in formal written French — legal proceedings, official declarations, and scholarly texts. The impersonal construction 'il + indirect object pronoun + incombe de' is a signature pattern of elevated French.

items.question: Nous _____ à votre entière disposition pour tout renseignement complémentaire.
items.translation: (We remain at your complete disposal for any further information.)
items.explanation: 'Demeurer' (to remain) is the formal equivalent of 'rester.' In professional correspondence, 'nous demeurons à votre disposition' is more elevated than 'nous restons.' 'Entière disposition' (complete disposal) and 'renseignement complémentaire' (supplementary information) further reinforce the formal tone. This closing formula is standard in French business letters and signals that the writer masters the conventions of professional communication. It's a mark of competence and respect.

items.question: Par la _____ lettre, je vous confirme notre accord.
items.translation: (By the present letter, I confirm our agreement to you.)
items.explanation: 'Par la présente' (by the present letter) is a standard formal opening in French official correspondence. 'Présente' here functions as both adjective and implicit noun — it refers to the letter itself. This formula immediately establishes the formal, often legal, nature of the communication. It's used in resignation letters, official notifications, legal notices, and administrative correspondence. The elegance of French formal writing lies in these traditional formulas that connect modern communication to centuries of epistolary tradition.

items.question: Je vous serais _____ de bien vouloir accuser réception de ce courrier.
items.translation: (I would be obliged if you would kindly acknowledge receipt of this letter.)
items.explanation: 'Je vous serais obligé de' (I would be obliged if you would) is a classic formal request formula. While 'reconnaissant' (grateful) is also formal, 'obligé' carries a stronger sense of formal indebtedness. 'Accuser réception' (to acknowledge receipt) is the standard formal term — never just 'confirmer.' The conditional 'serais' softens the request beautifully.
