items.translation: (I would like to take a break.)
items.explanation: 'Faire une pause' (to take a break) is the standard expression. 'Je souhaiterais faire une pause' uses the conditional of 'souhaiter' (to wish) — even more polite than 'je voudrais.' In French offices, 'la pause café' (coffee break) and 'la pause déjeuner' (lunch break) are important social rituals. The lunch break in France traditionally lasts 1-2 hours, reflecting the cultural importance of midday meals.

items.question: 'Le délai de ce projet ___ fixé au 15 mars.' — Stating a project deadline.
items.translation: (The deadline for this project is set for March 15.)
items.explanation: 'Le délai est fixé au 15 mars' uses the present tense for a current state. 'Délai' means 'deadline' or 'timeframe' — a false friend, as English 'delay' means a different thing. Other project vocabulary: 'échéance' (due date), 'livrable' (deliverable), 'planning' (schedule — borrowed from English but used differently), 'rétroplanning' (reverse planning/working backward from deadline).

items.question: 'N'hésitez pas à me ___ si vous avez des questions.' — Standard professional closing.
items.translation: (Don't hesitate to contact me if you have any questions.)
items.explanation: 'N'hésitez pas à me contacter' (Don't hesitate to contact me) is a standard closing formula in French professional emails and letters. This polite imperative ('n'hésitez pas' — don't hesitate) is ubiquitous in French business communication. Variations include: 'N'hésitez pas à me joindre' (Don't hesitate to reach me), 'Je reste à votre disposition' (I remain at your disposal — very formal).

items.question: 'Il a été ___ pour faute grave.' — Describing a dismissal.
items.translation: (He was fired for serious misconduct.)
items.explanation: 'Licencié' is the formal, legal term for being fired/dismissed. 'Licencié pour faute grave' means 'dismissed for serious misconduct.' The register matters: 'licencier' (formal/legal), 'renvoyer' (neutral), 'virer' (slang). 'Faute grave' (serious misconduct) is a legal term that affects severance rights. French labor law ('Code du travail') provides extensive employee protections, making 'licenciement' a highly regulated process.

items.question: 'Je ___ en congé la semaine prochaine.' — Announcing planned leave.
items.translation: (I'll be on leave next week.)
items.explanation: 'Être en congé' means 'to be on leave.' 'Je serai en congé la semaine prochaine' uses the future tense for a planned absence. French workers receive a minimum of 5 weeks paid vacation ('congés payés') per year by law. Types of leave: 'congé maladie' (sick leave), 'congé maternité' (maternity leave), 'RTT' (reduction du temps de travail — extra days off from the 35-hour workweek).

items.question: 'Merci de bien ___ noter ce changement.' — Formal memo instruction.
items.translation: (Please note this change.)
items.explanation: 'Merci de bien vouloir' (literally 'thank you for being willing to') is a formal polite request formula used in professional memos and emails. 'Merci de bien vouloir noter ce changement' means 'Please note this change.' This construction is more demanding than 'pourriez-vous' but wraps the command in politeness. It is characteristic of French administrative and corporate communication style.

items.question: 'Les résultats ___ au-delà de nos attentes.' — Presenting positive results.
items.translation: (The results exceeded our expectations.)
items.explanation: 'Aller au-delà de' (to go beyond) uses 'être' as its auxiliary in the passé composé since 'aller' is a movement verb. 'Les résultats sont allés au-delà de nos attentes' means 'The results went beyond our expectations.' Professional presentation vocabulary includes: 'perspectives' (outlook), 'prévisions' (forecasts), 'performances' (performance results), and 'croissance' (growth).

name: Technologie et Internet (Technology & Internet)
description: Express yourself about digital life, technology, and the internet using French-specific vocabulary and contemporary grammar structures

items.question: 'Je dois ___ mon mot de passe.' — Resetting login credentials.
items.translation: (I need to reset my password.)
items.explanation: 'Réinitialiser' (to reset) is the technical French term for resetting a password or device. 'Réinitialiser mon mot de passe' means 'to reset my password.' In French, 'mot de passe' (password — literally 'word of passage') is masculine. Common tech vocabulary: 'nom d'utilisateur' (username), 'identifiant' (login ID), 'se connecter' (to log in), 'se déconnecter' (to log out).

items.question: 'L'application ___ mise à jour automatiquement.' — Passive voice in tech context.
items.translation: (The app is updated automatically.)
items.explanation: The present passive 'est mise à jour' describes an ongoing automatic process. 'Mise à jour' (update — literally 'put up to date') is a feminine noun phrase. The past participle 'mise' agrees with 'l'application' (feminine). French tech vocabulary often differs from English: 'télécharger' covers both 'download' and 'upload,' requiring context to distinguish. 'Mettre à jour' (to update) is the verb form.

items.question: 'Il faut que tu ___ tes fichiers régulièrement.' — Advice about backing up files.
items.translation: (You need to back up your files regularly.)
items.explanation: 'Il faut que' requires the subjunctive. 'Il faut que tu sauvegardes tes fichiers' means 'You need to back up your files.' 'Sauvegarder' (to back up/save) has the subjunctive form 'sauvegardes' for 'tu.' French tech distinguishes between 'sauvegarder' (save a backup copy), 'enregistrer' (save/record), and 'stocker' (store). This semantic precision matters in technical communication.

items.question: 'Mon ordinateur ___ planté pendant la présentation.' — Describing a computer crash.
items.translation: (My computer crashed during the presentation.)
items.explanation: 'Planter' (to crash — informal but widely used) conjugates with 'avoir.' 'Mon ordinateur a planté' means 'My computer crashed.' While 'planter' originally means 'to plant,' its tech slang usage is universal in French. More formal: 'tomber en panne' (to break down), 'buguer' (to bug out), 'se bloquer' (to freeze). French readily borrows English tech terms but adapts their pronunciation and grammar.

items.question: 'Peux-tu m'___ le lien par courriel?' — Sharing digital content.
items.translation: (Can you send me the link by email?)
items.explanation: 'Envoyer par courriel' (to send by email) uses the formal French word 'courriel' — a contraction of 'courrier électronique' (electronic mail). While 'e-mail' and 'mail' are commonly used in spoken French, 'courriel' is the official term mandated by the Académie française and used in formal contexts. The pronoun pattern 'm'envoyer le lien' places the indirect object before the verb.

items.question: 'Ce site ___ n'est pas sécurisé.' — Warning about website security.
items.translation: (This website is not secure.)
items.explanation: 'Site web' (website) uses the English word 'web' directly in French. 'Ce site web n'est pas sécurisé' means 'This website is not secure.' While the Académie française suggests 'site de la Toile' (literally 'site on the Web'), 'site web' is universally used. Security vocabulary: 'sécurisé' (secure), 'chiffré' (encrypted), 'pare-feu' (firewall — literally 'fire-wall'), 'logiciel malveillant' (malware).

items.question: 'Je ___ abonné à cette chaîne YouTube.' — Subscribing to online content.
items.translation: (I subscribed to this YouTube channel.)
items.explanation: 'S'abonner à' (to subscribe to) is reflexive and conjugates with 'être' in the passé composé. 'Je me suis abonné' means 'I subscribed.' French uses 'abonnement' (subscription) for both digital and traditional services. Related terms: 'se désabonner' (to unsubscribe), 'abonnement mensuel' (monthly subscription), 'contenu exclusif' (exclusive content), 'notification' (notification).

items.question: 'Il ___ que de la désinformation circule sur les réseaux sociaux.' — Social media concerns.
items.translation: (It seems that misinformation circulates on social media.)
items.explanation: 'Il semble que' (it seems that) can take either indicative or subjunctive depending on certainty. 'Il semble que de la désinformation circule' uses the indicative, suggesting the speaker considers it factual. 'Les réseaux sociaux' (social networks) is the standard French term for social media. 'Désinformation' (disinformation) and 'infox' (fake news — official French neologism) are key media literacy terms.

items.question: 'N'oublie pas de ___ ton téléphone avant de partir.' — Device charging reminder.
items.translation: (Don't forget to charge your phone before leaving.)
items.explanation: 'Charger' (to charge) is the standard term for device charging. 'Charger ton téléphone' means 'to charge your phone.' While 'recharger' (recharge) is also correct, 'charger' is more common in everyday speech. Related vocabulary: 'chargeur' (charger), 'batterie' (battery), 'brancher' (to plug in), 'prise' (outlet/plug), 'câble' (cable). 'Mon téléphone est à plat' means 'My phone is dead' (battery empty).

items.question: 'Les données ___ stockées dans le cloud.' — Data storage using passive voice.
items.translation: (The data is stored in the cloud.)
items.explanation: 'Les données sont stockées' uses the present passive to describe the current state of data storage. 'Données' (data) is feminine plural in French. While France has attempted to replace 'le cloud' with 'l'informatique en nuage' (literally 'computing in the cloud'), 'le cloud' remains dominant in everyday usage. French takes 'data' concepts seriously — the RGPD (GDPR) 'Règlement Général sur la Protection des Données' is actively enforced.

items.question: 'Tu devrais ___ un antivirus sur ton ordinateur.' — Security recommendation.
items.translation: (You should install an antivirus on your computer.)
items.explanation: 'Installer' (to install) is the standard verb for software installation. 'Tu devrais installer un antivirus' uses the conditional of 'devoir' for a polite recommendation. French distinguishes between 'installer' (install — set up for use), 'télécharger' (download — get from internet), and 'désinstaller' (uninstall). The complete process: 'télécharger, installer, configurer' (download, install, configure).

items.question: 'Mon écran s'est ___ tout seul.' — Describing a tech malfunction.
items.translation: (My screen turned off by itself.)
items.explanation: 'S'éteindre' (to turn off — reflexive) uses the past participle 'éteint.' 'Mon écran s'est éteint' means 'My screen turned off.' As a reflexive verb conjugated with 'être,' the past participle agrees with the subject: 'l'écran' is masculine, so 'éteint' (not 'éteinte'). The opposite is 's'allumer' (to turn on). Tech troubleshooting: 'redémarrer' (restart), 'actualiser' (refresh), 'déboguer' (debug).

items.question: 'Il est interdit de ___ du contenu protégé par le droit d'auteur.' — Copyright warning.
items.translation: (It is prohibited to download copyrighted content.)
items.explanation: 'Il est interdit de' + infinitive is the impersonal construction for prohibitions. 'Télécharger du contenu protégé' means 'to download protected content.' 'Droit d'auteur' (copyright — literally 'author's right') reflects France's strong intellectual property tradition. The Hadopi law (now Arcom) monitors illegal downloading in France. This impersonal prohibition structure is used across all regulatory and legal contexts.

items.question: 'L'intelligence artificielle va ___ notre façon de travailler.' — AI's impact.
items.translation: (Artificial intelligence will transform our way of working.)
items.explanation: 'Aller + infinitive' (near future) expresses an expected change. 'L'intelligence artificielle va transformer notre façon de travailler' means 'AI will transform how we work.' 'Façon de travailler' (way of working) uses 'de' + infinitive after a noun. French AI vocabulary: 'apprentissage automatique' (machine learning), 'réseau neuronal' (neural network), 'algorithme' (algorithm), 'données massives' (big data).

items.question: 'Je ne ___ plus me passer de mon smartphone.' — Digital dependency.
items.translation: (I can no longer do without my smartphone.)
items.explanation: 'Ne plus pouvoir se passer de' means 'to no longer be able to do without.' 'Je ne peux plus me passer de mon smartphone' expresses digital dependency. 'Se passer de' (to do without) is a key expression that takes a reflexive construction. The negation 'ne...plus' (no longer) wraps around the conjugated verb 'peux.' France uses 'smartphone' (borrowed from English) alongside the official recommendation 'téléphone intelligent.'

name: Environnement et Nature (Environment & Nature)
description: Discuss environmental issues, nature, and sustainability using specialized French vocabulary and grammatical structures

items.question: 'Il faut ___ les ressources naturelles.' — Talking about conservation.
items.translation: (We must preserve natural resources.)
items.explanation: 'Préserver' (to preserve) is the precise French term for conservation efforts related to natural resources. While 'garder' means to keep and 'sauver' means to save, 'préserver les ressources naturelles' is the standard ecological expression in French, paralleling the English 'preserve natural resources.' This verb implies active protection and long-term care.

items.question: 'La ___ des espèces menacées est une priorité.' — Discussing endangered species.
items.translation: (The protection of endangered species is a priority.)
items.explanation: 'Protection' is the most commonly used French noun when discussing the safeguarding of endangered species (espèces menacées). While 'sauvegarde' also works in some contexts, 'la protection des espèces menacées' is the standard phrase in French environmental discourse. Note that 'menacées' (threatened) agrees in gender and number with 'espèces' (feminine plural).

items.question: 'Le réchauffement ___ affecte notre planète.' — Climate change discussion.
items.translation: (Global warming affects our planet.)
items.explanation: 'Le réchauffement climatique' is the standard French expression for 'global warming.' Unlike English, which uses 'global warming,' French uses 'climatique' (climatic) to modify 'réchauffement' (warming). This is a fixed expression in French environmental vocabulary. The adjective 'climatique' agrees with the masculine noun 'réchauffement' and is always placed after the noun.

items.question: 'Nous devons ___ notre empreinte carbone.' — Reducing environmental impact.
items.translation: (We must reduce our carbon footprint.)
items.explanation: 'Réduire' (to reduce) is the standard verb used with 'empreinte carbone' (carbon footprint) in French. While 'diminuer' and 'baisser' also mean to decrease, 'réduire son empreinte carbone' is the established collocation in French environmental language. 'Couper' (to cut) would not be appropriate in this context as it implies a physical cutting action rather than a gradual reduction.

items.question: 'Le tri ___ est obligatoire dans notre commune.' — Waste management.
items.translation: (Waste sorting is mandatory in our municipality.)
items.explanation: 'Le tri sélectif' is the specific French term for waste sorting or selective waste collection. This compound noun is a key piece of everyday French environmental vocabulary. 'Sélectif' (selective) modifies 'tri' (sorting) and together they describe the process of separating recyclable materials. In France, 'le tri sélectif' has been part of daily life since the 1990s and is a term every French speaker knows.

items.question: 'Les énergies ___ sont l'avenir.' — Talking about clean energy.
items.translation: (Renewable energies are the future.)
items.explanation: 'Les énergies renouvelables' (renewable energies) is the standard French term for energy from sustainable sources like solar, wind, and hydro. While 'propres' (clean) and 'vertes' (green) can describe energy, 'renouvelables' is the technical and most precise adjective. It agrees with the feminine plural noun 'énergies.' This term appears constantly in French media and policy discussions about energy transition.

items.question: 'La ___ de l'eau potable est un enjeu mondial.' — Water scarcity.
items.translation: (The scarcity of drinking water is a global issue.)
items.explanation: 'Pénurie' (scarcity/shortage) is the most precise French term when discussing resource shortages at a large scale. While 'manque' means lack and 'rareté' means rarity, 'pénurie' specifically conveys a critical shortage situation. 'Eau potable' means drinking water (literally 'drinkable water'). 'Pénurie d'eau potable' is the standard expression in French journalism and policy language.

items.question: 'Il est interdit de ___ des déchets dans la nature.' — Anti-littering.
items.translation: (It is forbidden to dump waste in nature.)
items.explanation: 'Jeter' (to throw/dump) is the standard verb used for disposing of waste improperly. In anti-littering contexts, 'jeter des déchets' (to throw waste) is the established French expression. 'Lancer' means to throw/launch (too forceful), 'abandonner' means to abandon, and 'déposer' means to deposit (too formal). The impersonal construction 'il est interdit de' + infinitive is a common way to express prohibitions in French.

items.question: 'La forêt ___ est en danger à cause de la déforestation.' — Deforestation.
items.translation: (The tropical forest is in danger due to deforestation.)
items.explanation: 'La forêt tropicale' (tropical forest/rainforest) is the standard French term. While 'équatoriale' (equatorial) is sometimes used, 'tropicale' is the most common adjective for this type of forest. 'La déforestation' is a direct cognate with English. Note the expression 'à cause de' (due to/because of), which introduces a negative cause in French — for positive causes, French prefers 'grâce à' (thanks to).

items.question: 'Les ___ à effet de serre augmentent chaque année.' — Greenhouse gases.
items.translation: (Greenhouse gas emissions increase every year.)
items.explanation: 'Les gaz à effet de serre' (greenhouse gases — literally 'gases with greenhouse effect') is the standard French scientific term. Note that 'gaz' is invariable in French — it does not change in the plural (un gaz, des gaz). The expression 'à effet de serre' functions as a fixed modifier. This term is essential in French climate change discourse and appears regularly in news media, scientific reports, and policy documents.

items.question: 'Le développement ___ concilie économie et écologie.' — Sustainability.
items.translation: (Sustainable development reconciles economy and ecology.)
items.explanation: 'Le développement durable' (sustainable development) is the official French term adopted in international discourse. While 'soutenable' is a direct translation of 'sustainable,' French has standardized on 'durable' (lasting/durable). This term was officially adopted after the 1992 Rio Earth Summit. 'Concilier' (to reconcile/balance) is an important verb in French discourse about balancing competing interests.

items.question: 'La ___ de la biodiversité est alarmante.' — Biodiversity loss.
items.translation: (The loss of biodiversity is alarming.)
items.explanation: 'La perte de la biodiversité' (the loss of biodiversity) is the standard French expression in environmental science. While 'disparition' (disappearance) could work, 'perte' directly parallels the English 'loss' and is the preferred term in official French environmental reports. 'Alarmante' (alarming) agrees with the feminine noun 'perte.' Note the use of the definite article 'la' before 'biodiversité,' which is standard in French abstract nouns.

items.question: 'Les pesticides ___ la qualité des sols.' — Soil quality.
items.translation: (Pesticides degrade the quality of soils.)
items.explanation: 'Dégrader' (to degrade) is the precise scientific French term for the gradual deterioration of environmental quality. While 'détruire' (destroy) is too absolute and 'abîmer' (damage) is too colloquial for this context, 'dégrader la qualité des sols' is the standard expression in French environmental science. 'Les sols' (soils) is used in the plural to indicate soil as a natural resource across different areas.

items.question: 'Cette région est classée ___ naturelle protégée.' — Protected areas.
items.translation: (This region is classified as a protected natural reserve.)
items.explanation: 'Réserve naturelle protégée' (protected natural reserve) is the official French administrative designation for protected areas. While 'parc' (park) and 'zone' (zone) exist as separate designations, 'réserve naturelle' specifically refers to areas with strict conservation regulations. The passive construction 'est classée' (is classified) uses the feminine form to agree with 'région.' French uses 'classé(e)' rather than 'catégorisé(e)' for official designations.

items.question: 'Il faut ___ les générations futures.' — Thinking about the future.
items.translation: (We must think about future generations.)
items.explanation: 'Penser à' (to think about) is the correct verb + preposition combination in French for considering or being mindful of something. 'Penser aux générations futures' (think about future generations) uses 'aux' which is the contraction of 'à + les.' In French, 'penser à' means to think about/be mindful of, while 'penser de' means to have an opinion about. This distinction is crucial and frequently tested in French grammar.

name: Éducation et Apprentissage (Education & Learning)
description: Navigate the French education system and academic contexts with proper vocabulary, expressions, and grammatical structures

items.question: 'Elle a ___ son examen avec mention.' — Exam results.
items.translation: (She passed her exam with honors.)
items.explanation: 'Réussir un examen' means to pass an exam successfully in French. This is a crucial distinction: 'passer un examen' means to take/sit an exam (not to pass it!), which is a common false friend for English speakers. 'Obtenir' means to obtain and 'gagner' means to win — neither is used for exam results. 'Avec mention' means 'with honors/distinction,' a standard French academic expression.

items.question: 'Les élèves doivent ___ leurs devoirs avant lundi.' — Homework deadlines.
items.translation: (Students must hand in their homework before Monday.)
items.explanation: 'Rendre' (to hand in/return) is the standard French verb for submitting homework or assignments. While 'soumettre' exists, it is overly formal for everyday school contexts. 'Rendre ses devoirs' (to hand in one's homework) is the expression every French student knows. Note that 'devoirs' (homework) is always plural in French, unlike English where it can be singular. 'Avant' (before) is followed directly by the time expression without 'de.'

items.question: 'Le professeur a ___ une nouvelle méthode d'enseignement.' — Teaching methods.
items.translation: (The teacher adopted a new teaching method.)
items.explanation: 'Adopter une méthode' (to adopt a method) is the standard French expression for implementing a new approach. While 'utiliser' (to use) and 'choisir' (to choose) are related, 'adopter' specifically conveys the intentional implementation of something new. 'Méthode d'enseignement' (teaching method) uses the preposition 'de' to link the two nouns, which is the French equivalent of the English compound noun construction.

items.question: 'Je me suis ___ à la bibliothèque pour étudier.' — Study habits.
items.translation: (I settled in the library to study.)
items.explanation: 'S'installer' (to settle/set up) is a reflexive verb meaning to establish oneself somewhere comfortably, typically for an extended period. In the passé composé with a reflexive verb, it uses 'être' as the auxiliary: 'je me suis installé(e).' The past participle agrees with the subject — add an 'e' for feminine. 'À la bibliothèque' uses the contracted form of 'à + la.' 'Pour + infinitive' expresses purpose.

items.question: 'Il a obtenu son ___ avec les félicitations du jury.' — University degrees.
items.translation: (He obtained his degree with the jury's highest honors.)
items.explanation: 'Diplôme' is the standard French word for a university degree or diploma. While 'certificat' is for certificates and 'brevet' is for patents or specific technical qualifications, 'diplôme' covers bachelor's, master's, and doctoral degrees. 'Les félicitations du jury' (the jury's congratulations) is the highest distinction in the French grading system, awarded only for exceptional academic work. This reflects the unique French academic tradition.

items.question: 'La ___ scolaire en France dure de septembre à juillet.' — School year.
items.translation: (The school year in France lasts from September to July.)
items.explanation: 'L'année scolaire' (the school year) is the standard French term. Note the distinction between 'année' (year — emphasizing duration) and 'an' (year — emphasizing a point in time). With 'scolaire' (school-related), always use 'année.' The construction 'durer de... à...' (to last from... to...) is the standard way to express a duration with defined start and end points in French.

items.question: 'Elle s'est ___ en littérature française.' — Academic specialization.
items.translation: (She specialized in French literature.)
items.explanation: 'Se spécialiser en' (to specialize in) is the standard French reflexive expression for academic specialization. Note the past participle agreement: 'spécialisée' takes an 'e' because the subject 'elle' is feminine. With reflexive verbs in passé composé, the past participle agrees with the reflexive pronoun (which refers to the subject). 'En littérature' uses the preposition 'en' for academic fields, not 'dans la.'

items.question: 'Le ___ est noté sur vingt en France.' — French grading system.
items.translation: (Grading is out of twenty in France.)
items.explanation: 'Le barème' (the grading scale/marking scheme) is a distinctly French educational term. In France, most academic work is graded on a scale of 0 to 20 ('sur vingt'). A score of 10/20 is passing, 14+ is considered good, and 16+ is excellent. This system differs fundamentally from the A-F system used in English-speaking countries. 'Noté sur vingt' means 'graded out of twenty,' using the passive construction with 'sur' to indicate the scale.

items.question: 'Les étudiants font un ___ en entreprise chaque année.' — Internships.
items.translation: (Students do an internship in a company each year.)
items.explanation: 'Un stage' (an internship/work placement) is the standard French word for a professional internship. This is a false friend alert: 'stage' in French means internship, not a theatrical stage (which is 'scène'). 'Faire un stage' (to do an internship) is the standard collocation. 'En entreprise' (in a company) uses 'en' without an article, which is standard for this fixed expression. 'Chaque année' (each year) never takes a plural marker.

items.question: 'Le ___ des matières ce semestre est très chargé.' — Course load.
items.translation: (The course program this semester is very full.)
items.explanation: 'Le programme des matières' (the course program/curriculum) is the standard French expression for the subjects studied during a semester. While 'calendrier' is for schedules and 'horaire' is for timetables, 'programme' encompasses the overall academic content. 'Chargé' (loaded/busy) is the idiomatic French adjective for a full or heavy workload. Note 'ce semestre' uses the demonstrative adjective 'ce' before a masculine noun.

items.question: 'Il a ___ sa thèse devant un jury de cinq professeurs.' — Thesis defense.
items.translation: (He defended his thesis before a panel of five professors.)
items.explanation: 'Soutenir sa thèse' (to defend one's thesis — literally 'to support one's thesis') is the specific French academic expression. While 'défendre' is the direct translation of 'to defend,' French academia uses 'soutenir' for this formal event. 'La soutenance de thèse' (thesis defense) is the noun form. 'Devant un jury' (before a panel) uses 'devant' (in front of) to indicate the formal setting of the academic examination.

items.question: 'Les cours ___ commencent à huit heures.' — Class schedules.
items.translation: (The compulsory classes start at eight o'clock.)
items.explanation: 'Obligatoire' (compulsory/mandatory) is the standard French adjective for required courses. Unlike English, which uses 'required' or 'mandatory' interchangeably, French strongly prefers 'obligatoire' in academic contexts. The adjective agrees with the plural noun 'cours' (which is invariable — same form singular and plural). 'Commencer à' + time is the standard way to express when something starts in French.

items.question: 'Elle a été ___ à l'université de la Sorbonne.' — University admission.
items.translation: (She was admitted to the Sorbonne University.)
items.explanation: 'Être admis(e)' (to be admitted) is the formal French expression for university admission. The past participle 'admise' takes the feminine 'e' ending to agree with 'elle.' While 'acceptée' could work in informal contexts, 'admis(e)' is the official administrative term. Note that French says 'à l'université' (at/to the university) using the contracted article. 'La Sorbonne' keeps its definite article as part of the proper name.

items.question: 'Le professeur a ___ les copies corrigées.' — Returning graded work.
items.translation: (The teacher returned the graded papers.)
items.explanation: 'Rendre' (to return/give back) is the standard French verb for returning graded assignments. While 'retourner' can mean to return, it refers to going back to a place, not handing something back. 'Les copies corrigées' (the corrected papers) uses 'copies' — the French word for exam papers or assignments — and 'corrigées' as a past participle used as an adjective agreeing with the feminine plural noun.

items.question: 'Ils ont ___ un exposé sur la Révolution française.' — Class presentations.
items.translation: (They gave a presentation on the French Revolution.)
items.explanation: 'Faire un exposé' (to give a presentation — literally 'to do a presentation') is the standard French expression for class presentations. While 'présenter' exists, the collocation 'faire un exposé' is the most natural and commonly used form. 'Un exposé' is a formal oral presentation, distinct from 'une présentation' which is more general. 'Sur' (on/about) introduces the topic. Note that 'Révolution française' capitalizes 'Révolution' but not 'française.'

name: Sentiments et Émotions (Feelings & Emotions)
description: Express a full range of feelings and emotional states using authentic French expressions, subjunctive triggers, and nuanced vocabulary

items.question: 'Je suis ___ que tu aies réussi.' — Expressing happiness about someone's success.
items.translation: (I am delighted that you passed.)
items.explanation: 'Ravi(e)' (delighted) is a strong expression of happiness in French that triggers the subjunctive in the subordinate clause. Notice 'que tu aies réussi' uses the subjunctive past (subjonctif passé) because the happiness is about a completed action. All emotion expressions with 'être + adjective + que' require the subjunctive in French. 'Ravi' conveys more intensity than 'content' (pleased) or 'heureux' (happy).

items.question: 'Elle a ___ de tristesse en apprenant la nouvelle.' — Deep sadness.
items.translation: (She cried with sadness upon hearing the news.)
items.explanation: 'Pleurer de tristesse' (to cry with/from sadness) uses 'de' to indicate the cause of crying. This construction 'verb + de + emotion' is characteristic of French: 'pleurer de joie' (cry with joy), 'trembler de peur' (tremble with fear). 'En apprenant' is the gérondif (present participle with 'en'), meaning 'upon/while learning.' This gérondif construction expresses simultaneity or immediate cause.

items.question: 'Il est ___ par cette situation injuste.' — Expressing outrage.
items.translation: (He is outraged by this unjust situation.)
items.explanation: 'Indigné' (outraged/indignant) expresses moral outrage at an unjustice, stronger than 'fâché' (angry) or 'énervé' (irritated). The preposition 'par' (by) introduces the cause of the emotion when using passive-like constructions. 'Injuste' (unjust) agrees with 'situation' (feminine). French distinguishes between levels of anger: 'agacé' (annoyed) < 'énervé' (irritated) < 'fâché' (angry) < 'furieux' (furious) < 'indigné' (morally outraged).

items.question: 'Nous avons ___ de la compassion pour les victimes.' — Expressing empathy.
items.translation: (We felt compassion for the victims.)
items.explanation: 'Éprouver de la compassion' (to feel compassion) is the most refined French expression for experiencing deep emotions. While 'ressentir' and 'sentir' also mean to feel, 'éprouver' carries a sense of profound, genuine emotional experience. 'De la' is the partitive article required before abstract uncountable nouns in French. 'Pour' (for) links the emotion to its object. This verb is characteristic of formal and literary French register.

items.question: 'Je crains qu'il ne ___ trop tard.' — Expressing fear.
items.translation: (I fear it may be too late.)
items.explanation: 'Craindre que' (to fear that) requires the subjunctive in the subordinate clause. 'Soit' is the subjunctive form of 'être.' Notice the expletive 'ne' (ne explétif) after 'craindre que' — this 'ne' does not make the sentence negative; it is a stylistic marker in formal French. This construction 'craindre que... ne + subjunctive' is a hallmark of educated French. 'Trop tard' (too late) is an invariable adverbial expression.

items.question: 'Elle est ___ de son comportement hier.' — Expressing shame.
items.translation: (She is ashamed of her behavior yesterday.)
items.explanation: 'Honteux/honteuse' (ashamed) is the strongest French word for shame. The feminine form 'honteuse' agrees with 'elle.' 'De' introduces the cause of shame. While 'embarrassée' and 'gênée' express milder discomfort, 'honteuse' conveys deep moral shame. French distinguishes carefully between 'honte' (shame — moral), 'gêne' (embarrassment — social), and 'embarras' (awkwardness — situational). 'Comportement' (behavior) is masculine in French.

items.question: 'Il se sent ___ depuis le départ de ses amis.' — Loneliness.
items.translation: (He feels lonely since his friends left.)
items.explanation: 'Se sentir seul' (to feel lonely/alone) is the most natural French expression for emotional loneliness. 'Se sentir' (to feel) is a reflexive verb used for describing emotional or physical states. 'Seul' remains in its masculine form to agree with 'il.' 'Depuis' (since) indicates an ongoing state that started in the past and continues — a key French temporal marker. 'Le départ de ses amis' (his friends' departure) uses the noun 'départ' rather than a verbal construction.

items.question: 'Je suis ___ de toute cette situation.' — Expressing exhaustion.
items.translation: (I am fed up with this whole situation.)
items.explanation: 'Las/lasse' (weary/fed up) expresses a deep, existential weariness rather than simple physical tiredness. This literary adjective conveys emotional exhaustion and frustration. While 'fatigué' means tired and 'épuisé' means exhausted, 'las' adds a layer of moral or psychological weariness. 'De toute cette situation' uses the demonstrative adjective 'cette' before 'situation' (feminine). 'Las' is a sophisticated vocabulary choice that elevates one's French register.

items.question: 'Ça me ___ qu'elle ne vienne pas.' — Expressing disappointment.
items.translation: (It disappoints me that she isn't coming.)
items.explanation: 'Décevoir' (to disappoint) conjugated as 'ça me déçoit' (it disappoints me) is the standard way to express disappointment in French. The construction 'ça me déçoit que + subjunctive' requires the subjunctive because it expresses an emotional reaction. 'Ne vienne pas' is the negative subjunctive of 'venir.' Note: 'décevoir' is a false friend — it means 'to disappoint,' not 'to deceive' (which is 'tromper' in French).

items.question: 'Elle ___ de bonheur en voyant son cadeau.' — Overwhelming joy.
items.translation: (She is beaming with happiness seeing her gift.)
items.explanation: 'Rayonner de bonheur' (to beam/radiate with happiness) is an elegant French expression that uses the metaphor of radiating light to convey intense joy. While 'briller' (to shine) and 'éclater' (to burst) can be used with emotions, 'rayonner' specifically suggests a visible, warm glow of happiness. 'En voyant' is the gérondif expressing simultaneity (while/upon seeing). This expression belongs to the poetic register of French emotion vocabulary.

items.question: 'J'ai le ___ gros en pensant à son départ.' — Heartache.
items.translation: (My heart is heavy thinking about his/her departure.)
items.explanation: 'Avoir le cœur gros' (to have a heavy heart — literally 'to have the heart big/swollen') is a classic French idiom expressing profound sadness or heartache. This expression dates back centuries in French literature. 'En pensant à' (thinking about) uses the gérondif with 'à' for the object of thought. Unlike English 'heavy heart,' French uses 'gros' (big/swollen) to convey the metaphor of a heart swollen with unshed tears.

items.question: 'Il est ___ par les résultats de son équipe.' — Pride.
items.translation: (He is proud of his team's results.)
items.explanation: 'Fier/fière' (proud) is the positive French adjective for pride in someone's achievements. Important distinction: 'fier' is positive (proud of achievements), while 'orgueilleux' (arrogant/proud) carries a negative connotation of excessive pride. 'Par' (by) here introduces the source of the pride. 'Son équipe' uses the possessive adjective that agrees with the possessed noun ('équipe' is feminine, but 'son' is used because 'équipe' starts with a vowel sound).

items.question: 'Elle éprouve de la ___ envers ses collègues.' — Gratitude.
items.translation: (She feels gratitude toward her colleagues.)
items.explanation: 'La reconnaissance' (gratitude/recognition) is the primary French word for gratitude. While 'gratitude' also exists in French, 'reconnaissance' is more commonly used in everyday speech. 'Éprouver de la reconnaissance' (to feel gratitude) uses the partitive article 'de la.' 'Envers' (toward) is the preposition used for the direction of feelings toward people — stronger and more formal than 'pour' in this context.

items.question: 'Je me sens ___ à l'aise dans cette nouvelle ville.' — Feeling comfortable.
items.translation: (I feel at ease in this new city.)
items.explanation: 'Tout à fait à l'aise' or simply 'tout à l'aise' means completely comfortable. The adverb 'tout' (completely/entirely) before 'à l'aise' intensifies the feeling of comfort. 'À l'aise' (at ease/comfortable) is an essential French expression. Note: 'tout' as an adverb is normally invariable but exceptionally takes feminine agreement before a feminine adjective starting with a consonant: 'toute contente.' Before 'à l'aise' (vowel sound), it stays 'tout.'

items.question: 'Il ne faut pas ___ ses émotions.' — Emotional expression.
items.translation: (One must not repress one's emotions.)
items.explanation: 'Refouler ses émotions' (to repress one's emotions) is the precise French psychological term for emotional suppression. While 'cacher' means to hide and 'supprimer' means to suppress/delete, 'refouler' specifically conveys the psychological concept of pushing emotions deep down. This word comes from Freudian psychoanalysis, which has been deeply influential in French intellectual culture. 'Il ne faut pas' + infinitive is the standard way to express what one should not do.

name: Commerce et Argent (Shopping & Money)
description: Master French shopping interactions, financial vocabulary, and transactional grammar structures for real-world commercial situations

items.question: 'Combien ___ ce pull ?' — Asking about prices.
items.translation: (How much does this sweater cost?)
items.explanation: 'Combien coûte...?' (How much does... cost?) is the standard French way to ask about prices. While 'Combien fait...?' is acceptable in casual speech, 'coûte' (from 'coûter') is the proper verb. Note the circumflex accent on 'coûte' (though modern spelling reform allows 'coute'). In French, you can also say 'Quel est le prix de...?' (What is the price of...?) for a more formal register. 'Ce pull' uses the demonstrative adjective 'ce' before a masculine noun.

items.question: 'Je voudrais ___ ce pantalon, s'il vous plaît.' — Trying on clothes.
items.translation: (I would like to try on these pants, please.)
items.explanation: 'Essayer' (to try on) is the standard French verb for trying on clothing. 'Tenter' means to attempt, 'tester' means to test (a product), and 'vérifier' means to verify — none are appropriate for trying on clothes. 'Je voudrais' (I would like) is the polite conditional form used in shops. 'S'il vous plaît' (please — literally 'if it pleases you') is essential for polite transactions in French. Note that 'pantalon' is singular in French, unlike the English plural 'pants.'

items.question: 'Vous payez par carte ou en ___ ?' — Payment methods.
items.translation: (Are you paying by card or in cash?)
items.explanation: 'En espèces' (in cash) is the formal and standard French expression for cash payment. While 'en liquide' is also used informally, 'espèces' is preferred in professional settings. Note the difference: 'espèces' = cash (always plural), 'monnaie' = change/currency, 'argent' = money (general). 'Par carte' (by card) uses 'par' for the method of payment. The French preposition system for payment is precise: 'par' + method, 'en' + form.

items.question: 'Il y a des ___ intéressantes en ce moment.' — Sales and discounts.
items.translation: (There are interesting sales right now.)
items.explanation: 'Les soldes' (sales) refers specifically to the regulated seasonal sales in France (January and July). This is always plural and can be masculine or feminine (traditionally feminine). 'Réductions' are discounts available anytime, while 'promotions' are special offers. In France, 'les soldes' are legally regulated — stores can only have sales during specific government-designated periods, a uniquely French commercial tradition. 'En ce moment' means 'right now/at this time.'

items.question: 'Est-ce que vous faites un ___ pour les étudiants ?' — Student discount.
items.translation: (Do you give a discount for students?)
items.explanation: 'Un tarif réduit' (a reduced rate) is the standard French expression for student or senior discounts. While 'rabais' means a price reduction and 'escompte' is a financial term for early payment discount, 'tarif réduit' is what you see on signs and ask for in shops, museums, and transport. 'Faire un tarif réduit' (to give a reduced rate) uses 'faire' as a versatile verb meaning 'to do/make/give' in commercial contexts.

items.question: 'Je cherche ce modèle en taille ___.' — Clothing sizes.
items.translation: (I'm looking for this model in size medium.)
items.explanation: 'Taille moyenne' (medium size) uses the feminine form 'moyenne' because 'taille' (size) is feminine in French. French clothing sizes are labeled S/M/L like English, but when speaking, you say 'taille petite/moyenne/grande.' 'Je cherche' (I'm looking for) is more natural than 'je regarde pour' in shopping contexts. Note: French drops the article before 'taille' in this expression, making it a bare noun modifier construction.

items.question: 'Pourrais-je avoir un ___, s'il vous plaît ?' — Getting a receipt.
items.translation: (Could I have a receipt, please?)
items.explanation: 'Un reçu' (a receipt) is the general French term for a proof of purchase. While 'ticket de caisse' (register receipt) is the slip from a cash register, 'reçu' is the broader, more formal term. 'Facture' is an invoice (for businesses), and 'note' is a bill (at a restaurant). 'Pourrais-je' (could I) is the polite conditional inversion, more elegant than 'est-ce que je pourrais,' and signals refined French. This form is standard in shops.

items.question: 'Cet article est en ___ de stock.' — Out of stock.
items.translation: (This item is out of stock.)
items.explanation: 'En rupture de stock' (out of stock) is the standard French commercial expression. 'Rupture' (break/rupture) here metaphorically means a break in the supply chain. This three-word expression is a fixed phrase that cannot be modified — you cannot say 'en manque de stock' or 'en fin de stock.' In French stores, you will often see signs saying 'Article en rupture de stock' or simply 'Rupture de stock.' 'Cet article' uses the demonstrative adjective 'cet' before a vowel.

items.question: 'Je souhaite ___ cet article, il est défectueux.' — Returns.
items.translation: (I would like to return this item, it is defective.)
items.explanation: 'Retourner un article' (to return an item) is the standard French expression for returning merchandise to a store. While 'rendre' means to give back and 'rapporter' means to bring back, 'retourner' is the specific commercial term. 'Défectueux' (defective) agrees with the masculine noun 'article.' 'Je souhaite' (I wish/would like) is a polite alternative to 'je veux' (I want) in customer service interactions, conveying politeness without being overly formal.

items.question: 'La carte de ___ est exigée pour tout remboursement.' — Loyalty cards.
items.translation: (The loyalty card is required for any refund.)
items.explanation: 'La carte de fidélité' (loyalty card) is the standard French term. 'Fidélité' (fidelity/loyalty) is preferred over 'loyauté' (which has more political/moral connotations). Nearly every French store offers a 'carte de fidélité' that accumulates points or provides discounts. 'Exigée' (required) agrees with 'carte' (feminine) and is stronger than 'demandée' (requested). 'Remboursement' (refund) is a key consumer vocabulary word in French.

items.question: 'Il a fait une bonne ___ au marché aux puces.' — Finding bargains.
items.translation: (He found a good bargain at the flea market.)
items.explanation: 'Faire une bonne affaire' (to make a good deal/find a bargain) is the idiomatic French expression for getting a good price. While 'trouvaille' means a lucky find and 'occasion' means a second-hand deal, 'faire une affaire' specifically emphasizes the financial advantage. 'Le marché aux puces' (flea market — literally 'market of fleas') is a beloved French cultural institution found in every major city и especially famous in Paris (Les Puces de Saint-Ouen).

items.question: 'Vous bénéficiez d'une ___ de 30% sur tout le magasin.' — Store promotions.
items.translation: (You benefit from a 30% discount throughout the store.)
items.explanation: 'Une remise' (a discount/reduction) is the standard French commercial term for a price reduction. While 'réduction' is also used, 'remise' is more formal and appears in official promotions. 'Bénéficier de' (to benefit from) is the verb used for receiving advantages or discounts. Note the construction: 'de 30%' uses 'de' before the percentage. 'Sur tout le magasin' (throughout the store) uses 'sur' to indicate scope, a common commercial French construction.

items.question: 'Je mets de l'argent de ___ chaque mois.' — Saving money.
items.translation: (I put money aside every month.)
items.explanation: 'Mettre de l'argent de côté' (to put money aside — literally 'to put money to the side') is the common French expression for saving money. While 'épargner' means to save (formal/financial), this idiomatic expression is used in everyday French. 'De l'argent' uses the partitive article for an unspecified amount of money. 'Chaque mois' (every month) uses the singular without an article, as 'chaque' is a determiner that replaces the article.

items.question: 'Le ___ de ce produit a augmenté de 10%.' — Price changes.
items.translation: (The price of this product has increased by 10%.)
items.explanation: 'Le prix' (the price) is the most common and versatile French word for the cost of goods. While 'coût' (cost — more economic/abstract), 'tarif' (rate — for services), and 'montant' (amount — for totals) exist, 'prix' is the everyday word for retail prices. 'Augmenter de' (to increase by) uses 'de' before the amount of change. Note that 'prix' is invariable — it has the same form in singular and plural: 'un prix, des prix.' The final 'x' is never pronounced.

items.question: 'Est-ce que ce magasin ___ le paiement en plusieurs fois ?' — Payment plans.
items.translation: (Does this store accept payment in installments?)
items.explanation: 'Accepter le paiement' (to accept payment) is the standard French expression. 'Le paiement en plusieurs fois' (payment in installments — literally 'payment in several times') is the French way to describe installment plans, a common option in French retail. While 'proposer' means to offer and 'permettre' means to allow, 'accepter' is the most natural verb for this context. 'En plusieurs fois' is a fixed expression that cannot be restructured.

name: Logement et Maison (Housing & Home)
description: Describe your living space, navigate French housing situations, and discuss domestic life with proper vocabulary and grammar

items.question: 'Nous avons ___ un appartement dans le centre-ville.' — Finding housing.
items.translation: (We rented an apartment in the city center.)
items.explanation: 'Louer' (to rent) is the standard French verb for renting a property. Importantly, 'louer' is used for both renting (as a tenant) and renting out (as a landlord) — context determines the meaning. 'Un appartement' (an apartment) is the standard French word (not 'apartment' without the double 'p'). 'Dans le centre-ville' (in the city center) uses the hyphenated 'centre-ville,' which is the standard French term for downtown.

items.question: 'Le ___ est compris dans les charges.' — Utility costs.
items.translation: (Heating is included in the utility charges.)
items.explanation: 'Le chauffage' (heating) is the standard French word for home heating systems. 'Réchauffement' refers to warming (as in 'réchauffement climatique' — global warming), not home heating. 'Les charges' (utility charges) is a crucial French housing term that covers common area maintenance, heating, water, and building insurance in apartment buildings. 'Compris dans' (included in) is the standard expression for what is covered by the rent or charges.

items.question: 'Il faut ___ un état des lieux avant d'emménager.' — Moving in procedures.
items.translation: (One must do an inventory check before moving in.)
items.explanation: 'Faire un état des lieux' (to do an inventory check — literally 'to make a state of the premises') is an essential French rental procedure. This inspection documents the condition of the property at move-in and move-out. 'Avant de + infinitive' (before + verb-ing) is the standard French construction. 'Emménager' means to move in (to a new home), while 'déménager' means to move out. This distinction (em-/dé-) follows a common French prefix pattern.

items.question: 'La ___ de bain est au bout du couloir.' — Room locations.
items.translation: (The bathroom is at the end of the hallway.)
items.explanation: 'La salle de bain' (bathroom — literally 'room of bath') is the standard French term. Important distinction: 'salle' is used for functional rooms (salle de bain, salle à manger, salle de séjour), while 'chambre' is specifically for bedrooms. 'Pièce' is a generic word for any room. 'Au bout du couloir' (at the end of the hallway) uses the contraction 'au' (à + le) with 'bout' (end). This phrase is indispensable for describing home layouts.

items.question: 'Nous avons fait ___ la cuisine l'année dernière.' — Home renovations.
items.translation: (We had the kitchen renovated last year.)
items.explanation: 'Faire refaire' (to have redone/renovated) uses the causative construction 'faire + infinitive' meaning 'to have something done.' 'Refaire la cuisine' means to redo/renovate the kitchen. This causative 'faire' is a fundamental French structure — the subject causes the action but doesn't perform it themselves. 'L'année dernière' (last year) uses the definite article 'l'' before 'année,' which is standard in French time expressions with 'dernier/dernière.'

items.question: 'Le ___ est au troisième étage sans ascenseur.' — Building features.
items.translation: (The apartment is on the third floor without an elevator.)
items.explanation: 'Le logement' (the dwelling/apartment) is the most versatile French word for one's living space. While 'domicile' is an official/legal term for one's residence and 'foyer' means home/household, 'logement' is the everyday term for any type of housing unit. Important cultural note: In France, 'le troisième étage' is actually the fourth floor by American counting, because the French ground floor is 'le rez-de-chaussée' (floor 0), not 'le premier étage.'

items.question: 'Le propriétaire a augmenté le ___ de 5%.' — Rent increases.
items.translation: (The landlord increased the rent by 5%.)
items.explanation: 'Le loyer' (the rent) is the specific French word for monthly rental payment. While 'prix' means price generally, 'loyer' is exclusively for rent. 'Le propriétaire' (the owner/landlord) is used for both male and female property owners (though 'la propriétaire' specifies female). 'Augmenter de' + percentage uses 'de' to introduce the amount of increase. In France, rent increases are legally regulated and typically limited to the IRL index (Indice de Référence des Loyers).

items.question: 'J'ai ___ les meubles dans le salon.' — Arranging furniture.
items.translation: (I arranged the furniture in the living room.)
items.explanation: 'Disposer' (to arrange/lay out) is the most precise French verb for arranging furniture in a purposeful layout. While 'placer' (to place) and 'mettre' (to put) work, 'disposer' implies an intentional, aesthetic arrangement. 'Les meubles' (furniture) is always plural in French, unlike English where 'furniture' is uncountable. 'Le salon' (the living room) is the standard term — 'salle de séjour' is an older alternative that is still used in formal contexts and real estate listings.

items.question: 'La ___ d'eau chaude ne fonctionne pas.' — Plumbing issues.
items.translation: (The hot water supply is not working.)
items.explanation: 'L'arrivée d'eau' (the water supply — literally 'the water arrival') is the French plumbing term for the water supply connection. While 'conduite' means pipe and 'source' means source, 'arrivée d'eau chaude' is the specific term for the hot water supply point. 'Ne... pas' surrounds the verb 'fonctionne' in standard French negation. 'Fonctionner' (to work/function) is used for machines and appliances — never use 'travailler' (which means to work as in labor).

items.question: 'Le bail est d'une durée de ___ ans renouvelable.' — Lease terms.
items.translation: (The lease is for a period of three years, renewable.)
items.explanation: 'Le bail' (the lease) in France is typically 'de trois ans' (for three years) for unfurnished apartments, which is the standard legal duration under French tenancy law (loi du 6 juillet 1989). Furnished apartments have a one-year lease by default. 'Renouvelable' (renewable) means the lease automatically renews. This is a key difference from many English-speaking countries where leases are often one year. 'D'une durée de' (for a duration of) is the formal expression for lease length.

items.question: 'Il a versé une ___ de deux mois de loyer.' — Security deposits.
items.translation: (He paid a deposit of two months' rent.)
items.explanation: 'La caution' (security deposit) is the standard French term for the deposit paid when signing a rental agreement. In France, the 'caution' is legally limited to one month's rent for unfurnished apartments and two months for furnished ones. 'Verser une caution' (to pay a deposit — literally 'to pour') is the standard expression. Note: 'caution' in French means deposit, not 'caution' as in warning (which is 'prudence' or 'avertissement').

items.question: 'Nous avons ___ dans une maison de campagne.' — Moving to the countryside.
items.translation: (We moved into a country house.)
items.explanation: 'Emménager' (to move into) specifically means moving into a new home, as opposed to 'déménager' (to move out/relocate). The prefix 'em-' (into) versus 'dé-' (out of/away from) creates this meaningful contrast. 'Une maison de campagne' (a country house) is a quintessentially French concept — many French families have or aspire to have a rural retreat. 'Dans' (in/into) is the preposition used with 'emménager' for the new location.

items.question: 'Le ___ syndic gère les parties communes de l'immeuble.' — Building management.
items.translation: (The building manager manages the common areas of the building.)
items.explanation: 'Le conseil syndical' (the building council/management committee) oversees the maintenance and administration of shared spaces in French apartment buildings. This is part of the 'copropriété' (co-ownership) system unique to French real estate. 'Les parties communes' (common areas) include hallways, elevators, gardens, and lobbies. 'Gérer' (to manage) is the standard verb. 'L'immeuble' (the building) specifically refers to a multi-story residential building in French.

items.question: 'La cuisine est ___ avec four et lave-vaisselle.' — Kitchen appliances.
items.translation: (The kitchen is equipped with oven and dishwasher.)
items.explanation: 'Une cuisine équipée' (an equipped/fitted kitchen) is a key French real estate term for a kitchen that comes with built-in appliances. In French housing listings, this is a major selling point. 'Équipée' agrees with 'cuisine' (feminine). 'Le four' (oven) and 'le lave-vaisselle' (dishwasher — literally 'wash-dishes') are essential kitchen vocabulary. French compound appliance names often follow the pattern 'verb-noun': lave-vaisselle, lave-linge (washing machine), sèche-linge (dryer).

items.question: 'Les ___ donnent sur un joli jardin.' — Views and windows.
items.translation: (The windows overlook a lovely garden.)
items.explanation: 'Les fenêtres' (the windows) is the standard word for windows as architectural features. 'Vitres' are the glass panes, 'carreaux' are windowpanes (smaller), and 'ouvertures' are openings (too technical). The expression 'donner sur' (to overlook/face) is an essential French housing expression — 'la chambre donne sur la rue' (the bedroom faces the street). 'Joli' (lovely/pretty) is placed before the noun, as are most short evaluative adjectives in French (joli, beau, petit, grand).

name: Culture et Société (Culture & Society)
description: Discuss French culture, social norms, traditions, and societal topics with appropriate vocabulary and nuanced expression

items.question: 'La France est connue pour son ___ culturel.' — Describing cultural heritage.
items.translation: (France is known for its cultural heritage.)
items.explanation: 'Patrimoine' is the standard French word for cultural heritage — it encompasses monuments, traditions, and intangible culture. While 'héritage' exists, it usually means personal inheritance (money, property). 'Patrimoine culturel' is the official UNESCO term used in French. The word comes from Latin 'patrimonium' (father's estate), reflecting how culture passes through generations. 'Trésor' (treasure) is too literal, and 'bagage' (baggage) usually refers to personal knowledge or experience.

items.question: 'Cette exposition ___ un grand succès auprès du public.' — Commenting on an art show.
items.translation: (This exhibition is a great success with the public.)
items.explanation: 'Remporter un succès' is a fixed expression meaning to achieve a success. While 'gagner' (to win) works in sports contexts, 'remporter' is preferred for artistic and cultural achievements. 'Auprès du public' (with the public) uses the compound preposition 'auprès de,' which indicates proximity or favor. This is more elegant than 'avec le public.' Note that 'obtenir' (to obtain) is too transactional for cultural contexts, and 'recevoir' (to receive) implies passivity rather than achievement.

items.question: 'Les ___ sont très importantes dans la culture française.' — Discussing traditions.
items.translation: (Traditions are very important in French culture.)
items.explanation: 'Traditions' in French is the same word as in English but pronounced with a nasal 'on' sound (trah-dee-SYOHN). While 'coutumes' (customs) is close in meaning, 'traditions' is broader and more commonly used in everyday speech. 'Habitudes' means habits (daily routines), not cultural practices. 'Rituels' (rituals) implies more formal, often religious ceremonies. The plural article 'les' is required before abstract nouns used in a general sense — a key difference from English, which omits the article.

items.question: 'Le musée organise une ___ d'art contemporain.' — Announcing a cultural event.
items.translation: (The museum is organizing a contemporary art exhibition.)
items.explanation: 'Exposition' is the correct French word for an art exhibition. Beware of the false friend 'exhibition,' which in French has connotations of showing off or even indecent exposure! This is a classic trap for English speakers. 'Présentation' is too vague, suggesting a business presentation, while 'démonstration' implies a practical demonstration. 'D'art contemporain' uses the partitive 'de' before the noun, as is standard when describing the type or nature of something.

items.question: 'La laïcité est un principe ___ de la République française.' — Discussing French values.
items.translation: (Secularism is a fundamental principle of the French Republic.)
items.explanation: 'Fondamental' means fundamental or foundational — it's the standard adjective for core principles. Note the agreement: 'principe' is masculine, so 'fondamental' stays in its base form (no -e). 'Basique' is a false register — it means basic/simple and sounds too casual for constitutional principles. 'La laïcité' (secularism) is a uniquely French concept of strict separation of church and state, central to French identity since the 1905 law. Understanding this term is essential for grasping French political discourse.

items.question: 'Ce film a été ___ au Festival de Cannes.' — Talking about cinema.
items.translation: (This film was screened at the Cannes Film Festival.)
items.explanation: 'Présenté' (presented/screened) is the standard verb for showing a film at a festival. While 'montré' (shown) is technically correct, it's too informal for festival contexts. 'Joué' (played) is used for theater performances, not film screenings. 'Affiché' (posted/displayed) refers to posters or public notices. The passive construction 'a été présenté' mirrors English passive voice. 'Au Festival de Cannes' — note that 'Festival' is capitalized as part of the proper name, and no article is needed before 'Cannes.'

items.question: 'La gastronomie française est inscrite au ___ mondial de l'UNESCO.' — Cultural recognition.
items.translation: (French gastronomy is listed on the UNESCO World Heritage list.)
items.explanation: 'Patrimoine mondial de l'UNESCO' is the official French term for UNESCO World Heritage. This fixed expression cannot be replaced with alternatives like 'registre' (register) or 'catalogue.' French gastronomy was indeed inscribed on this list in 2010 — a point of immense national pride. 'Inscrite' agrees with 'gastronomie' (feminine), showing past participle agreement with a preceding subject in passive constructions. This cultural reference is excellent conversation material with French speakers.

items.question: 'Les Français sont très ___ à leur mode de vie.' — Describing French attitudes.
items.translation: (The French are very attached to their way of life.)
items.explanation: 'Attaché à' means emotionally attached to — it's the standard expression for cultural or personal attachment. 'Liés' (linked) is more neutral and lacks emotional depth. 'Connectés' is a modern anglicism for digital connection. 'Reliés' (connected/bound) is used for physical connections. Note 'attachés' has the masculine plural -s because 'Français' is masculine plural. 'Leur mode de vie' (their way of life) — 'mode de vie' is a fixed expression. The French deeply value quality of life, and this phrase appears frequently in social and political discussions.

items.question: 'Il faut ___ les diversités culturelles.' — Advocating for tolerance.
items.translation: (We must respect cultural diversities.)
items.explanation: 'Respecter' (to respect) is the strongest and most positive verb for cultural discourse. 'Accepter' (to accept) implies reluctance — merely tolerating rather than valuing. 'Tolérer' (to tolerate) is even weaker, suggesting endurance rather than appreciation. 'Admettre' (to admit) implies conceding a point. 'Il faut' + infinitive is the impersonal construction meaning 'one must' or 'it is necessary to' — one of the most common French expressions for obligations. This structure avoids specifying who must act, making it ideal for general statements.

items.question: 'Cette ___ est classée monument historique.' — Discussing architecture.
items.translation: (This church is classified as a historic monument.)
items.explanation: 'Église' (church) is the general term for a Christian place of worship. 'Cathédrale' (cathedral) is specifically a bishop's church, 'chapelle' (chapel) is smaller, and 'basilique' (basilica) has special papal designation. 'Classée monument historique' is a specific French legal designation protecting historic buildings — similar to landmark status. Note 'classée' agrees in gender with 'église' (feminine). France has over 45,000 classified historical monuments, making this vocabulary essential for cultural discussions and travel.

items.question: 'La ___ de ce quartier a beaucoup changé ces dernières années.' — Urban evolution.
items.translation: (The atmosphere of this neighborhood has changed a lot in recent years.)
items.explanation: 'Ambiance' is the preferred French word for the feel or atmosphere of a place — it's actually a French word borrowed by English! While 'atmosphère' also works, 'ambiance' is more natural for neighborhoods and social settings. 'Humeur' means mood (personal emotion), not the feel of a place. 'Ces dernières années' (these last years/recently) is a common temporal expression — note the demonstrative 'ces' and the feminine plural agreement on 'dernières.' French neighborhoods ('quartiers') often have distinct personalities and histories.

items.question: 'Le ___ français comprend la Sécurité sociale et l'assurance maladie.' — Social systems.
items.translation: (The French model includes Social Security and health insurance.)
items.explanation: 'Modèle' (model) is the standard term when discussing the French social framework as a whole. 'Système' (system) works but is less encompassing. 'Régime' in this context means a specific program (like 'régime de retraite' — pension scheme), not the overall model. 'Plan' is too narrow — it suggests a specific initiative. 'La Sécurité sociale' (capitalized) is France's comprehensive social protection system, and 'l'assurance maladie' is specifically health insurance. Understanding these terms is crucial for life in France.

items.question: 'Cette œuvre d'art ___ une réflexion sur la société moderne.' — Art criticism.
items.translation: (This work of art inspires a reflection on modern society.)
items.explanation: 'Susciter' (to inspire/provoke) is the elegant, literary verb used in art criticism and intellectual discourse. It implies causing something to emerge naturally. 'Créer' (to create) and 'générer' (to generate) are too mechanical. 'Provoquer' (to provoke) has negative connotations of deliberately causing trouble. 'Œuvre d'art' is the standard expression for a work of art — note the special character 'œ' (o-e ligature), which is essential in French typography. 'Réflexion sur' (reflection on) uses 'sur' rather than 'de.'

items.question: 'Les ___ locales sont un élément important de l'identité régionale.' — Regional identity.
items.translation: (Local festivities are an important element of regional identity.)
items.explanation: 'Fêtes' (festivities/celebrations) is the authentic French word for local festivals and celebrations. 'Parties' is a dangerous false friend — in French, 'une partie' means a part/portion or a game, never a social gathering. 'Célébrations' exists but is more formal and typically reserved for official ceremonies. 'Événements' (events) is too generic. French regions take great pride in their local 'fêtes' — from village fairs to wine festivals. 'L'identité régionale' (regional identity) is a politically significant concept in France.

items.question: 'La littérature française a ___ de nombreux prix Nobel.' — Literary achievements.
items.translation: (French literature has produced many Nobel Prizes.)
items.explanation: 'Produire' (to produce) in the passé composé becomes 'a produit' — an irregular past participle. This verb elegantly expresses the idea of yielding results over time. 'Créé' (created) implies deliberate invention rather than natural emergence. 'Généré' (generated) is too technical. 'Fait' (made/done) is too vague for intellectual achievements. France has indeed won 16 Nobel Prizes in Literature — more than any other country. The expression 'de nombreux' (many/numerous) is more formal than 'beaucoup de' and suits academic discourse.

name: Sports et Loisirs (Sports & Leisure)
description: Talk about sports activities, hobbies, and leisure pursuits using correct French vocabulary, verb constructions, and idiomatic expressions

items.question: 'Je ___ du football tous les week-ends.' — Talking about regular sports.
items.translation: (I play football every weekend.)
items.explanation: 'Jouer au football' (to play football) uses 'jouer à' for ball sports and competitive games. This is a crucial distinction: 'jouer à' for sports with rules, 'faire de' for individual activities. With 'je,' the verb becomes 'joue' (dropping the -er ending and adding -e). The contraction 'du' (de + le) appears because 'football' is masculine. 'Faire du football' would mean practicing football as an activity, not playing a match. 'Pratique' is too formal and 'exerce' implies professional training.

items.question: 'Elle fait ___ la natation depuis cinq ans.' — Discussing long-term hobbies.
items.translation: (She has been doing swimming for five years.)
items.explanation: 'Faire de la natation' uses the construction 'faire de' + article for sports and physical activities. Since 'natation' is feminine, we need 'de la.' The answer 'de' is correct because the full form is 'de la' — but the question already provides 'la' after the blank. 'Depuis cinq ans' (for five years) indicates an action that started in the past and continues — French uses the present tense here, unlike English which uses present perfect continuous. This is one of the trickiest differences between French and English tense usage.

items.question: 'L'équipe a ___ le match par trois buts à zéro.' — Reporting sports results.
items.translation: (The team won the match three goals to nil.)
items.explanation: 'Remporter un match' is the standard French expression for winning a sporting event — it's more formal and precise than 'gagner.' While 'gagner' works colloquially, sports journalism consistently uses 'remporter.' 'Vaincu' (vanquished) requires a direct object (a person/team, not a match). 'Battu' (beaten) also takes a person as object. 'Par trois buts à zéro' — note 'par' (by) for scores and 'à' (to) between the numbers. In French sports reporting, 'zéro' is used, not 'nil' as in British English.

items.question: 'Nous allons ___ une randonnée en montagne ce week-end.' — Planning outdoor activities.
items.translation: (We are going to do a hike in the mountains this weekend.)
items.explanation: 'Faire une randonnée' (to go for a hike) is the standard French expression. You cannot say 'aller une randonnée' — that would be ungrammatical. 'Prendre' doesn't collocate with physical activities. 'Marcher' (to walk) is the action itself, not the activity as a whole. 'Aller + faire' creates the near future construction: 'nous allons faire' (we are going to do). 'En montagne' (in the mountains) uses 'en' without an article — a special preposition pattern for certain geographical contexts. French people are passionate about 'la randonnée.'

items.question: 'Il s'est ___ en jouant au tennis.' — Describing a sports injury.
items.translation: (He injured himself while playing tennis.)
items.explanation: 'Se blesser' (to injure oneself) is the standard reflexive verb for sports injuries. In the passé composé, reflexive verbs use 'être' as auxiliary: 'il s'est blessé.' The past participle agrees with the subject when using 'être.' 'Se casser' would mean to break something specific (se casser le bras — break one's arm). 'Se faire mal' (to hurt oneself) is more colloquial. 'S'abîmer' means to damage (used for objects, not people). 'En jouant' uses the gérondif (en + present participle) to express simultaneous actions — while playing.

items.question: 'Pendant mes ___, je lis et je fais du jardinage.' — Discussing leisure time.
items.translation: (During my free time, I read and I do gardening.)
items.explanation: 'Loisirs' (leisure activities) is the standard French noun for free time pursuits. Unlike 'temps libre' (free time), 'loisirs' specifically implies activities, not just available time. 'Vacances' means holidays/vacation — a specific period, not general leisure. 'Pauses' are short breaks during work. Note the plural form 'loisirs' is standard — the singular 'loisir' is rarely used in modern French. 'Faire du jardinage' (to do gardening) follows the 'faire de' + activity pattern. French conversation about 'loisirs' is excellent small talk material.

items.question: 'Le ___ est le sport le plus populaire en France.' — Discussing popular sports.
items.translation: (Football is the most popular sport in France.)
items.explanation: 'Football' is the formal French word for soccer (what Americans call soccer). In casual speech, the French shorten it to 'foot' — but in written or formal contexts, 'football' is standard. Never use 'soccer' in French — it's an Americanism that French speakers don't recognize. 'Ballon' means ball, not the sport. 'Le plus populaire' is the superlative construction (le/la/les + plus + adjective). Note that 'sport' is masculine in French. Football dominates French culture — the national team 'Les Bleus' unites the nation.

items.question: 'Elle ___ à la gymnastique trois fois par semaine.' — Regular schedule.
items.translation: (She goes to gymnastics three times a week.)
items.explanation: 'Aller à' (to go to) is the most natural construction for attending regular classes or sessions. 'Elle va à la gymnastique' means she physically goes to the gymnastics class. 'Faire de la gymnastique' would be valid but means doing gymnastics (the activity), not attending. 'Assister à' means to attend/observe (not assist — false friend!). 'Participer à' means to participate in, which implies more active involvement. 'Trois fois par semaine' — 'par' expresses frequency (per week), a very common collocation.

items.question: 'Il a ___ un nouveau record dans cette compétition.' — Athletic achievements.
items.translation: (He set a new record in this competition.)
items.explanation: 'Établir un record' (to set/establish a record) is the standard French expression in sports contexts. While 'battre un record' (to break a record) means surpassing an existing one, 'établir' means creating a new benchmark. 'Faire' is too vague for records. 'Mettre' and 'poser' don't collocate with 'record.' 'Dans cette compétition' uses 'dans' (in) for events and competitions. The past participle 'établi' is regular (from 'établir,' a second-group -ir verb). French sports commentary frequently uses this sophisticated vocabulary.

items.question: 'Nous avons ___ nos billets pour le match de rugby.' — Event planning.
items.translation: (We booked our tickets for the rugby match.)
items.explanation: 'Réserver' (to reserve/book) is the standard verb for securing tickets in advance. While 'acheter' (to buy) implies immediate purchase, 'réserver' implies advance booking — an important distinction. 'Prendre des billets' (to get tickets) is more casual. 'Commander' (to order) is used for products or food, not event tickets. 'Pour le match de rugby' uses 'pour' (for) + article + noun. Rugby is extremely popular in southern France, especially in Toulouse and the Basque Country — knowing this cultural context enriches conversations.

items.question: 'Les enfants aiment ___ dans le parc.' — Children's activities.
items.translation: (The children like to play in the park.)
items.explanation: 'Jouer' (to play) is the most natural verb for children's play. Unlike 'jouer à' (play a specific game/sport), standalone 'jouer' means general play. 'S'amuser' (to have fun) is broader — it includes any enjoyable activity. 'Courir' (to run) and 'se promener' (to stroll) are specific activities. 'Aimer + infinitive' (to like doing) uses the infinitive directly — no preposition needed. 'Dans le parc' uses 'dans' (in) for enclosed spaces. Note that 'les enfants' requires the definite article when speaking about children in general.

items.question: 'Je m'___ à la peinture depuis l'année dernière.' — Discovering new hobbies.
items.translation: (I have been interested in painting since last year.)
items.explanation: 'S'intéresser à' (to be interested in) is the standard reflexive construction for expressing interest. With 'je,' it becomes 'je m'intéresse' — the reflexive pronoun 'me' contracts to 'm'' before a vowel. 'S'adonner à' (to devote oneself to) is much stronger and implies passion. 'S'appliquer à' (to apply oneself to) emphasizes effort. 'Depuis l'année dernière' (since last year) again triggers the present tense in French — an ongoing action from the past. 'La peinture' means both painting (art) and paint (material).

items.question: 'Le ___ de France est la course cycliste la plus célèbre du monde.' — Iconic events.
items.translation: (The Tour de France is the most famous cycling race in the world.)
items.explanation: 'Le Tour de France' is a proper name — 'Tour' (tour/lap) is always capitalized. 'Circuit' implies a closed loop track. 'Parcours' means route/course (the path itself). 'Trajet' means journey/trip (point A to B). The superlative 'la plus célèbre' agrees with 'course' (feminine) — note 'la plus' not 'le plus.' 'Du monde' (in the world) uses the contracted form of 'de le.' The Tour de France, created in 1903, is a cultural institution that captivates the entire nation for three weeks every July.

items.question: 'Elle ___ très bien aux échecs.' — Board game skills.
items.translation: (She plays chess very well.)
items.explanation: 'Jouer aux échecs' (to play chess) follows the 'jouer à' pattern for all games with rules. The contraction 'aux' = 'à + les' because 'échecs' (chess) is always plural in French (literally 'checks/failures' from Persian 'shah'). This is different from English, where chess is singular. 'Fait' doesn't work with games. 'Participe' and 'concourt' imply formal competition. The adverb 'très bien' is placed between the verb and the prepositional phrase — French adverb placement follows the verb in simple tenses.
