items.question: 'Ils se sont ___ pour un marathon.' — Athletic preparation.
items.translation: (They trained for a marathon.)
items.explanation: 'S'entraîner' (to train) is the specific verb for athletic training. In the passé composé, reflexive verbs use 'être': 'ils se sont entraînés.' The past participle agrees with the subject — masculine plural adds -s. 'Se préparer' (to prepare) is broader and less specifically athletic. 'S'exercer' (to practice) implies repetitive skill work. 'Se former' (to train professionally) is for career/education training. 'Pour un marathon' uses 'pour' (for) to express purpose. Note the circumflex accent on 'entraînés' (though modern French spelling reform allows 'entrainés').

name: Médias et Communication (Media & Communication)
description: Navigate French media, discuss news, express opinions, and understand journalism vocabulary with proper register and accuracy

items.question: 'Les ___ ont annoncé les résultats des élections.' — News reporting.
items.translation: (The media announced the election results.)
items.explanation: 'Les médias' (the media) is always plural in French — there is no singular 'média' in standard usage (though it's technically possible). Unlike English, which treats 'media' as either singular or plural, French consistently uses plural agreement. 'Nouvelles' (news) refers to the content, not the outlets. 'Journaux' (newspapers) is only print media. 'Informations' (news/information) is the content broadcast. 'Ont annoncé' — third person plural passé composé. 'Les résultats des élections' uses 'des' (de + les) for possession.

items.question: 'Ce journaliste ___ un article sur la crise économique.' — Writing about current events.
items.translation: (This journalist is writing an article about the economic crisis.)
items.explanation: 'Rédiger' (to write/draft) is the professional journalism verb for composing articles. While 'écrire' (to write) is perfectly correct, 'rédiger' implies structured, professional writing — it elevates the register. 'Composer' is for music or poetry. 'Publier' (to publish) comes after writing. 'Sur la crise économique' (about the economic crisis) uses 'sur' for the topic. Note that 'économique' has an accent aigu on the first 'é' — a common spelling error for English speakers. French journalism vocabulary is essential for following current events.

items.question: 'L'___ télévisé de 20 heures est très regardé en France.' — TV habits.
items.translation: (The 8 PM television news is very watched in France.)
items.explanation: 'Le journal télévisé' (literally 'the televised newspaper') is the standard French term for TV news — often abbreviated 'JT.' This is a uniquely French expression that English speakers find surprising. 'De 20 heures' (at 8 PM) uses the 24-hour clock, standard in French. The 20h news on TF1 and France 2 remains a national ritual, with millions tuning in simultaneously. 'Très regardé' (very watched) is a common construction — past participle used as adjective. 'Information' would require the plural 'les informations' for news.

items.question: 'Cette émission ___ en direct depuis Paris.' — Broadcasting.
items.translation: (This show is broadcast live from Paris.)
items.explanation: 'Être diffusé(e)' (to be broadcast) is the standard verb for TV/radio transmission. Note the feminine agreement 'diffusée' because 'émission' is feminine. 'Montré' (shown) is too basic. 'Jouée' (played) is for theater. 'Transmise' (transmitted) is more technical/telecommunications. 'En direct' (live) is a fixed expression — never 'en vivant' (which would be a bizarre translation of 'live'). 'Depuis Paris' (from Paris) uses 'depuis' for point of origin in broadcasting contexts. French broadcasting has a rich tradition of live cultural programming.

items.question: 'Les réseaux ___ ont transformé la communication moderne.' — Digital media.
items.translation: (Social networks have transformed modern communication.)
items.explanation: 'Réseaux sociaux' (social networks) — 'sociaux' is the masculine plural of 'social.' The irregular plural follows a French pattern: words ending in -al change to -aux in the plural (social → sociaux, national → nationaux). 'Sociales' would be feminine plural, incorrect with 'réseaux' (masculine). 'De société' means 'of society' — a different meaning entirely (as in 'jeux de société,' board games). This -al → -aux pattern is one of French's distinctive plural rules and appears across many important adjectives.

items.question: 'Le ___ a interviewé le Premier ministre ce matin.' — Journalism roles.
items.translation: (The reporter interviewed the Prime Minister this morning.)
items.explanation: 'Journaliste' is the most common French word for a news reporter. While 'reporter' exists in French (borrowed from English, pronounced ruh-por-TEHR), 'journaliste' is the standard term. 'Présentateur' (presenter/anchor) reads the news on TV but doesn't conduct field interviews. 'Correspondant' (correspondent) is stationed in a specific location. Note that 'journaliste' is both masculine and feminine (un/une journaliste) — one of French's epicene nouns. 'Le Premier ministre' is not capitalized on 'ministre' in standard French usage.

items.question: 'Cette information n'a pas encore été ___.' — Fact-checking.
items.translation: (This information has not yet been verified.)
items.explanation: 'Vérifier' (to verify) is the standard journalism term for fact-checking. The past participle 'vérifiée' agrees with 'information' (feminine singular). The double negative 'n'a pas' wraps around the auxiliary 'avoir,' with 'encore' (yet) placed between 'pas' and 'été.' This creates a complex but elegant construction: ne + auxiliary + pas + encore + été + past participle. 'Confirmée' (confirmed) implies official validation. 'Prouvée' (proven) is stronger — requiring evidence. In the era of 'fake news,' understanding verification vocabulary is increasingly important.

items.question: 'Il a ___ son opinion dans une tribune du journal Le Monde.' — Public discourse.
items.translation: (He expressed his opinion in an op-ed in Le Monde newspaper.)
items.explanation: 'Exprimer son opinion' (to express one's opinion) is the standard formal expression. 'Dire' (to say) is too casual for written media contexts. 'Montrer' (to show) is visual. 'Donner son opinion' exists but is less formal than 'exprimer.' 'Une tribune' in journalism is an opinion piece or op-ed — not a 'tribune' as in a raised platform. 'Le Monde' is France's most prestigious newspaper — note it's always 'Le Monde' with the article. French intellectual tradition highly values 'l'expression d'opinions' in quality newspapers.

items.question: 'Le ___ de cette chaîne est en baisse depuis plusieurs mois.' — TV industry.
items.translation: (The viewership of this channel has been declining for several months.)
items.explanation: 'Audimat' is the French term for TV ratings/viewership — originally a brand name (like 'Kleenex' in English) that became the generic term. 'Public' (audience) refers to the people, not the measurement. 'Spectacle' (show) and 'programme' (program) are content, not metrics. 'En baisse' (declining/falling) is a common expression also used in finance. 'Depuis plusieurs mois' (for several months) again uses present tense in French for ongoing situations — one of the key tense differences from English. French media closely monitors 'l'audimat' for programming decisions.

items.question: 'La liberté de la ___ est un droit fondamental.' — Press freedom.
items.translation: (Freedom of the press is a fundamental right.)
items.explanation: 'La liberté de la presse' (freedom of the press) is a fixed legal and political expression in French. 'Presse' means press/media (from 'presser' — to press, as in a printing press). Don't confuse with 'pression' (pressure) — a common error. 'Publication' is the act of publishing. 'Impression' means printing or impression. This concept is enshrined in the French Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789) and remains central to French democratic discourse. 'Un droit fondamental' (a fundamental right) — 'droit' is masculine.

items.question: 'Ce podcast ___ chaque semaine un nouveau sujet de société.' — New media formats.
items.translation: (This podcast covers a new social topic every week.)
items.explanation: 'Aborder un sujet' (to tackle/address a subject) is the standard verb for covering a topic in media. 'Parler de' (to talk about) requires the preposition 'de.' 'Discuter' (to discuss) implies dialogue, not coverage. 'Traiter' (to deal with) works but is more academic. 'Chaque semaine' (every week) uses the singular — 'chaque' always takes singular nouns. 'Un sujet de société' (a social/societal topic) is a common French expression for issues affecting society. Podcasts ('podcasts' or 'balados' in Canadian French) are booming in francophone media.

items.question: 'La ___ de ce site d'information est en forte croissance.' — Digital metrics.
items.translation: (The readership of this news site is growing strongly.)
items.explanation: 'Fréquentation' (attendance/traffic) is the standard metric term for website visits and media consumption levels. 'Lecture' (reading) refers to the act, not the number of readers. 'Visite' (visit) is too generic. 'Consultation' (consultation) implies seeking advice. 'En forte croissance' (in strong growth) is a common expression — 'forte' agrees with 'croissance' (feminine). French digital media has grown enormously, with sites like Mediapart pioneering investigative journalism online. Understanding these metrics terms is essential for professional media discussions.

items.question: 'Il est important de ___ ses sources avant de partager une information.' — Media literacy.
items.translation: (It is important to check one's sources before sharing information.)
items.explanation: 'Vérifier ses sources' (to verify one's sources) is the standard expression in journalism and media literacy education. 'Regarder' (to look at) is too passive. 'Chercher' (to search for) implies not having found them yet. 'Trouver' (to find) is about discovery, not validation. 'Avant de + infinitive' (before doing) is the standard construction — never 'avant + infinitive' without 'de.' 'Partager une information' (to share information) — note the singular 'une information' in French where English might use the uncountable 'information.'

items.question: 'Les ___ en continu ont changé notre rapport à l'actualité.' — News consumption.
items.translation: (24-hour news channels have changed our relationship with current events.)
items.explanation: 'Chaînes d'information en continu' (24-hour news channels) is the standard French expression. 'Canal' (channel) in French refers to a waterway or a specific TV network (Canal+), not a generic TV channel. 'Station' is for radio. 'Réseau' means network in the organizational sense. 'En continu' (continuously/24/7) is a fixed adverbial expression. 'Notre rapport à' (our relationship with) uses 'rapport' in the sense of connection — different from 'relation.' 'L'actualité' (current events/news) is always singular, unlike English 'current events.'

items.question: 'Ce documentaire a ___ un débat national sur l'éducation.' — Media impact.
items.translation: (This documentary sparked a national debate about education.)
items.explanation: 'Déclencher un débat' (to trigger/spark a debate) is the most impactful and journalistic expression. It implies a powerful catalyst effect. 'Commencer' (to start) is too neutral. 'Ouvrir un débat' (to open a debate) works but suggests a deliberate, gentle beginning. 'Lancer un débat' (to launch a debate) implies intentional instigation. 'Sur l'éducation' (about education) uses 'sur' for the topic. French intellectual culture values public debate highly — television shows like 'C dans l'air' and 'Quotidien' regularly feature vigorous discussions on national issues.

name: Politique et Droit (Politics & Law)
description: Navigate political discussions, understand legal terminology, and express civic concepts using correct French vocabulary and formal register

items.question: 'Le président de la République ___ la politique étrangère de la France.' — Executive power.
items.translation: (The President of the Republic directs France's foreign policy.)
items.explanation: 'Diriger' (to direct/lead) is the standard verb for presidential authority over policy. Article 5 of the French Constitution establishes this role. 'Conduire' (to conduct) is for vehicles or orchestras. 'Mener' (to lead) works for campaigns or battles. 'Gérer' (to manage) implies day-to-day administration, beneath presidential dignity. 'Le président de la République' — note the lowercase 'président' in French writing (only capitalized at the start of a sentence). 'La politique étrangère' (foreign policy) — 'étrangère' agrees with 'politique' (feminine).

items.question: 'L'Assemblée nationale a ___ cette loi à une large majorité.' — Legislative process.
items.translation: (The National Assembly passed this law by a large majority.)
items.explanation: 'Voter une loi' (to vote/pass a law) is the standard legislative expression. 'Accepter' (to accept) is too informal for parliamentary context. 'Approuver' (to approve) is used by the executive, not the legislature. 'Décider' (to decide) doesn't take a law as direct object. 'L'Assemblée nationale' (National Assembly) is the lower house of French parliament — always capitalized. 'À une large majorité' (by a large majority) uses 'à' to express the margin. Understanding French political institutions is essential — France's Fifth Republic system differs significantly from both UK and US models.

items.question: 'Tout citoyen a le ___ de vote à partir de dix-huit ans.' — Civic rights.
items.translation: (Every citizen has the right to vote from age eighteen.)
items.explanation: 'Le droit de vote' (the right to vote) is a fixed constitutional expression. 'Pouvoir' (power/ability) suggests physical capacity, not legal entitlement. 'Devoir' (duty) is the obligation side — voting is a right, not a duty in France (unlike some countries). 'Privilège' (privilege) implies something earned or exclusive, contradicting universal suffrage. 'Tout citoyen' (every citizen) uses the singular without article — a formal construction. 'À partir de' (from/starting at) indicates the age threshold. Women gained voting rights in France in 1944.

items.question: 'L'avocat ___ les intérêts de son client devant le tribunal.' — Legal proceedings.
items.translation: (The lawyer defends the interests of his client before the court.)
items.explanation: 'Défendre' (to defend) is the standard legal verb for a lawyer's role. 'Défend' is the third person singular present tense (irregular: défendre → je défends, il défend). 'Protéger' (to protect) implies physical safety. 'Représenter' (to represent) is broader but less legally precise. 'Soutenir' (to support) implies moral backing. 'Devant le tribunal' (before the court) uses 'devant' (before/in front of) — the standard preposition for court appearances. French legal vocabulary draws heavily from Latin, reflecting the civil law tradition that influences most European legal systems.

items.question: 'Le Premier ministre a ___ sa démission au président.' — Government changes.
items.translation: (The Prime Minister submitted his resignation to the president.)
items.explanation: 'Présenter sa démission' (to present/submit one's resignation) is the fixed formal expression in French politics. 'Donner' (to give) is too casual for constitutional protocol. 'Soumettre' (to submit) is used for documents. 'Offrir' (to offer) implies voluntariness that may not be the case. In France's semi-presidential system, the PM serves at the president's pleasure. 'Au président' contracts 'à + le.' Note the distinction: 'président' (lowercase for general use) versus 'Président' (only when replacing a name). French political protocol is highly codified.

items.question: 'La Constitution ___ les droits fondamentaux des citoyens.' — Constitutional law.
items.translation: (The Constitution guarantees the fundamental rights of citizens.)
items.explanation: 'Garantir' (to guarantee) is the precise legal verb for constitutional protections. 'Garantit' is the third person singular (garantir → il garantit). 'Assurer' (to ensure) is broader and less binding. 'Protéger' (to protect) is reactive rather than proactive. 'Donner' (to give) implies rights can be taken away. The French Constitution of 1958 established the Fifth Republic. 'Les droits fondamentaux' (fundamental rights) include the 1789 Declaration, the 1946 Preamble, and the 2004 Environmental Charter — a uniquely layered constitutional tradition.

items.question: 'Le Sénat a ___ un amendement au projet de loi.' — Legislation details.
items.translation: (The Senate proposed an amendment to the bill.)
items.explanation: 'Proposer un amendement' (to propose an amendment) is the standard parliamentary expression. 'Suggérer' (to suggest) is too tentative for formal legislative action. 'Demander' (to request) implies a petition, not a formal proposal. 'Formuler' (to formulate) describes drafting the text, not presenting it. 'Le Sénat' is the upper house of French parliament. 'Un projet de loi' (a bill/proposed law) is different from 'une proposition de loi' — the former originates from government, the latter from parliament. This distinction matters in French legislative vocabulary.

items.question: 'Les ___ municipales auront lieu en mars prochain.' — Elections.
items.translation: (The municipal elections will take place next March.)
items.explanation: 'Élections municipales' (municipal elections) is the standard expression for local government elections. 'Votes' refers to the act of voting, not the election event. 'Scrutins' (ballots/polls) is the individual voting round within an election. 'Sondages' (polls/surveys) are opinion surveys, not actual elections. 'Auront lieu' (will take place) is the future tense of 'avoir lieu' — a fixed expression for events. 'En mars prochain' (next March) — note 'prochain' after the month name. French electoral system features multiple rounds ('tours') for most elections.

items.question: 'L'accusé a été ___ non coupable par le jury.' — Criminal justice.
items.translation: (The defendant was found not guilty by the jury.)
items.explanation: 'Déclarer non coupable' (to declare not guilty) is the standard French legal verdict expression. 'Trouvé' (found) would be a literal translation of English 'found not guilty' — unnatural in French legal language. 'Jugé' (judged) refers to the process, not the verdict. 'Prononcé' (pronounced) is used for sentences, not verdicts. 'L'accusé' (the accused/defendant) — note the past participle used as a noun. 'Par le jury' — French criminal courts use either 'juges professionnels' (professional judges) or 'cours d'assises' with citizen juries for serious crimes.

items.question: 'La maire a ___ un nouveau plan pour les transports en commun.' — Local government.
items.translation: (The mayor announced a new plan for public transportation.)
items.explanation: 'Annoncer' (to announce) is the standard verb for policy announcements. 'Déclarer' (to declare) is more solemn, used for formal legal or political declarations. 'Proclamer' (to proclaim) is extreme — used for independence or major state events. 'Révéler' (to reveal) implies secrets. 'La maire' — French now commonly uses the feminine article with 'maire' for women mayors, reflecting evolving gender-inclusive language. 'Les transports en commun' (public transportation) is always plural — a fixed expression. This collocation is essential for discussing urban policy.

items.question: 'Le gouvernement a pris des ___ pour lutter contre le chômage.' — Policy actions.
items.translation: (The government took measures to fight unemployment.)
items.explanation: 'Prendre des mesures' (to take measures) is the standard political expression for policy actions. 'Actions' (actions) is too vague. 'Décisions' (decisions) are choices, not implementations. 'Pas' would translate 'steps' literally but sounds awkward in French political discourse. 'Pour lutter contre' (to fight against) uses the construction purpose ('pour') + infinitive + opposition ('contre'). 'Le chômage' (unemployment) is a central topic in French politics. France's unemployment rate has historically been a key electoral issue.

items.question: 'Le tribunal a ___ l'entreprise à payer une amende.' — Court decisions.
items.translation: (The court sentenced the company to pay a fine.)
items.explanation: 'Condamner à' (to sentence to/condemn to) is the standard legal verb for court-imposed penalties. 'Ordonner' (to order) is used for injunctions, not penalties. 'Obliger' (to oblige) implies moral obligation. 'Forcer' (to force) implies physical coercion. 'À payer une amende' — the preposition 'à' connects the sentence to the penalty, followed by the infinitive. 'Une amende' (a fine) — don't confuse with 'amende honorable' (public apology). French courts can impose both criminal and civil penalties, following the civil law tradition that separates private and public law.

items.question: 'Les citoyens ont le droit de ___ pacifiquement.' — Civil liberties.
items.translation: (Citizens have the right to demonstrate peacefully.)
items.explanation: 'Manifester' (to demonstrate/protest) is the standard French verb for public demonstrations. 'Protester' exists but is less common for street protests. 'Démontrer' means to demonstrate (prove/show) — a false friend! It's for mathematical proofs, not political protests. 'Marcher' (to march/walk) is the physical act, not the political right. 'Pacifiquement' (peacefully) — the adverb is formed from 'pacifique' + -ment. France has a strong tradition of manifestations — public demonstrations are a fundamental part of French political culture and social negotiation.

items.question: 'La ___ des pouvoirs est un principe essentiel de la démocratie.' — Democratic theory.
items.translation: (The separation of powers is an essential principle of democracy.)
items.explanation: 'La séparation des pouvoirs' (separation of powers) is a fixed political science expression, codified by Montesquieu in 'De l'esprit des lois' (1748). 'Division' implies conflict. 'Distribution' is for goods or resources. 'Répartition' (allocation) is too administrative. 'Des pouvoirs' (of powers) uses the partitive 'des' (de + les). 'Un principe essentiel' — note 'essentiel' is masculine, agreeing with 'principe.' This concept, theorized by a Frenchman, became the foundation of modern democratic constitutions worldwide.

items.question: 'Le ___ a déposé une plainte pour diffamation.' — Legal actions.
items.translation: (The plaintiff filed a complaint for defamation.)
items.explanation: 'Le plaignant' (the plaintiff/complainant) is the standard legal term for the person filing a complaint. 'Accusateur' (accuser) is informal and biased. 'Demandeur' (claimant) is used in civil law, while 'plaignant' applies to criminal complaints. 'Poursuivant' (pursuer) is archaic. 'Déposer une plainte' (to file a complaint) is ❤️a fixed legal expression — literally 'to deposit a complaint.' 'Pour diffamation' (for defamation) — French defamation law is notably strict, protecting individuals' honor. The preposition 'pour' indicates the legal grounds for the complaint.

name: Science et Recherche (Science & Research)
description: Discuss scientific concepts, research findings, and academic topics using appropriate French terminology and formal academic register

items.question: 'Les chercheurs ont ___ une nouvelle espèce de papillon.' — Scientific discovery.
items.translation: (The researchers discovered a new species of butterfly.)
items.explanation: 'Découvrir' (to discover) has the irregular past participle 'découvert.' This is the standard verb for scientific discoveries — finding something that already existed but was unknown. 'Trouvé' (found) is too casual. 'Inventé' (invented) means creating something new, not discovering what exists. 'Détecté' (detected) implies technical instruments. 'Une nouvelle espèce' (a new species) — 'espèce' is feminine (une espèce), a common gender mistake. 'De papillon' (of butterfly) — note 'papillon' is singular after 'espèce de' even though there are many butterflies.

items.question: 'Cette ___ scientifique a été publiée dans une revue internationale.' — Academic publishing.
items.translation: (This scientific study was published in an international journal.)
items.explanation: 'Étude' (study) is the standard term for a formal scientific research paper. 'Recherche' (research) refers to the broader field or process. 'Analyse' (analysis) is a component of a study, not the study itself. 'Investigation' exists in French but carries police/detective connotations rather than academic ones. 'Une revue internationale' (an international journal) — 'revue' is the French word for academic journal, not 'journal' (which means newspaper). 'A été publiée' shows feminine agreement with 'étude.' Academic French requires mastery of these precise distinctions.

items.question: 'L'___ a confirmé que ce traitement est efficace.' — Medical research.
items.translation: (The experiment confirmed that this treatment is effective.)
items.explanation: 'Expérience' in French means both experiment AND experience — context determines the meaning. Here, with 'a confirmé,' it clearly means experiment. 'Experiment' is not a French word — a common anglicism to avoid. 'Essai' (trial) refers specifically to clinical trials ('essai clinique'). 'Test' exists in French but is more informal. 'Ce traitement est efficace' — 'efficace' (effective/efficient) doesn't change form for gender (un traitement efficace, une méthode efficace). The conjunction 'que' (that) introduces the subordinate clause.

items.question: 'Le ___ de cette recherche sera présenté lors d'une conférence.' — Academic events.
items.translation: (The result of this research will be presented at a conference.)
items.explanation: 'Résultat' (result) is the standard academic term for research outcomes. 'Effet' (effect) implies causation. 'Produit' (product) is for manufacturing. 'Fruit' (fruit/outcome) is literary — 'le fruit de son travail' (the fruit of one's labor). 'Sera présenté' (will be presented) is the passive future tense: future of 'être' + past participle. 'Lors de' (during/at) is the formal preposition for events — more elegant than 'pendant' or 'à.' Academic French extensively uses 'lors de' for conference contexts. 'Une conférence' can mean both a conference (event) and a lecture.

items.question: 'Les données ___ que le réchauffement climatique s'accélère.' — Climate science.
items.translation: (The data show that global warming is accelerating.)
items.explanation: 'Montrer' (to show) is the standard academic verb for data presentation — neutral and evidence-based. 'Prouver' (to prove) is too strong — science shows evidence, rarely final proof. 'Dire' (to say) anthropomorphizes data. 'Expliquer' (to explain) implies interpretation beyond the data. 'Les données' (the data) is always feminine plural in French — never singular. 'Le réchauffement climatique' (global warming) is the standard French term. 'S'accélérer' (to accelerate) is reflexive — the warming accelerates itself. Academic writing requires precise verb choices that convey the appropriate degree of certainty.

items.question: 'Le laboratoire a ___ de nouveaux équipements pour ses recherches.' — Research infrastructure.
items.translation: (The laboratory acquired new equipment for its research.)
items.explanation: 'Acquérir' (to acquire) has the irregular past participle 'acquis.' This is the formal verb for institutional procurement — more professional than 'acheter' (to buy). 'Obtenu' (obtained) implies winning or earning. 'Reçu' (received) implies a gift or delivery. 'De nouveaux équipements' (new equipment) — in French, 'équipement' can be countable (plural), unlike English 'equipment' which is uncountable. Note 'de' replaces 'des' before an adjective preceding a noun — this is a key French grammar rule often tested.

items.question: 'Cette ___ permet de mieux comprendre le fonctionnement du cerveau.' — Neuroscience.
items.translation: (This technique allows us to better understand how the brain works.)
items.explanation: 'Technique' (technique/method) is the standard scientific term for a specific approach or tool. 'Technologie' (technology) refers to a broader field, not a specific method. 'Méthode' (method) is more general and theoretical. 'Procédure' (procedure) implies step-by-step protocol. 'Permet de' (allows/enables) + infinitive is a crucial academic construction — 'permettre' requires 'de' before the infinitive. 'Mieux comprendre' — the adverb 'mieux' (better) precedes the infinitive. 'Le fonctionnement' (the functioning/workings) is a nominalization from 'fonctionner' (to function).

items.question: 'Les ___ de l'étude sont encourageants pour le développement d'un vaccin.' — Research progress.
items.translation: (The findings of the study are encouraging for vaccine development.)
items.explanation: 'Résultats' (results/findings) is the standard academic term for study outcomes. 'Conclusions' (conclusions) come at the end of analysis, not from the data itself. 'Découvertes' (discoveries) implies something entirely new. 'Observations' (observations) are raw data, not processed results. 'Encourageants' (encouraging) agrees with masculine plural 'résultats.' 'Pour le développement d'un vaccin' — note the nominalization 'développement' (development) rather than using a verb. French academic style favors nominalizations, creating a more formal and impersonal register.

items.question: 'Il faut ___ cette hypothèse avant de tirer des conclusions.' — Scientific method.
items.translation: (This hypothesis must be tested before drawing conclusions.)
items.explanation: 'Vérifier une hypothèse' (to verify/test a hypothesis) is the standard French scientific expression. While 'tester' exists (borrowed from English), 'vérifier' is more traditional and preferred in academic French. 'Prouver' (to prove) presupposes the outcome. 'Essayer' (to try) is too informal for scientific context. 'Avant de + infinitive' (before -ing) — never forget the 'de' after 'avant.' 'Tirer des conclusions' (to draw conclusions) is a fixed expression — literally 'to pull conclusions.' The scientific method vocabulary in French is remarkably precise and Latin-derived.

items.question: 'La communauté ___ internationale collabore sur ce projet.' — Global research.
items.translation: (The international scientific community is collaborating on this project.)
items.explanation: 'La communauté scientifique' (the scientific community) is the standard fixed expression. 'Académique' (academic) refers to universities, not the scientific community broadly. 'Savante' (learned/scholarly) is archaic. 'Intellectuelle' (intellectual) is too broad — including non-scientists. Note 'scientifique' is placed after the noun — in French, most adjectives follow the noun, unlike English. 'Collabore sur' (collaborates on) uses 'sur' for the project topic. 'Ce projet' (this project) — the demonstrative adjective 'ce' takes masculine singular form before a consonant.

items.question: 'L'énergie ___ est une alternative aux combustibles fossiles.' — Energy science.
items.translation: (Solar energy is an alternative to fossil fuels.)
items.explanation: 'Énergie solaire' (solar energy) is the fixed scientific term. 'Du soleil' (of the sun) would be descriptive rather than technical. 'Ensoleillée' (sunny) describes weather. 'Lumineuse' (luminous) relates to light quality. 'Solaire' comes from Latin 'solaris' — many French scientific terms retain Latin roots. 'Une alternative à' (an alternative to) uses the preposition 'à.' 'Les combustibles fossiles' (fossil fuels) — 'combustible' (fuel) is both noun and adjective. France generates about 70% of its electricity from nuclear power — a unique energy policy that makes energy vocabulary politically important.

items.question: 'Le professeur a ___ une conférence sur l'intelligence artificielle.' — Academic talks.
items.translation: (The professor gave a lecture on artificial intelligence.)
items.explanation: 'Donner une conférence' (to give a lecture) is the standard academic expression. While 'faire une conférence' also works colloquially, 'donner' is the formal, preferred phrasing. 'Présenter' (to present) is used for specific presentations within a conference. 'Tenir' (to hold) applies to organizing an event, not delivering content. 'Sur l'intelligence artificielle' uses 'sur' for the topic. Note 'intelligence artificielle' (IA in French, not AI) — French creates its own abbreviations from French words. French academia has a strong tradition of public lectures accessible to general audiences.

items.question: 'Les ___ génétiques ont révolutionné la médecine moderne.' — Biotechnology.
items.translation: (Genetic advances have revolutionized modern medicine.)
items.explanation: 'Avancées' (advances/breakthroughs) is the most dynamic term for scientific progress. Note the feminine plural form — 'avancée' is always feminine as a noun, even though 'avancé' (advanced) as an adjective follows normal agreement rules. 'Progrès' (progress) is uncountable and more general. 'Développements' (developments) is more gradual. 'Évolutions' (evolutions) implies natural change. 'Génétiques' agrees with 'avancées' (feminine plural). 'Ont révolutionné' — using 'avoir' as auxiliary. French bioethics law is among the most debated in Europe.

items.question: 'Ce phénomène ___ être étudié plus en profondeur.' — Research planning.
items.translation: (This phenomenon needs to be studied more in depth.)
items.explanation: 'Devoir' (must/need to) in present tense expresses strong necessity — 'doit être étudié' (must be studied). 'Peut' (can) expresses possibility, not necessity. 'Va' (will) is future intention without urgency. 'Devrait' (should) is conditional — softer than 'doit.' 'Plus en profondeur' (more in depth) is a common academic expression — 'en profondeur' is adverbial. 'Ce phénomène' (this phenomenon) — 'phénomène' is masculine in French (unlike some learners expect). The passive 'être étudié' (to be studied) is common in academic writing to maintain objectivity.

items.question: 'La ___ spatiale française est gérée par le CNES.' — Space science.
items.translation: (French space research is managed by CNES.)
items.explanation: 'Recherche spatiale' (space research) is the standard term for the scientific pursuit of space knowledge. 'Science' is too broad. 'Exploration' implies physical travel. 'Étude' is too narrow — a single study. 'Le CNES' (Centre national d'études spatiales) is France's space agency, equivalent to NASA. Note that French acronyms often keep the French word order — CNES not NSSC. 'Est gérée' (is managed) shows feminine agreement with 'recherche.' France is a major player in space science through CNES and ESA (Agence spatiale européenne), with the Ariane rockets launching from French Guiana.

name: Révision Générale (General Review)
description: Comprehensive review mixing grammar topics across all levels to consolidate French knowledge and identify areas needing reinforcement

items.question: 'Si j'_____ le temps, je voyagerais en France.' — Mixed conditional.
items.translation: (If I had the time, I would travel to France.)
items.explanation: This si-clause type 2 (hypothetical present) requires the imparfait in the 'si' clause and the conditionnel présent in the main clause. 'Si j'avais... je voyagerais' expresses an unlikely but possible condition. Using 'aurais' (conditionnel) after 'si' is a classic error — French never allows conditionnel directly after 'si.' The 'ai' (présent) would make it a type 1 (real possibility), changing the meaning. This pattern — si + imparfait, conditionnel — is one of the most tested structures in French and essential for expressing wishes, dreams, and hypothetical scenarios.

items.question: 'Elle _____ partie avant que nous arrivions.' — Tense sequencing.
items.translation: (She had left before we arrived.)
items.explanation: With 'partir' (an intransitive verb of movement), the plus-que-parfait uses 'être' as auxiliary: 'elle était partie.' This is the full form 'était partie' where 'était' is the imparfait of 'être' and 'partie' is the past participle agreeing with the feminine subject 'elle.' The plus-que-parfait marks an action completed before another past action ('avant que nous arrivions' — before we arrived). Note the past participle agreement: 'partie' (feminine -e) because the subject is 'elle.' Verbs using 'être' in compound tenses (DR MRS VANDERTRAMP) always agree.

items.question: 'Les livres _____ j'ai achetés sont intéressants.' — Relative pronoun + agreement.
items.translation: (The books that I bought are interesting.)
items.explanation: 'Que' is the direct object relative pronoun (the books = object of 'acheter'). Critical point: when 'que' precedes 'avoir' compound tenses, the past participle agrees with the preceding direct object. 'Les livres' is masculine plural, so 'achetés' gets the -s. Compare: 'les livres qui sont intéressants' (qui = subject). 'Dont' would mean 'of which.' 'Lesquels' is used after prepositions. This sentence tests three concepts simultaneously: relative pronoun choice, past participle agreement with preceding DO, and the que/qui distinction — a hallmark of advanced French mastery.

items.question: 'Il faut que tu _____ la vérité.' — Subjunctive trigger.
items.translation: (It is necessary that you know the truth.)
items.explanation: 'Il faut que' is one of the most common subjunctive triggers in French, expressing necessity or obligation. After 'il faut que,' you must use the subjonctif présent. 'Savoir' has an irregular subjunctive: que je sache, que tu saches, qu'il sache, que nous sachions, que vous sachiez, qu'ils sachent. 'Sais' (indicatif présent) would be grammatically incorrect here. 'Sauras' (futur) and 'savais' (imparfait) are also indicative forms. The subjunctive is required because 'il faut que' expresses an obligation — a subjective judgment about what should happen, not a statement of fact.

items.question: 'Nous nous _____ rencontrés à Paris il y a cinq ans.' — Reflexive passé composé.
items.translation: (We met each other in Paris five years ago.)
items.explanation: Reflexive verbs always use 'être' as their auxiliary in compound tenses: 'nous nous sommes rencontrés.' The structure is: subject + reflexive pronoun + être (conjugated) + past participle. With reflexive verbs using 'être,' the participle agrees with the subject when the reflexive pronoun is a direct object. Since 'nous' refers to a group including males (or mixed), 'rencontrés' takes masculine plural agreement (-s). 'Il y a cinq ans' (five years ago) — 'il y a' + time = ago. This sentence tests three rules simultaneously: reflexive auxiliary choice, participle agreement, and time expressions.

items.question: 'Je ne _____ ai donné ni les clés ni le code.' — Double negation + pronouns.
items.translation: (I gave them neither the keys nor the code.)
items.explanation: 'Leur' (to them) is the indirect object pronoun placed before the auxiliary 'ai.' The structure 'donner quelque chose à quelqu'un' requires an indirect object for the person. 'Leur' (invariable pronoun) replaces 'à eux/elles.' Don't confuse with 'leurs' (possessive adjective, variable). 'Les' would be a direct object pronoun (them = objects). 'Lui' is singular (to him/her). Note the double negation 'ni...ni' (neither...nor) which replaces 'pas': 'ne...ni...ni.' The pronoun order before the verb follows: me/te/nous/vous → le/la/les → lui/leur → y → en. Here 'leur' precedes 'ai donné' correctly.

items.question: 'C'est la plus belle ville _____ j'aie jamais visitée.' — Superlative + subjunctive.
items.translation: (It's the most beautiful city I have ever visited.)
items.explanation: 'Que' is the direct object relative pronoun (I visited the city). After superlatives ('la plus belle'), French uses the subjunctive in the relative clause — 'que j'aie visitée' rather than 'que j'ai visitée.' This is because the superlative expresses a subjective judgment. Notice the triple agreement: 'visitée' agrees with 'ville' (feminine singular) because with avoir, the past participle agrees with a preceding direct object ('que' = 'ville'). The subjunctive 'aie' (from avoir) signals that this is an opinion, not absolute fact. This construction — superlative + que + subjunctive + agreement — is a hallmark of sophisticated French.

items.question: '_____ avoir mangé, nous sommes allés nous promener.' — Infinitive past.
items.translation: (After having eaten, we went for a walk.)
items.explanation: 'Après avoir mangé' uses the infinitif passé (past infinitive) — 'avoir/être + past participle.' Only 'après' (after) takes the past infinitive to express completed action before the main clause. 'Avant de' (before) uses the present infinitive: 'avant de manger.' 'Pour' (in order to) expresses purpose with present infinitive. 'Sans' (without) also uses present infinitive: 'sans manger.' This time-sequencing construction is elegant and concise, replacing a full subordinate clause ('Après que nous avons mangé'). Note 'nous sommes allés nous promener' — double reflexive with 'se promener' using être.

items.question: '_____ tu viennes ou non, la fête aura lieu.' — Subjunctive with concession.
items.translation: (Whether you come or not, the party will take place.)
items.explanation: 'Que...ou non' (whether...or not) requires the subjunctive because it expresses uncertainty about the outcome. 'Viennes' is the subjunctive of 'venir' (que je vienne, que tu viennes). This concessive construction presents the condition as irrelevant to the result. 'Si' would require the indicative and a different structure ('Si tu viens ou non...' is less elegant). 'Quand' (when) and 'Comme' (as) don't fit this concessive meaning. 'Avoir lieu' is a fixed expression meaning 'to take place' — 'la fête aura lieu' (future tense). This structure showcases how the subjunctive expresses attitude rather than factual time.

items.question: 'Il s'est _____ les mains avant de passer à table.' — Reflexive with body part.
items.translation: (He washed his hands before sitting down to eat.)
items.explanation: 'Se laver les mains' — when a reflexive verb has a direct object ('les mains'), the reflexive pronoun becomes indirect, and the past participle does NOT agree. 'Il s'est lavé les mains' — 'lavé' stays masculine singular regardless. Compare: 'Il s'est lavé' (no direct object → agrees with subject). 'Les mains' is the direct object, making 'se' indirect. This is one of French's subtlest agreement rules. 'Lavés' would be wrong (no plural agreement needed). 'Lavée' is feminine (wrong gender). 'Avant de passer à table' — 'passer à table' is the idiomatic expression for sitting down to eat, not literally 'passing the table.'

items.question: 'Ce sont les étudiants _____ je t'ai parlé.' — Relative pronoun after preposition.
items.translation: (These are the students I told you about.)
items.explanation: 'Dont' replaces 'de + noun' in relative clauses. The verb 'parler de' (to talk about) requires 'de,' so when the complement becomes a relative pronoun, 'dont' is used. 'Parler de quelqu'un' → les étudiants dont je t'ai parlé. 'Que' is for direct objects (no preposition). 'Qui' is for subjects or after prepositions (but not 'de'). 'Desquels' is technically possible but overly formal and used mainly after compound prepositions ('à côté desquels'). 'Dont' is elegant and concise — one of French's most distinctive relative pronouns with no direct English equivalent. It replaces 'de qui,' 'duquel,' and 'de quoi' in most cases.

items.question: 'Elle a fait _____ ses enfants par la voisine.' — Causative faire.
items.translation: (She had her children looked after by the neighbor.)
items.explanation: The causative 'faire + infinitive' construction: 'faire garder' means 'to have (someone) look after.' The infinitive always stays unchanged after 'faire' — never conjugated or agreed. 'Fait' in 'a fait' does NOT agree with a preceding direct object when followed by an infinitive (special rule for faire + infinitive). So it's 'elle a fait garder' not 'elle a faite garder.' 'Par la voisine' (by the neighbor) introduces the agent, similar to passive voice. This causative construction is extremely productive in French: faire réparer (have repaired), faire construire (have built), faire comprendre (make understand).

items.question: 'Bien qu'il _____ fatigué, il a continué à travailler.' — Concessive subjunctive.
items.translation: (Although he was tired, he continued to work.)
items.explanation: 'Bien que' (although) is a conjunctive expression that always requires the subjunctive. 'Soit' is the subjunctive of 'être.' This is non-negotiable in formal French — 'bien qu'il est' is considered incorrect even though it's heard in spoken French. Other concessive conjunctions requiring subjunctive: 'quoique' (although), 'malgré que' (despite that — debated). The indicative alternatives express different meanings: 'est' (factual — wrong register), 'était' (imparfait — would need 'même si'), 'serait' (conditionnel — for hypothesis). 'Continuer à' (continue to) — note 'à' before the infinitive, not 'de.' This à/de distinction with infinitives is a persistent challenge.

items.question: '_____ sont vos projets pour l'été prochain?' — Interrogative adjective.
items.translation: (What are your plans for next summer?)
items.explanation: 'Quels' agrees with 'projets' (masculine plural). The interrogative adjective 'quel' has four forms: quel (m.sg.), quelle (f.sg.), quels (m.pl.), quelles (f.pl.). It must agree with the noun it modifies in both gender and number. 'Projets' is masculine plural → quels. 'Quel' (m.sg.) doesn't match the plural. 'Quelle/Quelles' are feminine forms. Note 'l'été prochain' — adjective 'prochain' comes AFTER time expressions with articles: 'l'été prochain' but 'prochain été' (without article). 'Pour' (for) introduces purpose/plans. Unlike English 'what,' the French interrogative adjective carries gender and number information.

items.question: 'Je m'en _____ occupé dès demain.' — Pronominal + future.
items.translation: (I will take care of it starting tomorrow.)
items.explanation: 'S'occuper de' is a pronominal verb using 'être' in compound tenses and future: 'je me serai occupé' (futur antérieur) or here 'je m'en serai occupé' — with 'en' replacing 'de cela.' Wait — actually this is futur simple with the promise sense: 'je m'en serai occupé' = I will have taken care of it. The pronoun 'en' replaces 'de + thing' from 's'occuper de quelque chose.' Pronoun order: me + en before the verb. 'Aurai' would be wrong because 's'occuper' uses 'être.' 'Serais' (conditionnel) would express hypothesis. 'Dès demain' (starting tomorrow/as early as tomorrow) is more emphatic than simple 'demain.'

name: Erreurs Courantes (Common Mistakes)
description: Identify and correct the most frequent errors English speakers make in French, building awareness of persistent pitfalls and false patterns

items.question: 'Je suis _____ avec mes amis hier soir.' — Correct auxiliary for sortir.
items.translation: (I went out with my friends last night.)
items.explanation: 'Sortir' (to go out) uses 'être' as its auxiliary in passé composé when intransitive (no direct object). With 'être,' the past participle agrees with the subject. If the speaker is male: 'je suis sorti.' If female: 'je suis sortie.' Without context indicating gender, the default masculine form 'sorti' is used. Common English-speaker mistake: using 'avoir' (*j'ai sorti) — which only applies when 'sortir' is transitive ('j'ai sorti la poubelle' = I took out the trash). The DR MRS VANDERTRAMP verbs (or 'House of Être') all use 'être' when intransitive. This auxiliary distinction doesn't exist in English.

items.question: 'Il _____ en France depuis trois ans.' — Present tense with 'depuis.'
items.translation: (He has been living in France for three years.)
items.explanation: One of the most systematic errors English speakers make! French uses the PRESENT tense with 'depuis' (since/for) when the action is still ongoing. English uses present perfect ('has been living'), but French says 'il habite' (present). 'A habité' (passé composé) would imply he stopped living there. 'Habitait' (imparfait) would be used with 'depuis' only in past narratives ('il habitait en France depuis trois ans quand...'). This rule extends to all ongoing situations: 'J'attends depuis une heure' (I have been waiting for an hour) — present tense in French, present perfect continuous in English. Master this and you'll sound immediately more natural.

items.question: 'Elle est plus grande _____ moi.' — Comparison structure.
items.translation: (She is taller than me.)
items.explanation: French comparisons use 'que' (than), not 'de' or 'comme.' 'Plus...que' (more...than), 'moins...que' (less...than), 'aussi...que' (as...as). English speakers often mistakenly use 'de' by analogy with other constructions, or 'comme' (which means 'like/as' but not 'than'). 'Plus grande que moi' — note that stressed pronouns (moi, toi, lui, elle, nous, vous, eux, elles) are used after 'que' in comparisons, not subject pronouns. Exception: 'plus de' is used before numbers: 'plus de trois ans' (more than three years). This number exception trips up many learners who then incorrectly extend 'de' to all comparisons.

items.question: 'J'ai _____ à Marie de venir.' — Verb + preposition confusion.
items.translation: (I asked Marie to come.)
items.explanation: 'Demander à quelqu'un de faire quelque chose' (to ask someone to do something) — this double preposition pattern (à + de) is a major stumbling block. English uses one construction ('ask someone to do'), but French requires 'à' before the person and 'de' before the infinitive. The participle 'demandé' does NOT agree with 'Marie' because she's an indirect object (à Marie). Common error: *'J'ai demandé Marie' (missing 'à'). Other verbs following this pattern: dire à qqn de, permettre à qqn de, conseiller à qqn de, interdire à qqn de. This à/de structure is one of French's most distinctive verb patterns and mastering it transforms your fluency.

items.question: 'Il fait _____ aujourd'hui.' — Weather expression.
items.translation: (It is hot today.)
items.explanation: Weather expressions with 'faire' use the masculine adjective without an article: 'il fait chaud' (it's hot), 'il fait froid' (it's cold), 'il fait beau' (it's nice). English speakers often try to add articles (*il fait le chaud) or use the noun form (*il fait chaleur). 'Chaude' (feminine) is wrong because these are fixed impersonal expressions — 'il' is not referring to anything specific, so no gender agreement applies. 'Chaleur' is the noun (heat/warmth), used differently: 'la chaleur est insupportable' (the heat is unbearable). Other weather: 'il pleut' (it's raining), 'il neige' (it's snowing) — these use specific verbs, not 'faire.'

items.question: 'Je _____ souviens de cette journée.' — Reflexive pronoun agreement.
items.translation: (I remember that day.)
items.explanation: 'Se souvenir de' (to remember) is a pronominal verb requiring the reflexive pronoun matching the subject. 'Je me souviens,' 'tu te souviens,' 'il se souvient.' English speakers often forget the reflexive pronoun entirely (*je souviens) because English 'remember' isn't reflexive. 'Moi' is a stressed/disjunctive pronoun, never used as a reflexive before a verb. 'Se' would match 'il/elle/on,' not 'je.' The reflexive pronoun 'me' contracts to 'm'' before vowels: 'je m'en souviens' (I remember it). Other commonly forgotten reflexives: 's'asseoir' (to sit down), 'se taire' (to be quiet), 'se plaindre' (to complain) — all require their reflexive pronouns.

items.question: 'Je cherche _____ clés.' — Possessive with body parts/belongings.
items.translation: (I'm looking for my keys.)
items.explanation: With 'chercher' (to look for), you use possessive adjectives for personal belongings: 'mes clés' (my keys). However, this contrasts with body parts, where French typically uses the definite article: 'je me lave les mains' (I wash my hands), NOT *'mes mains.' English speakers get confused about when to use possessives vs. definite articles. Rule: with reflexive verbs involving body parts, use 'les/la/le.' With other verbs and personal objects, use possessives. 'Des' (some) would mean unspecified keys. 'Mon' is masculine singular — 'clés' is feminine plural, requiring 'mes.' Also note: 'chercher' never takes a preposition — *'chercher pour' is a classic anglicism.

items.question: 'Nous avons _____ au cinéma samedi dernier.' — Correct preposition for places.
items.translation: (We went to the cinema last Saturday.)
items.explanation: Tricky! 'Nous avons été au cinéma' uses 'avoir' + 'été' as an informal/colloquial alternative to 'nous sommes allés.' In formal French, 'aller' uses 'être': 'nous sommes allés.' But 'être' used as a main verb meaning 'went to' takes 'avoir': 'j'ai été au marché.' This dual identity of 'être' confuses many learners. With 'avoir été,' the past participle 'été' is invariable — it NEVER agrees: 'elle a été' (not *'elle a étée'). 'Allé' without -s would not agree with 'nous.' 'Allés' with 'avoir' is incorrect (*nous avons allés) — 'aller' always takes 'être.' This is one of French's quirkiest grammar points.

items.question: 'Elle a _____ les gâteaux elle-même.' — Past participle with faire meaning 'to make.'
items.translation: (She made the cakes herself.)
items.explanation: 'Faire' has the irregular past participle 'fait.' When should it agree? With 'avoir,' the participle agrees with a preceding direct object. Here, 'les gâteaux' comes AFTER 'a fait,' so no agreement: 'elle a fait les gâteaux.' BUT if reworded: 'les gâteaux qu'elle a faits' — now 'les gâteaux' precedes via 'que,' so 'faits' (m.pl.) agrees. 'Faite' (f.sg.) and 'faits' (m.pl.) would be wrong here because the object follows the verb. 'Fais' is present tense (je/tu fais). 'Elle-même' (herself) adds emphasis — note the hyphen and that 'même' agrees: 'lui-même,' 'eux-mêmes,' but 'elle-même' stays singular. This agreement rule is mechanical: check if DO precedes the verb.

items.question: 'J'aime _____ France.' — Article with country names.
items.translation: (I love France.)
items.explanation: Country names in French almost always require a definite article: 'la France,' 'le Japon,' 'les États-Unis.' English uses no article ('I love France'), leading English speakers to say *'j'aime France.' The article choice depends on gender: feminine countries (most ending in -e): la France, la Chine, l'Italie. Masculine: le Canada, le Brésil, le Portugal. Exceptions: le Mexique, le Cambodge (end in -e but are masculine). Plural: les Pays-Bas, les Philippines. With 'en' (to/in), feminine countries drop the article: 'en France.' Masculine countries use 'au': 'au Japon.' This system has no English parallel, making it a persistent error source.

items.question: 'Le livre est _____ la table.' — Correct spatial preposition.
items.translation: (The book is on the table.)
items.explanation: 'Sur' (on) indicates physical surface contact. English speakers mix up 'sur,' 'en,' 'dans,' and 'à' because their usage doesn't map directly to English. 'Sur la table' (on the table) — physical surface. 'Dans la boîte' (in the box) — enclosed space. 'À la maison' (at home) — location. 'En France' (in France) — with feminine countries. Common error: *'en la table' — 'en' is never used for furniture surfaces. Another trap: 'dans la rue' (in/on the street) not *'sur la rue.' And 'à la page 10' (on page 10), not *'sur la page.' These preposition choices must often be memorized because they follow conventions, not always logic.

items.question: 'Il y a beaucoup _____ monde ici.' — Quantity expression.
items.translation: (There are a lot of people here.)
items.explanation: After quantity expressions ('beaucoup,' 'peu,' 'trop,' 'assez,' 'tant'), French uses 'de' alone — never 'du,' 'de la,' or 'des.' 'Beaucoup de monde' (a lot of people). English speakers often add the partitive article (*beaucoup du monde) by analogy with 'du pain.' Rule: quantity word + de + noun (no article). This applies to: 'trop de travail' (too much work), 'peu de temps' (little time), 'assez d'argent' (enough money). Exception: 'la plupart des' (most of the) keeps 'des.' Also: 'bien des' (many) keeps 'des.' 'Monde' here is uncountable (people in general), not 'world.' 'Il y a' (there is/are) is invariable — always 'il y a,' never *'ils y ont.'

items.question: 'Je vais _____ coiffeur demain.' — Contracted article.
items.translation: (I'm going to the hairdresser tomorrow.)
items.explanation: 'Chez' (at/to someone's place or business) is used for people and professionals: 'chez le coiffeur' (to the hairdresser's), 'chez le médecin' (to the doctor's), 'chez moi' (to my place). English speakers default to 'au' (*au coiffeur) which would mean 'at the hairdresser' without the personal/professional nuance. 'À le' doesn't exist — it contracts to 'au.' 'Dans le' implies being inside the building. 'Chez' also works for stores named after people: 'chez Carrefour' vs 'au supermarché.' Key distinction: 'chez' = person/professional, 'à/au' = place/establishment when not referring to the person. This subtlety is one of French's signature preposition challenges.

items.question: 'Je lui ai dit _____ ne pas s'inquiéter.' — Infinitive construction.
items.translation: (I told him/her not to worry.)
items.explanation: 'Dire à quelqu'un de faire quelque chose' — 'de' introduces the infinitive action requested. Negation of the infinitive places 'ne pas' BEFORE the infinitive: 'de ne pas s'inquiéter.' In English, 'I told him not to worry' doesn't need any preposition before 'not,' but French requires 'de.' Common error: *'je lui ai dit ne pas s'inquiéter' (missing 'de'). 'À' is wrong here — although 'dire à' introduces the person (indirect object), the action uses 'de.' 'Pour' (in order to) expresses purpose, not command. 'Que' would introduce a subordinate clause ('que + indicative'): 'je lui ai dit qu'il ne devait pas s'inquiéter.' The infinitive construction with 'de' is more concise and elegant.

items.question: 'Ils _____ chaque matin à sept heures.' — Regular daily habit.
items.translation: (They wake up every morning at seven o'clock.)
items.explanation: 'Se réveiller' (to wake up) requires the reflexive pronoun 'se' — you literally 'wake yourself.' English speakers frequently drop reflexive pronouns because English doesn't mark this distinction. 'Ils se réveillent' — 'se' before the verb is essential. Without it, 'ils réveillent' means 'they wake (someone else) up' — transitive. The difference between reflexive and non-reflexive can completely change meaning: 'appeler' (to call) vs 'S'appeler' (to be called/named), 'sentir' (to smell) vs 'se sentir' (to feel). 'Se réveillons' has mismatched pronoun (se ≠ nous). 'Sont réveillés' is passive (they are woken up by someone), not habitual action in present tense.

name: Stratégies d'Examen (Exam Strategies)
description: Practice tricky grammar patterns commonly tested in French proficiency exams (DELF/DALF), focusing on subtle distinctions and advanced constructions

items.question: 'Il se peut _____ elle ait raison.' — Subjunctive after impersonal expression.
items.translation: (It's possible that she may be right.)
items.explanation: 'Il se peut que' (it's possible that) triggers the subjunctive. Before 'elle' (vowel), 'que' elides to 'qu'': 'il se peut qu'elle ait raison.' 'Ait' is the subjunctive of 'avoir' (used in 'avoir raison' = to be right). DELF/DALF traps: forgetting elision (*que elle), using indicative (*qu'elle a raison), or confusing 'il se peut que' with 'il paraît que' (which takes indicative = it seems that). Other impersonal + subjunctive: 'il est possible que,' 'il est peu probable que,' 'il vaut mieux que.' But 'il est probable que' takes INDICATIVE (probability, not possibility). This distinction between possible/probable is a classic exam trick.

items.question: 'Dût-_____ m'en coûter, je dirai la vérité.' — Literary inversion.
items.translation: (Even if it should cost me, I will tell the truth.)
items.explanation: 'Dût-il m'en coûter' is a literary/formal concessive construction using the imperfect subjunctive with inversion. 'Dût' is the imperfect subjunctive of 'devoir' (with circumflex accent distinguishing it from 'dut' passé simple). This construction means 'even if it should.' The inverted 'dût-il' creates a concessive sense without 'même si.' This is tested at DALF C1/C2 levels. Modern equivalents: 'même si cela devait me coûter.' Other literary inversions: 'fût-il' (even if he were), 'eût-il' (even if he had). The circumflex accent on 'û' is the key signal of imperfect subjunctive vs. passé simple. This register is found in literature, legal texts, and formal speeches.

items.question: 'C'est _____ j'ai le plus besoin en ce moment.' — Emphatic construction + dont.
items.translation: (That's what I need the most right now.)
items.explanation: 'Avoir besoin de' (to need) requires 'de,' so the relative pronoun must be 'dont' (which replaces 'de' + noun). In a cleft sentence with 'c'est,' the indefinite antecedent 'ce' combines with 'dont': 'ce dont j'ai besoin.' 'Ce que' would be for direct objects ('ce que je veux'). 'Ce qui' is for subjects ('ce qui m'intéresse'). 'Celui que' refers to a specific masculine noun ('the one that'). This three-way distinction — ce qui (subject), ce que (direct object), ce dont (de + object) — is heavily tested. Add 'ce à quoi' (what... to/at) for verbs with 'à': 'ce à quoi je pense' (what I'm thinking about). Master these four and you've conquered relative clauses.

items.question: 'Toute fatiguée qu'elle _____, elle a terminé le marathon.' — Concessive with 'tout...que.'
items.translation: (Tired as she was, she finished the marathon.)
items.explanation: 'Tout(e)...que' + INDICATIVE is a concessive construction meaning 'however much... as.' Unlike 'bien que' (which takes subjunctive), 'tout...que' uses the indicative because it presents the concession as a FACT. 'Toute fatiguée qu'elle était' — factual: she WAS tired. Note 'toute' agrees with 'fatiguée' (feminine) — 'tout' before feminine adjectives starting with a consonant takes the feminine form 'toute.' Before vowels or masculine: invariable 'tout.' 'Soit' (subjunctive) would be wrong here. 'Fut' (passé simple) is possible in literary style. This tout/toute agreement + indicative mood distinction is a high-level exam discriminator.

items.question: 'Je ne sais pas _____ de ces deux solutions est la meilleure.' — Interrogative pronoun.
items.translation: (I don't know which of these two solutions is the best.)
items.explanation: 'Laquelle' (which one, feminine) is the interrogative pronoun used when choosing among specific items. 'De ces deux solutions' narrows the choice, requiring the pronoun form (laquelle) rather than the adjective form (quelle). 'Solutions' is feminine → 'laquelle' (not 'lequel'). Forms: lequel (m.sg.), laquelle (f.sg.), lesquels (m.pl.), lesquelles (f.pl.). With 'à': auquel, à laquelle. With 'de': duquel, de laquelle. 'Quelle' is an adjective requiring a noun (*quelle solution). 'Celle' is demonstrative ('the one'). 'Qui' (who) doesn't distinguish gender. This embedded question ('je ne sais pas + indirect question') keeps the declarative word order — no inversion after 'je ne sais pas.'

items.question: 'À peine étions-nous arrivés _____ il a commencé à pleuvoir.' — Literary inversion trigger.
items.translation: (We had barely arrived when it started to rain.)
items.explanation: 'À peine...que' (hardly/barely...when) is a correlative construction. When 'à peine' begins a sentence, it triggers subject-verb inversion: 'à peine étions-nous arrivés' (not *'à peine nous étions arrivés'). The second clause uses 'que' (not 'quand' or 'lorsque'). This is formal/literary style tested at B2-C1 levels. Normal order: 'Nous étions à peine arrivés quand il a commencé à pleuvoir.' Note 'étions arrivés' — plus-que-parfait with 'être' (arriver = movement verb). Past participle 'arrivés' agrees with 'nous' (assuming masculine plural). Other inversion triggers: 'peut-être,' 'à peine,' 'sans doute,' 'aussi' (therefore) — all trigger inversion in formal French.

items.question: 'Il n'est pas de problème _____ ne puisse être résolu.' — Expletive 'ne' + relative clause.
items.translation: (There is no problem that cannot be solved.)
items.explanation: 'Qui' is the subject relative pronoun (the problem cannot be solved — 'problème' is the subject of 'puisse être résolu'). This sentence features the expletive 'ne' (ne explétif): 'qui ne puisse être résolu' — the 'ne' doesn't negate; it's a formal stylistic marker after negative main clauses. The subjunctive 'puisse' appears because the relative clause follows an indefinite/negative antecedent ('pas de problème' — no problem). When the antecedent is negated or indefinite, the subjunctive is often used in the relative clause to express potentiality rather than fact. 'Être résolu' — passive infinitive. This triple construction (expletive ne + subjunctive + passive) is DALF-level sophistication.

items.question: 'Pour peu qu'il _____ beau, nous irons à la plage.' — Minimal condition.
items.translation: (Provided the weather is nice, we'll go to the beach.)
items.explanation: 'Pour peu que' (provided that/if only) requires the subjunctive because it presents a minimal condition — the slightest circumstance needed. 'Fasse' is the subjunctive of 'faire' (que je fasse, qu'il fasse). 'Il fait beau' (it's nice/sunny) becomes 'qu'il fasse beau' in the subjunctive. 'Fait' (indicative) is wrong after 'pour peu que.' 'Fera' (future) and 'ferait' (conditional) don't follow this construction. Similar minimal-condition expressions: 'à condition que' (on condition that), 'pourvu que' (provided that), 'à moins que' (unless) — all require subjunctive. Exam tip: when you see 'que' after a conjunction expressing condition, doubt, or desire, default to subjunctive.

items.question: 'C'est la seule personne en _____ j'aie confiance.' — Preposition + relative pronoun.
items.translation: (She's the only person I trust.)
items.explanation: 'Avoir confiance en quelqu'un' (to trust someone) → the preposition 'en' + relative pronoun for a person = 'en qui.' After prepositions referring to people, 'qui' is used (not 'que'): 'en qui,' 'pour qui,' 'avec qui,' 'sans qui.' 'Laquelle' (en laquelle) is grammatically possible but stylistically heavy. 'Dont' replaces 'de,' not 'en.' 'Que' is never used after prepositions. The subjunctive 'aie' (from avoir) appears because 'la seule' (the only) — like superlatives — can trigger subjunctive in the relative clause, expressing the unique/exceptional nature subjectively. This combination — preposition + qui + subjunctive after 'seul' — is a classic DALF discriminator.

items.question: 'Non seulement il parle français, _____ il le parle couramment.' — Correlative conjunction.
items.translation: (Not only does he speak French, but he speaks it fluently.)
items.explanation: 'Non seulement...mais encore' (not only...but also/moreover) is the formal correlative conjunction. In everyday French, 'mais aussi' is acceptable, but 'mais encore' is the literary/exam-level form that adds emphasis. 'Et aussi' is too simple and doesn't create the correlative structure. 'Et même' (and even) is close but lacks the 'non seulement' pairing. Note 'il le parle' — 'le' replaces 'français' as a direct object pronoun, avoiding repetition. 'Couramment' (fluently) — adverb formed from 'courant' + -ment (with the doubled 'm'). Exam variations: 'non seulement...mais en plus,' 'non seulement...mais qui plus est.' The register choice reveals your mastery level.

items.question: 'Quoi qu'il en _____, nous devons agir.' — Fixed subjunctive expression.
items.translation: (Be that as it may, we must act.)
items.explanation: 'Quoi qu'il en soit' (be that as it may/whatever the case) is a fixed expression using the subjunctive of 'être.' This is ALWAYS subjunctive — it's a set phrase expressing concession about unknown circumstances. Critical distinction: 'quoi que' (two words) = whatever + subjunctive vs. 'quoique' (one word) = although + subjunctive. 'Quoi qu'il en soit' — 'en' refers to the situation being discussed. 'Est' (indicative) would be wrong in this fixed expression. 'Sera' (future) and 'serait' (conditional) don't fit this structure. This expression is extremely common in formal spoken and written French — using it correctly signals advanced competence. It's equivalent to English 'at any rate' or 'regardless.'

items.question: 'Il s'en est _____ de peu.' — Pronominal + impersonal construction.
items.translation: (It was a close call. / He barely avoided it.)
items.explanation: 'S'en falloir de peu' (to be a close call/to nearly happen) — 'fallu' is the past participle of 'falloir' (invariable, irregular). This impersonal reflexive construction uses 'il s'en est fallu de peu' or 'il s'en est fallu de peu que + subjonctif.' 'Fallu' never agrees — 'falloir' is exclusively used with impersonal 'il.' Common misspellings: *'falu' (missing l), *'falli' (wrong pattern). 'Fallut' is the passé simple (literary past). The pronoun 'en' is integral to this expression — you can't say *'il s'est fallu de peu.' This construction tests multiple advanced concepts: impersonal verb, reflexive construction, 'en' pronoun, and invariable participle. Mastering such fixed expressions is key to C1 proficiency.

items.question: 'Quelque brillants que _____ ses arguments, il n'a convaincu personne.' — Concessive with 'quelque...que.'
items.translation: (However brilliant his arguments were, he convinced no one.)
items.explanation: 'Quelque + adjective + que + subjunctive' (however...may be) is a formal concessive construction. 'Quelque brillants que soient ses arguments' — 'quelque' here is an adverb (invariable) modifying the adjective 'brillants,' followed by 'que' + subjunctive. Don't confuse with 'quelques' (adjective, plural = some). 'Soient' is subjunctive of 'être.' 'Sont' (indicative) is wrong in this construction. 'Fussent' (imperfect subjunctive) is possible in very literary style. Note the inversion: 'que soient ses arguments' (not *'que ses arguments soient'). 'Convaincre' has the irregular participle 'convaincu.' 'Personne' as negative complement requires 'ne': 'il n'a convaincu personne.' This construction is rare but impressive at C1/C2 level.

items.question: 'Elle aurait aimé que nous _____ venus plus tôt.' — Subjunctive in past wish.
items.translation: (She would have liked us to have come earlier.)
items.explanation: 'Aimer que' + subjunctive — after 'aurait aimé' (conditional past of 'aimer'), the subordinate clause uses the subjonctif passé to express a past unfulfilled wish. 'Que nous soyons venus' — 'soyons' (subj. présent of être) + 'venus' (past participle) = subjonctif passé. In formal/literary French, 'fussions' (subj. imparfait of être) + 'venus' = subjonctif plus-que-parfait, which is the 'correct' literary form. Modern standard French uses subj. passé even after conditional past main verbs. 'Sommes' (indicative) is wrong after 'que' + emotion verb. 'Serions' (conditional) doesn't exist in subjunctive. 'Venus' agrees with masculine 'nous.' This sequence of tenses is a classic C1 exam point.

items.question: 'Il n'y a pas _____ je ne puisse comprendre dans cette leçon.' — Double negation scope.
items.translation: (There's nothing I can't understand in this lesson.)
items.explanation: 'Il n'y a pas ce que je ne puisse comprendre' — wait, actually the most natural form is 'il n'y a rien que je ne puisse comprendre.' But with the gap structure: 'pas + ce que' (not + what). 'Ce que' is the indefinite relative pronoun for direct objects (I understand something → ce que je comprends). 'Ce qui' would be for subjects. 'Que' alone needs a specific antecedent. 'Qui' is for people/subjects. The subjunctive 'puisse' appears after the negative main clause (negation of existence → doubt/potentiality). The expletive 'ne' in 'ne puisse' reinforces the negative scope without adding negation. The double 'ne' (pas...ne) creates an emphatic positive: 'there is nothing that I cannot understand' = I can understand everything. This is peak syntactic sophistication.
